**Summary.** This paper analyzes the spatial and particle-particle entanglement in 1D quantum walks of two bosonic particles, comparing results for distinguishable and indistinguishable cases. Using various entanglement measures, the authors show that entanglement dynamics are highly sensitive to the onsite repulsion strength ($U/J$) and the initial state preparation. The findings highlight the general applicability of certain entanglement measures across particle statistics.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work provides a detailed quantitative comparison of entanglement dynamics in bosonic quantum walks, offering insights into how particle indistinguishability affects entanglement measures, which is crucial for understanding quantum simulation fidelity in many-body systems.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper investigates the spatial and particle-particle entanglement dynamics in one-dimensional quantum walks involving two bosonic particles.

**Main result.** The entanglement measures exhibit non-monotonic behavior as the onsite repulsion ($U/J$) increases, and the left-right entanglement measure proves robustly applicable to both distinguishable and indistinguishable particle cases.

**Method.** The study employs exact time evolution calculations using the time-evolution operator and calculates entanglement using measures derived from the entropy of coarse-grained states and singular value decompositions (SVD) of the Fock state coefficients.

**Model / system.** The system models two bosonic particles undergoing continuous-time quantum walks on a 1D lattice, governed by a Hamiltonian related to the Bose-Hubbard model. The analysis compares results for distinguishable and indistinguishable particles.

**Key observables.** Left-right entanglement entropy ($E_{SLR}$), particle-particle entanglement (based on SVD of Fock state coefficients), and time-evolved occupation numbers $\langle n(x) angle$.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Onsite repulsion ratio ($U/J$), initial state type (separable, entangled, doubly occupied), and lattice size ($L=70$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis focuses on two-particle systems to manage computational complexity, and the left-right entanglement measure is shown to be a generalizable tool for both particle types.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the time evolution of entanglement measures (Left-right and $\uparrow, \downarrow$) for various initial states and varying $U/J$ ratios for both distinguishable and indistinguishable particles, alongside plots of occupation number dynamics.

**Paper structure.** The paper systematically introduces the physical model and Hamiltonian, defines multiple entanglement measures for both particle types, and then analyzes the time evolution and dependence of these measures on initial conditions and the onsite repulsion strength ($U/J$).

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We present entanglement measures between spatially separated regions and between two distinguishable or indistinguishable particles in one-dimensional two-particle continuous-time quantum walks governed by the Hubbard Hamiltonian. The left-right entanglement checks the entropy of coarse-grained states counting the numbers of particles on the left and right halves of the lattice while the particle-particle entanglement is based on the entropy of the singular values of the time-evolved Fock state. With separable, entangled, and doubly occupied initial states, we examine initial entanglement and the following growth in different entanglement measures. While the entanglement measures of the indistinguishable cases resemble those of the distinguishable cases when the initial states are comparable, the long-time limits of the entanglement measures are typically non-monotonic as the onsite repulsion increases. We also discuss possible implications for future research of entanglement in multi-particle quantum dynamics.

</details>

**Summary.** This work characterizes the magneto-optical properties of GeSn/SiGeSn quantum well heterostructures using advanced PL spectroscopy. By measuring shifts in photon energy under applied magnetic fields, the authors confirmed key parameters like the effective g-factor and excitonic mass. This research validates the material platform for developing scalable hole spin qubits for quantum information processing.

**Why it may be interesting.** The direct measurement of the effective g-factor and confirmation of spin-orbit coupling in a solid-state semiconductor platform directly addresses the requirements for realizing controllable spin qubits.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The study aims to characterize the magneto-optical properties of GeSn and GeSn/SiGeSn heterostructures to advance their use as platforms for hole spin qubits.

**Main result.** The measurements successfully confirmed theoretically expected magneto-optical effects, including diamagnetic shifts, Landau quantization, and Zeeman splitting, yielding an effective g-factor of ~2.

**Method.** Magneto-photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy was performed using double modulation Fourier transform infrared-based techniques on double quantum well heterostructures.

**Model / system.** The physical system is a GeSn/SiGeSn double quantum well heterostructure, which utilizes the direct bandgap potential of GeSn to host hole spin qubits for quantum information processing.

**Key observables.** PL intensity, photon energy dependence on temperature and magnetic field, diamagnetic shift, Landau quantization, and Zeeman splitting.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Effective g-factor (~2), excitonic reduced mass (~0.04 me), and exciton binding energy (calculated).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis assumes that the observed Zeeman splitting confirms strong spin-orbit interaction, enabling electrically driven spin control.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate temperature-dependent PL spectra, plots showing PL intensity and photon energy vs. temperature, and polarization-angle resolved PL spectra fitted to oscillatory functions under applied magnetic fields.

**Paper structure.** The paper follows a structure of initial characterization (temperature/power dependence) leading to detailed magneto-optical measurements (low and high magnetic fields) to extract fundamental parameters like the g-factor and exciton mass.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Hole spin qubits in germanium (Ge)-based heterostructures have demonstrated their potential for scalable quantum information processing using all-electrical gate operations. Furthermore, the emerging material platform of germanium-tin (GeSn) can feature a direct bandgap, which makes it promising for establishing spin-photon interfaces for quantum networking. Here, we perform magneto-photoluminescence measurements of a Ge0.88Sn0.12/Si0.02Ge0.89Sn0.09 double quantum well using the double modulation Fourier transform infrared-based photoluminescence spectroscopy. Our measurements reveal theoretically expected diamagnetic shift at low magnetic fields as well as the linear trend of zeroth-level Landau quantization at higher fields and Zeeman-induced polarization-dependent energy shifts at +/- 12 T. We extract an effective g-factor of ~ 2 and an excitonic reduced mass of ~ 0.04 me consistent with previous estimations for heavy-hole Γ-valley excitons. The observation of sizable Zeeman splitting is consistent with strong spin-orbit interaction in Ge-based hole systems, which can enable electrically driven spin control. Our analysis can be adopted for studying and evaluating group-IV semiconductor heterostructures as hosts for hole spin qubits toward scalable quantum information processing.

</details>

**Summary.** This paper details a method to engineer and measure large Chern numbers in optical Floquet quasicrystals using cold atoms. By analyzing the quasienergy spectrum and performing Thouless pumping measurements, the authors demonstrate that the transport properties are directly governed by the system's topological invariants. This opens new avenues for realizing and studying complex topological phases in controllable quantum simulators.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work directly bridges the concepts of topological invariants (Chern numbers) with controllable quantum simulation platforms (cold atoms in optical lattices) using time-periodic driving, which is a major focus in modern AMO physics.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The primary goal is to achieve and experimentally probe the realization of large Chern numbers in topological systems using optical Floquet quasicrystals.

**Main result.** The study proposes a scheme where Thouless pumping can directly measure these large Chern numbers, revealing a strong connection between transport features and the quasienergy spectrum.

**Method.** The analysis employs the gap labeling theorem to characterize Chern numbers and uses Thouless pumping measurements to probe the topological properties of the system.

**Model / system.** The system is an optical Floquet quasicrystal realized with cold atoms, modeled by a generalized Aubry-Andr´e-Harper (GAAH) Hamiltonian subjected to time-periodic driving.

**Key observables.** Chern numbers ($C_i$), Thouless pumping current/velocity, quasienergy spectrum, and the Inverse Participation Ratio (IPR).

**Important parameters / regimes.** The analysis explores different Floquet driving regimes, including off-resonant, resonant, and semi-adiabatic regimes, controlled by modulation frequency ($\omega$) and amplitude ($\gamma$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis relies on the high-frequency approximation for the effective Hamiltonian in the off-resonant regime, and approximations like the tight-binding limit.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate Thouless pumping dynamics, the quasienergy spectrum in different regimes (off-resonant vs. resonant), and the dependence of pumping velocities and IPR on modulation parameters.

**Paper structure.** The paper builds from establishing the model (GAAH Hamiltonian) to analyzing the quasienergy spectrum using gap labeling, then connecting this to transport properties via Thouless pumping measurements across various driving regimes.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Chern numbers are central to correlated and topological phenomena, yet most topological systems are associated with Chern numbers of order unity. Here we propose a scheme to achieve large Chern numbers in an optical Floquet quasicrystal with cold atoms, which can be directly measured via Thouless pumping. We study the quasienergy spectrum of Floquet quasicrystals and characterize the emergent Chern numbers using gap labeling theorem. We further investigate the Thouless pumping in the Floquet quasicrystal at different driving frequencies and amplitudes, revealing the connection between transport features and the quasienergy spectrum. Our findings open new avenues for exploring rich topological dynamics in Floquet quasicrystals and realizing fractional Chern insulating states.

</details>

### [Is Quantum Mechanics Universal? EWF Experiments and Non Absoluteness of Events](http://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01349v1)

**Authors:** Herve Zwirn  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2606.01349v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** 🔥 `quantum measurements` **4/5** · `measurement-induced transitions` **3/5**

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>
<div class="paper-figures-scroll">
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2606.01349_figures/2606.01349_fig1.jpg" alt="Figure 1">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> Figure 1: Charlie and Debbie inside their laboratories measure a pair of entangled particles and get respectively the results noted c and d. Super observers Alice and Bob outside of the laboratories make space-like separated measurements on the laboratories depending respectively on x and y and get results noted a and b.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2606.01349_figures/2606.01349_fig2.jpg" alt="Figure 2">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> Fig 2. Tame and Feral quantum relativism</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper tackles the measurement problem by arguing that the conflict between unitary evolution and collapse postulates stems from assuming absolute, observer-independent reality. It proposes Convivial Solipsism, a formal framework asserting that physical events are relative to the observer's perspective. This shifts the foundation of scientific objectivity from shared facts to the internal coherence of individual perspectives.

**Why it may be interesting.** This is highly relevant as it tackles the foundational interpretation of quantum mechanics, which underpins all applications in AMO and quantum information theory by defining what 'measurement' and 'reality' mean in practice.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper addresses the fundamental conceptual tension in quantum mechanics arising from the measurement problem, specifically the conflict between unitary evolution and the projection postulate.

**Main result.** The authors propose Convivial Solipsism (ConSol) as a framework that preserves quantum universality by asserting that events are not absolute facts, but rather coherent structures within individual perspectives.

**Method.** The analysis employs conceptual structure analysis, comparative taxonomy of quantum interpretations (e.g., QBism, RQM), and the development of a formal logic to model perspectival statements.

**Model / system.** The discussion centers on abstract quantum systems (qubits, general quantum states) and the conceptual framework of observation, using thought experiments like Wigner's friend to test assumptions.

**Key observables.** The state vector ($\psi$), the outcome of measurements (Type-1 elements $	ext{PO}_{ij}$), and the consistency of personal experience.

**Important parameters / regimes.** The distinction between absolute facts and perspectival descriptions; the structural requirements for intersubjectivity.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The core assumption is that scientific objectivity relies on the internal coherence of perspectival structures rather than observer-independent facts.

**Figures summary.** The notes mention a comparative table (Table 1) summarizing how various interpretations address foundational questions regarding reality and state vector status.

**Paper structure.** The paper progresses by first detailing the measurement problem contradiction, then comparing major interpretations via a taxonomy, and finally developing a formal logic (syntax and axioms) for Convivial Solipsism to formalize the non-absoluteness of events.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Extended Wigner-type experiments reveal a fundamental tension between the universality of quantum mechanics, the absoluteness of observed events, and the structure of physical reality. While recent no-go theorems suggest that these assumptions cannot be jointly maintained, their interpretation remains unclear. In particular, the claim that events are not absolute is often left at the level of an interpretive slogan, without a precise conceptual formulation. In this paper, we analyse the conceptual structure underlying these results and show that the non-absoluteness of events requires a precise formulation in order to avoid ambiguity. We argue that many existing approaches fail to provide such a formulation, either by implicitly reintroducing observer-independent structure or by leaving key notions underdetermined. Within this framework, Convivial Solipsism offers a coherent and fully articulated account of non-absolute events, preserving the universality of quantum mechanics while avoiding the need for a global viewpoint. We show in particular that this approach resolves the apparent conflict between perspectival outcomes and scientific intersubjectivity. These results suggest that scientific objectivity does not rely on observer-independent facts, but on the internal coherence of perspectival structures.

</details>

### [Quantum Signal Processing for Linear PDEs: Circuit Design and Experimental Validation](http://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00368v1)

**Authors:** Hyeongjin Kim, Revathi Jambunathan, Jan Balewski, Daan Camps  
**Type:** both · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2606.00368v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

(a) Our methodology for running the quantum circuit on a quantum processing unit (QPU) consists of (1) an initial state preparation step to load the state |ψ(x, t = 0)⟩at time t = 0, (2) an inverse quantum Fourier transform (QFT) to transform the problem to frequency space, (3) solve the system in frequency space using small-angle approximation (SAA) or quantum signal processing (QSP), and (4) transforming back to position space using QFT to obtain |ψ(x, t)⟩. This prepares the state |ψ(x, t)⟩at time t. (b) We develop end-to-end implementations for simulating the advection equation (left), wave equation (middle), and Poisson equation (right).</figcaption>

The real part of the solution ψR is plotted on the top panel, while the bottom panel shows the difference from the classical solution. Parameters used: n = 6 qubits and c = 1.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper presents an end-to-end quantum circuit implementation for solving linear PDEs, including advection, wave, and Poisson equations. By comparing Small-Angle Approximation (SAA) against Quantum Signal Processing (QSP), the authors demonstrate that QSP offers superior accuracy for simulating these dynamics on NISQ hardware. The methodology is successfully generalized to handle complex non-homogeneous boundary conditions derived from plasma physics simulations.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work provides concrete, implementable quantum circuits for simulating fundamental physical dynamics (PDEs), directly bridging theoretical quantum algorithms with measurable results on near-term quantum hardware.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Developing and benchmarking end-to-end quantum algorithms for solving linear Partial Differential Equations (PDEs) like advection, wave, and Poisson equations.

**Main result.** The Quantum Signal Processing (QSP) augmented algorithm was experimentally shown to provide more accurate solutions than the Small-Angle Approximation (SAA) method under realistic NISQ hardware constraints.

**Method.** The approach involves transforming PDEs into the frequency domain using the Quantum Fourier Transform (QFT) and then implementing the time evolution operator using either SAA or the more robust QSP polynomial approximation.

**Model / system.** The study focuses on three linear PDEs: advection, wave, and Poisson equations. The implementation is benchmarked on numerical simulations and actual IBMQ quantum hardware, utilizing techniques like odd extension for boundary conditions.

**Key observables.** The state $|\psi(x, t)angle$ at time $t$, the difference ($\Delta$) between quantum and classical solutions, and the Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Time ($t$), system size ($N$), and the truncation degree ($d$) used in the QSP approximation.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The SAA method suffers from uncontrollable approximation error, while the QSP method requires post-selection for the Poisson solver, leading to very low success probabilities on current hardware.

**Figures summary.** Figures compare classical, SAA, and QSP quantum simulations for the advection equation, showing QSP's superior convergence. Other figures illustrate the circuit construction and the application to the Poisson equation with plasma source terms.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the quantum simulation workflow (QFT $ightarrow$ Solve in Frequency Space $ightarrow$ Inverse QFT). It details the comparison between SAA and QSP for the advection equation, then extends the methodology to solve the Poisson equation with non-homogeneous Dirichlet boundary conditions, culminating in hardware benchmarking.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Quantum algorithms offer new avenues for solving partial differential equations (PDEs). While the potential for end-to-end quantum advantage is at present not well understood, recent literature presents explicit circuit constructions for solving certain classes of linear PDEs in the frequency domain and thus offers concrete examples to study. In this work, we develop end-to-end implementations of these quantum circuits compiled to machine-level instructions and benchmark them in both numerical simulations and IBMQ hardware experiments. We focus on the advection, wave, and Poisson equations and study quantum circuits that propagate the dynamics in frequency space via the quantum Fourier transform using approximate methods based on a first-order approximation which offer compact representations with uncontrollable approximation error, and polynomial approximation methods based on quantum signal processing (QSP) leading to deeper circuits with tunable algorithmic error. In addition, we experimentally demonstrate that the QSP-augmented algorithm can provide accurate solutions under realistic hardware constraints. Finally, we extend our method to address non-homogeneous Dirichlet boundary conditions and verify it numerically for a Poisson equation with source term obtained from high-fidelity physics simulations of a capacitively coupled plasma.

</details>

The analyzer phase is varied to extract the transition detuning at the selected trigger delay. (b) Measured detuning versus TLT with compensation disabled and enabled. The trigger delay is scanned over one full period of the line signal to reconstruct the detuning throughout the AC cycle. Without compensation, the fitted detuning follows the calibrated AC line signal, shown by the...</figcaption>
</figure>

<img src="2606.00358_figures/2606.00358_fig3.jpg" alt="Figure 3">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 3.</b> FIG. 3. Haar-randomized benchmarking of magnetic-field-sensitive and magnetic-field-insensitive optical transitions with and without AC line-synchronous compensation. (a) Randomized benchmarking with compensation disabled. The magnetic- field-sensitive transition, with sensitivity κ ≈3.2 MHz/G, exhibits large sequence-dependent fluctuations and cannot be well described by the zeroth-order randomized-benchmarking decay model, indicating coherent errors from uncompensated line- synchronous detuning during the random sequences. The comparatively magnetic-field-insensitive transition, with sensitivity approximately 40 Hz/G, is less affected and gives a fitted average fidelity of Favg =...</figcaption>
</figure>

(a) Measured output distribution for a d = 16 qudit Bernstein–Vazirani experiment with AC line-synchronous compensation disabled. The probability is broadly distributed over incorrect measured values, giving an average success probability of Psucc = 10(7)%. (b) Output distribution for the same d = 16 experiment with AC line-synchronous compensation enabled. The population is concentrated along the diagonal, where the measured value equals the hidden value, increasing the success probability to Psucc = 70(9)%. The color scale in panels (a) and (b) denotes the measured probability in...</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper presents a novel, software-only technique to compensate for systematic errors induced by the AC mains power line in trapped-ion quantum computing. By precisely measuring the line-synchronous detuning and phase accumulation, the authors update control pulses to cancel these deterministic errors. This results in substantial improvements in gate fidelity and the successful execution of complex qudit algorithms.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work provides a crucial, non-hardware-intensive method for error mitigation in trapped-ion quantum computing, directly addressing a major source of systematic noise that limits scalability.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** AC mains power line-synchronous disturbances introduce coherent, time-dependent errors (detuning and phase) into precision quantum control experiments, degrading gate fidelity.

**Main result.** A software-based compensation protocol successfully mitigates these errors, recovering high gate fidelities (99.93%) and significantly boosting the success probability for qudit algorithms (e.g., 10% to 70%).

**Method.** The authors measure the line-synchronous error components (detuning and phase) in a line-triggered frame and update the control pulse sequences accordingly to cancel the deterministic error contribution.

**Model / system.** The experiment uses a trapped $^{137}	ext{Ba}^+$ ion as a quantum system, controlling transitions in both qubit and multilevel qudit regimes. The error is modeled as a time-dependent magnetic-field-induced shift in energy levels.

**Key observables.** Average gate fidelity ($F_{avg}$), success probability ($	ext{P}_{	ext{succ}}$) for algorithms like Bernstein-Vazirani, residual detuning ($\delta\omega(t)$), and accumulated phase ($\Phi_{AC}(t)$).

**Important parameters / regimes.** The compensation reduces the calibrated AC line contribution to detuning by factors up to $21(9)	imes$, and the phase residual by $90	imes$.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The method assumes the noise perturbation is reproducible with respect to the mains electrical phase, allowing for deterministic calibration.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the compensation protocol, showing the required detuning and phase corrections, and compare gate performance (e.g., Randomized Benchmarking decay or qudit success probability) with and without compensation enabled.

**Paper structure.** The paper first identifies the problem of line-synchronous noise, details the measurement of detuning and phase using line-triggered Ramsey sequences, develops the software compensation protocol for both detuning and phase, and finally validates the method by testing gate fidelity via RB and algorithmic performance on qudits.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

AC mains power line-synchronous disturbances are a common source of coherent, time-dependent error in precision quantum-control experiments. We show that when these disturbances are reproducible with respect to the mains phase, their effect can be measured in a line-triggered frame and compensated through software updates to control sequences. In our system, the disturbances manifest as magnetic-field-induced shifts in the energy level structure of a trapped $^{137}\text{Ba}^+$ ion, resulting in time-dependent detunings between the ion transitions and a local oscillator, as well as additional phases accumulated on superpositions of energy levels. We demonstrate a compensation protocol that corrects for the instantaneous oscillator detuning during control pulses, and for the phase accumulated by the energy levels between pulses. The calibrated AC line contribution to the detuning is reduced by $21(9)\times$, while the fitted AC phase amplitude is reduced below the measurement uncertainty. We then study gate performance on a magnetic-field-sensitive qubit and find that uncompensated mains-synchronous errors produce time-dependent fluctuations that make the usual randomized-benchmarking decay model unreliable. With compensation enabled, these fluctuations are suppressed sufficiently to recover a standard benchmarking decay and extract an average gate fidelity of $99.93(1)\%$. Finally, we extend the framework to multilevel qudit control and apply it to a single-qudit Bernstein-Vazirani algorithm, where AC compensation increases the success probability on a 16-level qudit from $10(7)\%$ to $70(9)\%$. These results show that reproducible line-synchronous noise can be treated as a calibrated control-frame error and corrected without additional hardware.

</details>

**Summary.** This paper explores how to engineer topological flat bands in ultracold atoms by satisfying the Aharonov-Casher condition within a $\Lambda$-type coupling scheme. The authors demonstrate that while ideal conditions yield perfect topology, a specific combination of slight imperfections—deviation from perfect tuning and finite coupling strength—can actually restore the desired perfectly flat, topologically robust lowest energy band. This makes the system a powerful simulator for fractional quantum Hall physics.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work provides a concrete, controllable platform (ultracold atoms) to engineer and observe topological phenomena like Chern insulators, offering a pathway to simulate fractional quantum Hall states in a highly tunable setting.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The study investigates the conditions required to realize a perfectly flat, topologically non-trivial energy band (Chern band) for ultracold atoms by adiabatically following the dark state.

**Main result.** A completely flat lowest band with perfect topology can be achieved by combining two imperfections: deviation from perfect fine-tuning and finite atom-light coupling strength.

**Method.** The analysis uses the Aharonov-Casher (AC) condition, relating geometric potentials, and models the Rabi frequencies as superpositions of plane waves to engineer the required effective magnetic field.

**Model / system.** The system involves ultracold atoms in a $\Lambda$-type atom-light coupling scheme, where the dynamics are governed by the dark state manifold derived from the atom-light interaction Hamiltonian.

**Key observables.** Lowest energy band flatness, Chern number, geometric vector potential (A) and scalar potential ($\phi$), and the effective magnetic field ($B_z$).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Rabi frequencies ($\Omega_1, \Omega_2$), lattice parameters ($N=3, 4, 6$), amplitude imbalance ($1-b$), and phase mismatch ($\gamma$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis relies on the adiabatic approximation, which is valid for sufficiently strong coupling ($\Omega_0 	o \infty$).

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the Rabi frequencies and effective magnetic field for different plane wave superpositions ($N=3, 4, 5, 6$), showing the transition from ideal flux quantization to band broadening upon deviation from perfect tuning.

**Paper structure.** The paper establishes the AC condition, derives the necessary structure for Rabi frequencies using plane wave superpositions, analyzes specific cases ($N=3, 4$), and concludes by showing that combined imperfections can restore the ideal flat band topology.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We consider the Aharonov-Casher (AC) condition for ultracold atoms adiabatically following the dark-state in a $Λ$-type atom-light coupling scheme. The AC condition establishes a relation between the geometric scalar potential and the synthetic magnetic field, resulting in a fully degenerate lowest Landau-level-like band even if the magnetic field is inhomogeneous but has a proper sign. We derive a general requirement for the atom-light coupling under which the AC condition applies. The requirement holds if the Rabi frequencies of the $Λ$ scheme are the superposition of plane waves with the appropriate amplitudes and phases. In particular, the Rabi frequencies made of $N=3,\,4,\,6$ fine tuned plane waves yield a smooth background magnetic field of definite sign, as well as an array of non-measurable Aharonov-Bohm flux singularities. Departing from the fine tuning, the latter singularities broaden into narrow subwavelength patches of the opposite magnetic field, which broaden the lowest energy band. The lowest band is broadened also for the fine tuned situation due to deviation from the adiabatic approach because of the finite atom-light coupling strength. It is shown that a proper combination of the two imperfections (departure from fine tuning and finite atom-light coupling strength) can lead to a completely flat lowest band, which furthermore is characterized by the perfect topology needed for simulating the fractional Hall states.

</details>

**Summary.** The authors demonstrate a technique called gate-biased illumination to precisely and repeatably tune the operating voltages of Si/SiGe quantum dot qubits. By trapping charges at the oxide interface using near-infrared light, they can achieve uniform, low-voltage operation across multiple gates without detrimentally affecting the measured charge noise, advancing the scalability of solid-state quantum computing.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work provides a novel, electrically controllable method to engineer the electrostatic environment of solid-state qubits, which is crucial for scaling up quantum processors and minimizing power consumption.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Semiconductor quantum dot qubits require significantly different gate voltages on each gate to reach the correct operating point, complicating device tuning and limiting low-power operation.

**Main result.** Gate-biased illumination allows for the controllable and repeatable tuning of operating voltages on a gate-by-gate basis, enabling uniform and small operating voltages without increasing charge noise.

**Method.** The technique involves illuminating the Si/SiGe quantum dot device with near-infrared light while applying specific DC gate voltages, which modifies the trapped charge distribution at the oxide-semiconductor interface.

**Model / system.** The platform is a Si/SiGe quantum dot device patterned on a heterostructure. The mechanism relies on photo-generated electron-hole pairs trapping charges at the oxide-semiconductor interface, which shifts the local electric fields and thus the operating voltages.

**Key observables.** Operating voltages, pinch-off voltages ($V_{PO}$), and charge noise ($S_{1/2}^{\epsilon}$ at 1 Hz).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Near-infrared illumination (e.g., 780 nm laser), gate voltages, and the resulting voltage shifts (e.g., reducing voltage difference from 340 mV to 114 mV).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The trapped charge distribution established during illumination remains 'frozen' in place, and the charge noise measurement is unaffected by this voltage shift.

**Figures summary.** Figures show the device structure, stability diagrams illustrating low-voltage operation after illumination, and comparisons of charge noise spectra before and after tuning.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of non-uniform gate voltages, details the gate-biased illumination method, presents experimental measurements of voltage shifts and charge noise, and validates the underlying physics using self-consistent Schrödinger-Poisson simulations.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Semiconductor quantum dot qubits often require very different voltages on each gate to bring them to a correct operating point. Here, we present a method by which one can controllably and repeatably alter the nanoscale trapped charge distribution at an oxide-semiconductor interface. We demonstrate this method on a Si/SiGe quantum dot device, and we find that the operating voltages can be controlled and made much more uniform. The method relies on illumination with near-infrared light in the presence of applied gate voltages, and it enables the tuning of the device operating point on a gate-by-gate basis. We present an explanation of the underlying physics using self-consistent Schrödinger-Poisson simulations. As an application of this method, we tune a triple quantum dot to have uniform and small operating voltages in the (1,1,1) charge configuration. Importantly, we show that shifting the operating voltages in this way does not change the measured charge noise.

</details>

**Authors:** Aaron Holman, Ximo Sun, Bojeong Seo, Joshua Corn, Zezheng Zhu, Yuan Xu, Jiahao Wu, Nanfang Yu, Dmytro Filin, Marianna Safronova, Sebastian Will

The tweezer light is linearly polarized. (b) Loss spectrum of tweezer-trapped atoms following illumination with 2.9 µm light. Here, ∆is defined as the detuning relative to the free-space resonance. (a) In the absence of a magnetic field, a linewidth of Γ/(2π) = 74(10) kHz is measured, obtained from a Lorentzian fit. (b) In the presence of a magnetic field the previously degenerate transitions mJ = ±2 → ±3 split symmetrically. The splitting is about 0.4 MHz. The mag- netic field is about 0.14(1) G and aligned along the polarization vec- tor of the optical tweezers. The linewidth of the left dip and the right dip are Γ/(2π) = 90(15) and 107(20) kHz, respectively.</figcaption>
</figure>

**Summary.** The research establishes a novel platform using strontium atoms trapped in optical tweezer arrays, specifically engineered to access a mid-infrared transition at 2,923 nm. Key achievements include high-fidelity single-atom trapping and demonstrating resolved-sideband cooling in the metastable state. This platform opens new avenues for studying dipolar many-body physics and quantum control beyond traditional lattice setups.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work significantly expands the accessible physics regime for trapped atoms by utilizing mid-infrared transitions, enabling studies of dipolar interactions and collective effects in flexible, subwavelength geometries.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** To develop a versatile platform using subwavelength atomic tweezer arrays to study collective emission phenomena, such as superradiance and subradiance, by accessing mid-infrared transitions.

**Main result.** The authors successfully demonstrated single-atom preparation, high-fidelity imaging, and resolved-sideband cooling of trapped strontium atoms using a mid-infrared transition at 2,923 nm.

**Method.** The experiment utilizes optical tweezer arrays generated via holographic metasurfaces to trap strontium atoms, followed by precision spectroscopy and cooling measurements on the mid-IR transition.

**Model / system.** The system involves Strontium atoms trapped in optical tweezer arrays, leveraging a mid-infrared transition ($5s5p~^3P_2 ightarrow 5s4d~^3D_3$) at 2,923 nm. The trapping is stabilized using a magic wavelength at 597.14 nm.

**Key observables.** Single-atom imaging fidelity (99.7%), cooling temperature ($\approx 14 \mu	ext{K}$ radial), and linewidth measurements ($\Gamma/(2\pi) = 74 \pm 10 	ext{ kHz}$).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Mid-IR transition wavelength (2,923 nm), magic trapping wavelength (597.14 nm), and trap frequencies ($\approx 95 	ext{ kHz}$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The model for temperature extraction assumes the axial temperature remains constant during radial cooling measurements.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the potential of the 2.9 $\mu$m transition for subwavelength arrays and qubit control; spectroscopy shows level structure and magnetic field effects; and cooling figures compare uncooled vs. cooled recapture probabilities.

**Paper structure.** The paper details the platform realization using metasurfaces, followed by single-atom preparation and imaging; then, it presents precision spectroscopy of the mid-IR transition; finally, it demonstrates resolved-sideband cooling using time-of-flight measurements.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Subwavelength atomic tweezer arrays, in which atoms can be positioned at distances smaller than their emission wavelength, have been proposed as a versatile platform to study collective emission phenomena, such as superradiance and subradiance. Experimentally, the realization of such arrays has been a challenge as typical emission wavelengths in the visible or near-infrared are short compared to typical tweezer spacings in the micrometer range. Here, we use $^{88}$Sr atoms in optical tweezer arrays to access a mid-infrared transition at 2,923 nm ($5s5p\:^{3}P_{2} \rightarrow\, 5s4d\:^{3}D_{3}$). We identify a magic trapping wavelength at 597.14(3) nm and demonstrate single-atom preparation and imaging with high fidelity. In addition, using 2,923 nm light, we demonstrate resolved-sideband cooling of tweezer-trapped strontium. Beyond enabling studies of collective emission phenomena in flexible arrangements of atoms, our platform opens novel opportunities for dipolar many-body physics and enhanced control over Rydberg dynamics and the strontium fine-structure qubit.

</details>

### [Vibrational resonance in a one-dimensional dissipative Bose-Josephson junction](http://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02157v1)

**Authors:** Abhik Kumar Saha  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum gases · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2606.02157v1>

**Summary.** This study analyzes the vibrational and nonlinear response of a 1D dissipative Bose-Josephson junction driven by two distinct frequencies. By employing advanced perturbation techniques, the authors show that the high-frequency drive breaks symmetry, allowing for a measurable second-harmonic response. This confirms a controllable mechanism for generating nonlinear dynamics in such quantum bosonic systems.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work provides a concrete, analytically tractable model for observing nonlinear harmonic generation and vibrational resonance in a system relevant to ultracold atomic physics (Bose-Josephson junctions).

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper investigates the linear and nonlinear response of a one-dimensional dissipative Bose-Josephson junction (BJJ) when subjected to simultaneous weak low-frequency probing and rapid high-frequency external driving.

**Main result.** The high-frequency modulation dynamically breaks the system's symmetry, enabling a finite second-harmonic response that would otherwise be forbidden, demonstrating controllable vibrational and nonlinear resonance.

**Method.** The authors employed time-scale separation and perturbative analysis on the dissipative two-mode BJJ equations to derive analytical expressions for the linear and nonlinear response amplitudes.

**Model / system.** The physical system is a one-dimensional dissipative Bose-Josephson junction, modeled using a two-mode approach derived from Bose-Einstein condensates in double-well potentials. The dynamics are governed by coupled, dissipative equations.

**Key observables.** Linear response amplitude ($Q_L(\omega)$) at the fundamental frequency and nonlinear response amplitude ($Q_{NL}(2\omega)$) at the second harmonic.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Dissipation ($\gamma$), interaction strength ($\Lambda_0$), low-frequency probe amplitude ($c$), and high-frequency driving strength ($g$ or $\Omega$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis relies on small approximations for the population imbalance ($z$) and relative phase ($	heta$), and the use of time-scale separation to decouple slow and fast dynamics.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the resonance enhancement of both linear and nonlinear responses by varying the driving strength ($g$ or $\Omega$), and show the dependence of the resonance position ($g_{max}$) on the interaction parameter ($\Lambda_0$) and the probe amplitude ($c$).

**Paper structure.** The paper progresses by deriving an effective higher-order nonlinear equation, applying time-scale separation to simplify the dynamics, and then using perturbative analysis to calculate the analytical expressions for the linear and nonlinear responses, validating these results against direct numerical simulations.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We investigate the linear and nonlinear response of a one-dimensional dissipative Bose-Josephson junction subjected simultaneously to a weak low-frequency probe and a rapidly oscillating high-frequency external drive. Starting from the dissipative two-mode Bose-Josephson equations, we derive an effective higher-order nonlinear equation for the population imbalance by retaining the leading nonlinear correction. Using time-scale separation and perturbative analysis, we obtain analytical expressions for both the linear response at the fundamental frequency and the nonlinear response at the second harmonic. We show that the high-frequency modulation modifies the effective potential landscape and dynamically breaks the symmetry around the stationary state, giving rise to a finite second-harmonic response that is absent without the rapidly oscillating field. Both the linear and nonlinear response amplitudes exhibit resonance-like enhancement for optimal values of the high-frequency driving strength. We further analyze the dependence of the resonance characteristics on interaction strength, dissipation, and driving parameters in both the zero-phase and $π$-phase modes and compare the analytical predictions with direct numerical simulations. Our results demonstrate a controllable mechanism toward realizing linear and nonlinear vibrational resonance in a one-dimensional dissipative Bose-Josephson junction and open new possibilities for controlling collective dynamics in driven ultracold bosonic systems.

</details>

<a id="paper-2606.00259"></a>
### [Geometric Instability and Self-Limitation in Driven Quantum Systems](http://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00259v1)

**Authors:** A. M. Tishin  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2606.00259v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `driven-dissipative phase transition` **3/5** · `methods for driven-dissipative` **3/5** · `non-equilibrium dynamics` **3/5** · `Keldysh / 2PI / non-Gaussian methods` **2/5** · `analog quantum simulation` **2/5** · `non-equilibrium universality` **2/5** · `correlated / nonlocal dissipation` **1/5** · `entanglement & information structure` **1/5**

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>
<div class="paper-figures-scroll">
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2606.00259_figures/2606.00259_fig1.jpg" alt="Figure 1">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> Figure 1. Numerical verification of the geometric non-adiabaticity framework. (a) Exact verification</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper establishes a universal geometric framework using the normalized Fubini–Study distinguishability speed to quantify non-adiabatic instability in driven quantum systems. It introduces a dimensionless parameter ($\mathcal{A}_n$) that predicts instability onset, connects this to quantum critical phenomena, and proves a self-limitation theorem for nonlinear systems. The theory offers a unified diagnostic tool applicable from superconducting qubits to magnonic resonators.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work provides a powerful, unified geometric diagnostic tool ($\mathcal{A}_n$) applicable across diverse quantum platforms (qubits, magnonics) to predict fundamental limits on control speed and stability.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper develops a unified geometric framework to quantify local non-adiabaticity and instability in general driven quantum systems, moving beyond traditional adiabatic theorems.

**Main result.** It introduces a universal, gauge-invariant instability parameter ($\mathcal{A}_n$) that governs the onset of non-adiabaticity, and proves a 'Geometric Self-Limitation' theorem for nonlinear systems.

**Method.** The framework utilizes the Fubini–Study metric and the Quantum Geometric Tensor (QGT) to define the instability parameter, which is then analyzed in both closed and open quantum system settings.

**Model / system.** The theory applies to general driven quantum systems, exemplified by parametric oscillators, coupled bosonic modes, and is extended to open systems using the Bures metric.

**Key observables.** Universal instability parameter ($\mathcal{A}_n$), Fubini–Study speed ($v_{FS}$), spectral gap ($\Delta_n$), and the maximum eigenvalue of the instability matrix ($\lambda_{\max}$).

**Important parameters / regimes.** The instability threshold ($\mathcal{A}_n \sim 1$), the minimum gate time ($T_{min} = 1/\sqrt{8\omega}$), and the nonlinear regulator strength ($U$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis relies on the assumption that the accessible low-occupancy manifold is compact and that the nonlinear regulator provides a monotonic gap condition for its self-limitation proof.

**Figures summary.** The notes reference calculations confirming the relationship between the FS metric and the geometric parameter ($\eta^2\Omega^2/8$), and show how the dominant eigenvalue ($\lambda_{\max}$) increases with inter-mode coupling ($J$).

**Paper structure.** The paper builds from defining the general geometric instability criterion ($\mathcal{A}_n$) using the FS speed and spectral gap, demonstrating its equivalence to older parameters, and then extending this to analyze self-limitation via nonlinear regulators and multi-mode coupling.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We develop a unified geometric framework for local non-adiabaticity in driven quantum systems. We show that the previously introduced AMT non adiabaticity parameter arises as a special realization of a more general geometric instability criterion governed by the normalized Fubini Study distinguishability speed. The local geometric evolution speed is identified as the physically relevant quantity controlling the onset of non-adiabatic instability.   We introduce a universal dimensionless instability parameter measuring the competition between quantum-state evolution speed and spectral-gap protection. This quantity provides a local, gauge-invariant, and basis-independent criterion for arbitrary driven Hamiltonians. Near quantum critical points, the instability parameter diverges through inverse gap amplification, recovering the Kibble Zurek freeze-out condition directly from local geometric data.   We prove that monotonic occupation-dependent nonlinear regulators geometrically compress the quantum metric, establishing a self-limitation theorem in which nonlinear spectral deformation confines the accessible region of projective Hilbert space under strong driving. The multimode extension yields a matrix-valued instability criterion that identifies collective instability channels invisible to scalar descriptions.   The framework naturally extends to open quantum systems through the Bures metric and quantum Fisher geometry, where thermal mixing and Lindblad decay increase the instability threshold through geometric suppression of state distinguishability. The instability threshold further implies a universal geometric lower bound on coherent control time and quantum gate duration.

</details>

**Summary.** This work analyzes particle localization on random arrays of filaments, drawing inspiration from biological microtubules. By modeling the system using an advection-diffusion equation, the authors show that particle accumulation (localization) is robust and strongest when the particle's active transport length is intermediate. This highlights how active processes interact with quenched disorder to create stable spatial organization.

**Why it may be interesting.** The framework of mapping transport dynamics onto an effective energy landscape due to quenched disorder is highly relevant to understanding energy barriers and trapping mechanisms in complex quantum or classical systems.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper investigates how disordered arrays of filaments govern the distribution of intermittently moving particles that switch between diffusive and processive transport.

**Main result.** The system exhibits robust particle localization, which is strongest for intermediate run-lengths, demonstrating emergent spatial organization from the interplay of active transport and quenched disorder.

**Method.** The analysis models the system using an advection-diffusion equation derived from the rapid attachment-detachment limit, mapping the steady-state density onto an effective energy landscape.

**Model / system.** The physical system is a 1D random array of parallel filaments, motivated by mixed-polarity microtubules in dendrites. Particles switch between passive diffusion and active, biased motion along these fixed filaments.

**Key observables.** Localization strength, quantified by the Inverse Participation Ratio (IPR), and the resulting particle density profile $ho_i(x) \propto e^{-\epsilon_i(x)}$.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Diffusivity ($D$), attachment/detachment rates ($k_a, k_d$), bias parameter ($b$), and the particle run-length ($\lambda_v$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis relies on a 'single-layer' approximation, assuming attachment/detachment rates are much faster than filament traversal times, and the system is analyzed in a 1D axial coordinate.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate simulation snapshots of biased particles, the schematic of motility state transitions, the resulting energy landscape, and plots showing the condition for tight localization based on kinetic parameters.

**Paper structure.** The paper first establishes the physical model and the governing advection-diffusion equation. It then derives the effective energy landscape and analyzes the conditions (intermediate run-lengths) under which localization is maximized, providing quantitative diagnostics like the IPR.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Quenched disorder in the environment can fundamentally alter transport dynamics in both active and passive systems. We explore how disordered arrays of filaments govern the distribution of intermittently moving particles which switch between diffusive and processive transport. Motivated by the mixed-polarity arrangements of parallel microtubules observed in mammalian dendrites, we show that such arrays tend to result in localization of particles at regions of convergent filament orientation. In the rapid attachment-detachment limit, the disordered system can be described by a noisy one-dimensional effective energy landscape, whose structure is approximated by a random walk. The depth and width of wells on this landscape are expressed as a function of the transport kinetics and system geometry. Localization is shown to be strongest at intermediate run-lengths, where biased transport persists long enough to sense the quenched filament polarity but not so long as to facilitate escape from local traps. These results demonstrate robust localization of particles moving on random filament networks, highlighting the emergent spatial organization that arises from an interplay of active transport and quenched disorder.

</details>

### [Noise spectroscopy of two-body loss as a probe of dynamical bulk viscosity in ultracold atomic gases](http://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01835v1)

**Authors:** Tingyu Zhang, Hiroyuki Tajima, Takeo Kato  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum gases · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2606.01835v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `correlated / nonlocal dissipation` **3/5** · `methods for driven-dissipative` **3/5** · `Keldysh / 2PI / non-Gaussian methods` **2/5** · `analog quantum simulation` **2/5** · `driven-dissipative phase transition` **2/5** · `non-equilibrium dynamics` **2/5** · `non-equilibrium universality` **2/5** · `quantum measurements` **1/5**

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>

</figure>

<img src="2606.01835_figures/2606.01835_fig2.jpg" alt="Figure 2">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> FIG. 2. Fano factor for correlated noise of pure two-body noise in DC limit as a function of v. Γ is a dimensionless normalizing factor given by Eq. (38). F(0) becomes zero at the unitary limit where v = 0, due to scale invariance. Its value becomes small at large positive v, namely, the system is in BEC regime.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper proposes using the correlated noise generated by two-body particle loss in ultracold atomic gases to measure the dynamical bulk viscosity. By analyzing the noise power spectrum beyond the simple shot-noise background, the authors demonstrate a direct theoretical link between this measurable fluctuation and the bulk viscosity coefficient. This offers a powerful new experimental avenue for studying transport phenomena in quantum fluids.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work provides a novel, measurable diagnostic tool—noise spectroscopy—to probe a fundamental, but experimentally elusive, transport property (bulk viscosity) in quantum gases.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The research aims to establish a measurable link between the correlated noise spectrum of two-body particle loss and the dynamical bulk viscosity in weakly dissipative quantum gases.

**Main result.** The noise power spectrum of the two-body loss current is found to be directly proportional to the equilibrium correlation function of the contact operator, which in turn is related to the dynamical bulk viscosity via the Kubo formula.

**Method.** The authors derive the loss-current operator from the Lindblad equation and calculate the noise power spectrum, subtracting the Poissonian background to isolate the transport-sensitive terms.

**Model / system.** The system modeled is a two-component Fermi gas (ultracold atomic gas) undergoing weak inelastic two-body losses. The dynamics are governed by a Lindblad master equation, incorporating contact interactions.

**Key observables.** Loss-Current Noise Power Spectrum ($S_N$), Fano Factor ($F(\omega)$), and the dynamical bulk viscosity ($\zeta(\omega)$).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Weak-loss regime, unitarity, and the scattering length parameter ($v$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis relies on the Lindblad equation for weak losses and utilizes the Kubo formula to connect noise fluctuations to transport coefficients.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the dimensionless noise power spectrum dependence on the scattering length parameter, and Figure 2 illustrates the Fano factor vanishing at unitarity.

**Paper structure.** The paper proceeds by deriving the loss-current operator, calculating the noise correlator, and then establishing the proportionality between this noise spectrum and the retarded contact correlator, which is linked to the bulk viscosity via the Kubo formula.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We show that the correlated noise of the two-body loss current provides access to the dynamical bulk viscosity in weakly dissipative quantum gases. Starting from the Lindblad equation for weak inelastic losses, we derive the loss-current operator. After subtracting the leading Poissonian shot-noise background, the remaining noise power spectrum of two-body loss current is found proportional to the equilibrium correlation function of the contact operator. Combining this result with the exact relation between contact correlations and bulk viscosity, we demonstrate the correspondence between the measurable loss-current noise and the bulk-viscosity. Our result identifies the higher-order fluctuation of two-body loss as a probe of dynamical bulk viscosity, whose measurement has remained elusive in experiments.

</details>

### [Quantum Ergodicity and Thermalization in Interval Quantum Mechanics](http://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00749v1)

**Authors:** Abbas Edalat

**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `non-equilibrium dynamics` **3/5** · `measurement-induced transitions` **2/5** · `methods for driven-dissipative` **2/5** · `quantum measurements` **2/5** · `scars & prethermalization` **2/5** · `Keldysh / 2PI / non-Gaussian methods` **1/5** · `analog quantum simulation` **1/5** · `correlated / nonlocal dissipation` **1/5** · `driven-dissipative phase transition` **1/5** · `entanglement & information structure` **1/5** · `non-equilibrium universality` **1/5**

**Summary.** This paper develops a framework using Interval Quantum Mechanics (IQM) to analyze quantum thermalization. It shows that when the system's knowledge is represented by a 'quantum parcel,' the expectation values of observables converge to microcanonical values, provided the parcel has sufficient effective dimension. The method is particularly strong for tracking multiple, separated components of the state simultaneously.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work provides a powerful, geometrically rigorous tool for studying thermalization in open or partially known quantum systems, moving beyond point-state assumptions common in standard quantum dynamics.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper combines quantum ergodicity theorems with Interval Quantum Mechanics (IQM) to rigorously study how quantum states, represented by epistemic 'quantum parcels,' approach thermal equilibrium.

**Main result.** It proves that for parcels with sufficient effective dimension, the expectation values of observables concentrate around the microcanonical ensemble values for late times, and this concentration is preserved even when tracking separated state components.

**Method.** The analysis uses Reimann's spectral typicality theorem within the IQM framework, treating quantum states as convex sets defined by measurement intervals.

**Model / system.** The system is a general quantum mechanical system governed by a Hamiltonian H. The state is not a point but a 'quantum parcel' ($\mathcal{O}$), which is a weak open convex set of density matrices reflecting finite-precision measurement knowledge.

**Key observables.** Expectation intervals of observables, effective dimension ($	ext{deff}$), and the separation maintained by a conserved quantity $Q^*$.

**Important parameters / regimes.** The minimal effective dimension $D$ (required to ensure thermalization) and the time $t$ (late times).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis requires the initial state parcel to have a large effective dimension ($	ext{deff} \geq D \gg 1$) to ensure the system is in the thermalizing regime.

**Paper structure.** The paper builds from establishing the general framework of IQM for quantum states, applying Reimann's theorem to show single-parcel thermalization, and culminating in the proof for double parcels, which maintains both thermalization and separation under evolution.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We combine Reimann's spectral typicality theorem -- a modern formulation of quantum ergodicity -- with the framework of Interval Quantum Mechanics (IQM). In IQM, quantum states are represented not by points but by \emph{quantum parcels}: weak open convex sets of density matrices defined by finitely many expectation intervals. Such parcels are the exact mathematical representation of the epistemic knowledge obtained from finite-precision measurements of macroscopic observables.   We prove that for a single parcel in which every state has large effective dimension (a condition that ensures thermalization), the expectation interval of any bounded observable becomes concentrated around the microcanonical value for most late times. The asymptotic bound depends only on the minimal effective dimension within the parcel, not on its detailed shape.   For a double parcel \((O_1,O_2)\) with both components contained in an energy shell, separated by a conserved quantity \(Q^*\) that is supported on the range of the measurement projector, we show that the expectation intervals of both parcels become concentrated near the microcanonical values of bounded observables, the separation is preserved exactly, and the updated double parcel after a fuzzy measurement remains valid.

</details>

<a id="paper-2606.00698"></a>
### [Wasserstein-2 gradient flows and the geometry of entropy production in classical and quantum stochastic thermodynamics](http://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00698v1)

**Authors:** Olga Movilla Miangolarra, Ralph Sabbagh, Artemy Kolchinsky

**Summary.** This paper unifies the geometric understanding of entropy production in classical and quantum thermodynamics using Wasserstein-2 metrics derived from free energy gradient flows. It shows that these metrics provide finite-time refinements to the second law and rigorously connects optimal transport theory to fundamental thermodynamic quantities like thermodynamic length. The framework extends beyond simple dissipation to incorporate coherent (Hamiltonian) dynamics.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work provides a powerful geometric lens (optimal transport) to analyze non-equilibrium processes in quantum and classical systems, offering quantitative bounds on irreversible processes beyond the standard second law.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** To provide a unified geometric description of entropy production in classical and quantum stochastic thermodynamics, extending the second law beyond purely dissipative dynamics.

**Main result.** The framework establishes that the Wasserstein-2 distance bounds entropy production, offering a finite-time refinement of the second law, and connects optimal transport to thermodynamic length.

**Method.** The authors utilize Wasserstein-2 structures arising from gradient flows of free energy, formulating relaxation dynamics for continuous, discrete, and quantum systems.

**Model / system.** The work covers classical overdamped diffusions, discrete Markov chains, and open quantum systems evolving under Lindblad dynamics. The geometry is defined on the space of states (probability distributions or density matrices).

**Key observables.** Entropy production, Free energy functional, Wasserstein-2 distance, Thermodynamic length.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Dissipative mobilities, Hamiltonian coupling strength ($\gamma$), Time ($	au$), Temperature ($eta$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis often assumes the existence of a smooth manifold of states and relies on establishing equivalence bounds between different geometric formulations (e.g., Hamiltonian-dissipative vs. purely dissipative).

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 summarizes the Wasserstein-2 geometric picture for purely dissipative and mixed Hamiltonian-dissipative systems. Figure 2 illustrates the gradient flow of the free energy functional on the state space.

**Paper structure.** The paper systematically reviews the gradient flow formulation across continuous, discrete, and quantum settings, deriving the associated Wasserstein-2 metrics. It then establishes bounds relating entropy production to these geometric distances, culminating in the recovery of thermodynamic length at equilibrium.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

The second law does more than set the direction of thermodynamic evolution: it endows nonequilibrium transformations with an underlying geometry. In this work, we provide a unified geometric description of entropy production in classical and quantum thermodynamics based on Wasserstein-2 structures arising from gradient flows of free energy. We review how relaxation to equilibrium, in overdamped diffusions, discrete detailed-balanced Markov chains, and dissipative Lindblad dynamics, can be formulated as a gradient flow on the space of states. The associated Wasserstein-2 distance bounds entropy production, yielding a finite-time refinement of the second law. We extend this framework beyond purely dissipative dynamics by introducing generalized Wasserstein-2 metrics that incorporate conservative (Hamiltonian) dynamics in both classical inertial systems and open quantum systems, yielding intrinsic distances that exactly characterize minimal entropy production under fixed dissipative mobilities. We establish equivalence bounds between purely dissipative and Hamiltonian-dissipative geometries, explicitly quantifying how inertial or coherent dynamics can reduce dissipation. Finally, when restricted to equilibrium distributions, we recover the thermodynamic length of linear response-including the quantum thermodynamic length-thereby linking optimal transport, thermodynamic length, and counterdiabatic protocols within a single geometric framework. All in all, our results extend the Riemannian program of thermodynamics further from equilibrium and provide a geometric foundation for optimal protocols beyond the overdamped setting.

</details>

**Summary.** This paper models stress relaxation in biological fiber networks subjected to random fiber severing whose rate depends on the local tension. By simulating different feedback mechanisms, the authors show that tuning this mechanochemical feedback can significantly alter the network's mechanical stability, shifting the critical point where the structure loses its rigidity. This provides insight into how biological systems might actively regulate their mechanical properties.

**Why it may be interesting.** While focused on classical mechanics, the concept of non-equilibrium phase transitions, critical points, and the influence of local feedback mechanisms on macroscopic stability echoes principles found in open quantum systems and non-equilibrium statistical mechanics.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The study investigates how stress relaxation occurs in fiber networks when the severing of fibers is stochastically dependent on the local mechanical stress.

**Main result.** The mechanochemical feedback mechanism significantly shifts the critical connectivity required for the network to maintain rigidity, delaying or accelerating the loss of mechanical integrity depending on whether severing is tension-suppressed or enhanced.

**Method.** The authors developed and employed a computational model using 2D triangular lattice simulations and an event-driven simulation approach (Gillespie algorithm) to track stress and connectivity changes over time.

**Model / system.** The system models fiber networks (like those in biological tissues) as spring networks subjected to applied shear strain. The model incorporates both stretching and bending energies, and the severing rate is governed by a feedback parameter ($c$) related to local tension.

**Key observables.** Stress relaxation ($\sigma(t)$), network connectivity ($Z(t)$), and the onset of the mechanical rigidity transition.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Applied shear strain ($\gamma$), network connectivity ($z$), and the feedback parameter ($c$) which defines tension-suppressed ($c<0$) or tension-enhanced ($c>0$) severing.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis assumes fast fragmentation and relaxation timescales compared to the severing timescale, and the model simplifies by ignoring polymerization/depolymerization reactions.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the mechanical phase transition ($\gamma$ vs. $z$) and stress vs. time curves for different feedback limits, showing the shift in the critical transition point.

**Paper structure.** The paper establishes the physical model, develops the computational framework incorporating force-dependent severing, analyzes the stress relaxation dynamics under different feedback regimes, and concludes by discussing the implications for designing mechanically robust systems.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Fiber networks contribute to the mechanical stability of various biological systems, from cells to tissues. Such systems have been modeled by networks of springs or fibers that exhibit rigidity transitions as a function of either connectivity or applied strain. For a fiber network under constant applied strain, severing can reduce the connectivity and destabilize an initially rigid structure. Here, we investigate stress relaxation in spring and fiber networks in the presence of stochastic, force-dependent severing. A computational model to predict stress relaxation with mechanochemical feedback of stress on severing is developed. We also examine the effects of severing on the network topology and onset of rigidity transition. Using 2D triangular lattice-based computer simulations, we explore different limits of the feedback and demonstrate the shift in the onset of rigidity depending on the limit. The limit of tension-suppressed severing delays stress relaxation and shifts the transition into the bending-dominated regime to lower-than-expected connectivity. In contrast, tension-enhanced severing accelerates relaxation and shifts the transition to higher-than-expected connectivity. It is also found that the magnitude of this shift depends on the applied shear strain and the strength of the feedback. Our theoretical approach clarifies some microscopic aspects of these phenomena. Understanding the impact of such feedback mechanisms can provide valuable insights into designing systems by tuning the feedback to the desired response.

</details>

**Summary.** This paper theoretically investigates single-photon routing in quantum electrodynamics ladders built with arrays of giant atoms. By analyzing different atomic arrangements—separated, braided, and nested—the authors demonstrate that the braided geometry offers superior, wideband, and robust routing capabilities. The results show that increasing the number of atoms enhances performance and provides resilience against environmental loss.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work is highly relevant to AMO physics and quantum optics as it uses engineered quantum systems (giant atoms) to control single-photon quantum information flow, moving beyond simple cavity QED setups into complex waveguide arrays.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper addresses the challenge of achieving highly efficient, deterministic, and chirality-free single-photon routing in complex quantum optical circuits.

**Main result.** The braided topology of multiple giant atoms proves to be the most robust architecture, enabling near-perfect routing even when environmental dissipation is present.

**Method.** The analysis employs a real-space quantum optics approach, solving the time-independent Schrödinger equation in the single-photon excitation sector using techniques derived from the Bethe Ansatz.

**Model / system.** The system models up to five 'giant atoms' coupled to two linear waveguides, forming various topological arrangements (separated, braided, nested). The dynamics are governed by a Hamiltonian incorporating atomic transitions, waveguide propagation, and coupling strengths.

**Key observables.** Photon routing efficiency ($T_q$ or $|t_2|^2$), which quantifies the probability of a photon entering the target waveguide.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Detuning ($\Delta$), phase accumulation ($	heta$), coupling rates ($\Gamma$), and the environmental loss parameter ($\gamma/\Gamma$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis is restricted to the single-photon excitation subspace, and the initial models assume specific coupling geometries (e.g., coupling at exactly two points for single-atom analysis).

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the schematic setups for the three topologies (separated, braided, nested) for $n$ atoms, and plot the routing probability $|t_2|^2$ as a function of detuning and phase, both in the lossless and lossy limits.

**Paper structure.** The paper progresses from single-atom analysis to two-atom systems, systematically comparing the performance of the three topologies. It then scales up to $n$ atoms, investigating the impact of direct interatomic interactions (all-to-all vs. nearest-neighbor) and environmental dissipation ($\gamma$).

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

In this paper, we present an in-depth examination of single-photon routing in a multi-emitter waveguide quantum electrodynamics ladder with up to five giant atoms simultaneously coupled across two linear waveguides. Using a real-space approach, we analyze a non-chiral routing architecture and evaluate the impact of scaling the number of giant atoms in three topologically distinct configurations: fully separated, simply braided, and fully nested. Using numerical results, we show that increasing the number of giant atoms, in separate or braided configurations, greatly expands the operational parameter space for near-deterministic ($\sim$100%) photon routing into the target waveguide, achieving directionality through quantum interference alone. In contrast, the nested architecture is limited to a maximum routing efficiency of 25% due to rigid geometric symmetries, although this limitation could be mitigated by employing less symmetric coupling distances. Finally, we assess the system under direct interatomic interactions and environmental dissipation. Our results show that, while spontaneous emission reduces transport probabilities, the multi-atom-separated configuration maintains high routing efficiencies across broad operational windows of incoming photon frequency without requiring chiral coupling.

</details>

### [Giant magneto-optical rotation in a Rydberg atomic gas via symmetry-breaking wave mixing](http://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01030v1)
