**Summary.** This theoretical study proposes using a far-detuned wave-mixing field in a Rydberg atomic gas to overcome an inherent energy-symmetry blockade limiting nonlinear magneto-optical rotation. By breaking this symmetry, the authors demonstrate a massive enhancement (over 24 times) of the measurable third-order nonlinear Kerr effect. This offers a highly efficient mechanism for developing next-generation ultrasensitive atomic magnetometers.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work provides a sophisticated theoretical blueprint for enhancing nonlinear optical effects in quantum media by actively engineering the system's symmetry using auxiliary fields, which is highly relevant to quantum sensing and nonlinear optics.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper addresses the limitation of the nonlinear magneto-optical rotation (NMOR) signal in conventional single-beam excitation systems due to an energy-symmetry-induced propagation blockade.

**Main result.** By introducing a far-detuned, counterpropagating wave-mixing field, the symmetry blockade is broken, leading to a giant enhancement (factor > 24) of the third-order nonlinear rotation angle.

**Method.** The authors employ adiabatic elimination, a perturbation expansion, and the Reduced Density-Matrix Expansion (RDME) method to self-consistently solve the many-body dynamics.

**Model / system.** The system is an ultracold five-level Rydberg atomic gas interacting with multiple laser fields (probe, control, wave-mixing) and long-range van der Waals interactions. The dynamics are governed by the Lindblad quantum master equation.

**Key observables.** Magneto-optical rotation angle ($\psi(z)$), pure third-order nonlinear rotation angle ($\psi_{	ext{nonlin}}$), and polarization asymmetry ratio ($|\Omega_{p+}|/|\Omega_{p-}|$).

**Important parameters / regimes.** The enhancement factor (>24), the Rydberg blockade radius ($R_b$), and the small parameters governing the approximations ($\epsilon_p, \epsilon_W$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The WM field is treated as a steady-state dressing field, and the analysis relies on the weak-field and adiabatic approximations ($\epsilon_p \ll 1, \epsilon_W \ll 1$).

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the excitation scheme involving five levels and show the spatial evolution of Rabi frequency envelopes and the resulting polarization state on the Poincaré sphere.

**Paper structure.** The paper systematically develops the theoretical framework by first applying adiabatic elimination to incorporate the WM field's effect, followed by a perturbation expansion and the use of RDME to solve the resulting coupled, nonlocal differential equations for the macroscopic polarization state.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

The nonlinear magneto-optical rotation effect is central to precision measurements of weak magnetic fields and optical quantum information processing. In conventional single-beam excitation systems, the propagation of the nonlinear signal is restricted by an energy-symmetry-induced propagation blockade. This blockade originates from the symmetrical evolution of the orthogonal circularly polarized components of the probe field, which prevents spatial accumulation of the nonlinear polarization. We propose introducing a far-detuned, counterpropagating wave-mixing (WM) field into an ultracold five-level Rydberg atomic gas to actively break the excitation symmetry. Theoretically, the far-detuned WM field is treated as a steady-state dressing field. Through adiabatic elimination, the conventional third-order wave-mixing process is effectively reduced and incorporated into the first-order linear background of the system. Combined with the reduced density-matrix expansion method, this approach goes beyond both the mean-field and ground-state approximations, allowing for a self-consistent solution of the many-body dynamics that include nonlocal cascaded integrals governed by long-range van der Waals interactions. Our analytical derivations and numerical calculations demonstrate that this symmetry-breaking mechanism breaks the propagation blockade, enabling efficient utilization of the nonlocal Rydberg Kerr effect. As a result, the third-order nonlinear rotation angle is enhanced by a factor exceeding 24, offering a highly efficient mechanism for ultrasensitive atomic magnetometry and all-optical quantum information processing.

</details>

**Summary.** This work translates the concept of geometrical frustration from condensed matter physics into a minimal theory for neural timing. By mapping repulsive phase oscillators to an antiferromagnetic XY model, the authors show that local timing incompatibilities create structured energy landscapes. This suggests that weak global coherence in the brain may be a signature of complex, locally ordered dynamics rather than mere desynchronization.

**Why it may be interesting.** The use of established tools from frustrated magnetism (e.g., ground-state manifolds, metastable states) to diagnose collective behavior in neural oscillators provides a powerful, non-equilibrium diagnostic framework.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** To develop a minimal theoretical framework that maps the concept of geometrical frustration, central to condensed matter physics, onto the dynamics of repulsively coupled rhythmic units in neural systems.

**Main result.** The paper shows that weak global coherence in neural systems can reflect structured local timing order dictated by a frustrated dynamical landscape, rather than indicating simple disorder.

**Method.** The authors use the language of antiferromagnetic XY models and analyze the energy landscapes and relaxation dynamics across a hierarchy of geometries (triangle, tetrahedron, kagome lattice).

**Model / system.** The model consists of repulsively coupled phase oscillators, mathematically analogous to the XY model Hamiltonian, where local constraints (anti-synchronizing phase preferences) are incompatible on non-bipartite motifs.

**Key observables.** Local chirality ($\chi_	riangle$), mean bond frustration ($ar{f}$), basin probabilities ($p_A$), and the Kuramoto order parameter ($R$).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Temperature ($T$) (noise strength), coupling strength ($K$), and the geometry of the lattice (e.g., triangle, kagome).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The theory relies on a minimal phase-oscillator limit, neglecting amplitude dynamics, synaptic delays, and assuming symmetric coupling.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the $120^\circ$ pattern on a triangle and the general energy landscape analysis for various frustrated geometries.

**Paper structure.** The paper establishes a dictionary mapping magnetic concepts to neural timing analogs, derives the phase energy function for the XY model, and then analyzes the relaxation dynamics across increasing complexity of frustrated geometries.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Geometrical frustration, a central paradigm in condensed matter physics, provides a unifying language for systems in which locally preferred interactions cannot be made globally compatible. Here, we use this language to formulate a minimal theory of frustrated neural timing, mapping repulsively coupled rhythmic units onto antiferromagnetic XY models. Within this framework, the condensed-matter concepts of local constraints, degenerate ground-state manifolds, metastability, and quench dynamics become a concrete diagnostic framework for structured neural phase dynamics. We analyze a hierarchy of geometries: a triangle as the minimal frustrated motif with two chiral 120° timing states, a tetrahedron whose reduced ground-state manifold consists of intersecting continuous branches associated with antipodal pairings, and a kagome lattice on which local constraints define a constrained three-coloring manifold. The kagome lattice reveals the central dynamical result: zero-temperature relaxation suppresses global synchrony but typically selects low-energy metastable torque-balanced states rather than exact ground states. Finally, we show how the phase theory can be carried back towards biophysical neural models by treating it as an effective-interaction target, where geometrical timing frustration is realized through preferred phase lags that become incompatible around closed motifs. This perspective suggests that weak global coherence in neural systems does not necessarily signal disordered activity, but can reflect structured local timing order shaped by a frustrated dynamical landscape.

</details>

**Summary.** This paper explores the relationship between the diffusion coefficient and Lyapunov exponents for a particle in a periodically driven potential. Despite no general theoretical link, the authors successfully propose and validate an approximate formula that reconstructs the diffusion coefficient using the maximal Lyapunov exponent from the zero-temperature deterministic system. This provides a novel diagnostic tool for characterizing non-equilibrium transport phenomena.

**Why it may be interesting.** The connection between transport coefficients (like D) and deterministic chaos indicators (like $\lambda$) is a fundamental topic in non-equilibrium statistical mechanics, providing a powerful tool to characterize complex dynamics.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper investigates whether the Brownian diffusion coefficient of a driven particle can be reconstructed from the maximal Lyapunov exponent of the corresponding deterministic system.

**Main result.** The authors demonstrate a strong, quantifiable relationship, proposing an approximate formula that allows the reconstruction of the quasiperiodic diffusion coefficient from the maximal Lyapunov exponent of the zero-temperature deterministic system.

**Method.** The analysis combines numerical simulations of the Langevin equation with theoretical approximations derived from analyzing the deterministic counterpart's periodic orbits and linearized dynamics.

**Model / system.** The system models a particle moving in a spatially periodic and symmetric potential, subjected to an AC-driven external force and thermal noise (Brownian motion).

**Key observables.** Diffusion Coefficient (D), Maximal Lyapunov Exponent ($\lambda_{	ext{max}}$), Mean Square Displacement ($\langle \Delta x^2(t) angle / 2t$).

**Important parameters / regimes.** External driving amplitude ($a$), friction coefficient ($\gamma$), temperature/noise intensity ($Q$), and system frequency ($\omega$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The reconstruction formula is proposed as an approximation, showing good agreement but noting discrepancies near local maxima of D. The analysis relies on the system being ergodic at non-zero temperatures.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the dependence of D and $\lambda_{	ext{max}}$ on the driving amplitude $a$, showing quasiperiodic behavior, and also show the influence of temperature $Q$ and damping $\gamma$ on these quantities.

**Paper structure.** The paper first establishes the physical model using the Langevin equation, then analyzes the dynamics in different parameter regimes (zero vs. non-zero temperature). It proceeds to derive and test an approximate formula linking D to $\lambda_{	ext{max}}$ by comparing theoretical predictions with extensive numerical simulations.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We consider an ac-driven particle moving in a spatially periodic and symmetric potential. In the zero- temperature limit, for the analyzed parameter set, its dynamics is non-chaotic and the particle does not manifest diffusive properties. At non-zero temperatures, the asymptotic long-time motion follows normal (Brownian) diffusion. Recent studies have shown that within tailored parameter regimes, the diffusion coef- ficient is a quasiperiodic function of the external driving amplitude [1]. Although no general relation between Lyapunov exponents and Brownian diffusion exists, we demonstrate that the quasiperiodic diffusion coefficient at non-zero temperature can be accurately reconstructed from the maximal Lyapunov exponent of the corresponding deterministic system (at vanishing temperature). We propose an approximate formula for this purpose, which shows good agreement with numerical simulations, although some discrepancies are detected in the vicinity of the local maxima of the diffusion coefficient. Finally, we examine the robustness of the correlation between diffusion and the Lyapunov exponent under variations of the system parameters.

</details>

**Summary.** This research reports the first experimental observation of strong-field stabilization in trapped atoms, confirming a decades-old prediction that intense laser fields can stabilize quantum states. By measuring ionization rates and observing wavepacket bifurcation, the authors validate the theory using ultracold atoms as an analogue for bound electrons. This work advances the understanding of quantum dynamics in extreme electromagnetic environments.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work provides a crucial experimental realization of a long-standing theoretical prediction in extreme quantum dynamics, offering a complementary tool to probe strong-field physics inaccessible with current pulsed laser technology.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper investigates the counter-intuitive phenomenon of strong-field stabilization, where intense oscillating fields stabilize bound quantum states against ionization.

**Main result.** The authors experimentally observe strong-field stabilization in trapped atoms and confirm the predicted wavefunction bifurcation, showing stabilization persists even at surprisingly low drive frequencies.

**Method.** The study combines experimental measurements on trapped ultracold atoms with detailed numerical simulations using the Gross-Pitaevskii Equation (GPE) to model the dynamics.

**Model / system.** The system uses trapped neutral atoms (e.g., Sr BEC) subjected to time-dependent inertial forces to emulate the dynamics of bound electrons in an intense laser field, modeled by the Kramers-Henneberger potential.

**Key observables.** Ionization rate (loss rate), spatial bifurcation/dichotomy of the wavepacket, and surviving population fraction as a function of field amplitude.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Quiver amplitude ($A$), drive frequency ($\omega$), and the critical amplitude ($A_{	ext{crit}}$) required to induce stabilization.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The single-active electron approximation is used in some analyses, and the GPE simulations are noted to neglect finite-temperature or beyond-mean-field effects.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the experimental setup, sub-cycle dynamics, wavepacket bifurcation, and quantitative plots showing the non-monotonic dependence of the ionization rate on the quiver amplitude, confirming stabilization.

**Paper structure.** The paper progresses from establishing the theoretical prediction of stabilization, detailing the experimental platform using trapped atoms, presenting measurements of ionization rates and wavepacket bifurcation across varying field parameters, and concluding with a general confirmation of the phenomenon's robustness.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Strong oscillating fields are expected to tear apart bound quantum states. Theoretical studies predict a striking reversal: that as the field intensity is raised above some threshold, bound states like atoms can become increasingly stable, accompanied by a spatial bifurcation of the bound state wavefunction. This strong field stabilization was predicted decades ago in the context of atoms in pulsed laser fields, but has resisted experimental observation due to extreme intensity requirements and theoretical controversy. Here we report the experimental observation of strong-field stabilization of a ground state, using trapped neutral atoms to emulate the dynamics of bound electrons in an extremely strong laser field. We image the predicted wavepacket bifurcation, measure an ionization rate non-monotonic in field amplitude, and map out the regime of stabilization. Stabilization persists down to surprisingly low drive frequencies, on the order of the lowest-energy excitations. These results confirm a long-standing prediction in extreme quantum dynamics, and showcase a complementary tool for probing strong-field phenomena near and beyond the frontier of current laser technology.

</details>

<a id="paper-2606.01988"></a>
### [Linear optimal protocol for physical constraints in weakly driven processes](http://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01988v1)

**Authors:** Pierre Nazé  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** statistical mechanics · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2606.01988v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `methods for driven-dissipative` **3/5** · `correlated / nonlocal dissipation` **2/5** · `driven-dissipative phase transition` **2/5** · `non-equilibrium dynamics` **2/5** · `non-equilibrium universality` **2/5** · `Keldysh / 2PI / non-Gaussian methods` **1/5**

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>
<div class="paper-figures-scroll">
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2606.01988_figures/2606.01988_fig1.jpg" alt="Figure 1">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> FIG. 1. Linear protocol observed for all relaxation functions proposed in this work and τ/τR = 0.1, 1, 10.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This work determines the minimum irreversible work required to drive a weakly coupled quantum system between states under specific constraints. By reformulating the problem using periodic boundary conditions, the authors show that the optimal driving protocol is universally linear. This result highlights that the minimum dissipation is dictated by the global memory properties of the system, independent of the specific details of the relaxation kernel.

**Why it may be interesting.** It provides a rigorous, spectral understanding of optimal dissipation in non-equilibrium quantum processes, showing that simple linear protocols minimize work even when complex memory effects are present.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper minimizes the irreversible work done in weakly driven quantum systems while respecting physical constraints on the driving protocol's derivative.

**Main result.** The global optimal protocol is found to be linear, resulting in an optimal work value that depends only on the integrated relaxation function.

**Method.** The problem is formulated as a constrained variational minimization, leading to a shifted eigenvalue equation solved by exploiting the periodicity of the relaxation kernel in a Fourier basis.

**Model / system.** The study concerns weakly driven quantum systems analyzed within the framework of linear response theory. The dynamics are governed by an irreversible work functional involving a relaxation function $\Psi_0(t)$.

**Key observables.** Irreversible work ($	ext{Wirr}[v]$), the relaxation function $\Psi_0(t)$, and the optimal work ($	ext{W}^*_{	ext{irr}}$).

**Important parameters / regimes.** The driving time $	au$, the constraints on total variation ($\delta\lambda$), and the quadratic bound on the driving speed ($C$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis assumes the system is in the weak driving regime and requires extending the relaxation kernel periodically to restore translational invariance.

**Figures summary.** Tables compare numerical results for the optimal work across various relaxation functions ($\exp(-|t|/	au_R)$, $J_0$, $\sin$, $\cos$), showing convergence to an analytical prediction. Figure 1 illustrates the resulting linear protocol.

**Paper structure.** The paper establishes the variational problem, derives the associated shifted eigenvalue equation, solves it using Fourier analysis on a periodic domain, and confirms the universal linear solution through numerical testing across multiple kernel types.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

The minimization of irreversible work in weakly driven systems within linear response under physical constraints on the protocol derivative is studied. The problem reduces to a shifted eigenvalue equation involving the relaxation function. Owing to its dependence on time differences and its evenness, the relaxation kernel is naturally defined over a symmetric interval, where a periodic representation arises as a consistent closure that restores continuous translational invariance. Also, it shows how the irreversible work is defined in practice. Within this framework, the operator becomes diagonal in a Fourier basis. The global optimal solution is the zero mode, yielding a constant driving speed and a linear protocol. The corresponding optimal work depends only on the integrated relaxation function. Numerical results obtained via genetic programming confirm the robustness of this solution across different kernels.

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

### [Modular theory and affine representations on the Rindler horizon](http://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01071v1)

**Authors:** Michele Arzano, Paolo Palumbo  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** statistical mechanics · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2606.01071v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `entanglement & information structure` **3/5** · `Keldysh / 2PI / non-Gaussian methods` **2/5** · `methods for driven-dissipative` **2/5** · `non-equilibrium dynamics` **2/5** · `correlated / nonlocal dissipation` **1/5** · `driven-dissipative phase transition` **1/5** · `non-equilibrium universality` **1/5**

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>
<div class="paper-figures-scroll">
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2606.01071_figures/2606.01071_fig1.jpg" alt="Figure 1">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> FIG. 1: Visualization of the algebra A+ and its v-translated U(γ)A+U †(γ) for positive γ. Notice that U(γ)A+U †(γ) ̸⊆A+ for γ &lt; 0, since points are moved across the horizon (in this sense, the origin).</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper provides a deep mathematical framework for understanding the Unruh effect by interpreting it through the lens of affine symmetry and modular theory. It shows that the thermal nature observed by an accelerated observer is mathematically equivalent to the modular flow of the restricted quantum field algebra. This establishes the affine group as the minimal symmetry governing horizon thermality.

**Why it may be interesting.** The connection between the Unruh effect, which is fundamentally about thermalization in curved spacetime, and the rigorous mathematical machinery of modular theory provides deep insights into how entanglement and thermal properties emerge from local algebraic structures.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper develops a group-theoretic interpretation of the Unruh effect by relating it to modular theory and affine symmetry on the Rindler horizon.

**Main result.** The affine group structure captures the minimal symmetry underlying the Unruh effect, which is confirmed by modular theory showing the dilation flow implements the KMS condition on the restricted vacuum.

**Method.** The analysis uses light-cone coordinates, compares Minkowski and Rindler mode expansions via the Mellin transform, and applies Tomita-Takesaki modular theory to characterize the thermal nature.

**Model / system.** The system is a massless scalar field in two spacetime dimensions, analyzed by restricting its vacuum state to a single Rindler wedge near a horizon.

**Key observables.** The thermal factor $e^{-2\pi\omega}$ derived from the overlap between Minkowski and Rindler bases, and the satisfaction of the KMS condition.

**Important parameters / regimes.** The proper acceleration $\alpha$ (related to the temperature $T=1/(2\pi)$), and the spectral parameter $\omega$.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis assumes the existence of a suitable vacuum state $|\Omegaangle$ and relies on the algebraic structure of the half-line algebra $A^+$.

**Figures summary.** Not specified.

**Paper structure.** The paper progresses from establishing the physical problem (Unruh effect) to developing the mathematical tools (affine symmetry, Mellin transform) and finally confirming the thermal nature using the rigorous framework of modular theory and the KMS condition.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We develop a group-theoretic interpretation of the Unruh effect based on affine symmetry on a light ray and relate it to modular theory. For a massless scalar field in two spacetime dimensions inertial and uniformly accelerated observers select two different flows within the same chiral one-particle structure, respectively, null translations and dilations. Minkowski modes are adapted to translations, while Rindler modes are adapted to dilations, with the Mellin transform providing the natural bridge between them. When a Minkowski positive-frequency mode is restricted to a single Rindler wedge, its comparison with Rindler modes is non-unitary within the positive-frequency sector. Modular theory gives the corresponding operator-algebraic interpretation: on the horizon the modular flow of the half-line algebra is implemented by dilations, and the restricted vacuum satisfies the KMS condition. The affine group thus appears as the minimal symmetry structure underlying thermality on the Rindler horizon.

</details>

**Authors:** Li Zhenpeng  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** disordered systems and neural networks · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2606.02239v1>

Dashed lines are power-law fits for τp ∈[2, 32], yielding α values shown in the legend. The black dashed line indicates the KPZ prediction τcorr ∼τ 1/3 p . The annotation shows the finite-size extrapolation to α(∞) = 0.3333 ± 0.0134.</figcaption>

Error bars represent the standard error of the fitted exponent α obtained from weighted linear regression over τp ∈ [2, 32] (for T = 1.0) or τp ∈[4, 32] (for other temperatures).</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This work uses persistent Langevin dynamics to study the energy landscape of the $p=3$ spherical spin glass. By tuning the walker's inertia via the persistence time $	au_p$, the authors measure the energy correlation time and find it scales with the predicted KPZ exponent ($\alpha=1/3$). This confirms the scale-free hierarchical nature of the landscape and reveals distinct dynamical regimes based on temperature.

**Why it may be interesting.** The application of persistent dynamics to probe the energy landscape topology of a spin glass connects concepts from non-equilibrium statistical mechanics (KPZ universality) to disordered systems, which is relevant for understanding complex energy minima in quantum many-body systems.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The study investigates how a persistent random walker perceives the complex energy landscape of the $p=3$ spherical spin glass.

**Main result.** The energy correlation time scales with the persistence time as $	au_{	ext{corr}} \sim 	au_p^{1/3}$, confirming that the $p=3$ spherical spin glass belongs to the Kardar–Parisi–Zhang (KPZ) universality class.

**Method.** The authors use persistent Langevin dynamics simulations to measure the energy correlation time ($	au_{	ext{corr}}$) as a function of the persistence time ($	au_p$).

**Model / system.** The system is the $p=3$ spherical spin glass, governed by a Hamiltonian $H(\mathbf{x}) = rac{1}{3!} \sum_{i,j,k} J_{ijk} x_i x_j x_k$. The dynamics are simulated using persistent Langevin dynamics, which incorporates inertia via the persistence time $	au_p$.

**Key observables.** Energy correlation time ($	au_{	ext{corr}}$) and its scaling exponent $\alpha$ defined by $	au_{	ext{corr}} \sim 	au_p^\alpha$.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Persistence time $	au_p$, Temperature $T$, System size $N$. The analysis identifies three regimes: ballistic/inertial, KPZ, and noise-dominated.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis relies on the assumption that $	au_p$ acts as a tunable probe for the energy resolution ($\delta E \sim 1/	au_p$), allowing the detection of the scale-free hierarchy.

**Figures summary.** The results show a U-shaped curve for $\alpha(T)$, identifying the three dynamical regimes, and confirm the KPZ scaling exponent $\alpha=1/3$ through finite-size scaling.

**Paper structure.** The paper establishes the physical problem, details the use of persistent Langevin dynamics, presents the key finding of KPZ scaling for $p=3$, and analyzes the temperature dependence to map out distinct dynamical regimes.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

How does a persistent random walker perceive a complex energy landscape? We address this question by studying the persistent Langevin dynamics of the p=3 spherical spin glass, a paradigmatic mean-field model with a scale-free hierarchical landscape. By tuning the persistence time tau_p, which controls the walker's inertia and effectively sets its energy resolution delta E proportional to 1/tau_p, we measure the energy correlation time tau_corr. At temperature T=1.0, we find tau_corr scales as tau_p to the power alpha with alpha = 0.337 plus or minus 0.035 for tau_p in the range [2,32] (for N=64), in excellent agreement with the Kardar-Parisi-Zhang (KPZ) universality class prediction alpha = 1/3. Finite-size scaling using N = 16,32,48,64,128 yields the thermodynamic limit alpha(infinity) = 0.3333 plus or minus 0.0134, fully consistent with 1/3. Thus, tau_p acts as a tunable probe that reveals the predicted scale-free hierarchy of the landscape. Moreover, the temperature dependence alpha(T) for T = 0.5,1.0,1.5,2.0 exhibits a clear U-shaped curve, identifying three dynamical regimes: ballistic/inertial, KPZ, and noise-dominated. Our results establish persistent Langevin dynamics as a powerful tool for uncovering hidden landscape topology and demonstrate that the p=3 spherical spin glass belongs to the KPZ universality class.

</details>

**Summary.** This thesis tackles the foundations of physics by bridging quantum theory, thermodynamics, and gravity. It develops a novel, self-consistent semiclassical gravity model where quantum collapse dynamics dictate the source of spacetime curvature. The work aims to resolve fundamental issues like the arrow of time and the measurement problem through testable predictions for gravitational experiments.

**Why it may be interesting.** While highly theoretical, the focus on objective collapse models and their coupling to gravity provides a unique, non-standard extension to quantum dynamics that could inform open quantum systems research by providing a mechanism for decoherence/collapse in a relativistic setting.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The central goal is to reconcile quantum mechanics with general relativity and to provide a fundamental understanding of physical laws, particularly addressing the arrow of time and the nature of measurement.

**Main result.** The work proposes a self-consistent semiclassical gravity framework where objective quantum collapse dynamics source the classical Einstein tensor, yielding testable predictions for gravitational entanglement experiments.

**Method.** The approach involves analyzing foundational paradoxes (like absolute velocity detection) and developing a novel coupling mechanism between quantum collapse models and classical general relativity.

**Model / system.** The models span Newtonian mechanics, de Broglie-Bohm pilot-wave theory, objective collapse theories (like GRW), and a semiclassical gravity framework coupling the expectation value of the energy-momentum tensor to the Einstein tensor.

**Key observables.** Relative phases in gravitationally-induced entanglement experiments, and the detectability of absolute velocities in hypothetical universes.

**Important parameters / regimes.** The energy-momentum density $	ext{Tab}(x)$ defining the source for gravity, and the time scale required for phase shifts ($	au \sim 3.5$s).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The framework assumes that the expectation value of the energy-momentum tensor provides a Lorentz-invariant and self-consistent source for classical gravity, and that fundamental physical properties are, in principle, detectable.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the spacetime metric components ($ds^2$) for static, spherically symmetric systems, detailing the matching conditions across Core, Shell, and Exterior regions.

**Paper structure.** The thesis is structured in three interconnected parts: I) Investigating detectability in various regimes (Newtonian/QM), II) Explaining the approach to thermodynamic equilibrium, and III) Developing the self-consistent semiclassical gravity model.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

The work leading to this thesis focuses on assessing and extending quantum theories in order to explore and test their implications across various regimes -- including thermodynamics, semiclassical and quantum gravity scenarios, and the in principle detectable predictions of such theories. The general motivation stems from a basic desire to understand the world form its very foundations. For instance, how can we bridge the gap between what we observe or `perceive' and the fundamental quantum nature in our theories. In particular, this work originated from the search to a better understanding of the nature of time according to our physical theories and the common perception that it invariably `flows' to the future, or, in other words, why do we observe distinct natural processes evolving asymmetrically in time? These motivations led to three distinct, yet interconnected and successful, lines of research, presented here in three separate parts: I. On Possible Detections within Physical Theories; II. On Explaining the Approach to Thermodynamic Equilibrium; and III. Relativistic Collapse Theories and a Self-Consistent Model of Semiclassical Gravity.

</details>

**Authors:** Snehit Panghal, Apostolos Pilaftsis  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2606.01405v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `methods for driven-dissipative` **3/5** · `Keldysh / 2PI / non-Gaussian methods` **1/5** · `Kondo & dissipative impurity` **1/5** · `analog quantum simulation` **1/5** · `correlated / nonlocal dissipation` **1/5** · `driven-dissipative phase transition` **1/5** · `non-equilibrium dynamics` **1/5** · `non-equilibrium universality` **1/5** · `quantum measurements` **1/5**

**Summary.** This paper analyzes the quantum dynamics of Critical Unstable Qubits (CUQs) using the density matrix formalism in a co-decaying frame. It demonstrates that CUQs exhibit unique anharmonic oscillations, distinct from standard Rabi oscillations. The authors provide novel geometric methods to map the complex trajectories of these unstable qubits onto the Bloch sphere, with implications for non-Hermitian quantum simulations.

**Why it may be interesting.** The study provides a detailed geometric understanding of non-Hermitian dynamics on the Bloch sphere, which is crucial for understanding open quantum systems where decay and dissipation are present, linking fundamental quantum mechanics to practical quantum simulation.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper investigates the dynamics and trajectories of a novel class of unstable two-level systems called Critical Unstable Qubits (CUQs). It aims to characterize the state evolution in the Bloch sphere for these non-Hermitian systems.

**Main result.** CUQ states exhibit anharmonic and coherence-decoherence oscillations in the co-decaying frame, contrasting sharply with the harmonic Rabi oscillations of stable Hermitian qubits. The authors provide explicit geometric constructions for these trajectories.

**Method.** The analysis heavily relies on the density matrix formalism and the Bloch vector representation, solving the master equation in a specialized co-decaying frame.

**Model / system.** The system is an unstable two-level qubit governed by a non-Hermitian effective Hamiltonian. The dynamics are studied using the density matrix $\rho(t)$ and its evolution in the co-decaying frame, $\hat{\rho}(t)$.

**Key observables.** The Bloch vector $\mathbf{b}(t)$ describes the state trajectory; key observables include the anharmonicity of oscillations and the eccentricity of the resulting elliptical paths.

**Important parameters / regimes.** The ratio $r = |\Gamma|/(2|E|)$ characterizes the instability, and the initial coherence $\mathbf{b}_0$ determines the specific trajectory.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis utilizes the Weisskopf-Wigner approximation and requires working in the co-decaying frame to maintain a physically meaningful description of coherence.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the comparison between Rabi (Hermitian) and CUQ (non-Hermitian) oscillations, showing the anharmonicity and the geometric construction of the resulting elliptical orbits on the Bloch sphere.

**Paper structure.** The paper progresses from defining the CUQ model and its master equation, to deriving the Bloch vector evolution equations, and finally presenting novel geometric constructions for calculating the trajectories of both pure and mixed states.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We extend previous studies on a novel class of unstable two-level systems which were called Critical Unstable Qubits (CUQs). In an appropriately defined co-decaying frame, the CUQs exhibit striking phenomena of indefinite anharmonic oscillations between two states and coherence-decoherence oscillations of mixed states. These features are distinct from the usual Rabi oscillations observed in the Hermitian counterpart of two-level systems, which are harmonic and preserve the coherence of the quantum state. We employ the density matrix formalism to study these phenomena for mixed states and delve into the nature of the trajectory traversed by these states in the Bloch sphere by studying the time evolution of the Bloch vector that describes the quantum state of the unstable qubit. In particular, we provide for the first time explicit geometric constructions to obtain trajectories of both pure and mixed CUQs in and on the Bloch sphere. This enables us to identify the stationary points of CUQs, at which the states do not evolve in time in the co-decaying frame. The potential implications of our findings for particle cosmology and quantum simulations of non-Hermitian Hamiltonians are discussed.

</details>

**Summary.** This work develops a theoretical framework to describe how charge fluctuations evolve in the rapidly expanding QGP, moving beyond simple Fickian diffusion. By incorporating finite current relaxation time (Maxwell-Cattaneo diffusion), the authors show that memory effects significantly alter the measurable cumulants. This provides a more accurate diagnostic tool for probing the QCD critical point.

**Why it may be interesting.** The rigorous mathematical treatment of memory effects in transport coefficients, leading to modified evolution equations for correlation functions, provides a generalizable framework for non-equilibrium dynamics in complex systems, relevant to open quantum systems modeling.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper addresses the complex, non-equilibrium evolution of fluctuation correlators for conserved charges in the Quark-Gluon Plasma (QGP) fireball, especially when current relaxation effects are significant.

**Main result.** Finite current memory, modeled by Maxwell-Cattaneo diffusion, introduces an additional memory effect beyond standard diffusive lag, which can suppress, shift, and reshape the non-monotonic behavior of higher-order cumulants.

**Method.** The authors derive closed evolution equations for multi-point Wigner functions, extending the standard Fickian diffusion baseline to include finite current relaxation time scales.

**Model / system.** The system is the QGP undergoing rapid expansion, analyzed using theoretical frameworks that extend standard hydrodynamics to incorporate memory effects via the Maxwell-Cattaneo constitutive relation.

**Key observables.** Acceptance-dependent cumulants ($C_N(\Delta)$) of conserved charges, and the evolution of multi-point Wigner functions ($W_N$).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Current relaxation time ($	au$), diffusive lag time, and the proximity to the QCD critical point.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis assumes the ability to derive closed deterministic evolution equations for multi-point correlators and compares results against the $	au 	o 0$ (Fickian) limit.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the comparison of cumulant evolution under different diffusion models and potentially show background trajectories in the QCD phase diagram.

**Paper structure.** The paper builds from establishing the link between cumulants and correlators, deriving the Maxwell-Cattaneo evolution equations for the correlators, and finally numerically comparing the resulting cumulant dynamics against the standard Fickian baseline.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Fluctuations of conserved charges are among the main proposed signatures of the quantum chromodynamics (QCD) critical point, but their interpretation requires a dynamical description of how fluctuation correlators evolve during the finite lifetime of the quark--gluon plasma (QGP) fireball. The standard baseline for this evolution is Fickian diffusion, in which the diffusive current follows the local density gradient instantaneously. This instantaneous-current limit can miss delayed-response effects when the current-relaxation time becomes comparable to the relaxation time of the relevant fluctuation modes. In this work we extend this baseline to Maxwell--Cattaneo diffusion, where the current relaxes on a finite time scale and therefore retains memory. We derive closed evolution equations for multi-point Wigner functions and convert the freezeout correlators into acceptance-dependent cumulants along representative trajectories in the QCD phase diagram. While Fickian diffusion already causes the correlators to lag behind their instantaneous equilibrium values, finite current relaxation introduces an additional memory effect beyond this diffusive lag. As a result, current memory can suppress, shift, and reshape the non-monotonic behavior of the cumulants relative to both instantaneous equilibrium and Fickian diffusion, with the most visible effects appearing in higher-order cumulants and their ratios.

</details>

Snapshots of two L = 48 replicas at β = 1.09 near the last specific heat peak with coexistence between a configuration in the IC phase (left panel) with four defect lines (k = 11/48) and a configuration in the ⟨2, 2⟩phase (k = 12/48).</figcaption>

</figure>

</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This work investigates the phase transitions of the 2D ANNNI model using sophisticated simulation methods. The authors introduce and validate a powerful combination of Population Annealing and a two-replica cluster algorithm. This new technique successfully resolves the complex physics of the incommensurate floating phase, demonstrating superior equilibration capabilities over existing simulation tools.

**Why it may be interesting.** The development and rigorous testing of the two-replica cluster algorithm combined with PA represent a significant advancement in computational techniques for simulating highly frustrated magnetic systems, providing insights applicable to other complex lattice models.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The study focuses on resolving the sequence of sharp specific heat peaks that characterize the finite-size incommensurate floating (IC) phase in the 2D ANNNI model.

**Main result.** The combination of population annealing with a two-replica cluster algorithm proves significantly more effective at equilibrating the system than single-replica or Metropolis methods in the strongly frustrated regime.

**Method.** The authors employ advanced Monte Carlo techniques, specifically Population Annealing (PA) coupled with a novel two-replica cluster algorithm, to simulate the system's thermal properties.

**Model / system.** The system is the 2D Axial Next-Nearest-Neighbor Ising (ANNNI) model, described by a Hamiltonian involving nearest-neighbor and competing next-nearest-neighbor interactions. The model exhibits multiple phases, including ferromagnetic, ordered $\langle 2, 2 angle$, and incommensurate floating (IC) phases.

**Key observables.** Specific heat ($c$), Binder cumulant of the overlap ($B$), line magnetization ($m_\ell$), and free energy estimators ($ar{F}$).

**Important parameters / regimes.** The coupling constant $\kappa$ (which dictates the phase), system size $L$ (tested up to 256), and the inverse temperature $eta$.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis relies on assumptions regarding the physical origin of the specific heat peaks (e.g., coexistence of states differing by four three-spin defect lines) and the extrapolation methods used for transition temperatures.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate snapshots of replicas showing defect lines, comparisons of specific heat peaks across different algorithms (Metropolis vs. TR), and the evolution of the Binder cumulant $B$ across different phases.

**Paper structure.** The paper progresses by first establishing the physical model and the need for advanced simulation techniques. It then details the implementation and comparison of various Monte Carlo algorithms (Metropolis, Wolff, Two-replica Cluster) within the framework of Population Annealing. Finally, it analyzes key observables like specific heat and the Binder cumulant to characterize the phase transitions.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

The axial next-nearest-neighbor Ising (ANNNI) model in two dimensions is studied using population annealing combined with a two-replica cluster algorithm. We are able to fully resolve the sequence of sharp specific heat peaks that characterize the finite-size incommensurate floating phase. We also show that the two-replica cluster algorithm is much more effective in equilibrating the system than either single-replica cluster methods or the Metropolis algorithm when these are combined with population annealing. We argue that effectiveness of the new algorithm is due to its ability to move groups of defect lines between replicas combined with resampling in population annealing, which removes replicas from the population that have larger numbers of defect lines.

</details>

### [Magnon bandstructure and topology in a periodically deformed Kagome lattice with DM interaction](http://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00696v1)

**Authors:** Mohammed Abdullah Hammadi, Mohammed Salman Alsadah, Husam Abdulmajeed Noorwli, Hocine Bahlouli, Michael Vogl  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** strongly correlated electrons · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2606.00696v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `analog quantum simulation` **3/5** · `exotic spin models in light-matter` **3/5** · `spintronics-quantum-optics interface` **3/5**

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>

**Summary.** This work theoretically examines how periodic deformations on a Kagome lattice modify the magnon band structure when DM interactions are present. By comparing anti-ferromagnetic and ferromagnetic couplings, the authors show that deformation can induce rich topological features, evidenced by large Chern numbers. This highlights lattice engineering as a key tool for realizing novel topological physics in magnetic materials.

**Why it may be interesting.** The explicit connection between geometric deformation, magnetic interactions, and the emergence of non-trivial topology (quantized Chern numbers) provides a powerful mechanism for engineering exotic quantum states in condensed matter magnets.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The study investigates the magnon band structure and topological properties of a magnetic system subjected to periodic lattice deformations and Dzyaloshinskii-Moriya (DM) interactions.

**Main result.** The lattice deformation enriches the band structure, leading to rich topological features, particularly in the ferromagnetic coupling case where Chern numbers can reach $\pm 2$.

**Method.** The analysis employs linear spin-wave theory via the Holstein-Primakoff transformation, followed by diagonalizing the resulting bosonic Hamiltonian in momentum space to calculate topological invariants like the Chern number.

**Model / system.** The system is a Heisenberg model with DM interaction defined on a Kagome lattice. The model is analyzed on a periodically deformed lattice, which modifies the exchange and DM couplings.

**Key observables.** Magnon band structure, Chern numbers ($C_n$), and the presence/closing of band gaps.

**Important parameters / regimes.** The nature of coupling (anti-ferromagnetic vs. ferromagnetic), the strength of the DM interaction ($D_z/J$), and the strength of the lattice deformation ($C$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis relies on linear spin-wave theory and a $1/S$ expansion, which are approximations valid under certain conditions.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate band structures comparing undeformed vs. deformed lattices, and show the dependence of Chern numbers and band gaps on the deformation strength and DM interaction strength.

**Paper structure.** The paper systematically analyzes the Hamiltonian, first establishing the theoretical framework using spin-wave theory. It then compares the topological properties in the anti-ferromagnetic (trivial) versus ferromagnetic (rich) regimes, demonstrating how lattice deformation drives topological phase transitions.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We study the band structure and topology of magnons for a Heisenberg model with DM interaction on a deformed Kagome lattice. For simplicity, we focus on a periodically deformed lattice with hexagonal symmetry and an enlarged unit cell. This enlarged unit cell gives rise to a richer band structure than in the undeformed case. Analyzing band topology, there is a distinction between the topologically trivial case with anti-ferromagnetic coupling and the topologically rich case with ferromagnetic coupling. In the anti-ferromagnetic case, a spin-space symmetry, also present in the classical ground state, enforces these topologically trivial states. In the ferromagnetic case, this symmetry is spontaneously broken by the classical ground state. Consequently, the band structure also hosts rich topological features. Specifically, we observe many topological transitions and bands with Chern numbers ranging from $+2$ to $-2$, which makes the system richer than in the undeformed case. This emergent rich topological structure demonstrates that deformed magnets can host new and exciting physics.

</details>

### [Quantum-inspired Topographic Stereovision](http://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02197v1)

**Authors:** Fanglin Bao, Youfei Xie  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2606.02197v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `interference shaping light` **3/5** · `quantum optics experiment` **3/5** · `quantum measurements` **2/5** · `entanglement & information structure` **1/5**

This pro- cess resembles a Mach-Zehnder interferometer, in which the contrast between two spatial modes encodes the topography of the thermal light source. We introduce a central spatial mode (C) as the local oscillator (LO) for balanced homodyne detection (BHD) to fully extract the topographic information. M: mirror. P: variable phase retarder. Q: quarter-wave phase retarder. Arrows with varying lengths:...</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This work re-evaluates stereovision, arguing that topography is more fundamental than distance for distant objects. The authors propose a quantum-inspired topographic interferometer that uses quantum correlations to measure topography with precision surpassing classical limits. This establishes a new quantum framework for heat-assisted detection in fields like astronomy.

**Why it may be interesting.** It applies advanced quantum estimation theory (Quantum Fisher Information) to a complex, non-equilibrium sensing problem (thermal imaging), providing a quantum-enhanced framework for remote sensing.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper challenges the traditional reliance on triangulation for stereovision, arguing that for distant objects, topography is the more relevant observable than absolute distance.

**Main result.** The proposed topographic interferometer enables quantum-enhanced stereovision of thermal sources beyond the Rayleigh limit by exploiting cross-detector correlations.

**Method.** It introduces stereo regularization and proposes a topographic interferometer that uses Mach-Zehnder arms and balanced homodyne detection to probe topography.

**Model / system.** The system models the optical field from a thermal source in the X-Z plane, analyzing the superposition of photons across stereo spatial modes (L, R) fed into a beam splitter.

**Key observables.** Topography ($	heta$), absolute distance ($Z$), disparity ($\delta_q$), and the quantum Fisher information ($K_h$).

**Important parameters / regimes.** The analysis focuses on the long-range and weak-source limits ($\epsilon \ll 1$) and the incompatibility quantified by $\Delta^2 Z + \Delta^2 	heta \ge 	ext{constant}$.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The method assumes the sub-Rayleigh regime and addresses stereo anisotropies violating emitter-number conservation.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 compares traditional stereovision (measuring Z) versus topographic stereovision.

**Paper structure.** The paper first establishes the observable-measurement mismatch, then introduces stereo regularization, and finally details the construction and analysis of the topographic interferometer to achieve quantum-enhanced ranging.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We challenge the long-unquestioned triangulation in distant stereovision, where shape rather than distance is the relevant observable. Our information-regret analysis reveals that the optimal measurements for absolute distance and distance gradient are unexpectedly different and incompatible. To resolve this observable-measurement mismatch, we introduce stereo regularization to address stereo anisotropies that violate prevailing emitter-number conservation, and propose the topographic interferometer, which exploits cross-detector correlations to probe topography without measuring the distance profile. Our interferometer turns parallaxing paths into Mach-Zehnder arms and incorporates a central path as the local oscillator for balanced homodyne detection, saturating the quantum Fisher information with improved topographic error scaling. Our work enables topographic stereovision of thermal sources beyond the Rayleigh limit, thereby establishing a quantum-inspired framework for heat-assisted detection and ranging in remote sensing and astronomy.

</details>

### [Locality-Induced Hierarchical Backflow Wavefunctions for Correlated Fermions](http://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00924v1)

**Authors:** Yu-Tong Zhou, Zheng-Wei Zhou, Wen-Yuan Liu  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** strongly correlated electrons · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2606.00924v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `analog quantum simulation` **3/5** · `Keldysh / 2PI / non-Gaussian methods` **2/5** · `methods for driven-dissipative` **2/5** · `entanglement & information structure` **1/5**

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>
<div class="paper-figures-scroll">
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2606.00924_figures/2606.00924_fig1.jpg" alt="Figure 1">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> FIG. 1. Orbital structure of hierarchical backﬂow (HB) wavefunc- tions at a given site i for diﬀerent path depths K, illustrating how K controls the range of backﬂow correlations. (a) K = 0: the orbital de- pends only on site i, corresponding to the Hartree-Fock state with no backﬂow correlations. (b) K = 1: the orbital at site i depends on its nearby sites j. (c) K = 2: the orbital further includes sites p that are neighbors of j; increasing K systematically enhances the expressive power. (d) Although each orbital depends only on local paths (here shown for K = 2), the Slater determinant couples them to generate many-body correlations in the wavefunction amplitude W(s).</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2606.00924_figures/2606.00924_fig2.jpg" alt="Figure 2">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> FIG. 2. Energies of the two-dimensional Hubbard model under PBC with U = 8 as a function of the path depth K (K = 0, 1, 2) at (a) half-ﬁlling and (b) nh = 0.125 hole doping. K = 0 corresponds to the Hartree-Fock state. Increasing K systematically lowers the energy. (c) Comparison of staggered spin correlation functions C(r) = (−1)x+y⟨S(0, 0) · S(x, y)⟩as a function of distance r = p</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2606.00924_figures/2606.00924_fig3.jpg" alt="Figure 3">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 3.</b> FIG. 4. Energy comparison with NNB [31] and HFDS [32] for dif- ferent sizes at nh = 0.125 and U = 8, including HB K = 1, K = 2 and the RHB.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2606.00924_figures/2606.00924_fig4.jpg" alt="Figure 4">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 4.</b> FIG. 3. The distribution of hole density (color map) and local spin S z moment (arrow length proportional to magnitude) on (a) 12 × 16 and (b) 16 × 16 PBC lattice at nh = 0.125 hole doping with U = 8.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2606.00924_figures/2606.00924_fig5.jpg" alt="Figure 5">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 5.</b> FIG. 5. Conventional neural network states employ a global archi- tecture (left), whereas our physics-grounded ansatz features a local- nonlocal structure (right). For the same number of determinants, which provides similar expressive power, the number of variational parameters in our representation scales as O(NneuronN) + O(N2), fa- vorable compared to O(NneuronN2) for global architectures.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This work introduces the Hierarchical Backflow (HB) wavefunction, a novel, systematically improvable ansatz for correlated fermions, by leveraging the principle of locality. By extending this to the Residual HB (RHB) ansatz, the authors achieve high accuracy in simulating the Hubbard model, demonstrating a powerful, scalable framework for strongly correlated electron physics.

**Why it may be interesting.** While focused on condensed matter, the development of a structured, systematically improvable ansatz (HB/RHB) that bridges determinantal forms with neural network representations is highly relevant for developing general-purpose quantum state representations applicable across different physical domains.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper addresses the challenge of simulating and representing the ground state of strongly correlated fermionic systems, such as those described by the Hubbard model, due to the exponentially large Hilbert space.

**Main result.** The proposed Hierarchical Backflow (HB) and Residual Hierarchical Backflow (RHB) wavefunctions provide a systematically improvable and highly accurate variational ansatz, achieving results comparable to advanced quantum Monte Carlo methods.

**Method.** The core method involves constructing a variational wavefunction based on the backflow concept, which is systematically refined by introducing a path depth parameter K, and further enhanced by a nonlocal neural network factor (RHB).

**Model / system.** The study focuses on the two-dimensional Hubbard model, characterized by hopping ($t_{ij}$) and on-site repulsion ($U_{ik}$), applied to various doping levels and system sizes.

**Key observables.** Ground-state energy per site, staggered spin correlation function, and local spin/hole density distributions.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Path depth K (controlling correlation range), doping level ($n_h$), and interaction strength ($U$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The method relies on the locality principle to organize backflow correlations, and the RHB ansatz uses a two-stage optimization scheme to overcome trapping in the optimization landscape.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the orbital structure dependence on K, energy convergence curves showing systematic improvement with K, and spatial maps revealing the formation of stripe phases at finite doping.

**Paper structure.** The paper develops the HB ansatz by proposing locality as the organizing principle, systematically improving its expressive power via K. It then extends this to the RHB ansatz, which incorporates nonlocal correlations via a neural network, and validates this structure using advanced optimization techniques and comparisons to established benchmarks.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We show that locality provides a natural principle to hierarchically organize backflow wavefunctions. This leads us to propose a family of variational fermionic states, termed hierarchical backflow (HB) wavefunctions. The expressive power of HB is systematically improvable, controlled by a path depth $K$ which reflects the range of backflow correlations. At half-filling, the HB with $K=1$ already achieves high energy precision, with an accuracy around $0.5\%$ for system sizes from $4\times 4$ to $10\times 10$. At hole doping $n_h=0.125$, the method scales efficiently to $12\times16$ and $16\times16$ systems, and the energy systematically achieves higher accuracy with $K$ increasing, yielding a clear stripe phase. The HB further enables a local-nonlocal decomposition, naturally bridging to neural quantum states, while featuring compact representations and efficient optimization. Our work reveals locality as a natural organizing principle of backflow wavefunctions, opening a new framework with systematic improvability and interpretability for large-scale simulations of correlated fermion systems.

</details>

**Summary.** This work experimentally investigates the predicted $p$-wave spin splitting in the correlated material CeNiAsO, which is theoretically expected based on magnetic symmetry. The authors report that ARPES measurements fail to detect this splitting, demonstrating that the strong electronic correlations force the Ce 4f electrons into a localized regime. This establishes a key many-body constraint: symmetry alone is insufficient to guarantee spin splitting if electron localization dominates the magnetic coupling.

**Why it may be interesting.** The core finding—that symmetry is necessary but not sufficient—provides a crucial lesson for modeling correlated electron systems, particularly those where local moments interact with itinerant carriers, which is relevant to understanding exotic quantum states in condensed matter.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper investigates the theoretical prediction versus experimental observation of nonrelativistic p-wave spin splitting in the heavy-fermion oxypnictide CeNiAsO, challenging symmetry-based predictions for magnetism.

**Main result.** Experimental ARPES data show no evidence of the expected spin splitting or band folding across magnetic transitions, suggesting that the Ce 4f electrons remain localized and decouple from the conduction bands.

**Method.** The study combines ultra-low-temperature, high-resolution ARPES measurements with theoretical calculations (DFT) comparing different treatments of the f-electrons.

**Model / system.** The system is the layered heavy-fermion oxypnictide CeNiAsO, which is studied across magnetic transitions into SDW and coplanar antiferromagnetic states. The physics involves the interplay between itinerant conduction electrons and localized Ce 4f moments.

**Key observables.** ARPES band dispersions, Fermi surface mapping, observation of $p$-wave band splitting, and the spectral weight of the Ce 4f electrons.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Magnetic transition temperatures ($T_{N1} \approx 9 	ext{ K}$, $T_{N2} \approx 6 	ext{ K}$), the ratio of Kondo coherence temperature ($T^*$) to Néel temperature ($T_N$), and the localized vs. itinerant limit.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The initial theoretical framework assumes that extending spin-space group symmetries predicts the splitting, but the experiment tests the validity of this assumption under strong correlations.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate magnetic transitions via transport measurements, ARPES intensity maps showing the absence of predicted band folding or spin splitting, and theoretical comparisons between different electronic structure treatments.

**Paper structure.** The paper establishes the theoretical prediction based on symmetry, presents the experimental ARPES measurements across magnetic phases, analyzes the failure to observe the predicted splitting, and concludes by revising the theoretical understanding to incorporate the many-body constraint imposed by electron localization.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

The extending of spin-space group symmetries to coplanar antiferromagnets has predicted the emergence of odd-parity nonrelativistic spin splittings, making the identification of a practical $p$-wave magnet a central pursuit in spintronics. The layered heavy-fermion oxypnictide CeNiAsO has been widely regarded as the prototypical platform to verify this paradigm, as its commensurate coplanar magnetic configuration is theoretically expected to induce a robust $p$-wave band splitting. Here, we investigate the electronic structure of single-crystal CeNiAsO using ultra-low-temperature, high-resolution, and resonant angle-resolved photoemission spectroscopy (ARPES). Across the consecutive magnetic transitions into the ordered phases, our spectroscopic data reveal neither the expected band folding associated with a spin density wave nor any observable $p$-wave band splitting, demonstrating that the conduction bands retain full Kramers degeneracy. By tracking the temperature dependence of the Ce 4$f$ spectral weight via resonant ARPES, we find no evidence of coherent $c-f$ hybridization near the Fermi level within the magnetically ordered states, confirming that the Ce 4$f$ electrons operate in the localized limit. Our findings establish a clear many-body constraint on projecting real-space magnetic symmetries onto momentum-space electronic bands, demonstrating that geometric symmetry classifications constitute a necessary framework but are not a sufficient condition for nonrelativistic spin splittings in the presence of strong electronic correlations.

</details>

<a id="paper-2606.00607"></a>
### [Qutrit-based Synthetic Three-Level System](http://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00607v1)

**Authors:** Surajit Sen, Tushar Kanti Dey  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2606.00607v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `analog quantum simulation` **3/5** · `entanglement & information structure` **3/5** · `Rydberg arrays` **1/5** · `non-equilibrium dynamics` **1/5**

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>
<div class="paper-figures-scroll">
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2606.00607_figures/2606.00607_fig1.jpg" alt="Figure 1">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> FIG. 1. Schematic illustration of the mapping of a two-qutrit system onto synthetic V , Ξ and Λ</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This theoretical work develops an SU(3) framework to engineer synthetic three-level quantum systems using two coupled qutrits. By leveraging the group's algebraic structure, the authors derive effective Hamiltonians for three distinct configurations without relying on problematic Rydberg interactions. Entanglement dynamics are rigorously quantified using novel SU(3) concurrence measures.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work provides a powerful, algebraically grounded alternative to conventional Rydberg-based quantum simulation platforms, offering a cleaner theoretical pathway to engineer controlled multi-level quantum dynamics.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper aims to construct synthetic three-level quantum systems using a two-qutrit architecture, specifically avoiding the use of Rydberg states and associated van der Waals interactions.

**Main result.** The authors successfully map the system dynamics onto an effective synthetic three-level manifold using the algebraic structure of SU(3) entangled states.

**Method.** The analysis involves deriving effective Hamiltonians by examining non-vanishing commutator relations between the free and interaction Hamiltonians, followed by quantifying entanglement using generalized SU(3) concurrence measures.

**Model / system.** The model is a two-qutrit system operating in the composite Hilbert space $\mathcal{H}_3 \otimes \mathcal{H}_3$, governed by an SU(3) framework. Three distinct synthetic configurations (VT, TU, UV) are analyzed.

**Key observables.** SU(3) I-concurrence and generalized Wootters-type SU(3) concurrence are used to quantify entanglement dynamics.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Rabi coupling strengths ($\Omega(x)$) and detuning offsets ($\Delta E_{Mij}$) define the Hamiltonian parameters.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The method relies on the algebraic structure of the SU(3) group and assumes the coupling mechanism circumvents the need for Rydberg-mediated interactions.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 schematically illustrates the mapping of the two-qutrit system onto synthetic V, $\Xi$, and $\Lambda$ type three-level configurations.

**Paper structure.** The paper first establishes the theoretical framework using SU(3) algebra, then analyzes the dynamics by calculating commutators for the three configurations, derives the effective Hamiltonians for the reduced manifolds, and finally quantifies the entanglement using the specialized concurrence measures.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We present a theoretical framework based on the $SU(3)$ group to construct synthetic three-level configurations from a two-qutrit system consisting of two three-level subsystems. Utilizing its underlying algebraic structure and a set of nine $SU(3)$ entangled states, we show that the system Hamiltonian can be mapped onto an effective synthetic three-level manifold without introducing Rydberg states. We investigate the entanglement dynamics of these synthetic configurations by introducing the $SU(3)$ I-concurrence and a generalized Wootters-type $SU(3)$ concurrence as quantitative measures of entanglement in such system.

</details>

**Authors:** Matthew C. O'Brien, Eduardo Fradkin  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** strongly correlated electrons · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2606.00211v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

In (b), the physical stiffness, which is modified by higher derivative inter- actions, is compared to the renormalized stiffness parameter shown by the dashed gray line, which is the stiffness of the theory in the absence of higher derivative interactions.</figcaption>

In (b), the dimensionless physical stiffness is compared to the dimensionless renormalized stiffness param- eter ρR/µ√b1 shown by the dashed gray line.</figcaption>

The inset shows a magnified view of the region in the dashed box. The dotted vertical line indicates the location of the pole in Γ(0,2)[0].</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This theoretical work investigates the complex phase diagram of a frustrated quantum antiferromagnet using advanced field theory techniques. It reveals that the system can stabilize a vestigial nematic order, even when long-range magnetic order is destroyed by quantum fluctuations. This provides a detailed theoretical framework for understanding symmetry breaking in frustrated quantum magnets.

**Why it may be interesting.** The focus on symmetry breaking (nematicity) and quantum fluctuations near a critical point is highly relevant to understanding exotic quantum phases in strongly correlated materials like cuprates.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper addresses the long-standing open problem of determining the quantum phase diagram of the two-dimensional $J_1$-$J_3$ Heisenberg antiferromagnet.

**Main result.** The study shows that previously overlooked interactions are crucial for stabilizing a vestigial nematic phase, which persists down to zero temperature, separating different quantum disordered and ordered regimes.

**Method.** The analysis employs a semiclassical approach using a continuum nonlinear sigma model effective field theory, enhanced by the nonperturbative large-$N$ technique.

**Model / system.** The system is the two-dimensional quantum $J_1$-$J_3$ Heisenberg antiferromagnet on a square lattice, described by a Hamiltonian involving nearest-neighbor ($J_1$) and next-nearest-neighbor ($J_3$) interactions.

**Key observables.** Nematic order parameters ($\Psi_1, \Psi_2$), static structure factor $S(q)$, and the correlation length $\xi$.

**Important parameters / regimes.** The ratio of coupling constants ($J_1, J_3$), the large-$N$ limit, and the proximity to the classical Lifshitz point.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The method is semiclassical and formally exact only in the limit $S 	o \infty$; the analysis often relies on assuming certain order parameters are energetically suppressed ($\Psi_1 \equiv 0$).

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the phase diagram showing the coexistence of spiral, vestigial nematic, and quantum disordered regions, and plot susceptibilities and structure factors across different parameter regimes.

**Paper structure.** The paper builds from introducing the continuum effective field theory, applying the large-$N$ technique to derive the effective potential, and finally solving the resulting saddle-point equations to map out the phase diagram.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

The phase diagram of the two-dimensional quantum $J_1$-$J_3$ Heisenberg antiferromagnet on a square lattice is a long-standing open problem. Despite recent advances in numerical techniques for quantum spin models, a detailed analytical theory is still lacking. We address this problem using a semiclassical approach based on a continuum nonlinear sigma model effective field theory, applying the nonperturbative large-$N$ technique to map out the phase diagram and determine the magnetic correlations. We show that previously-overlooked interactions are crucial for stabilizing a vestigial nematic phase, both at finite and zero temperature. Our results reveal that the spontaneous breaking of global symmetries in the $J_1$-$J_3$ model is controlled by the strength of infrared quantum fluctuations which are enhanced by proximity to the classical Lifshitz point.

</details>

### [Interplay between Quantum Metric and Hybridized Collective Modes in Flat-Band Superfluids](http://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01235v1)

**Authors:** Yi Liu, Mingyan Wang, Penghui Hu, Yao Lu  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum gases · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2606.01235v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `analog quantum simulation` **3/5** · `Keldysh / 2PI / non-Gaussian methods` **2/5** · `methods for driven-dissipative` **2/5**

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>

The spectrum features a pair of isolated perfectly flat bands separated by a distinct energy gap. (All energies are in units of J, and a = 1.)</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2606.01235_figures/2606.01235_fig3.jpg" alt="Figure 3">
