(a) The extracted low-energy dispersion curves ω(q) for various staggered hopping parameters δ. (b) Log-log plot of the dispersion data. The parallel linear fits yield power-law exponents of α ≈2.0, confirming the parabolic nature of the excitations and the absence of a linear term. (The frequency ω is plotted in units of J.)</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2606.01235_figures/2606.01235_fig4.jpg" alt="Figure 4">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 4.</b> FIG. 3: The low-energy collective mode dispersion ω(qx, qy), obtained by numerically solving det[M(q, ω)] = 0 for small momenta at a fixed staggered hopping δ = 0.3J. The gapless excitation spectrum forms a parabolic surface. (Energies are in units of J, with a = 1.)</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2606.01235_figures/2606.01235_fig5.jpg" alt="Figure 5">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 5.</b> FIG. 5: Collective mode dispersion curvature κ (evaluated along the qx direction) versus the average normal-state quantum metric under varying staggered hoppings δ. The circular markers represent the data obtained directly from the multi-channel response matrix, while the solid line represents the analytical prediction κ = 2E</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This theoretical work investigates collective excitations in flat-band superfluids, showing that the mode dispersion is not linear (like conventional superfluids) but quadratic ($\omega \propto q^2$). The key finding is that the curvature of this parabolic dispersion is universally determined by the normal-state quantum metric of the underlying flat band.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work provides a rigorous, geometric understanding of superfluid dynamics in highly constrained systems (flat bands), linking macroscopic excitation properties directly to fundamental quantum geometric invariants (the quantum metric).

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** To determine the low-energy collective excitation dispersion relation in flat-band superfluids by analyzing the coupled dynamics of pairing, amplitude, and density fluctuations.

**Main result.** The collective mode exhibits a quadratic dispersion ($\omega \propto q^2$) at small momenta, and the coefficient governing this curvature is determined by the normal-state quantum metric of the flat band.

**Method.** The authors employ a path integral formalism with a Generalized Hubbard-Stratonovich transformation, analyzing the full dynamic response matrix via a multi-channel fluctuation expansion.

**Model / system.** The system is a time-reversal symmetric superfluid characterized by an isolated flat band, modeled using a mean-field Hamiltonian projected onto the flat-band subspace.

**Key observables.** Collective excitation dispersion relation $\omega(q)$, the quadratic curvature coefficient $\kappa$, and the quantum metric $g_{qq}(k)$.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Zero temperature ($T=0$), long-wavelength limit ($q 	o 0$), and the existence of an isolated flat band.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis assumes the flat band is energetically well separated from other bands, and that the low-energy dynamics can be captured by projecting onto the degenerate null space of the static response matrix.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the schematic band structure, the parabolic nature of the low-energy dispersion ($\omega \propto q^2$), and a direct comparison showing the fitted curvature tracking the theoretical quantum metric.

**Paper structure.** The paper progresses from defining the coupled fluctuation dynamics (GHS transformation) to constructing the full dynamic response matrix, solving for the zero-frequency modes, and finally calculating the second-order spatial derivative of the matrix to extract the curvature coefficient.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We investigate collective excitations in flat-band superfluids by incorporating the coupled dynamics of pairing (phase and amplitude) and density fluctuations. We demonstrate that for any time-reversal symmetric superfluid system with an isolated flat band, only a single low-energy collective mode emerges in the long-wavelength limit. In contrast to the linearly dispersive Goldstone mode in conventional superfluids, this hybridized mode is gapless at zero momentum but exhibits a quadratic dispersion ($ω\propto q^2$) at small momenta. Analytically, we reveal that the dispersion coefficient of this collective mode is governed by the normal-state quantum metric of the flat band. These analytical predictions are in excellent agreement with numerical calculations. Our results are universally applicable to any generic $s$-wave flat-band superfluid, provided the flat band is energetically well separated from other dispersive bands.

</details>

**Authors:** Morgan Berkane, Sahel Ashhab  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** disordered systems and neural networks · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2606.02291v1>

(15), and panel (b.2) shows the associated lattice configuration.</figcaption>

**Summary.** This work explores engineering disorder in lattice systems to achieve maximal quantum state localization. By optimizing on-site energies, the authors show that engineered patterns outperform random disorder, and crucially, they demonstrate that the optimal single-particle settings can guide the design of highly decoupled qubit processors.

**Why it may be interesting.** The methodology of mapping complex many-body localization problems onto simpler, solvable single-particle optimization problems is highly valuable for designing quantum hardware.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper investigates whether engineered disorder can achieve stronger quantum state localization than purely random disorder in lattice systems.

**Main result.** Optimizing on-site energies to maximize localization in the single-particle model successfully identifies optimal settings that achieve maximum decoupling in the complex, many-body qubit lattice.

**Method.** The authors use optimization techniques (like genetic algorithms) on cost functions derived from autocorrelation functions and perturbation theory to suppress quantum transport.

**Model / system.** The study analyzes two models: a single particle on a 2D square lattice using a tight-binding Hamiltonian, and a two-dimensional square qubit lattice relevant for quantum processors.

**Key observables.** Site-averaged autocorrelation function C(t) (localization measure) and the cost function L_FHS (for qubit decoupling).

**Important parameters / regimes.** On-site energies {W_i}, hopping amplitudes J_ij, and the detuning parameters $\Delta_{ij} = |W_i - W_j|$.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The single-particle optimization constraints are assumed to be sufficient to guide the optimization for the exponentially complex full qubit Hilbert space.

**Figures summary.** Figures compare the time evolution of the autocorrelation function C(t) for random, optimized, and regular lattice configurations, demonstrating superior localization in the engineered cases.

**Paper structure.** The paper progresses from analyzing single-particle localization using cost functions, to applying these concepts to the full qubit lattice dynamics, and finally refining the optimization using perturbation theory to account for higher-order hopping processes.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We investigate localization in two models: a single particle in a two-dimensional square lattice described by the tight binding Hamiltonian, and a two-dimensional square qubit lattice. It is well-known that Anderson localization occurs under suitable conditions in which the system parameters are chosen randomly from some statistical distribution. We propose a situation in which the parameters, specifically the on-site energies, are carefully chosen in such a way that a localization-quantifying parameter is maximized. We demonstrate the optimization procedure with numerical calculations in which the engineered localization significantly exceeds the average localization caused by a random distribution of the on-site energies. We explore the relation between spatial patterns and localization efficiency. Furthermore, we use perturbation theory to gain insight into the localization mechanism and obtain an improved cost function for optimization calculations, leading to enhanced localization in both the single-particle and full Hilbert spaces. Although large-scale simulations for qubit lattices are computationally infeasible, we use small-system simulations to demonstrate that results obtained using the single-particle tight binding model can be adapted to identify optimal settings for qubit lattice systems to achieve maximum decoupling between the qubits, which can be valuable for optimizing the idle-state settings on a quantum processor.

</details>

**Summary.** The paper experimentally demonstrates a novel MDI-QKD protocol that utilizes a large alphabet set encoded across multiple time-bins. This technique overcomes the low key rate limitation of standard MDI-QKD by mapping each successful coincidence event to multiple bits. The results show substantial improvements in the achievable secret key rate, paving the way for more scalable quantum communication networks.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work directly addresses the scalability of quantum key distribution by increasing the information density per transmitted quantum state, which is a key area for quantum network development.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The primary goal is to improve the secret key rate (SKR) of Measurement-Device-Independent Quantum Key Distribution (MDI-QKD), which is inherently limited by coincidence measurements.

**Main result.** By encoding states across a large alphabet set using time-bin encoding, the authors achieved significant SKR improvements (e.g., 3-times improvement) compared to conventional two-state encoding.

**Method.** The protocol involves encoding states across multiple time-bins (large alphabet set $d$) and utilizing level-$d$ coincidence measurements at the central node to boost information capacity.

**Model / system.** The experiment uses a standard MDI-QKD setup implemented with real fiber spools and single-photon avalanche photodetectors (SPADs), employing time-bin encoding for the large alphabet set.

**Key observables.** Secret Key Rate (SKR) in bps, coincidence gain ($Q_s$), and phase-error rate ($\epsilon$).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Encoding dimension ($d$), link length (2 km and 50 km), and the comparison against two-state encoding and COW-QKD.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis assumes that level-$d$ coincidence events are operationally equivalent to Bell-state measurements, and that discarding same-slot detection events is necessary due to detector deadtime limitations.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the time-bin encoding scheme, the level-$d$ coincidence measurement outcomes, and plot the SKR as a function of link length and encoding dimension ($d$).

**Paper structure.** The paper details the protocol by describing state preparation (Z and X basis encoding), the measurement process using level-$d$ coincidences, parameter estimation via decoy states, and finally, calculating the secure key rate.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We report on the experimental demonstration of an expanded basis set (called here as alphabet set) time-bin encoded measurement-device-independent quantum key distribution (MDI-QKD). While MDI-QKD is known to prevent detector-side attacks, it inherently suffers from reduced secret key rate (SKR) due to coincidence measurements performed at the central measurement node. To address this limitation, we encode states across multiple time-bins thereby increasing possible coincidence events and mapping each successful alphabet exchange to multiple bits, thereby increasing the information capacity per alphabet transmitted. Using a standard MDI-QKD set-up with real fiber spools and single-photon avalanche photodetectors, we achieve SKRs of 401 (133.6) bps and 28 (10.7) bps for 8 (2) encoded states for distances of 2 and 50 km, respectively, resulting in 3- and 2.63-times improvement, respectively when compared to the conventional two-state encoding. Furthemore, the large alphabet set MDIQKD results are compared with a similar encoding scheme implemented for coherent-one-way (COW) protocol. This comparison reveals a clear advantage of using a larger alphabet set for MDI-QKD, where increased Z-basis coincidence events yields increased SKR. These results provide important insights into the scalability of MDI-QKD key-rates without requiring additional hardware modifications, paving the way for next-generation, quantum key distribution networks.

</details>

**Summary.** This theoretical work models magnetic frustration arising from magnetoelastic coupling in a kagomé lattice. It shows that the system transitions through a self-frustrated phase, where structural distortions generate inherent magnetic frustration even when the initial magnetic interactions are simple. This mechanism provides a novel route to understanding complex magnetic ground states in correlated materials.

**Why it may be interesting.** The concept of dynamically generated frustration via lattice coupling is highly relevant to understanding emergent quantum phases in strongly correlated materials, particularly those exhibiting structural instabilities.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper investigates 'magnetic self-frustration,' a phenomenon where magnetoelastic coupling induces magnetic frustration in an otherwise non-frustrated system.

**Main result.** The system undergoes a magnetostructural transition into a self-frustrated (SF) phase, characterized by spontaneous structural distortions and residual magnetic entropy, separating the simple ferromagnetic and highly ordered checkerboard phases.

**Method.** The study employs classical Monte Carlo simulations and analytical linear approximations to model the coupled spin-lattice Hamiltonian.

**Model / system.** The system is an Ising spin model on a kagomé lattice coupled to local elastic distortions via a magnetoelastic coupling constant $\alpha$. The Hamiltonian includes terms for magnetic exchange and elastic energy.

**Key observables.** Magnetization modulus ($|M|$), Breathing order parameter ($OP_{Breath}$), Specific heat ($C_V$), and residual entropy.

**Important parameters / regimes.** The magnetoelastic coupling constant $\alpha$ relative to a critical value $\alpha_c$, and temperature $T$.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis relies on expanding the exchange coupling to first order in the distance change and using harmonic oscillator models for site displacements.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the phase diagram ($\alpha-T$) showing FM, SF, and CB boundaries, and show histograms of magnetic exchange couplings demonstrating the robustness of certain AFM bonds in the SF phase.

**Paper structure.** The paper progresses by defining the Hamiltonian, simulating the system across different $\alpha$ regimes (FM $\to$ SF $\to$ CB), and analyzing the resulting structural and magnetic order parameters to map out the phase diagram.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

In frustrated magnetism, lattice distortions mediated by magnetoelastic coupling are commonly invoked as an escape route from extensive degeneracy toward an ordered ground state and, in some cases, the onset of multiferroicity. Here we present a minimal classical model that illustrates the converse phenomenon, which we term ``magnetic self-frustration''. Monte Carlo simulations reveal that a kagomé lattice with trivial magnetic interactions -- namely, nearest-neighbor Ising ferromagnetism -- undergoes a magnetostructural transition into a breathing-like phase, characterized by irregular bond dimerization along the three kagomé directions. Structurally, the equilateral triangles belonging to one of the two kagomé sublattices spontaneously distort, expanding into isosceles triangles. An analysis to first order in the magnetoelastic coupling constant $α$ shows that the shape of these triangles is remarkably robust. Acting like rigid building blocks in a puzzle, their vertices determine the geometry of the second sublattice, giving rise to contracted ferromagnetic triangles with a variety of shapes. The magnetic sector can be mapped onto an effective antiferromagnetic triangular lattice, which remains disordered down to low temperatures and retains a finite residual entropy of one third of that of Wannier. This self-frustrated phase takes place at intermediate values of $α$, separating the conventional undistorted ferromagnetic phase at weak coupling from a strongly coupled ordered phase characterized by a regular dimerized up-up-down-down antiferromagnetic pattern along the three kagomé directions, built from ferromagnetic triangles and hexagons.

</details>

**Authors:** Neil F. Johnson, Ferney J. Rodriguez, Luis Quiroga  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2606.00254v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

N interacting quantum particles in a parabolic confinement (frequency ω0). Examples of the quantum particles include, but are not limited to, electrons or holes in a semiconductor system. A perpendicular magnetic field B can then be used to manipulate the relative-motion modes. Stray linear electric fields entering from any direction and at any temporal frequency couple only to the center-of-mass coordinate (generalized Kohn theorem) and therefore cannot...</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper outlines a novel, general architecture for quantum computing that achieves noise protection by leveraging the generalized Kohn theorem in parabolic confinement. It integrates this protection with precise control using twisted-light orbital angular momentum and solves the physical integration challenge using metamaterial nanofocusing on Weyl semimetals. The resulting framework is highly portable, applicable to diverse systems like cold atoms and semiconductor dots.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work is highly relevant as it proposes a unified, platform-agnostic blueprint for fault-tolerant quantum computation, bridging fundamental symmetry principles with cutting-edge photonic and solid-state engineering.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Proposing a general, practical architecture for quantum computing that combines symmetry protection, structured light control, and nanofocusing to overcome current limitations in qubit coherence and addressability.

**Main result.** The architecture successfully unifies the generalized Kohn theorem (symmetry protection), twisted-light OAM control, and metamaterial nanofocusing into a coherent, portable framework for quantum information processing.

**Method.** The approach combines theoretical analysis of symmetry protection (Kohn theorem) with advanced concepts from quantum optics (OAM-carrying light) and condensed matter physics (Weyl semimetal plasmonics).

**Model / system.** The system is modeled using interacting particles confined by a parabolic potential, applicable to semiconductor quantum dots, cold atoms, and trapped ions. The qubit encoding utilizes relative-motion correlation sectors.

**Key observables.** Qubit splitting frequency ($\hbar\omega_{	ext{qubit}}$), OAM selection rules ($\Delta|m|$), and the residual quadrupolar noise floor.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Parabolic confinement frequency ($\omega_0$), OAM quantum number ($l+\sigma$), and the characteristic length scales related to the wavelength-pitch mismatch.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The core assumption is the applicability of the generalized Kohn theorem for protection, and the technical assumption is the realizability of OAM-selective nanofocusing using magnetic Weyl semimetals.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 schematically illustrates the architecture: N interacting particles in parabolic confinement ($\omega_0$) controlled by a twisted-light pulse ($\omega_T$) via OAM selection, leaving relative modes protected by the Kohn theorem.

**Paper structure.** The paper develops the architecture by first establishing the need for general symmetry protection (Kohn theorem), then detailing the control mechanism using OAM-light, and finally solving the integration challenge via metamaterial nanofocusing, presenting a portable framework applicable across multiple physical platforms.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We propose a new architecture for practical quantum computing that combines three established principles: symmetry protection of relative-motion qubits via the generalized Kohn theorem, control via twisted-light orbital angular momentum, and metamaterial nanofocusing (e.g. using Weyl-semimetal plasmonics). Crucially, the core mechanism is generic: it applies to any current or future quantum computing system involving parabolic confinement, including cold atoms, ions, and semiconductor dots.

</details>

**Summary.** This work applies Cluster Perturbation Theory to the Hubbard model on a square lattice to map out the electronic and spin properties across different doping and temperature regimes. It successfully reproduces key features of the pseudogap state observed in cuprates, highlighting the critical role of short-range antiferromagnetic correlations in shaping the electronic spectrum. The study advances the theoretical understanding of strongly correlated electron systems near superconducting phases.

**Why it may be interesting.** The detailed study of the PG phase in the Hubbard model provides a theoretical framework for understanding high-temperature superconductivity, which involves complex, strongly correlated quantum dynamics relevant to open quantum systems.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The study investigates the doping and temperature dependencies of the electronic structure and dynamic spin susceptibility in the Hubbard model, focusing on the mysterious Pseudogap (PG) state observed in cuprate superconductors.

**Main result.** The calculations show that the electronic structure exhibits a nodal-antinodal dichotomy, and the PG signature is visible as a strong suppression of spectral weight, with short-range antiferromagnetism being crucial for this behavior.

**Method.** The primary theoretical tool is Cluster Perturbation Theory (CPT), supplemented by a Generalized Mean-Field Approximation (GMFA) to analyze spectral functions and spin dynamics.

**Model / system.** The system is modeled by the Hubbard Hamiltonian on a square lattice, incorporating nearest and next-nearest hopping terms and a local Coulomb repulsion U. The analysis covers varying doping levels and temperatures.

**Key observables.** Electronic spectral function A(k, omega), dynamic transverse spin susceptibility spectrum, and the degree of spectral weight suppression at the Fermi level.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Doping ($p$), Temperature ($T$), Coulomb repulsion ($U$), and hopping parameters ($t, t'$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The use of CPT and GMFA introduces approximations, notably that the GMFA alone is insufficient to capture the full frequency dependence of the PG drop, suggesting the need for dynamic correlation terms.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the electronic spectral function across different doping/temperature regimes, showing the nodal-antinodal dichotomy, and present the dynamic spin susceptibility spectra for specific doping levels.

**Paper structure.** The paper systematically analyzes the electronic and spin spectra using CPT, comparing results with experimental data (ARPES, INS). It progresses from general spectral analysis to discussing the role of short-range correlations in forming the PG, concluding with limitations regarding dynamic correlation inclusion.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

The doping and temperature dependencies of the electronic structure and the dynamic spin susceptibility of the Hubbard model on a square lattice are studied within cluster perturbation theory (CPT). The most general features of both electronic and spin spectra qualitatively agree with the experimental data on cuprates. Generalized mean-field calculations of the electronic structure using static short-range magnetic correlations from CPT are also implemented and compared with the results of CPT, allowing us to discuss the role of short-range antiferromagnetism in the formation of the pseudogap.

</details>

### [Information Hierarchy in Many-Body Berry Phase](http://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02155v1)

**Authors:** Kai Watanabe  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** strongly correlated electrons · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2606.02155v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `entanglement & information structure` **3/5** · `analog quantum simulation` **2/5** · `Keldysh / 2PI / non-Gaussian methods` **1/5**

</div>

**Summary.** This theoretical work establishes a rigorous information hierarchy showing that global topological invariants, like the Berry phase, generally cannot be determined solely from a finite number of local correlation measurements in interacting many-body systems. The authors identify that this limitation is overcome only in special cases, such as when the system is quasi-free or when strong symmetries are present.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work provides a fundamental theoretical boundary condition for how local measurements constrain global topological properties, which is critical for designing experimental protocols or interpreting numerical simulations of topological phases.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The central problem is determining the extent to which the geometric information of an interacting many-body ground state, specifically the Berry phase, can be inferred from a finite set of local correlation functions.

**Main result.** In general, no finite set of local correlators is sufficient to uniquely determine the global Berry phase in the thermodynamic limit, establishing a strict information hierarchy. Exceptions exist for quasi-free models or when symmetries impose strong constraints.

**Method.** The analysis uses the Resta formula, viewing the logarithm of the partition function as a cumulant generating function, and systematically compares the constraints imposed by low-order versus high-order density correlators.

**Model / system.** The study applies to general interacting many-body quantum systems, using the Berry phase derived from the expectation value of a unitary operator (LSM) threading a flux. Examples include analyzing chain rings and considering general fermionic Hamiltonians.

**Key observables.** The Berry phase ($\gamma$), the cumulants ($\kappa_p$), and various $p$-point density correlators ($\langle \hat{n}_{j_1} \cdots \hat{n}_{j_p} angle$).

**Important parameters / regimes.** The system size ($L$), the number of particles ($N$), and the order of the correlation ($p$). The thermodynamic limit ($L 	o \infty$) is crucial.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The argument is analytic and does not rely on a specific microscopic Hamiltonian, though specific cases like quasi-free models or symmetry constraints are analyzed.

**Figures summary.** The notes mention Table I, which explicitly calculates exact cumulants ($\kappa_p$) for various $(L, N)$ pairs, demonstrating the growth of these cumulants with system size.

**Paper structure.** The paper establishes the general information hierarchy using the cumulant generating function approach, presents a concrete counterexample (e.g., $L=6$ chain ring) showing the failure of low-order correlators to determine the phase, and then details the two exceptional 'yes-go' cases (quasi-free and symmetry-enforced) where the hierarchy closes.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Many-body topology is a central concept in modern theories of solids, and identifying effective degrees of freedom that capture it is important both fundamentally and practically. This work studies the extent to which geometric information of an interacting many-body ground state can be inferred from a finite number of local correlations. Starting from the Resta formula, $ z=\left\langle \exp\!\left(\frac{2πi}{L}\hat X\right)\right\rangle$, we view $\log z$ as the cumulant generating function and establish a generic information hierarchy across cumulant orders. We show that, for an $N$-particle system, even complete knowledge of all density correlators up to order $N-1$ does not, in general, uniquely determine the Berry phase $γ=\operatorname{Im}\log z \, (\mathrm{mod}\ 2π)$. In the thermodynamic limit, the statement becomes stronger: no finite set of local correlators suffices to determine the global holonomy. We also identify two exceptional yes-go cases in which the hierarchy is broken. First, for quasi-free models, all cumulants are determined by the particle two-point correlation function. Second, symmetry-enforced constraints can reduce the infinite cumulant sum entering $\log z$ to finite information. The argument is analytic and does not rely on a specific microscopic Hamiltonian. Our results clarify a limitation of approaches based on local degrees of freedom for many-body holonomy and provide a minimal framework for distinguishing when global holonomies are encoded in local correlations and when they are not. We also comment on the possibility of analogous hierarchies in other contexts, such as the quantum marginal problem in quantum information theory and many-body scattering problems. Finally, we discuss implications for future numerical work, including machine-learning approaches to the search for topological phases.

</details>

Phase diagram of the localizer index for the two-dimensional BHZ model comparing the effect of on-site Anderson and structural disorder, with a small fraction of spin-frame disordered sites η = 0.15, for a system size L = 30. Each point is calculated with A = 1, and averaged over 100 realisations of disorder. The phase diagram is calculated in each case as a function of the parameter M in Eq. (4) and the respective type of disorder. The spectral localizer index given by Eq. (8) is computed for κ = 1 and E, x at the centre of the energy spectrum and the system, respectively. (a) The localizer index for the BHZ model as a function of Anderson disorder strength W. (b-d) The localizer...</figcaption>
</figure>

Phase diagram of the localizer index for the three-dimensional topological insulating model in the presence of Anderson and struc- tural disorder. Each point is calculated with A = 1, and averaged over 100 realizations of disorder, for a system size L = 10. The localizer index, Eq. (10), is computed for κ = 2 and E, x at the bulk of the energy spectrum and the system, respectively. (b-d) Localizer index as a function of M and the strength of Gaussian structural disorder, characterized by its standard deviation σ with the inter-site repulsion ρ. As in Fig. 4, three...</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This work develops robust theoretical tools, particularly the spectral localizer, to diagnose topological phases in the presence of structural disorder. It reveals that structural disorder's effect is highly dimension-dependent, favoring topological stability in 2D lattices with repulsive interactions but being generally destructive in 3D. This provides key design principles for realizing topological materials in amorphous or disordered solid-state systems.

**Why it may be interesting.** The development of basis-independent topological diagnostics like the spectral localizer is crucial for analyzing complex, disordered quantum systems where symmetries are easily broken or ill-defined.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper investigates how structural disorder, as opposed to on-site disorder, affects the ability to induce and sustain topological phases in quantum materials.

**Main result.** Structural disorder can enhance and sustain topological phases in two dimensions if atomic sites are penalized from being too close, but this effect is generally detrimental in three dimensions.

**Method.** The authors employ advanced theoretical diagnostics, notably the spectral localizer, to calculate topological invariants ($\mathbb{Z}_2$ invariants) in real space, circumventing limitations of traditional markers when spin-frame disorder is present.

**Model / system.** The study analyzes topological insulators (e.g., BHZ model) on lattices, modeling disorder through structural shifts ($\sigma$) and inter-site repulsion ($ho$). The Hamiltonian incorporates hopping strengths exponentially dependent on atomic separation.

**Key observables.** Topological invariants ($\mathbb{Z}_2$ invariants), local gap, structure factor, and the spectral localizer index.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Dimensionality (2D vs 3D), structural disorder strength ($\sigma$), inter-site repulsion ($ho$), and the disorder type (structural vs. on-site Anderson disorder $W$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The spectral localizer is assumed to provide a basis-independent and robust diagnostic tool, especially when global spin-reference frames are scrambled by disorder.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate phase diagrams comparing topological regimes under Anderson vs. structural disorder in 2D and 3D, and show structure factors comparing systems with and without inter-site repulsion.

**Paper structure.** The paper progresses by first establishing the limitations of standard topological markers under disorder, then introducing and validating the spectral localizer, and finally applying this tool to systematically compare the effects of structural disorder in 2D versus 3D systems.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

On-site disorder can be leveraged to induce a transition from a trivial to a topological insulator. However it is unclear if structural disorder in the absence of on-site disorder can aid a similar transition and, if so, which kind of structural disorder is more favourable. We numerically show that structural disorder can enhance and sustain a topological phase up to strong disorder in two dimensions provided that one penalises atomic sites from being close to one another. However, we find this effect is absent in three dimensions, where structural disorder appears generically detrimental to the phase. In our calculations we include disorder that can scramble the global spin-reference frame, an overlooked type of disorder expected to exist in strongly disordered solids. This disorder fatally scrambles the information necessary for the spin-Bott and the spin-Chern marker to correctly diagnose a topological phase. By using the spectral localizer, a local marker directly defined using the time-reversal symmetry operator rather than a spin-projection, we show how one can circumvent this limitation, providing a basis-indifferent theory for calculating Z2 invariants. Our work showcases that not all structural disorders are equally beneficial to topology, and highlights guiding principles to enhance and detect topological phases in both solid-state and metamaterial realisations.

</details>

**Summary.** This paper introduces the Stimulated Raman Shortcut-to-Adiabatic Passage (STIRSAP) method for manipulating the isomeric state of the $^{229}\mathrm{Th}$ nucleus. By employing counter-diabatic driving, the authors successfully accelerate the population transfer process from milliseconds down to microseconds. This advancement significantly enhances the feasibility of controlling nuclear isomers using electronic structure interactions.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work is highly relevant to AMO physics and open quantum systems as it applies advanced quantum control techniques (STAs/counter-diabatic driving) to manipulate nuclear quantum states using electronic structure as a bridge.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** To significantly shorten the operation time for the isomeric population transfer of the $^{229}\mathrm{Th}$ nucleus while maintaining high efficiency.

**Main result.** The Stimulated Raman Shortcut-to-Adiabatic Passage (STIRSAP) method achieves transfer in hundreds of microseconds, dramatically faster than previous methods, while maintaining high efficiency and showing improved robustness.

**Method.** The study employs the STIRSAP quantum control technique, which utilizes counter-diabatic driving to accelerate the population transfer process compared to standard STIRAP.

**Model / system.** The system is modeled as a six-level structure involving the $^{229}\mathrm{Th}$ nucleus and its hyperfine electronic bridge. The time evolution is governed by a quantum master equation incorporating decay processes.

**Key observables.** Population transfer efficiency from the initial state to the isomeric state, and the operation time ($t_{	ext{total}}$).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Operation times (hundreds of microseconds vs. hundreds of milliseconds), two-photon detuning ($\delta$), and one-photon detuning ($\Delta$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The system is modeled as a six-level system, and the analysis assumes the initial state is prepared in the metastable $7s_{1/2}$ level.

**Figures summary.** Figures compare the efficiency of STIRSAP versus STIRAP as a function of total operation time, detuning, Rabi frequency, and two-photon detuning, demonstrating STIRSAP's superior speed and robustness.

**Paper structure.** The paper first establishes the physical model and the need for faster control. It then derives the Hamiltonian and applies the STIRSAP technique using counter-diabatic driving. Finally, it systematically compares the results of STIRSAP against STIRAP across various parameter regimes (time, detuning) to prove its superiority.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

The $^{229}$Th nucleus is well known for its exceptionally low-lying nuclear isomeric level, which provides a unique platform for exploring electron-nucleus interactions and gives rise to a variety of rich physical phenomena. One such phenomenon is the hyperfine electronic bridge, which has recently been shown to enable efficient and precise manipulation of the nuclear isomeric levels of $^{229}$Th [W. Wang $et~ al.$, Phys. Rev. Lett. \textbf{133}, 223001 (2024)]. However, that study used the stimulated Raman adiabatic passage method, which requires relatively long operation times. In this work, we employ the stimulated Raman shortcut-to-adiabatic passage method, which dramatically shortens the operation time from the order of hundreds of milliseconds to hundreds of microseconds while maintaining a transfer efficiency of about $79.38\%$.

</details>

### [Correlated Quantum Sensing at the Seemingly Classical Limit](http://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01673v1)

**Authors:** K. P. Athulya, Sreenath K. Manikandan  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2606.01673v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `quantum measurements` **3/5** · `Keldysh / 2PI / non-Gaussian methods` **1/5** · `correlated / nonlocal dissipation` **1/5**

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>
<div class="paper-figures-scroll">
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2606.01673_figures/2606.01673_fig1.jpg" alt="Figure 1">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> FIG. 1. (a) Cross-correlated quantum sensing using two or more high-quality, but barely functional resonant quantum harmonic detectors in the bolometric regime can offer a statistical advantage for probing the coherence properties of weak fields in the seemingly classical limit of large ⟨N⟩. We compare (b) the bolometric regime of good resonant harmonic detectors for probing quantum aspects of light to (c) the bolometric regime of barely functional resonant mass detectors for probing the quantum nature of gravitational radiation. The comparison is made in the functional detector regime, i.e., γ0τ⟨N⟩∼1 for both cases.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper details advanced quantum sensing techniques to detect faint quantum fields, such as gravitons, by analyzing correlations across multiple detectors. It establishes that correlated measurements offer a statistical advantage over single-detector measurements, particularly in the weak coupling regime. The methodology involves deriving complex cross-correlators for counting and quadrature measurements, providing a theoretical roadmap for tabletop quantum gravity experiments.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work is highly relevant to quantum optics and open quantum systems because it extends the concept of quantum noise measurement into the challenging regime of weak coupling and high photon numbers, providing concrete statistical tools for experimental proposals.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The core problem is developing methods to detect the quanta of weakly interacting radiation fields, such as gravitons, by probing their quantum statistics, especially when the field is in a seemingly classical limit ($\langle N angle \gg 1$).

**Main result.** The authors show that correlated quantum sensing strategies, utilizing cross-correlators ($\kappa_2, \kappa_3$) or quadrature measurements (Homodyne/Heterodyne), provide a unique statistical advantage for detecting quantum noise even when detectors are only 'barely functional' (weakly coupled bolometric regime).

**Method.** The analysis employs quantum field theory techniques, calculating expectation values and correlators using the Glauber-Sudarshan P representation for multi-detector setups.

**Model / system.** The system involves coupling a quantized radiation field (photons or gravitons) to one or more resonant quantum harmonic detectors (e.g., mass quadrupole oscillators). The interaction is modeled via a time-dependent Hamiltonian, analyzed in the weak coupling limit ($\gamma_0 	au \ll 1$).

**Key observables.** Cross-correlators ($\kappa_2, \kappa_3$), mean number of detected quanta ($ar{n}$), number fluctuations ($\Delta n^2$), and quadrature correlations ($\langle x_1 x_2 angle - \langle x_1 angle \langle x_2 angle$).

**Important parameters / regimes.** The critical regime is the weak coupling bolometric limit where $\gamma_0 	au \ll 1$ but the product $\gamma_0 	au \langle N angle \sim 1$.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis assumes Markovian evolution and that the detectors are initialized in zero-mean preparations, such as thermal states.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 compares cross-correlated quantum sensing across three regimes: quantum optics, and resonant mass detectors for gravitational waves (GW).

**Paper structure.** The paper systematically develops detection strategies by comparing correlated counting, homodyne, and heterodyne measurements for $k=2$ and $k=3$ detectors, deriving the necessary correlators for each scheme.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

It is a difficult task to detect the indivisible quanta of weakly interacting radiation fields, and even more challenging to probe their quantum statistics. Nevertheless, if barely functional high-quality resonant detectors are feasible for weakly interacting radiation fields, they do come with certain statistical advantages to probe quantum effects at the seemingly classical limit of a large number of quanta of the incoming radiation field. We present correlated counting, homodyne, and heterodyne detection strategies using high-quality resonant quantum harmonic detectors operating at this limit, initialized in bolometry-inspired zero-mean preparations such as thermal states. We compare the bolometric regime of good resonant harmonic detectors in quantum optics to the bolometric regime of barely functional resonant mass quadrupole oscillators as detectors for quantum gravity. Simple statistical tests are proposed using symmetric correlators for two and three such barely functional resonant mass detectors that could reveal the complementary quantum noise characteristics of gravitons in tabletop experiments.

</details>

### [Parallelizing Large-Scale Tensor Network Contraction on Multiple GPUs](http://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01852v1)

**Authors:** Feng Pan, Hanfeng Gu, Paul Springer, Xipeng Li  
**Type:** experiment · **Category:** numerical methods · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2606.01852v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `analog quantum simulation` **3/5** · `entanglement & information structure` **1/5** · `methods for driven-dissipative` **1/5**

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>
<div class="paper-figures-scroll">
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2606.01852_figures/2606.01852_fig1.jpg" alt="Figure 1">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> Fig. 1. Theoretical complexity reduction from distributing intermediate tensors across GPUs for six workloads: (a) quantum circuit simulation (Zuchongzhi n60m24), (b) exact QEC decoding (distance-7 rotated surface code), (c) independent-set enumeration on King’s subgraphs, and many-body dynamics on (d) rectangular, (e) hexagonal, and (f) triangular lattices. Red curves show log10(total FLOPs); blue curves show the number of sliced indices. In all cases, contraction cost drops rapidly—often close to exponentially—as more GPUs contribute shared memory. Many-body panels show mean ± std over 15 independent path-finding runs.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2606.01852_figures/2606.01852_fig2.jpg" alt="Figure 2">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> Fig. 2. End-to-end workflow. The offline planner takes a fixed contraction path and applies two transformations: mode reordering produces GEMM- ready tensor layouts via lifetime ordering, and distribution planning selects distributed modes and redistribution points via DP search. The resulting annotated schedule is replayed at runtime by the cuTENSORMp executor with compute–communication overlap.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2606.01852_figures/2606.01852_fig3.jpg" alt="Figure 3">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 3.</b> Fig. 4. Slicing vs. distribution. (a) Slicing fixes indices to create independent sub-tasks with no communication but redundant computation. (b) Distribution partitions a single tensor across GPUs, reducing total FLOPs at the cost of communication that our planner minimizes.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2606.01852_figures/2606.01852_fig4.jpg" alt="Figure 4">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 4.</b> Fig. 3. GEMM-oriented mode reordering on a two-step subtree. Dashed edges show connections to the rest of the contraction tree. Panel A: arbitrary local orders plus the downstream request I5 = gahe. Panel B: the backward pass visits step 2 and then step 1; at each visit, each input is rebuilt as “shared modes in consumer order, then modes reduced here.” Panel C: the resulting tree has [retained | reduced] operand layouts (dashed split line), with modes shaded by remaining lifetime and reduction status. The output I4 interleaves modes from I1 and I2 by lifetime, so step 2 can use I4 as [retained | reduced] without a transpose.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2606.01852_figures/2606.01852_fig5.jpg" alt="Figure 5">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 5.</b> Fig. 5. DP redistribution-point selection for the Zuchongzhi n60m24 bench- mark on 8 H100 GPUs. The shaded area shows the distributed tensor’s output size along the use-chain. Blue dots are equations where distributed modes are kept (no communication); red diamonds mark DP-chosen redistributions. The DP avoids redistributing on the 256 GB plateau, instead concentrating transfers in the valley and at local minima of the second peak.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper presents a novel, communication-aware multi-GPU framework to accelerate large-scale tensor network contractions, which are vital for quantum simulation. By intelligently reordering modes and distributing intermediate tensors across multiple GPUs, it overcomes the exponential scaling limits of traditional slicing methods. The results demonstrate massive, super-linear speedups, pushing the boundaries of what is computationally feasible for complex quantum systems.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work provides a critical computational tool for simulating complex quantum many-body systems by efficiently managing the massive data movement inherent in tensor network contractions, directly impacting the feasibility of large-scale quantum simulations.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The core challenge is accelerating exact tensor network (TN) contraction, which is fundamental to simulating quantum circuits and many-body systems, because standard parallelization techniques like slicing are inefficient.

**Main result.** The proposed communication-aware distribution framework achieves massive speedups (up to 67,869x extra speedup over slicing) on large GPU clusters, significantly surpassing the scaling limits of existing methods.

**Method.** The authors introduce an offline scheduling strategy involving GEMM-oriented mode reordering and communication-aware mode distribution planning to convert a fixed contraction path into an efficient, overlapping multi-GPU execution schedule.

**Model / system.** The work focuses on Tensor Networks (TNs) used to model quantum circuits, quantum error correction codes, and many-body dynamics on lattices (e.g., hexagonal, triangular). The implementation utilizes cuTENSORMp on NVIDIA DGX H100 nodes.

**Key observables.** Extra speedup beyond embarrassingly parallel slicing, projected full-contraction time, and complexity reduction ($R_P$).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Scaling from 8 GPUs (NVLink) to 1024 GPUs (InfiniBand); the ratio of communication cost to arithmetic computation cost.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The framework assumes an existing, fixed binary contraction path from an upstream optimizer, and the performance is limited by the interconnect bandwidth (communication).

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the theoretical complexity reduction across various workloads versus GPU count, and detail the end-to-end workflow from a fixed path to the annotated, overlapping execution schedule.

**Paper structure.** The paper details the limitations of slicing, proposes the two-stage offline planning (reordering and distribution planning), describes the runtime execution via cuTENSORMp, and validates the method through extensive scaling benchmarks on both single and multi-node hardware.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Exact tensor network contraction underpins quantum circuit simulation, quantum error correction, combinatorial optimization, and many-body dynamics. The dominant parallelization strategy, slicing, scales exponentially and incurs redundant computation. We present a multi-GPU framework that instead distributes intermediate tensors across devices with explicit communication, converting a fixed contraction path into a communication-efficient schedule via GEMM-oriented mode reordering and communication-aware mode distribution planning. Within a single DGX H100 node (8 GPUs, NVLink), distribution delivers $7$--$173\times$ extra speedup beyond embarrassingly parallel slicing, capturing nearly all of the available compute reduction (87--101%) because NVLink's high bandwidth keeps communication small relative to compute. Scaling the same four workloads to 1024 H100 GPUs over InfiniBand, the extra speedup beyond slicing ranges from $42\times$ to $67{,}869\times$, demonstrating that communication-aware distributed contraction far surpasses slicing-based scaling limits for frontier tensor networks.

</details>

<a id="paper-2606.01291"></a>
### [Quantum Algorithm for Distributed Reduction of Entanglements (QADR): A Trainable and Simulation-Efficient QML Framework](http://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01291v1)

**Authors:** Syed Farhan Ahmad, Gregory T. Byrd  
**Type:** both · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2606.01291v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `entanglement & information structure` **3/5** · `analog quantum simulation` **1/5** · `methods for driven-dissipative` **1/5**

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>

<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> Fig. 1. Causal cone geometry for n = 9, d = 2. Top (interior cone): target q4 sits at the center of a full-width 2d+1 = 5-qubit cone (d qubits left + target + d right). Bottom (boundary cone): target q1 is only one hop from the left edge, so the cone is clipped to four qubits, with one left neighbor instead of two. There are always exactly 2d boundary cones.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2606.01291_figures/2606.01291_fig2.jpg" alt="Figure 2">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> Fig. 2. QADR Pipeline: an n-qubit classification task is decomposed into n fixed-width local sub-circuits, each operating within its causal light cone C(i, d). Local ⟨Ztarget⟩expectation values are fused by a lightweight Classical Orchestrator, reducing simulation memory from O(2n) to O(n · 22d+1) while preserving end-to-end trainability.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2606.01291_figures/2606.01291_fig3.jpg" alt="Figure 3">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 3.</b> Fig. 3. Theoretical statevector simulation amplitude complexity comparison between Global VQC (O(2n)) and QADR (O(n·22d+1)) with a cone radius of d = 2 on a logarithmic scale.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2606.01291_figures/2606.01291_fig4.jpg" alt="Figure 4">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 4.</b> Fig. 4. ROC curves for QADR and SVM at nfeatures = 2000 (SelectKBest) on the NASA IMS fault classification task. Only these two architectures were capable of operating at this scale where a “curve of dimensionality” exists. QADR achieves an ROC-AUC of 0.9995 versus 0.9958 for SVM.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper introduces QADR, a novel hybrid framework designed to overcome the exponential memory and barren plateau issues plaguing VQC training. By decomposing the quantum circuit into localized sub-circuits based on causal light cones, QADR achieves polynomial scaling in system size. It demonstrates that this approach maintains high classification accuracy even on massive, high-dimensional datasets where standard global quantum methods fail.

**Why it may be interesting.** While focused on ML, the concept of decomposing global quantum computations based on locality (causal light cones) is highly relevant to simulating complex quantum many-body systems or quantum field theories where entanglement structure dictates computational feasibility.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper addresses the severe computational limitations encountered when training Variational Quantum Circuits (VQCs) under NISQ constraints, specifically exponential memory scaling and barren plateaus.

**Main result.** The proposed QADR framework successfully scales to high-dimensional feature spaces (up to 2000) where global VQCs fail, matching or exceeding the performance of optimized classical machine learning models.

**Method.** QADR decomposes a global VQC into localized sub-circuits operating within the causal light cones of individual qubits, which are then processed by a classical orchestrator.

**Model / system.** The model is a hybrid quantum-classical machine learning framework applied to parameterized quantum unitaries (VQCs). It simulates quantum circuits on classical hardware, focusing on feature extraction from localized quantum expectations.

**Key observables.** Classification accuracy (MNIST, NASA IMS), ROC-AUC, and computational scaling complexity (memory and time).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Number of qubits ($n$), light cone radius ($d$), and feature dimensionality ($n_{	ext{features}}$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The core assumption is that localizing the circuit operations via causal light cones can effectively approximate the global entanglement structure while drastically reducing computational overhead.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the causal cone geometry, the QADR pipeline decomposition, and compare theoretical statevector simulation complexity between global and localized approaches.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem limitations, proposes the QADR decomposition method, details the hybrid training pipeline (local expectation gathering $ightarrow$ classical projection), and benchmarks performance against global VQCs and advanced classical ML models on multiple datasets.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Training Variational Quantum Circuits (VQCs) under Noisy Intermediate-Scale Quantum (NISQ) constraints introduces severe computational limitations: classical statevector simulation memory scales exponentially ($\mathcal{O}(2^n)$), and global cost functions suffer from barren plateaus where gradient variance decays exponentially ($\mathcal{O}(1/2^n)$). This paper introduces and evaluates the Quantum Algorithm for Distributed Reduction of Entanglements (QADR), a hybrid quantum-classical machine learning framework that decomposes a global $n$-qubit VQC into localized sub-circuits operating approximately within the causal light cones of individual target qubits. QADR reduces classical simulation memory scaling from $\mathcal{O}(2^n)$ to $\mathcal{O}(n \cdot 2^{2d+1})$ for a light cone radius $d$, while naturally mitigating global barren plateaus. We benchmark QADR against standard global VQCs, Support Vector Machines (SVM), and two customized classical parameter-matched neural networks (CANN and PMNN) on the MNIST dataset and the high-dimensional NASA IMS wind turbine drivetrain diagnostic task. QADR demonstrates excellent scalability, operating successfully at $n_{\text{features}}=2000$ where standard global VQCs crash due to memory exhaustion, while matching or exceeding the performance of optimized classical architectures.

</details>

**Summary.** This work explores how lattice anisotropy destabilizes Fractional Chern Insulator (FCI) phases in moiré materials. By tuning anisotropy, the system undergoes quantum phase transitions from a topological insulator to competing charge-density-wave or Fermi-liquid states. This establishes anisotropy as a powerful tool for engineering and exploring diverse correlated phases in moiré heterostructures.

**Why it may be interesting.** The mechanism of anisotropy-driven phase transitions from topological to symmetry-broken/compressible states provides a general framework for engineering correlated phases in moiré systems, relevant to understanding emergent quantum states in artificial lattices.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The study investigates the stability and nature of Fractional Chern Insulator (FCI) phases in moiré heterostructures when subjected to varying degrees of lattice anisotropy.

**Main result.** Sufficiently strong lattice anisotropy drives quantum phase transitions, destabilizing the incompressible FCI state into competing charge-density-wave (CDW) or Fermi-liquid (FL) phases.

**Method.** The research employs theoretical modeling using continuum Hamiltonians and analyzing key many-body observables like the structure factor and topological gap.

**Model / system.** The system is based on moiré heterostructures, such as twisted TMDs ($	ext{MoTe}_2$), modeled via a continuum Hamiltonian incorporating interlayer tunneling and electron-electron interactions.

**Key observables.** Hall conductivity ($\sigma_{xy}$), topological gap size, structure factor $S(\mathbf{q})$, and Bloch state occupation numbers ($n_k$).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Lattice anisotropy, controlled by interlayer momentum shift ($\gamma_q b(t)$) induced by heterostrain, or lattice stretching ($eta$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis often utilizes the single Landau Level (LL) approximation and treats the interaction via projection onto the Chern band.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the phase transition from FCI (topological gap) to CDW/FL as anisotropy increases, showing changes in $S(\mathbf{q})$ and $n_k$.

**Paper structure.** The paper systematically analyzes the effect of anisotropy on the FCI state, first showing robustness to moderate strain, then detailing the transition mechanism to CDW/FL states, and finally comparing these findings to anisotropic FQHE models.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Recently, moiré heterostructures have been observed to host fractional Chern insulator (FCI) phases at zero magnetic field. In this work, we show that the FCI phases are robust against moderate lattice anisotropy, while sufficiently strong anisotropy drives quantum phase transitions from incompressible topological phases to competing charge-density-wave (CDW) and Fermi-liquid (FL) phases. Specifically, in the case of twisted transition metal dichalcogenides (such as MoTe$_2$), the anisotropy arises from an interlayer momentum shift induced by heterostrain. We find that increasing anisotropy suppresses the many-body topological gap, eventually destabilizing the fractional topological phase. Beyond a critical anisotropy value, the system gradually transitions to a symmetry-broken state characterized by stripe-like CDW order. It has a Landau Level counterpart, where effective mass anisotropy provides an additional tuning knob for the fractional quantum Hall state; sufficiently strong anisotropy drives a transition to a charge-ordered phase. Moreover, in the anisotropic ideal Chern band model with lattice stretching, a Fermi liquid phase emerges as anisotropy increases. Our results establish that anisotropy provides a direct route for engineering and exploring competing correlated phases in FCI states based on moiré materials.

</details>

**Authors:** Prabhav Jain, Nikolai Miklin, Mariami Gachechiladze  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2606.02416v1>

**Summary.** This paper develops an extended version of the Information Causality principle to place stronger constraints on quantum correlations and nonlocality. It applies these tools to analyze the performance limits of entanglement-assisted random access codes. While the extension yields stronger bounds in some Bell tests, it ultimately shows that for the specific RACs analyzed, the original IC bounds remain optimal.

**Why it may be interesting.** The rigorous derivation of new, tighter bounds on nonlocality using information-theoretic principles provides fundamental constraints on physical theories beyond quantum mechanics, which is crucial for understanding the limits of quantum correlations.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper aims to derive tighter theoretical bounds on quantum correlations (nonlocality) and the success probabilities of Random Access Codes (RACs) by extending the Information Causality (IC) principle.

**Main result.** The extended IC principle yields new, tighter quantum Bell inequalities in certain scenarios, although for entanglement-assisted random access codes, the extended principle does not improve the bounds over the original IC principle.

**Method.** The authors use advanced information-theoretic techniques, including deriving quantum Bell inequalities from the extended IC statement and applying optimization methods (like KKT conditions) to bound specific expressions.

**Model / system.** The analysis is conducted on non-signaling correlations (Bell scenarios) and applied to the theoretical framework of entanglement-assisted random access codes (EARACs) and general communication channels.

**Key observables.** Quantum Bell inequalities (e.g., $	ext{Inn}_{22}$ expression), winning probabilities for RACs, and Shannon mutual information.

**Important parameters / regimes.** The analysis explores limits such as zero classical channel capacity ($\gamma 	o 0$) and varying input correlation parameters ($\epsilon$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis relies on extending the IC principle to allow correlations among Alice's inputs, and in some parts, assumes uniform marginals or specific error channel structures.

**Figures summary.** Figures compare the bounds derived from the original IC, the extended IC, and other known bounds (ML, QM) across various dimensions ($d^2d$ scenario).

**Paper structure.** The paper progresses from establishing the extended IC principle and deriving new Bell inequalities for general scenarios, to applying these inequalities to derive bounds on EARACs, and finally confirming the optimality of the original IC bounds for the specific EARAC case.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Information Causality was introduced as a physical principle for constraining the set of nonlocal correlations. In recent work, we proposed an extension of Information Causality that allows correlations among Alice's inputs. This extended principle yields tighter constraints than the original formulation and recovers part of the quantum boundary in certain Bell scenarios. In this work, we further investigate the implications of extended Information Causality and apply it to scenarios beyond binary inputs and outputs. We derive a family of quantum Bell inequalities that strengthen previously known constraints on quantum correlations. Using these inequalities, we obtain an improved analytical bound for the Collins-Gisin family of Bell inequalities. We also apply Information Causality to entanglement-assisted random access codes and derive new theory-independent analytical bounds on the winning probability. For this latter task, we prove that, despite being stronger in general, the extended principle does not improve the bounds obtained from the original Information Causality principle. This suggests that the existing Information Causality bounds are optimal for this class of random access codes.

</details>

**Summary.** This work introduces a novel technique to measure the central charge of a CFT by analyzing the ground-state overlap of a system subjected to a $q$-Möbius spatial deformation. By applying this method to critical 1D chains and 2D topological insulators, the authors show that the overlap provides a simple, robust, and universal probe of fundamental CFT data using only wave-function properties.

**Why it may be interesting.** This provides a powerful, purely wave-function-based diagnostic tool to probe universal CFT properties (like $c$) in complex, interacting quantum many-body systems, which is highly relevant for understanding critical points in condensed matter.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The central problem is developing a method to extract the central charge ($c$) of a $(1+1)$-dimensional Conformal Field Theory (CFT) using only ground-state overlaps derived from spatially deformed Hamiltonians.

**Main result.** The ground-state overlap under $q$-Möbius deformations provides a simple and robust, wave-function-based probe for the central charge in critical quantum chains and topological edge modes.

**Method.** The authors derive an exact overlap formula using CFT techniques applied to the $q$-Möbius deformation, and then construct a lattice estimator based on this overlap.

**Model / system.** The study applies to gapless 1D quantum systems, including critical chains (Ising, Potts, Heisenberg) and the edge modes of 2D Chern insulators (QWZ model). The Hamiltonian is subjected to a spatially modulated $q$-Möbius deformation.

**Key observables.** Central charge ($c$), ground-state overlaps ($\langle 0|0_q(	heta) angle$), entanglement spectrum, and entanglement entropy ($S_A$).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Deformation parameters $q$ (modulation wave number) and $	heta$ (deformation strength); system size $L$.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The method relies on the deformation being generated by a finite conformal transformation to maintain analytical tractability.

**Figures summary.** Figures demonstrate the finite-size extrapolation of the effective central charge ($c_{	ext{eff}}$) for various critical chains, and show the entanglement spectrum and entropy for deformed Ising and Potts chains, as well as the 2D QWZ model.

**Paper structure.** The paper progresses from the CFT derivation of the overlap formula, to its lattice realization, and finally applies this estimator to calculate $c$ and analyze entanglement structures for both 1D and 2D topological models.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We show that the conformal anomaly of a $(1+1)$-dimensional conformal field theory can be extracted directly from a ground-state wave-function overlap associated with a spatial conformal deformation. Focusing on the $q$-Möbius deformation, we derive an exact overlap formula between the deformed and undeformed ground states, whose exponent depends only on the central charge. Motivated by this result, we construct a lattice estimator based solely on ground-state overlaps and apply it to representative critical quantum chains and the gapless edge modes of a two-dimensional Chern insulator. Numerical results demonstrate that the resulting overlaps provide a simple and robust probe of the central charge in microscopic models. We further demonstrate that the deformed ground states retain universal geometric structures in their entanglement spectra and entanglement entropies. These results provide a simple wave-function-based route to probing conformal data in critical systems and topological edge modes.

</details>

### [Finite-Sample Selected Covariance Spectra in Classical Shadows](http://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00527v1)

**Authors:** Masahito Hayashi  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2606.00527v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `quantum measurements` **3/5** · `entanglement & information structure` **1/5**

<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 5.</b> Low-resolution page preview, page 6</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper develops a rigorous finite-sample theory for estimating the covariance spectrum of quantum measurements reconstructed via classical shadows. It proves that for local measurement settings, the required sample size to estimate the selected covariance is independent of the total system dimension. This is a significant result for quantum tomography, as it shows that local structure can overcome the curse of dimensionality.

**Why it may be interesting.** It provides rigorous, quantitative tools for characterizing the statistical information contained in quantum measurements (shadows), which is crucial for understanding the limits of quantum state reconstruction.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper addresses the finite-sample estimation of selected covariance matrices derived from classical-shadow outputs in quantum measurements.

**Main result.** It establishes dimension-independent sample size bounds for selected covariance estimation when the measurement protocol is local (e.g., local Pauli shadows), contrasting this with protocols where the required sample size scales with system dimension.

**Method.** The analysis combines matrix Bernstein concentration inequalities with exact rank-one centering identities to control the error in the sample-centered empirical covariance matrix.

**Model / system.** The study uses the framework of classical shadows, where quantum state tomography is approximated by measuring a set of observables (Pauli strings) and reconstructing a shadow snapshot. The system is analyzed in terms of local product measurements versus global measurements.

**Key observables.** Selected covariance matrix ($\Sigma_l(ho)$), shadow-output vector ($\hat{x}$), and the selected one-shot radius ($B_l$).

**Important parameters / regimes.** The selected dimension ($l$), the local support sizes, and the local reconstruction coefficients are critical parameters determining the sample complexity.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The primary assumption for the dimension-independent result is that the selected set of observables (or their associated coefficients) is bounded and local in nature (e.g., local product shadows).

**Figures summary.** The notes mention TABLE I comparing the work to other literature strands and TABLE II/III defining notation for local/global measurements, but no specific figure content is detailed.

**Paper structure.** The paper develops the theory by first defining the shadow-output vector and covariance, then proving the general finite-sample theorem, specializing to local product shadows, and finally deriving explicit, dimension-independent covariance formulas for biased local Pauli shadows.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We study finite-sample estimation of selected covariance matrices of classical-shadow outputs. For a general shadow-output vector, we consider its covariance matrix and a fixed selected compression. Our main theorem applies to arbitrary shadow protocols and gives an operator-norm error bound for the selected sample-centered empirical covariance. When the protocol-dependent constants appearing in this bound remain independent of the ambient system size, the required sample size is also independent of the ambient dimension. The proof combines matrix Bernstein concentration, an exact rank-one centering identity, and Weyl and Davis--Kahan perturbation bounds.   We verify this bounded-output condition for local measurement settings. For general local product shadow protocols with fixed local dimension, finite-weight product observables lead to bounds controlled by support sizes and local reconstruction coefficients, not by the total number of tensor factors. Hence uniform bounds on selected set size, observable weight, and local reconstruction coefficients imply dimension-independent selected covariance estimation.   For biased local Pauli shadows, we evaluate the relevant bound in closed form from the selected Pauli supports and local basis-selection probabilities. We also derive an exact covariance formula governed by Pauli compatibility and inverse-probability overlap factors, showing how measurement bias affects both diagonal variances and off-diagonal statistical couplings. A comparison with global Clifford shadows shows that this dimension-independent local behavior is not automatic for every shadow protocol.

</details>

**Summary.** This research thoroughly investigates how the optical properties of NV centers in diamond change under extreme hydrostatic pressures up to 120 GPa. By combining advanced DFT calculations with DAC experiments, the authors demonstrate that the NV center remains a robust quantum sensor. The findings provide critical spectroscopic guidelines for utilizing NV centers in high-pressure quantum sensing applications.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work is highly relevant as it addresses the stability of a solid-state quantum emitter (NV center) under extreme conditions, which is crucial for developing quantum sensors for high-pressure physics.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** To investigate the optical properties and stability of the Nitrogen Vacancy (NV) center in diamond under extreme hydrostatic pressures up to 120 GPa to optimize its use as a quantum sensor.

**Main result.** The NV center remains a robust quantum sensor under extreme hydrostatic pressures, maintaining stable quantum efficiency for optical readout, despite enhanced electron-phonon coupling.

**Method.** The study combines theoretical calculations using DFT (HSE06, r2SCAN) with experimental measurements performed in a Diamond Anvil Cell (DAC) setup.

**Model / system.** The physical system is the negatively charged nitrogen vacancy (NV-) center in diamond. The analysis models its electronic structure, optical transitions (ZPL, PSB), and photoionization pathways under varying hydrostatic pressures.

**Key observables.** Zero-Phonon Line (ZPL) position shift, radiative lifetimes, optical lineshapes, photoionization thresholds, and electron-phonon coupling parameters (HR factor, $K_2$).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Pressures up to 120 GPa; ZPL shift exceeding 400 meV at 120 GPa.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis assumes near-hydrostatic conditions for comprehensive study, and theoretical modeling uses approximations like adiabatic Huang-Rhys theory for emission.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the pressure dependence of ZPL shifts, electron-phonon coupling parameters, PL/absorption lineshapes, and photoionization thresholds up to 120 GPa.

**Paper structure.** The paper systematically investigates the pressure dependence of various optical parameters, comparing theoretical predictions (DFT calculations) with experimental results obtained using a DAC setup, culminating in guidelines for high-pressure optical measurements.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

The nitrogen vacancy (NV) center has emerged as a powerful quantum sensor in high-pressure research, with the observation of optically detected magnetic resonance at megabar pressures. However, some aspects of NV physics require further investigation to optimize the development of NV-based sensing under pressure. Here, we study both experimentally and theoretically the optical properties of the NV center under hydrostatic pressure. We investigate the evolution of the zero-phonon line (ZPL) position, radiative lifetimes, optical lineshapes, and photoionization thresholds of the NV center under pressures up to ~120 GPa. We also provide spectroscopic guidelines for performing high-pressure optical experiments. Our results confirm that the NV center remains a robust quantum sensor under extreme hydrostatic pressures, especially for magnetic characterization.

</details>
