### [Fundamental Limit for One versus Two Point Sources Detection using Direct Imaging](http://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00968v1)

**Authors:** Parth Hemant Darekar, Amit Kumar Jha, Michael R. Grace, Aqil Sajjad, Saikat Guha  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2606.00968v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `interference shaping light` **1/5** · `quantum measurements` **1/5**

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>
<div class="paper-figures-scroll">
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2606.00968_figures/2606.00968_fig1.jpg" alt="Figure 1">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> Fig. 1. Schematic of the setup. Under H1, we have a weak thermal source (in blue) at the origin. Under H2, we have two equally bright, weak thermal sources (in red) with an image plane separation of θ that is scaled to the PSF width. For small separation, the intensity distributions of light on the image plane under each hypothesis become difficult to distinguish.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2606.00968_figures/2606.00968_fig2.jpg" alt="Figure 2">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> Fig. 2. Bhattacharyya distance for ideal direct imaging for the Gaussian Amplitude Spread Function (ASF) as a function of θ. The numerical results (red, dashed) show good agreement with the leading order term (blue, dotted) when the separation θ is small. The Bhattacharyya distance scales as O ␀ θ4</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2606.00968_figures/2606.00968_fig3.jpg" alt="Figure 3">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 3.</b> Fig. 3. Bhattacharyya distance for ideal direct imaging for the Sinc Amplitude Spread Function (ASF) as a function of θ. The numerical results (red, dashed) show good agreement with the leading order term (blue, dotted) when the separation θ is small. The Bhattacharyya distance scales as O ␀ θ3 in the sub-Rayleigh regime (θ ≪1). The black dashed line is the quantum Chernoff exponent that scales as O ␀ θ2 .</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This theoretical paper determines the fundamental limit for distinguishing between one or two weak optical point sources using ideal direct imaging. It calculates the Bhattacharyya distance, finding that the achievable scaling depends on the system's Amplitude Spread Function (ASF). Specifically, the scaling is $O(\theta^4)$ if the ASF has no zeros, but improves to $O(\theta^3)$ if the ASF has zeros, providing a crucial comparison point against the quantum limit.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work provides a rigorous, quantitative comparison of measurement limits in quantum optics, showing how the mathematical properties (zeros) of the system's transfer function (ASF) fundamentally alter the achievable detection scaling, which is crucial for designing optimal quantum measurements.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper addresses the fundamental limit for distinguishing between a single weak incoherent optical point source and two symmetrically placed weak incoherent point sources using ideal direct imaging.

**Main result.** The scaling of the Bhattacharyya distance depends critically on the Amplitude Spread Function (ASF): it scales as $O(\theta^4)$ if the ASF has no zeros, but improves to $O(\theta^3)$ if the ASF possesses zeros.

**Method.** The analysis uses the Bhattacharyya distance, derived from comparing the probability distributions of the single-source and two-source intensity patterns, employing Maclaurin series expansions and asymptotic analysis near zeros.

**Model / system.** The system is modeled using ideal direct imaging with a focal plane array (FPA) of intensity detectors. The image intensity is governed by the Point Spread Function (PSF), which is the squared magnitude of the Amplitude Spread Function (ASF), $\psi(x)$.

**Key observables.** Bhattacharyya distance ($\xi_{	ext{direct}}(	heta)$), which quantifies the distinguishability between the two source configurations.

**Important parameters / regimes.** $	heta$, the separation between the two point sources scaled to the PSF width; the sub-Rayleigh regime ($	heta \ll 1$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis assumes ideal direct imaging conditions and relies on asymptotic expansions that fail or require modification when the ASF has zeros.

**Figures summary.** Figure 2 illustrates the $O(\theta^4)$ scaling for a Gaussian ASF, while Figure 3 demonstrates the improved $O(\theta^3)$ scaling for a Sinc ASF.

**Paper structure.** The paper first sets up the problem using the Bhattacharyya distance. It then analyzes the case where the ASF has no zeros, yielding an $O(\theta^4)$ scaling. Subsequently, it tackles the more complex case where the ASF has zeros, requiring a specialized asymptotic analysis that reveals an $O(\theta^3)$ scaling.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We consider the task of distinguishing between a single weak incoherent optical point source and two weak incoherent optical point sources located symmetrically about the first source. $θ$ is the separation between the two point sources scaled to the Point Spread Function (PSF) width in the image plane. Using an ideal focal plane array of intensity detectors (ideal direct imaging), we quantify the performance using the Bhattacharyya distance and find the scaling of its leading order term in terms of $θ$ in the sub-Rayleigh regime. A suite of previous analyses of this problem lacked a comprehensive analysis for when the amplitude spread function (ASF) of the imaging system has zeros and reported a scaling that we find to be incorrect. We complete this analysis by explicitly calculating the leading order term of the Bhattacharyya distance for ideal direct imaging with any ASF, for small $θ$ and show the difference in scaling based on the presence or absence of zeros in the ASF. This is similar to the ASF dependent performance in the task of estimating the separation between the two point sources and the task of detecting a change to an object. We then apply our results to the specific example of a Gaussian and a Sinc ASF and show good agreement with numerical calculations. Our results allow the accurate comparison of other measurement schemes with ideal direct imaging, and to the quantum limit.

</details>

### [Low-Temperature Suppression of Intertwined Orders in La$_{1/3}$Sr$_{2/3}$FeO$_{3}$ Thin Films](http://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00769v1)

**Authors:** Mayia A. Vranas, Robin Glefke, Katherine Matthews, Xiaoke Li, Tianxing Wang, Henry Navarro, I-Ching Lin, Sarmistha Das, Biswajit Sahoo, Rourav Basak, Wei He, Holden Bauer, Ella Di Liberti, Jacob Butler, Elliot Kisiel, Erik Lamb, Jun-Sik Lee, Cheng-Tai Kuo, Heemin Lee, Christie Nelson, Sophie Morley, Sujoy Roy, Christoph Klewe, Fanny Rodolakis, Jessica McChesney, Oleg Shpyrko, Yimei Zhu, Eric Fullerton, Ivan Schuller, Alex Frano

The L-edge peaks are labeled according to excited core state (L3 - 2p3/2; L2 - 2p1/2) and final crystal field split valence states (3d eg and t2g)[8, 26]. c) The difference in absorption spectra from 220 K to 120 K (orange) and from 120 K to 18 K (blue), highlighting the changes in spectral weight across both transition temperatures. d) Field-cooled (FC) and zero field-cooled (ZFC) moment vs temperature measurements of LSFO. e) Difference...</figcaption>

The low temperature and high temperature dynamics show different timescales. d) Temperature-dependent contrast of speckles for six exposure times, showing an increase in contrast at low temperatures.</figcaption>

**Summary.** This work uses synchrotron X-ray scattering to investigate the low-temperature phase diagram of LSFO thin films, which exhibit coupled magnetic and charge ordering. The primary finding is the sharp suppression of both orders below 25 K, suggesting the system enters a novel, less ordered ground state. The research highlights the critical role of dimensionality and competing interactions in determining the final electronic state of strongly correlated perovskites.

**Why it may be interesting.** The study provides detailed experimental evidence of how competing interactions and reduced dimensionality can destabilize complex, coupled electronic orders in strongly correlated oxides, which is a key area of modern condensed matter physics.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The study investigates the low-temperature behavior of intertwined magnetic and charge orders in La1/3Sr2/3FeO3 (LSFO) thin films, focusing on the regime below 100 K.

**Main result.** The paper reports the complete suppression of both the antiferromagnetic and charge ordering signals below approximately 25 K, suggesting a complex, low-temperature ground state.

**Method.** The research employs advanced X-ray scattering and spectroscopy techniques, including Resonant X-ray Scattering (RSXS), X-ray Photon Correlation Spectroscopy (XPCS), and X-ray Magnetic Circular Dichroism (XMCD).

**Model / system.** The system is the perovskite oxide La1/3Sr2/3FeO3 (LSFO) grown as epitaxial thin films on various substrates. The material exhibits strong coupling between spin, charge, and lattice degrees of freedom, leading to coupled magnetic and charge ordering.

**Key observables.** Temperature dependence of AFM and CD Bragg peak amplitudes, out-of-plane correlation lengths, dynamic timescales ($	au$) from XPCS, and valence state ratios from XAS.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Low temperature regime ($<100 	ext{ K}$), critical suppression temperatures ($T^* \sim 60 	ext{ K}$, $T_{	ext{supp}} \sim 20 	ext{ K}$), and film thickness/dimensionality effects.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The order suppression is intrinsic to the LSFO film, ruling out substrate-induced effects, and the low-temperature phase is not ferromagnetic.

**Figures summary.** Figures show temperature dependence of magnetic/charge peaks (RSXS), correlation length analysis (CD stable, AFM decreases), and XPCS waterfall plots illustrating dynamic changes near transition temperatures.

**Paper structure.** The paper systematically uses multiple techniques (RSXS, XPCS, XMCD, XAS) to map the phase transitions, first establishing the high-temperature coupled order, then tracking its decay upon cooling, and finally proposing that the collapse is driven by the instability of the long-range AFM order in reduced dimensions.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

The strong coupling between spin, charge, and lattice degrees of freedom in perovskite oxides leads to an array of exotic phenomena, giving these materials rich phase diagrams that can include coupled orders. This is exemplified by the A-site doped ferrite La$_{1/3}$Sr$_{2/3}$FeO$_{3}$ (LSFO), which exhibits a coupled paramagnetic-antiferromagnetic and charge ordering phase transition at $\sim$190 K that has been well studied in thin films, bulk, and polycrystalline samples. However, the low temperature behavior of LSFO thin films below $\sim$100 K has not been thoroughly explored. This work uses several X-ray scattering and spectroscopy techniques to directly probe LSFO's magnetic and charge order down to low temperature. Using resonant X-ray scattering, we observe a complete suppression of LSFO's known antiferromagnetic and charge order below $\sim$25 K. Further spectroscopy and coherent scattering measurements provide insight into LSFO's electronic structure and domain dynamics in this new low temperature phase, and we propose possible explanations for the observed order suppression based on reduced dimensionality of domains in our thin films. Our findings provide insight into the effects of competing interactions in strongly correlated materials, particularly those with coupled orders.

</details>

<a id="paper-2606.00256"></a>
### [No-Go Theorem for Ancilla-Assisted Gaussian Enhancement in Passive-Unitary Estimation](http://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00256v1)

**Authors:** Zihao Gong, Saikat Guha  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2606.00256v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `entanglement & information structure` **1/5** · `quantum measurements` **1/5**

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>
<div class="paper-figures-scroll">
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2606.00256_figures/2606.00256_fig1.jpg" alt="Figure 1">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> Fig. 1. Setup for ancilla-assisted single-parameter estimation using Gaussian probes. The probe consists of mA ancilla modes and mB signal modes. The initial state |ψin⟩is taken to be a tensor product of single-mode squeezed coherent states. By applying a passive linear interferometer R, we obtain the general pure Gaussian probe state |ψ⟩. The mB signal modes are transmitted through the passive lossless Gaussian unitary Rφ = I(A) ⊗R(B) φ , which encodes the unknown parameter φ, while the mA ancilla modes remain unchanged. The final output state is denoted by |ψφ⟩.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper derives a no-go theorem for quantum parameter estimation in lossless passive-unitary channels. It proves that constraining the energy only in the signal modes, while allowing infinite energy in ancilla modes, does not improve the ultimate estimation precision (QFI). The result shows that the optimal measurement is entirely determined by the energy budget allocated to the signal modes.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work provides fundamental limits on quantum estimation precision in passive quantum channels, which is crucial for understanding the ultimate performance of quantum sensors and quantum communication protocols.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper investigates the maximum Quantum Fisher Information (QFI) achievable for estimating a parameter encoded in a passive-unitary transformation applied to a multimode Gaussian state.

**Main result.** It establishes a no-go theorem proving that allowing arbitrarily large energy in locally retained ancilla modes does not enhance the maximum QFI beyond the limit set by the signal-energy constraint.

**Method.** The authors calculate the QFI by deriving and bounding contributions from the covariance matrix and displacement vector, utilizing matrix inequalities and analyzing the structure of optimal probe states.

**Model / system.** The system models a bipartite sensing setup involving $m_A$ ancilla modes and $m_B$ signal modes, where the probe state is a pure Gaussian state evolving under a passive Gaussian unitary. The parameter is encoded via a passive Gaussian unitary acting only on the signal modes.

**Key observables.** Quantum Fisher Information (QFI), $	ext{F}_\varphi$; covariance matrix ($\Gamma$); mean photon number ($ar{n}$).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Mean photon number constraint ($ar{n}$) on the signal modes; the structure of the passive Gaussian unitary transformation.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The estimation process is restricted to lossless passive-unitary transformations, and the probe state is a pure Gaussian state.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 illustrates the general setup involving ancilla and signal modes for the estimation process.

**Paper structure.** The paper analyzes the QFI by decomposing it into parts related to the covariance matrix and displacement vector, deriving upper bounds using matrix inequalities, and ultimately proving the no-go theorem by showing the optimal state requires zero signal displacement energy.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We study the maximum quantum Fisher information (QFI) for estimating a single parameter embedded in a generic multimode lossless passive Gaussian unitary using general Gaussian probes under a signal-energy constraint. Unlike previous work, which imposed a total energy constraint on the full probe, we constrain only the transmitted signal modes while allowing an arbitrary number of locally retained ancilla modes with arbitrarily large energy. We prove that this additional freedom does not increase the maximum achievable QFI; the optimum remains identical to that attainable without extra ancilla energy. The same conclusion also extends to the sequential setting under a total energy constraint. We also characterize the family of optimal probe states and show that entanglement is not necessary to attain the optimum in the lossless setting. This extends the result of Matsubara et al. to the physically motivated signal-energy-constrained scenario and establishes a no-go theorem for ancilla-assisted Gaussian enhancement in noiseless passive-unitary estimation.

</details>

### [Significance of time-convolutionless mode-coupling theory in capturing the dynamics of glass-forming liquids](http://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01696v1)

**Authors:** Michio Tokuyama  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** statistical mechanics · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2606.01696v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `Keldysh / 2PI / non-Gaussian methods` **1/5** · `non-equilibrium dynamics` **1/5**

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>
<div class="paper-figures-scroll">
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2606.01696_figures/2606.01696_fig1.jpg" alt="Figure 1">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> FIG. 1: (Color online) Classiﬁcation of the basic equations discussed in the present paper into three stages, [N], [K], and [H], depending on a space (r)-time (t) scale, where ℓi and τi indicate the characteristic length and time of interest, respectively. Numbers in the ﬁgure indicate equation numbers.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper rigorously compares Time-Convolutionless Mode-Coupling Theory (TMCT) against standard MCT for glass-forming liquids. It argues that the choice between time-convolution (TC) and time-convolutionless (TCL) formalisms is crucial, showing that TMCT correctly captures the dynamics across different time scales. The results indicate that TMCT yields more physically accurate predictions for the glass transition compared to ideal MCT.

**Why it may be interesting.** The rigorous comparison of different theoretical frameworks (MCT vs. TMCT) for non-equilibrium dynamics, especially concerning the choice of mathematical formalism (TC vs. TCL), provides deep insights into the limitations of standard theoretical tools when applied to complex many-body systems.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper aims to demonstrate the superiority of the Time-Convolutionless Mode-Coupling Theory (TMCT) over conventional Mode-Coupling Theory (MCT) in accurately describing the complex dynamics of glass-forming liquids.

**Main result.** TMCT provides a fundamentally different and more physically consistent description of the dynamics, particularly in the intermediate and long-time regimes, leading to critical parameters that align better with experimental expectations than ideal MCT.

**Method.** The authors compare two projection operator methods (Time-Convolution vs. Time-Convolutionless) and apply them to derive and solve coupled equations for correlation functions, utilizing Laplace transforms and asymptotic analysis.

**Model / system.** The system studied is a three-dimensional equilibrium glass-forming liquid, modeled using density-density correlation functions and analyzed across different space-time scales (microscopic, kinetic, hydrodynamic).

**Key observables.** Intermediate scattering function $f_\alpha(q, t)$, non-Gaussian parameter $\alpha_2(t)$, self-diffusion coefficient $D_L^s$, and the non-ergodicity parameter $f_\alpha(q)$.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Critical volume fraction ($\phi_c$ or $\phi_g$), temperature $T$, and wavevector $q$.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis relies on comparing the full TMCT against ideal MCT, noting that approximations like (A2) lead to unphysical results ($\alpha_2(t) < 0$) in both formalisms.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 illustrates the classification of basic equations into three distinct space-time scale stages: microscopic [N], kinetic [K], and hydrodynamic [H].

**Paper structure.** The paper reviews the theoretical basis by comparing TC and TCL projection operators, applies these to the glass-forming liquid dynamics, derives the full TMCT equation, simplifies it into a recursion equation, and finally compares its predictions against ideal MCT across various physical observables and asymptotic limits.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

This paper demonstrates the significance of the recently proposed time-convolutionless mode-coupling theory (TMCT) in capturing the dynamics of glass-forming liquids. The origin of the primary differences between ideal MCT and TMCT is comprehensively explored from a unified perspective. First, we review two distinct projection operator methods in the Heisenberg picture: the time-convolution (TC) formulation proposed by Mori and the time-convolutionless (TCL) formulation proposed by Tokuyama and Mori. We show that the appropriate choice between these frameworks fundamentally depends on the space-time scales of the relevant variables. In TMCT, the TC formulation is applied to the current density, whereas the TCL formulation is applied to the number density because the latter operates on a significantly longer space-time scale. In contrast, ideal MCT applies the TC formulation to both densities. Consequently, the governing equation in TMCT is timeconvolutionless, whereas the ideal MCT equation features a time-convolution form. This fundamental difference significantly affects various physical quantities near the glass transition. Finally, the full TMCT equation is transformed into a simplified recursion equation to facilitate numerical analysis, and the key predictions of TMCT are compared with those of ideal MCT

</details>

### [Surface Excitations, Energy Loss, and Decoherence in Electron Interferometry](http://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01004v1)

**Authors:** David Kordahl  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2606.01004v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `correlated / nonlocal dissipation` **1/5** · `interference shaping light` **1/5**

Mode-resolved contributions to the decoherence rate integrand in the (kx, ω) plane for an electron beam at height b = 7.0 µm above an n-doped silicon surface, with path sepa- ration d = 8.1 µm, and at temperature T = 293 K. (Dielectric parameters are given in Eq. 38.) Top left: differential EEL spectrum d3P/(dz dω dkx). Top right: product of the EEL spectrum with the path weighting factor 1 −cos(kxd). Bot- tom left: product of EEL spectrum with the thermal factor coth(ℏω/2kBT). Bottom right: the full integrand entering γ(b, d, T)—the differential EEL spectrum multiplied both by the path and the thermal weighting.</figcaption>

Visibility V as a function of surface distance b for several fixed path separations d (indicated in each panel), for a split electron passing over n-doped silicon. Discrete points show digitized results from Kerker et al. Dashed curves cor- respond to non-retarded models (Eq. 28), and solid curves correspond to retarded models (Eq. 32); blue curves use the zero-temperature decoherence rate (Eq. 22), while red curves include the thermal correction at T = 293 K (Eq. 37). Both thermal and retardation effects are important to the experi- mental signal.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2606.01004_figures/2606.01004_fig4.jpg" alt="Figure 4">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 4.</b> FIG. 5. Fringe visibility V as a function of substrate temper- ature T for three impact parameters b, at fixed path separa- tion d = 6.0 µm, computed using the retarded model (Eq. 32) with thermal weighting (Eq. 37) for n-doped silicon parame- ters (Eq. 38) and for gold parameters (Eq. 39).</figcaption>

The retarded predictions require a low-frequency cut- off to avoid the ω →0 Drude divergence, discussed in text. As in silicon, both thermal and retardation effects are impor- tant, but here the retardation effects dominate.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper models decoherence in electron interferometry near surfaces by linking the loss of fringe visibility to the substrate's electronic excitation spectrum (EELS). The authors show that the decoherence rate depends on the EELS spectrum weighted by the path separation, concluding that both finite temperature and retardation effects are crucial for accurate quantitative predictions. This suggests that fringe visibility can be used as a sensitive, near-field thermometer for the substrate.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work provides a concrete, measurable link between quantum interference phenomena (visibility loss) and the continuum of excitations in a material (EELS), offering a novel, near-field thermometric probe.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper develops a mode-resolved description of decoherence in split-beam electron interferometry near dielectric surfaces and connects this process to Electron Energy-Loss Spectroscopy (EELS).

**Main result.** The decoherence rate is found to be a weighted integral over the differential EELS spectrum, and both retardation and finite temperature effects are necessary for quantitative agreement with experiments.

**Method.** The analysis involves deriving a rate equation for fringe visibility decay, relating the decoherence rate to the EELS spectrum via a path distinguishability factor.

**Model / system.** The system is electron interferometry using two electron trajectories separated by distance $d$ above a dielectric substrate. The substrate response is modeled using its dielectric function $\epsilon(\omega)$.

**Key observables.** Fringe visibility ($V$), decoherence rate ($\gamma(b, d)$), and the differential EELS spectrum.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Path separation ($d$), electron height ($b$), interaction length ($L$), temperature ($T$), and the substrate's dielectric function $\epsilon(\omega)$.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis uses the Markovian approximation and initially considers first-order coupling to the substrate excitations.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the geometry of the interaction, compare predicted visibility vs. beam height for different models (thermal vs. retarded), and show temperature dependence of visibility.

**Paper structure.** The paper builds from the fundamental decoherence rate calculation, derives the rate equation for visibility decay, and then compares the resulting model against established experimental results, emphasizing the necessity of thermal and retardation corrections.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We develop a mode-resolved description of decoherence in split-beam electron interferometry near dielectric surfaces, and show how it connects to models from electron energy-loss spectroscopy (EELS). The central result is that the decoherence rate is a weighted integral over the differential EELS spectrum, with a factor $1-\cos(k_x d)$ encoding the path distinguishability of the two electron trajectories. Incorporating retardation and thermal occupation reproduces the macroscopic QED model of Scheel and Buhmann. We compare non-retarded vs. retarded and non-thermal vs. thermally weighted variants of the model against published results from Kerker et al., finding that both retardation and finite temperature are required for reasonable quantitative agreement. The thermal weighting suggests an application: fringe visibility as a near-field thermometric probe of the substrate, sensitive to the evanescent regime inaccessible to far-field methods.

</details>

(f) X-gauge fixing of A1 from (240) back into the bare code (up to local Cliffords and qubit...</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper introduces a powerful graphical method using the ZX-calculus to study dynamical automorphisms in quantum error correction codes. By modeling code switching as a path in the space of stabilizer codes, the authors provide a systematic way to construct these automorphisms. This machinery allows for the theoretical design and search for implementations of complex logical quantum gates.

**Why it may be interesting.** The rigorous mathematical framework connecting gauge fixing, graph theory, and code structure provides powerful tools for understanding the underlying symmetries and computational capabilities of quantum error-correcting codes.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** To develop a machine-interpretable method for constructing dynamical automorphisms for quantum error correction (QEC) codes, particularly for handling dynamical stabilizer codes.

**Main result.** The authors show that dynamical automorphisms can be constructed by viewing code switching as a closed loop in the space of stabilizer codes, which they term 'chutes and ladders' relative to local Clifford operations.

**Method.** The core method extends the ZX-calculus to model gauge fixing, which is used to implement these paths in the space of stabilizer codes by combining gauge-fixing steps.

**Model / system.** The work focuses on stabilizer codes, represented by their ZX-encoding graphs, and extends this framework to subsystem codes and dynamical codes. The mathematical tools involve the ZX-calculus, graph theory, and symplectic representations.

**Key observables.** Dynamical automorphisms, logical gates (e.g., logical S or phase gate), and the structure of the Local Clifford (LC) orbit graph.

**Important parameters / regimes.** The code parameters $[[n, k, d]]$ (e.g., the 7-qubit code), and the local Clifford group elements.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The method relies on the connection between measurement-based code-switching and gauge fixing, and the ability to define a unique normal form for ZX-encoding graphs.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate ZX-encoding graphs, showing how gauge fixing creates closed loops (dynamical automorphisms) in the space of stabilizer codes, and demonstrating the implementation of specific logical gates.

**Paper structure.** The paper systematically develops the ZX-calculus formalism for subsystem codes, establishes a unique normal form for these graphs, and then applies this framework to show that gauge fixing generates dynamical automorphisms that can implement desired logical gates.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

The ZX-calculus is a powerful graphical language for manipulating quantum circuits, which has recently found many applications in quantum error correction. We extend this language to handle Floquet and other dynamical stabilizer codes via the connection between measurement-based code switching and gauge fixing (arXiv:1810.10037). We combine gauge-fixing steps to implement a closed loop in the space of stabilizer codes, returning to the original codespace up to a logical Clifford gate. These measurement-based paths in the space of stabilizer codes can be viewed as shortcuts, or "chutes and ladders", relative to single-qubit Clifford operations and qubit permutations. This yields a machine-interpretable method for constructing dynamical automorphisms and facilitates the search for implementations of desired logical gates. As an example, we implement a logical phase gate via distance-preserving code switching for the seven-qubit code bare code (arXiv:1702.01155), which has no non-trivial logical Clifford gates based on single-qubit Clifford operations and qubit permutations (arXiv:2409.18175).

</details>

### [Computational Phase Transitions in Binary Compressed Sensing: Quantum Annealing Inside the Relaxation Gap](http://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00806v1)

**Authors:** William Hahn, Natalia Romero  
**Type:** both · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2606.00806v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `QC/QI experiment` **1/5**

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>

<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> Fig. 1. Phase transition in exact recovery rate versus measurement ratio m/n for k = 2 (left) and k = 5 (right) at n = 32. Shaded region indicates the relaxation gap. At k = 2, D-Wave Hybrid leads at low m/n; AMP is competitive. At k = 5, D-Wave is the only solver with any recovery at m/n ≤0.25. Bands: 95% Wilson CIs.</figcaption>
</figure>

Right: k/n ≈0.16. The QPU advantage at n = 32 does not extend to n = 64, where AMP dominates. The embedding boundary is visible as the gap between QPU curves at the two scales.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2606.00806_figures/2606.00806_fig3.jpg" alt="Figure 3">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 3.</b> Fig. 4 (right panel) maps the quantum advantage topol- ogy ∆R = RD-Wave −Rclassical across the full (k/n, m/n) plane. The positive (blue) region corresponds to the re- laxation gap: the advantage is largest at low k/n and low m/n, precisely where the Donoho-Tanner theory predicts ℓ1 failure. This landscape structure—where the QUBO ground state is shallow relative to surrounding incorrect basins—may generalize to other dense QUBO problems arising from Gram matrices AT A, suggesting quantum annealing’s advantage is not specific to compressed sensing</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2606.00806_figures/2606.00806_fig4.jpg" alt="Figure 4">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 4.</b> Fig. 3. QUBO energy distribution for D-Wave QPU successes versus failures at n = 32. Successful recoveries find lower energies (ground state basin). Failures are trapped in higher-energy local minima. Dashed lines: means.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2606.00806_figures/2606.00806_fig5.jpg" alt="Figure 5">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 5.</b> Fig. 4. Recovery heatmaps with Donoho-Tanner phase transition boundary [4]–[6] (dashed). Left: D-Wave best (hybrid or QPU). Center: Best classical solver. Right: Quantum advantage topology ∆R = RD-Wave−Rclassical (blue = quantum advantage, red = classical advantage). The advantage concentrates below the DT boundary.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper explores quantum annealing for solving sparse signal recovery problems in compressed sensing, focusing on a regime called the 'relaxation gap'. The authors report that quantum annealing successfully recovers signals in this gap where even the best classical algorithm, AMP, fails. While the advantage is shown empirically for small systems, the work suggests a potential role for quantum tunneling in solving classically intractable optimization barriers.

**Why it may be interesting.** The work directly applies quantum computation (quantum annealing) to a hard optimization problem (sparse recovery), providing empirical evidence of quantum advantage in a regime where classical methods are theoretically optimal, which is highly relevant to quantum algorithms for optimization.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper investigates the computational phase transition boundary in binary compressed sensing, specifically identifying a regime where quantum annealing outperforms all tested classical recovery methods.

**Main result.** At a specific point in the 'relaxation gap' ($n=32$), quantum annealing achieved exact signal recovery (7%) where the best classical solver (AMP) failed entirely (0%), suggesting a quantum advantage in this finite-size setting.

**Method.** The authors compared the performance of D-Wave quantum annealing against established classical algorithms like AMP, using energy landscape analysis to hypothesize that quantum tunneling is responsible for the observed advantage.

**Model / system.** The problem is formulated as recovering sparse binary signals ($\mathbf{x} \in \{0, 1\}^n$) from measurements ($\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{x}$), mapped onto a QUBO Hamiltonian solved on a D-Wave quantum annealer.

**Key observables.** Exact recovery rate (%), QUBO ground state energy, and comparison against the Bayes-optimal classical threshold.

**Important parameters / regimes.** The critical regime is the 'relaxation gap' (below the Donoho-Tanner $\ell_1$ threshold), with specific tested parameters being $n=32, k=5, m/n=0.19$.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The observed advantage is preliminary, finite-size evidence, and the mechanism relies on the hypothesis that quantum tunneling overcomes local minima trapping classical search.

**Figures summary.** Figures map the phase transition boundaries and compare recovery rates across different system sizes ($n=32$ vs $n=64$), highlighting the quantum advantage in the gap region.

**Paper structure.** The paper establishes the problem in compressed sensing, details the comparison between quantum and classical solvers, presents empirical results showing quantum advantage in the gap, analyzes the energy landscape, and concludes by outlining the limitations and need for larger-scale confirmation.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We map the computational phase transition boundary in binary compressed sensing and identify a regime where D-Wave's quantum annealer recovers signals in a region where all tested classical methods fail, including Approximate Message Passing (AMP), which achieves the Bayes-optimal recovery threshold asymptotically for Gaussian matrices. In 19,775 experiments (n in {32, 64}, nine classical solvers, two D-Wave modes), we find that quantum annealing recovers sparse binary signals in the relaxation gap -- the regime below the Donoho-Tanner l1 phase transition where the l0 solution exists but convex relaxations fail. At n=32, k=5, m/n=0.19, D-Wave achieves 7% exact recovery while AMP and eight other solvers score 0% across 250 combined trials (Fisher exact p=0.018). At n=64, embedding overhead limits the QPU, but D-Wave's hybrid solver remains competitive with AMP. Energy landscape analysis reveals that the QUBO ground state contains the true signal, but incorrect solutions occupy shallower local basins that trap classical search -- a structure consistent with quantum tunneling dynamics. To our knowledge, this constitutes preliminary finite-size evidence that quantum annealing succeeds in a narrow regime where all tested classical methods, including the Bayes-optimal AMP, fail within a well-characterized combinatorial inference problem. Confirmation at larger n, higher trial counts, and with stronger classical controls remains an open problem.

</details>

### [Defect Holonomy Near Rank-Deficient Mixed States](http://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02343v1)

**Authors:** Yu-Huan Huang, Xu-Yang Hou, Hao Guo  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2606.02343v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `entanglement & information structure` **1/5**

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>
<div class="paper-figures-scroll">
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2606.02343_figures/2606.02343_fig1.jpg" alt="Figure 1">

</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This work analyzes the geometry of mixed quantum states near rank-changing singularities, identifying these points as geometric defects. The authors show that while local curvature diverges path-dependently, the global topological invariant—the Uhlmann holonomy—remains robust. This holonomy provides a continuous classification of the defect, generalizing concepts from Berry phase physics to mixed systems.

**Why it may be interesting.** It provides a powerful geometric tool (holonomy) to characterize singularities in the space of quantum states that are robust against local perturbations, which is crucial for understanding quantum phase transitions or decoherence effects.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper investigates the geometric structure of mixed quantum states near points where the rank of the density matrix changes, treating these singularities as effective geometric defects.

**Main result.** The robust geometric information is encoded not in the path-dependent Uhlmann curvature, but in the global, asymptotically robust Uhlmann holonomy around the defect, which is classified by a continuous, non-quantized variable.

**Method.** The authors use the Uhlmann connection and holonomy, generalizing Berry phase concepts to mixed states, by analyzing the topology of the punctured state manifold.

**Model / system.** The analysis is performed on the manifold of mixed quantum states ($ho$), using an exactly solvable qutrit model ($	ext{SU}(2)$ sector) as a primary test case.

**Key observables.** Uhlmann holonomy, Wilson loop variable $W(C) = 	ext{Tr} U(C)$, and the Uhlmann curvature $\lVert F_U Vert$.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Spectral ratios of vanishing eigenvalues ($\zeta$), and the scaling exponents ($\alpha_a$) governing eigenvalue approach to the singularity.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis requires restricting to the full-rank sector and working on the punctured manifold $M^	imes$ to define the holonomy.

**Figures summary.** Figures schematically illustrate the punctured manifold $M^	imes$ and the non-trivial fundamental group $\pi_1(M^	imes) \simeq \mathbb{Z}$. They also plot the continuous dependence of the Wilson loop variable $W(C)$ on spectral ratios and the power-law scaling of the curvature magnitude.

**Paper structure.** The paper establishes the singularity at rank-deficient points, contrasts the path-dependent curvature singularity (Theorem 2) with the robust asymptotic holonomy (Theorem 1), and derives the continuous classification of the defect via the Wilson loop variable.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We investigate the geometry of mixed quantum states near rank-changing points, showing that these singularities function as effective geometric defects. The Uhlmann connection is well-defined only on the full-rank sector of the density-matrix manifold, while rank-deficient states form singular boundary strata where the bundle structure degenerates. By restricting to a punctured state manifold that excludes the singular set, we obtain a well-defined gauge structure and identify an asymptotically robust invariant: the Uhlmann holonomy around noncontractible loops encircling the defect. In an exactly solvable qutrit model, a restricted submanifold emerges on which the connection is locally flat yet carries nontrivial monodromy, analogous to flat connections with Aharonov--Bohm-type transport. The holonomy depends only on the ratios of the vanishing eigenvalues under frozen radial dependence of the eigenbasis geometry and a fixed angular loop. In contrast, the Uhlmann curvature may diverge path-dependently when eigenvalues shrink with distinct powers, with a leading spectral-prefactor scaling law, establishing that the holonomy survives as a universal asymptotic invariant while the curvature remains non-universal. Within the effective SU(2) defect sector, the conjugacy class of the holonomy, equivalently the Wilson loop variable, provides a continuous, non-quantized classification of the asymptotic monodromy surrounding the rank-deficient defect. This non-quantization does not imply a lack of robustness: the asymptotic holonomy is protected by the topology of the punctured manifold and is insensitive to smooth deformations of the loop or the radial profile.

</details>

**Authors:** Nabin Bhatta, Djordje Minic, Tatsu Takeuchi  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2606.02421v1>

**Summary.** This theoretical paper introduces 'Density Cube Theory' to model quantum dynamics beyond standard pairwise interference by using a three-index density cube. The authors derive the evolution equation from quantizing ternary Nambu dynamics, predicting that triple-path interference terms exhibit oscillatory behavior. This work suggests a new framework for understanding higher-order quantum correlations.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work proposes a fundamental extension to quantum dynamics that models higher-order interference effects, which is highly relevant for theoretical investigations into quantum gravity or non-standard quantum foundations.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper aims to extend standard quantum mechanics formalism beyond pairwise path interference to incorporate and describe triple-path interference.

**Main result.** The authors derive an equation of motion for the density cube based on quantizing ternary Nambu dynamics, finding that pairs of triple-path interferences oscillate into each other.

**Method.** The formalism involves extending the density matrix to a three-index 'density cube' ($ho_{ijk}$) and deriving its dynamics using a quantum analog of the Nambu bracket.

**Model / system.** The model is the 'Density Cube Theory,' which describes quantum states using a three-index tensor ($ho_{ijk}$). The dynamics are governed by a structure derived from ternary Nambu dynamics, requiring the use of a specific 'Kawamura Product' to maintain algebraic consistency.

**Key observables.** Triple-path interference elements ($ho_{ijk}$ with distinct indices) and the conservation of the trace ($	ext{Tr}ho = 1$).

**Important parameters / regimes.** The system is analyzed for $N$-level systems, with specific calculations shown for $N=3$ and $N=4$.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The dynamics are assumed to follow the form $dho/dt = i[ho, H_1, H_2]$, and the authors adopt the Kawamura Product to bypass full associativity requirements.

**Figures summary.** Not specified (The notes only mention equations and structural results, not figure content).

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the density cube formalism, derives the equation of motion from quantized ternary Nambu dynamics, calculates the structure constants for the resulting ternary algebra, and finally demonstrates the oscillatory behavior of the triple-path interference terms.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Density cube theory extends the canonical quantum density matrix $ρ_{ij}$ with the addition of an extra index to $ρ_{ijk}$. The elements of the density cube with two different indices, $ρ_{iij}$ and $ρ_{ijj}$, correspond to the real and imaginary parts of the off-diagonal element $ρ_{ij}$ of the density matrix and describe double-path interference, while those with three different indices describe non-canonical triple-path interference. In this letter, we propose an equation of motion for the density cube, obtained from the quantization of ternary Nambu dynamics, and find that pairs of triple-path interferences oscillate into each other.

</details>

**Summary.** This work uses ARPES on Co-doped iron-based superconductors to study how multiple topological electronic states coexist and interact under electron doping. The key finding is the direct observation of two distinct topological bands intersecting at the Fermi level, which establishes the material as an excellent platform for exploring complex Majorana mode physics in correlated superconductors.

**Why it may be interesting.** The explicit focus on correlated topological superconductivity and the detailed mapping of multiple interacting topological states (TI/TDS) provides a rich playground for understanding emergent quantum phenomena beyond simple BCS theory.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The study investigates the complex interplay between multiple coexisting topological superconducting states and strong correlation effects within iron-based superconductors.

**Main result.** The authors directly resolved two distinct intertwining topological states (TI surface and TDS bulk) in Co-doped Fe(Te,Se), showing that their intersection at the Fermi level enables the existence of multiple Majorana modes.

**Method.** The research employed high-resolution Angle-Resolved Photoelectron Spectroscopy (ARPES) to map the band structure evolution, supplemented by X-ray diffraction and magnetic susceptibility measurements.

**Model / system.** The physical system is the iron-based superconductor $	ext{Fe}(	ext{Te},	ext{Se})$, specifically $	ext{Co}$-doped $	ext{Fe}(	ext{Te},	ext{Se})$, which serves as an ideal platform to study correlated topological superconductivity.

**Key observables.** Band dispersion, Dirac point positions (TI and TDS), superconducting transition temperature ($T_c$) dependence on doping, and the presence/evolution of Majorana zero modes.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Electron doping level ($x$ via Co substitution), Fermi level position ($E_F$), and the energy separation between the two topological bands ($\approx 20 	ext{ meV}$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The topological states observed are significantly affected by the strong correlation effect, and the $	ext{Co}$ substitution successfully tunes the system to isolate the relevant topological physics.

**Figures summary.** Figures show the evolution of TI and TDS Dirac bands with Co doping, confirming their relative positions and the shift of Dirac points. Other figures illustrate the $k_z$ dispersion and schematic representations of hybrid Majorana vortex phases.

**Paper structure.** The paper progresses by first characterizing the material structure and doping dependence (Fig. 1), then using ARPES to map the coexisting topological bands (Fig. 2), and finally analyzing the implications for Majorana modes by tracking band evolution and theoretical modeling (Fig. 3, 4).

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Multiple topological electronic phases can coexist within a single quantum material and induce different topological superconducting states, offering deeper insights into interplay of topological superconducting states and Majorana modes, which may also be influenced and modified by correlation effect. Iron-based superconductors, with both topological states and correlation effect, is an ideal platform to study these phenomena. Here, with high resolution angle resolved photoelectron spectroscopy, we directly resolve two distinct intertwining topological states in iron-based superconductor Co-doped Fe(Te,Se), and study their evolution with electron doping. We identify a region where both topological insulator surface states and topological Dirac semimetal states intersect the Fermi level. The topological states are affected by the strong correlation effect and are isolated from trivial bulk states. The evolution between distinct topological phases offers a good opportunity to study various Majorana modes from different superconducting phases according to theoretical analysis. Our findings establish an ideal platform for exploring the interaction between multiple topological superconducting states and the related Majorana modes.

</details>

**Summary.** This theoretical work demonstrates that the competition between excitonic and superconducting orders can lead to a novel coexistence regime driven by Fermi surface mismatch. This coexistence forces the superconducting state to inherit an anisotropic structure, resulting in intrinsic nematic superconductivity even without external symmetry-breaking fields. The findings suggest a general mechanism for generating electronic nematicity in correlated quantum materials.

**Why it may be interesting.** The mechanism of generating intrinsic nematicity purely from the competition between two competing many-body orders (EI and SC) is a general concept applicable to understanding anisotropic electronic states in correlated materials, which is highly relevant to modern condensed matter theory.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper investigates the competition and coexistence between Excitonic Insulating (EI) and Superconducting (SC) orders, which are generally considered mutually exclusive instabilities.

**Main result.** The intrinsic mismatch between electron and hole Fermi surfaces stabilizes a finite-momentum excitonic order, which reconstructs the pairing phase space to allow for the coexistence of EI and SC states, leading to emergent nematic superconductivity.

**Method.** The study employs a self-consistent mean-field theory, solving coupled gap equations for both SC and EI order parameters.

**Model / system.** The model considers correlated quantum materials, using a two-band Hamiltonian approach that incorporates both superconducting pairing and interband Coulomb interactions driving the excitonic instability.

**Key observables.** Superfluid density tensor components ($ho_{s,xx}, ho_{s,yy}$), EI gap ($\Delta_{EI}$), SC gap ($\Delta_{SC}$), and the excitonic ordering wave vector ($\mathbf{q}$).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Carrier density ($n$), temperature ($T$), and the degree of Fermi surface mismatch.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis relies on mean-field approximations and often assumes an isotropic parabolic band structure for initial simplicity, though extensions consider intervalley effects.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the schematic pairing on mismatched Fermi surfaces, the momentum-resolved SC correlation, and the superfluid density tensor components showing anisotropy ($ho_{s,xx} > ho_{s,yy}$) in the coexistence regime.

**Paper structure.** The paper progresses by establishing the theoretical framework (Hamiltonian, gap equations), analyzing the role of Fermi surface mismatch in stabilizing finite-momentum excitonic order, demonstrating the resulting phase space reconstruction, and finally calculating the anisotropic superfluid response as the signature of nematic coexistence.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Excitonic insulating (EI) and superconducting (SC) orders are generally regarded as mutually exclusive electronic instabilities. Within a self-consistent microscopic theory, we study electronic systems hosting an EI phase in the presence of SC pairing and show that an intrinsic mismatch between electron and hole Fermi surfaces fundamentally reshapes this competition. This mismatch stabilizes FFLO-like electron-hole pairing and drives spontaneous symmetry breaking of the EI state. The resulting symmetry breaking reconstructs the pairing phase space for SC and EI state, such that different regions of the Fermi surface complementarily support either EI or SC correlations, leading to a natural coexistence of the two orders. Notably, the emergent SC state consequently breaks rotational symmetry and develops intrinsic nematic superconductivity, even in the absence of explicit symmetry-breaking fields (such as magnetic fields, spin-orbit coupling, or bare band-structure anisotropy). Our results suggest that candidate materials such as monolayer 1T$'$-MoTe$_2$ and the square-net semimetal NaAlSi may provide promising platforms for observing this phenomenon. More broadly, these findings reveal a unique mechanism by which competing many-body orders generate electronic nematicity, suggesting a broader route toward spontaneous anisotropic electronic states in correlated quantum materials.

</details>

### [How to make quantum cheese: efficient geometry oracles for exponentially many pseudorandom microstructures](http://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00222v1)

**Authors:** Alice Barthe  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2606.00222v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `analog quantum simulation` **1/5**

</figure>

<img src="2606.00222_figures/2606.00222_fig2.jpg" alt="Figure 2">

The pseudorandom permutation P (k) made of multiple rounds of Figure 1 is applied to y. The result is compared to M using a comparator &lt; M raising flag f. The result is stored in g, and uncomputation is performed to leave y and f clean.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2606.00222_figures/2606.00222_fig3.jpg" alt="Figure 3">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 3.</b> Figure 4: Quantum circuit for the fixed-shape hole oracle, for a set ∆of T = 3 offsets δt. Each Q(k,δt) retrieves whether the site shifted by δt is a centre in a flag qubit ft using the circuit in Figure 2 . Then the result of W</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2606.00222_figures/2606.00222_fig4.jpg" alt="Figure 4">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 4.</b> Figure 5: A realisation of quantum cheese: For a quan- tum oracle solving Problem 1 for a material where holes of fixed shape have been hollowed out, result of simu- lating the oracle by querying all coordinates (x, y) on a 26 ×26 = 64×64 grid. We select M = 512 holes shaped as in Figure 3, with rotations parameters (u, v) = (1, 7) and m = 5 rounds with key k = [38, 51, 28, 14, 42].</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2606.00222_figures/2606.00222_fig5.jpg" alt="Figure 5">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 5.</b> Figure 6: Quantum circuit to apply a convolution with the kernel Λ = {(ηr, αr)}1≤r≤R (here R = 3) to the binary field given by Q(k,0), that is the permutation oracle shifted by 0, as in Figure 2. For each shift ηr, the flag f is raised or not. If it is raised, the controlled operation on f adds αr to the accumulator register z. The result of the convolution is stored in z, that can be further thresholded with a comparator to yield a geometry oracle.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper develops a theory for efficiently encoding the geometry of complex, structured materials into quantum oracles. It proves that while arbitrary material patterns are computationally hard to query, patterns generated by local, pseudorandom rules can be efficiently simulated using polynomial-size quantum circuits. This bridges quantum computation theory with condensed matter physics modeling.

**Why it may be interesting.** The work provides a rigorous quantum computational framework for simulating complex, disordered materials, moving beyond simple Hamiltonian simulation by addressing the computational cost of defining the system's geometry itself.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper investigates the fundamental question of when quantum oracles for materials defined by generative rules (rather than exhaustive lists) can be implemented efficiently by polynomial-size quantum circuits.

**Main result.** It shows that while arbitrary geometries lead to intractable oracles, imposing local structure, such as those derived from pseudorandom generation or convolution, allows for the construction of efficient, polynomial-size quantum oracles.

**Method.** The authors use complexity theory arguments, establishing lower bounds via reductions to unstructured search problems, and then provide explicit circuit constructions based on pseudorandom permutations and local convolution operations.

**Model / system.** The model involves textured materials (e.g., porous media, foams) whose geometry is determined by local rules acting on an exponentially large grid ($N=2^n$). The complexity is analyzed by constructing oracles for membership testing in these structured geometries.

**Key observables.** The primary observable is the quantum query complexity required to evaluate the geometry oracle, compared against the circuit size (gate count) required for implementation.

**Important parameters / regimes.** The key parameters include the grid size $N=2^n$, the number of holes $M$, and the structure of the local kernel $\Lambda$ used in the convolution.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The efficiency relies critically on the geometry possessing sufficient local structure (e.g., being generated by a pseudorandom process or a fixed kernel convolution).

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the quantum circuits for random-centre oracles and convolution oracles, and show examples of complex, physically relevant textures (like reaction-diffusion patterns) generated using these structured methods.

**Paper structure.** The paper progresses from establishing the general intractability of unstructured geometry oracles to introducing structured methods. It details the random-centre oracle, generalizes this via convolution and thresholding to model complex microstructures, and concludes with cost analyses and connections to quantum sampling hardness.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Quantum algorithms for simulating linear systems are often formulated under oracle access assumptions. A central question is when such oracles can be implemented by polynomial-size quantum circuits. In this paper, we study this question for materials specified by rules rather than by exhaustive descriptions. We focus on textured materials with exponentially many geometric features. In two settings, we show that, without additional structure, describing such geometries yields Grover-type lower bounds, making the corresponding quantum oracles intractable in general. In contrast, when suitable structure is imposed, we identify a broad family of pseudorandom locally textured materials whose geometry can be queried through a polynomial-size quantum circuit. We provide explicit circuit constructions for these oracles and verify their behaviour through numerical simulation.

</details>

In all...</figcaption>

All models are trained on a dataset of 20 000 elements. The fidelity decreases systematically with both δt and z: larger time steps displace the target propagators away from the group identity, while larger values of z broaden the training distribution, both increasing the difficulty of the reconstruction task.</figcaption>

The number over the bars represent the differences between them. The exact and reconstructed entropy profiles are in close agreement for small z, while the discrepancy grows with increasing z, mirroring the trend observed in the gate fidelity and confirming that reconstruction accuracy degrades as the training distribution broadens.</figcaption>
</figure>

**Summary.** This paper introduces a novel, scalable method called LA-CPR to generate quantum optimal control pulses for arbitrary unitary operations in superconducting qubits. By mapping the unitary structure onto a Lie algebra and training neural networks, it bypasses the need for computationally expensive optimization at runtime. This allows for the rapid design of control sequences for complex quantum simulations, such as those modeling neutrino oscillations.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work provides a powerful, scalable tool for quantum control, moving beyond iterative optimization methods by leveraging deep learning on the underlying mathematical structure of quantum evolution, which is highly relevant for designing complex quantum circuits.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper addresses the computational bottleneck in quantum optimal control by developing a method to generate control pulses for arbitrary unitary operations without requiring explicit, time-consuming optimization at inference time.

**Main result.** The proposed Lie Algebra-based Control Pulse Reconstruction (LA-CPR) framework successfully trains neural networks to map target unitaries to their required control pulses, achieving high reconstruction fidelity for systems up to 4 qubits.

**Method.** It combines Lie group theory to parameterize the space of target unitaries ($SU(N)$) with feed-forward neural networks trained on a dataset of pre-optimized control pulses to enable fast, non-iterative pulse generation.

**Model / system.** The study focuses on controlling superconducting qubit systems, modeling the dynamics using Hamiltonians involving drift, interaction, and time-dependent control terms. The underlying mathematical structure is the Lie group $SU(N)$ acting on the $N$-dimensional Hilbert space.

**Key observables.** Reconstruction Fidelity ($F$), Single-qubit von Neumann Entropy ($S_k$), and the control envelopes ($\Omega_x(t), \Omega_y(t)$).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Number of qubits ($N_q=2, 3, 4$), sampling radius ($z$), and time step ($\delta t$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The method assumes that the target unitaries can be effectively sampled from a neighborhood of the identity element within the Lie group, and that the required controls can be approximated by a mapping learned from this sampled data.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the reconstruction fidelity vs. dataset size and sampling radius, and show the reconstructed control field profiles for various qubit numbers and physical applications like neutrino oscillations.

**Paper structure.** The paper first establishes the physical problem (QOC bottleneck), then details the mathematical framework using Lie algebra theory to parameterize the unitary space. It introduces the LA-CPR method involving dataset sampling, NN training, and finally demonstrates its application to complex systems like neutrino flavor oscillations.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We present a framework combining Lie group theory and feed-forward neural networks to efficiently generate quantum optimal control pulses for arbitrary unitary operations in superconducting qubit systems, bypassing the need for explicit optimization at inference time. The exponential scaling of the Hilbert space dimension with qubit number makes standard optimization approaches computationally prohibitive when large ensembles of distinct propagators must be processed, a bottleneck that is particularly acute in Trotterized quantum simulation. Our method addresses this limitation by pre-computing a representative set of control pulses via Lie group theory and training neural networks to map target propagators to their corresponding pulses. We demonstrate the approach on superconducting qubit systems of 2, 3, and 4 qubits, finding high reconstruction fidelity for specific combinations of Lie algebra parameters. As a physically motivated benchmark, we apply the methodology to reconstruct control pulses for the Trotter propagators of a neutrino system undergoing collective flavor oscillations. The successful generalization across system types demonstrates that a single model -- trained once on hardware-specific random propagators -- can serve as a universal control-pulse generator for any target quantum system of compatible Hilbert space dimension, offering a promising route toward scalable quantum simulation.

</details>

**Summary.** This paper develops a comprehensive, unified theoretical model to determine the practical limits on generating and measuring highly squeezed light in integrated photonic circuits. It quantifies how various noise sources—including internal loss, pump saturation, and phase noise—degrade the quantum squeezing. The resulting model offers crucial design guidance for advancing integrated quantum light sources.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work provides essential theoretical tools for designing and benchmarking quantum optical devices, directly impacting the feasibility of quantum sensing and communication protocols that rely on non-classical light.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper quantifies the fundamental limits imposed by various noise sources on measuring highly squeezed light, particularly for next-generation integrated squeezed-light systems.

**Main result.** A unified model is developed that combines limitations from generation (e.g., internal loss, pump saturation) and detection (e.g., phase noise, electronic noise) into a single variance budget.

**Method.** The authors use the Gaussian quantum optics framework, deriving variance evolution equations and applying noise mixing approximations to model the cumulative effect of multiple loss and noise processes.

**Model / system.** The focus is on integrated nonlinear photonic circuits used as squeezed light sources (e.g., OPA). The analysis covers nonresonant, pump-resonant, and signal-resonant squeezing regimes.

**Key observables.** Squeezed quadrature variance ($V_{-}$), anti-squeezed variance ($V_{+}$), and the overall achievable squeezing level (in dB).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Nonlinearity-to-loss ratio ($g/\alpha$), internal loss rates ($\eta_i$), external loss ($\eta_e$), and phase error ($\delta	heta$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis relies on the Gaussian quantum optics framework and often uses the low-loss approximation for noise summation ($1 - \eta \ll 1$).

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate noise sources in a generic measurement setup, the comprehensive variance budget combining multiple noise terms, and plots showing fundamental bounds on achievable squeezing versus gain rate.

**Paper structure.** The paper progresses by first establishing the general variance budget, then deriving specific gain formulas for different squeezer types (nonresonant, signal-resonant), and finally unifying these limitations into a practical, comprehensive model.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Recent experiments have demonstrated the successful generation and detection of moderately squeezed vacuum states with integrated photonics. However, in order to benefit from the reduced noise of highly squeezed light, many different noise sources must be mitigated. Here, we quantify the fundamental limits these noise sources impose on squeezing measurements and find surprising generality across different platforms and designs. We combine these different limitations into a simple model that provides practical guidance for the design and benchmarking of next-generation integrated squeezed-light systems.

</details>

### [Relativistic transformation of temperature revisited](http://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00521v1)

**Authors:** Soroor Pouryazdan, Babak Vakili  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** statistical mechanics · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2606.00521v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

Curves are obtained from the relation Teﬀ/T = [γ2(1 + v2/(AF + 3))]1/4 and show the smooth interpolation between the ultra- relativistic (Teﬀ≃T(1 + 1</figcaption>

**Summary.** This paper re-examines the relativistic transformation of temperature using a rigorous thermodynamic approach based on the energy-momentum tensor. It demonstrates that temperature is not a Lorentz invariant scalar, showing that the effective temperature measured by a moving observer increases with velocity. The work unifies these concepts by introducing the inverse-temperature four-vector into a consistent relativistic framework.

**Why it may be interesting.** While focused on classical thermodynamics, the rigorous handling of observer-dependence and the covariant formulation of temperature are foundational concepts relevant to relativistic quantum field theory and non-equilibrium statistical mechanics.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper addresses the long-standing controversy regarding the relativistic transformation of temperature, as classical laws yield conflicting results.

**Main result.** The effective temperature inferred by a moving observer consistently increases with velocity, supporting the Ott-Eddington interpretation, and temperature is shown to be an observer-dependent quantity.

**Method.** The analysis uses a relativistic thermodynamic and statistical framework, starting from the energy-momentum tensor of an isotropic perfect fluid and defining an effective temperature from transformed energy densities.

**Model / system.** The study analyzes three paradigmatic systems: a photon gas (blackbody radiation), a relativistic ideal gas (Maxwell-Jüttner), and a relativistic electron gas (Fermi-Dirac statistics).

**Key observables.** Effective temperature ($T_{	ext{eff}}$), the ratio $T_{	ext{eff}}/T$, and the inverse-temperature four-vector ($eta_\mu$).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Observer velocity ($v$), system equation of state ($P/ho$), and the mass-to-temperature ratio ($m/T$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis assumes the standard energy-momentum tensor for a perfect fluid, neglecting dissipative effects like heat conduction or shear viscosity.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the smooth interpolation of the effective temperature ratio ($T_{	ext{eff}}/T$) versus velocity for various particle mass regimes, confirming the dependence on the equation of state.

**Paper structure.** The paper reviews conflicting classical transformation laws, develops the general formalism using the energy-momentum tensor, analyzes specific gases (photon, ideal, Fermi), and concludes by unifying the description via the inverse-temperature four-vector.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

The relativistic transformation of temperature has long remained controversial, with the classical laws of Planck-Einstein, Ott-Eddington-Moller and Landsberg yielding conflicting results. We reexamine this issue from a relativistic thermodynamic and statistical perspective, starting from the energy-momentum tensor of an isotropic system and defining the effective temperature Teff as that inferred by a moving observer from the transformed energy density. Analyses of a photon gas, a relativistic ideal gas and an electron gas show that Teff consistently increases with velocity, supporting the Ott-Eddington interpretation while depending on the system's equation of state. These results indicate that temperature is not a Lorentz-invariant scalar but an observer-dependent quantity. A consistent relativistic description emerges when temperature is related to the inverse-temperature four-vector beta, linking operational and invariant viewpoints within a unified thermodynamic framework.

</details>

**Summary.** This work revisits and significantly extends the Quantum-Guided Cluster Algorithm (QGCA) for solving Max-Cut problems on spin glasses. By incorporating structural information like next-nearest-neighbor correlations and developing an MCMC variant, the authors demonstrate substantial performance gains over classical methods. The findings highlight the power of using quantum-derived information to accelerate sampling and optimization in disordered quantum systems.

**Why it may be interesting.** The use of quantum-inspired correlations (like those from QAOA or SDP) to guide classical sampling algorithms for hard optimization problems is highly relevant to developing efficient simulation techniques for complex quantum many-body systems.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper addresses the NP-hard Max-Cut problem by improving classical optimization algorithms using quantum-inspired correlation information.

**Main result.** The Quantum-Guided Cluster Algorithm (QGCA), especially when incorporating Next-Nearest-Neighbor (NNN) information or advanced correlations like SDP, significantly outperforms standard Simulated Annealing and simpler cluster updates.

**Method.** The core method is an iterative cluster update algorithm guided by precomputed two-point correlations derived from various sources (SDP, QAOA, thermal MC).

**Model / system.** The model is the Max-Cut problem on undirected graphs, mathematically equivalent to finding the ground state of an Ising spin glass Hamiltonian. The analysis is performed on random regular graphs and tile-planted instances.

**Key observables.** Percentage of optimal solutions found, Time-to-Solution (TTS), autocorrelation functions of magnetization and energy, and approximation ratios.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Inverse temperature ($eta$), QAOA depth ($p=1, 3$), graph degree (e.g., 3, 27), and the inclusion of NNN interactions.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis often assumes the validity of the MCMC formulation for sampling the Boltzmann distribution and relies on the quality of the input correlation sources.

**Figures summary.** Figures compare optimal solution percentages and autocorrelation decay rates for various algorithms (QGCA vs. SA vs. SSF) across different graph degrees and correlation depths.

**Paper structure.** The paper builds from the basic correlation-guided CA, extends it to include NNN information, and culminates in developing and analyzing its extension into a full Markov-Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) sampling algorithm.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We study correlation-guided cluster algorithms for solving the Max-Cut problem that iteratively try to improve solutions by updating clusters of nodes. Building on the recently proposed quantum-guided cluster algorithm (QGCA) [arXiv:2508.10656], which leverages precomputed two-point correlations to guide collective updates, we extend the cluster construction by incorporating next-nearest-neighbor (NNN) information. We evaluate this extension across different correlation sources on random regular graphs and non-degenerate tile-planted instances. Notably, we observe particularly strong performance on non-degenerate instances and provide a scaling analysis for this class. Finally, we outline an extension toward a correlation-guided Markov-chain Monte Carlo algorithm, whose detailed analysis remains an open direction for future work.

</details>

<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 4.</b> FIG. 4. Numerical calculation results of the smooth velocity shuttling protocol. From top to bottom, the changes of the signal wave s(t), the applied gate voltage Vn(t), the shuttling velocity v(t), and the quantum dot size ax(t) against the normalized time t/T are shown. It can be confirmed that the acceleration during transport is modulated by the velocity shaping parameter β, and the high-frequency components are suppressed.</figcaption>
</figure>

Random spatial variations of the valley coupling are shown for various disorder strengths characterized by the ratio |∆0|/σ∆. A smaller ratio corresponds to a stronger influence of disorder induced by alloy fluctuations.</figcaption>
