**Summary.** This paper introduces a novel, analytically derived method—smooth velocity shuttling—to suppress detrimental valley excitations in silicon quantum dots. By shaping the gate voltage using frequency modulation, the authors suppress high-frequency components in the shuttling velocity spectrum. This technique significantly improves spin coherence and fidelity, offering a robust pathway for large-scale silicon quantum computing architectures.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work provides a concrete, control-level engineering solution for mitigating decoherence sources (valley mixing) in solid-state qubits, which is highly relevant to building scalable quantum processors.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The primary challenge is suppressing non-adiabatic valley excitations during electron shuttling in silicon quantum dots, as these transitions cause spin dephasing and limit qubit fidelity.

**Main result.** The authors propose a smooth velocity shuttling protocol, mathematically equivalent to window function design, which significantly reduces spin infidelity in the moderate-to-low disorder regime.

**Method.** They derive an analytical guideline by mapping the time-domain velocity profile design onto signal processing window function theory, implementing this via frequency-modulated gate voltages.

**Model / system.** The system is modeled using Si/SiGe quantum dots hosting spin qubits, analyzed via an effective Hamiltonian that includes valley and spin degrees of freedom. The dynamics are governed by the time-dependent shuttling velocity $v(t)$.

**Key observables.** Average spin infidelity, spin purity $P_s(t)$, and the probability of valley excitation $p_v$.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Disorder strength ratio $|\Delta_0|/\sigma_\Delta$, the smoothness parameter $eta$, and the deterministic valley coupling $|\Delta_0|$.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis relies on the perturbation approximation for valley transitions and assumes the applicability of the effective mass approximation for the potential landscape.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the comparison of velocity waveforms (conventional vs. smooth/Tukey-window based) and show the time evolution of spin impurity and infidelity, demonstrating fidelity maintenance with smooth control.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem (valley excitation during shuttling), derives the connection to signal processing window functions, proposes the FM gate voltage implementation, and validates the method through numerical simulations showing fidelity improvement.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Coherent electron shuttling is a key requirement for realizing scalable silicon quantum computing architectures. However, in silicon qubits, the existence of nearly degenerate conduction-band valleys poses a significant challenge because non-adiabatic transitions to excited valley states cause spin dephasing via spin-valley mixing. In this paper, we propose a smooth velocity shuttling protocol to suppress these valley excitations. By mapping the time-domain design of the shuttling velocity profile onto the design problem of window functions in signal processing, we establish an analytical and intuitive design guideline that does not require computationally expensive numerical optimization. We demonstrate that the high-frequency sidelobes of the shuttling velocity spectrum can be effectively suppressed by applying a frequency-modulated gate voltage based on the Tukey window. Through numerical simulations incorporating realistic spatial randomness of the valley landscape, we show that the proposed smooth velocity control significantly reduces the average spin infidelity in the moderate-to-low disorder regime ($|Δ_0|/σ_Δ\simeq \mathcal{O}(1)$). Furthermore, we clarify that in devices designed with a large deterministic valley coupling $|Δ_0|$, combining it with this smoothing technique improves robustness against valley disorder. Our results underscore that this simple, control-level velocity shaping provides a robust pathway toward high-fidelity spin transport in large-scale silicon quantum processors.

</details>

**Summary.** This paper analyzes the utility of Semidefinite Programming (SDP) for studying quantum spin chains, testing its ability to calculate ground state energies and correlation functions for models like the Ising and Potts chains. While SDP provides exact results for free fermion systems, its performance degrades for interacting models, highlighting the need for incorporating deeper physical constraints to make the method scalable and accurate.

**Why it may be interesting.** The rigorous application of convex optimization (SDP) to quantum many-body systems provides a powerful, albeit computationally demanding, theoretical tool for bounding and calculating quantum observables, especially useful for understanding critical phenomena.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper investigates the effectiveness and limitations of using Semidefinite Programming (SDP) methods to analyze spin chain Hamiltonians, aiming to determine when and why the method successfully extracts physical properties.

**Main result.** SDP successfully finds the exact ground state energy and two-point functions for free fermion systems, but its ability to estimate higher-order correlators degrades significantly for interacting models, and the method suffers from poor scaling with lattice volume.

**Method.** The core method is formulating the problem as a convex optimization problem (minimizing the expectation value of the Hamiltonian) subject to positivity constraints on the density matrix, which is solved via SDP.

**Model / system.** The study focuses on spin chain Hamiltonians, specifically applying the technique to the transverse-field Ising model and the 3-state Potts model, and analyzing the behavior in the free fermion limit.

**Key observables.** Ground state energy ($E_0/N$), first excited state energy ($E_1/N$), ground state correlators, and conformal data (central charge, critical exponents) at criticality.

**Important parameters / regimes.** The coupling constant ($\mu$), lattice volume ($N$), and the specific symmetry constraints (charge, Virasoro generators) used in the SDP formulation.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The method relies on the ability to express the problem as a convex optimization problem, and its success is strongly tied to the system being exactly solvable (free fermions).

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the convergence of SDP results for $E_0/N$ and $E_1/N$ for the Ising and Potts models, and show the construction and consistency checks using lattice Virasoro generators.

**Paper structure.** The paper systematically introduces the SDP formulation, applies it to specific models (Ising, Potts), and then explores advanced diagnostics by incorporating symmetry constraints (charge, Virasoro) and analyzing the free fermion limit.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We study semidefinite programming (SDP) methods to analyze spin chain Hamiltonians. We examine the ground state energy, the first excited charged states and ground state correlators in two simple models: the Ising model in a transverse magnetic field and the closely related 3-state Potts model. Our goal is to understand precisely what the SDP program is doing and when it works well, why it does so. We focus on the following novel ingredients: using charge constraints to obtain excited states and to see if additional constraints from integrable models are effective at improving the method.   At criticality we also explore to what extent we can use approximate Virasoro correlators to extract conformal data: the central charge and some critical exponents of charged states. We also use these to identify the location of the phase transition. In the special case where the system is made of free fermions we prove that the SDP finds the exact energy of the ground state and produces the correct two point functions of the fermions. Away from free fermion theories, the SDP gets progressively worse at estimating data beyond the value of the ground state energy (like correlation functions), although it qualitatively matches these. In order to be effective, the SDP seems to run into scaling issues where the amount of input needed scales poorly with the lattice volume.

</details>

**Summary.** This paper introduces a NISQ-aware hybrid framework to solve large combinatorial optimization problems by breaking them down into manageable subproblems. It uses a quantum genetic algorithm enhanced by periodic amplitude amplification for local search, followed by classical refinement. The method proves scalable and robust, offering a practical paradigm for quantum advantage in the near-term quantum era.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work directly addresses the practical limitations of current quantum hardware by proposing a structured, resource-aware methodology for applying quantum computation to classically hard optimization problems, which is a key area for quantum information scientists.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The core challenge is achieving scalable combinatorial optimization for NP-hard problems like TSP under the severe resource constraints of Noisy Intermediate-Scale Quantum (NISQ) hardware.

**Main result.** The proposed hybrid framework consistently outperforms classical and quantum-inspired baselines, demonstrating that scalability is achieved through structured decomposition rather than increasing quantum circuit complexity.

**Method.** It employs a three-stage pipeline: clustering-based decomposition to create resource-bounded subproblems, followed by a Quantum Genetic Algorithm (QGA) for probabilistic local search, and concluding with a classical refinement stage for global consistency.

**Model / system.** The model is a hybrid quantum-classical optimization framework designed for NISQ devices. It operates by evolving a probabilistic distribution over the solution space, utilizing parameterized quantum states and localized amplitude amplification.

**Key observables.** Solution quality (minimum path length), scalability (performance gain vs. problem size), and robustness under simulated noise.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Subproblem size limit (M), amplitude amplification interval (Delta G), and noise levels (up to 10%).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The framework assumes that large problems can be effectively decomposed into smaller, qubit-compatible subproblems, and that periodic, localized quantum enhancements are sufficient for convergence.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the three-stage workflow, localized Grover amplitude amplification circuits, and comparative performance ratios/scaling trends against various baselines.

**Paper structure.** The paper details the necessity of the hybrid approach due to NISQ limitations, introduces the decomposition $ightarrow$ QGA/Amplitude Amplification $ightarrow$ Classical Refinement pipeline, validates performance on benchmark datasets, and analyzes robustness via ablation studies.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Scalable combinatorial optimization under resource-constrained quantum hardware remains a fundamental challenge in the Noisy Intermediate-Scale Quantum (NISQ) era, due to the mismatch between exponentially growing solution spaces and limited quantum computational capacity. In this work, we propose a NISQ-aware hybrid quantum-classical optimization framework that reformulates large-scale combinatorial optimization as a resource-bounded distribution evolution process. Instead of directly optimizing individual solutions, the proposed framework operates on a probabilistic representation of the solution space, enabling efficient exploration under hardware constraints. Specifically, large problem instances are decomposed into qubit-compatible subproblems via clustering-based decomposition, ensuring resource-bounded optimization. Within each subproblem, a quantum genetic algorithm evolves the solution distribution, while periodically embedded amplitude amplification acts as a controlled quantum enhancement mechanism that accelerates convergence without increasing circuit depth. A classical refinement stage ensures global solution consistency. Extensive experiments on benchmark and synthetic datasets demonstrate that the proposed framework consistently outperforms classical and quantum-inspired baselines, with performance gains that become more pronounced as problem scale increases. This scale-dependent behavior indicates that scalability is achieved through structured decomposition rather than increased quantum complexity. Noise simulations further confirm robustness under realistic NISQ conditions, and ablation studies validate that both quantum evolutionary search and amplitude amplification contribute significantly to performance improvements.

</details>

**Summary.** This paper presents a novel, energy-efficient neuromorphic system for camouflage object detection by building an Optical Spiking Neuron (OSHN) based on a 2D phototransistor. The OSHN successfully mimics complex retinal functions, including Center-Surround Receptive Fields and visual adaptation. By integrating these bio-realistic stages into an SNN, the system achieves state-of-the-art accuracy while operating with ultra-low power consumption.

**Why it may be interesting.** The work represents a strong convergence of condensed matter physics (2D materials, phototransistors) and computational neuroscience (SNNs, retinal modeling), offering a hardware-level approach to bio-inspired computation.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Developing energy-efficient, low-latency, and highly accurate vision systems capable of detecting camouflaged objects by mimicking complex retinal preprocessing functions.

**Main result.** A Center-Surround Receptive Field (CSRF)-augmented Spiking Neural Network (SNN) achieved significant accuracy improvements (up to 28.4%) over conventional methods on camouflage detection benchmarks.

**Method.** The system integrates a Hodgkin-Huxley-based Optical Spiking Neuron (OSHN) built from a 2D phototransistor (2DSpT) to emulate retinal pathways like CSRF, visual adaptation, and color opponency.

**Model / system.** The core is the OSHN, which uses a 2DSpT operating in the subthreshold regime to achieve low energy per spike (e.g., 2 pJ at 480 nm). The overall framework models the photoreceptor $	o$ Bipolar Cell $	o$ RGC pathway.

**Key observables.** Spike rate (0-2 kHz), Energy per Spike (0.9 pJ - 24.5 pJ), Classification Accuracy (up to 95.9% on synthetic data), and Center-Surround spiking patterns.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Wavelengths (480 nm, 800 nm), Time constants ($	au_s = 30 	ext{ ms}$, $	au_p = 27 	ext{ ms}$), and specific gate biases for the 2DSpT.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The system assumes that CSRF processing and visual adaptation are key mechanisms for enhancing contrast sensitivity, which is crucial for distinguishing camouflaged objects.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the 2DSpT structure, the OSHN action potential under varying light conditions, the CSRF implementation using DoG filters, and the final performance comparison of the SNN on COD datasets.

**Paper structure.** The paper progresses from detailing the physical device (2DSpT) and its phototransduction mechanism, to modeling the OSHN circuit dynamics (HH model), then implementing multi-stage biomimetic preprocessing (CSRF, VA), and finally applying the full framework to achieve high performance in camouflaged object detection.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Advanced vision systems require retinomorphic, energy-efficient spike-based preprocessing of dynamic visual scenes. Here, we demonstrate multiple retinal preprocessing functionalities by leveraging a Hodgkin-Huxley-based optical spiking neuron (OSHN) that incorporates a two-dimensional anti-ambipolar phototransistor operated in the subthreshold regime to minimize power consumption. OSHN exhibits wavelength- and intensity-sensitive spike encoding with energy consumption per spike of 0.9 pJ under dark, 2 pJ at 480 nm (mid wavelength, M), and 24.5 pJ at 800 nm (long wavelength, L). The low (biological)-to-high spiking rate (0 - 2 kHz) with substantially faster response times (4.2 $μ$s - 1.25 ms) than the human retina (30 ms - 60 ms), reveal OSHN's fast decision-making capability. OSHN facilitates concurrent spectral-spatial processing by emulating retinal antagonistic center-surround receptive fields (CSRFs) at a single wavelength (480 nm or 800 nm) with varying intensities, visual adaptation (at 480 nm) to prevent system saturation, and L-M cone opponency in midget ganglion cells. Finally, a CSRF-augmented spiking neural network (SNN) has been developed for camouflaged object detection, achieving 4.4%, 10.4%, and 28.4% improvements in accuracy over conventional SNN on FMNIST, COD10K, and synthetic camouflaged datasets, outperforming existing photoactive spiking architectures while enabling event-driven intelligent edge vision systems.

</details>

**Authors:** Yanjie Li, Sihong Shao

**Summary.** This paper introduces the Asymptotic-Recovery Fourier Spectral Method (AR-FSM) to solve the eigenvalue problem for the Schrödinger equation with singular potentials. It rigorously proves that AR-FSM significantly improves the convergence rates compared to standard Fourier Spectral Methods. This advancement is crucial for accurately simulating quantum systems governed by potentials with point singularities.

**Why it may be interesting.** The development of AR-FSM provides a powerful, theoretically grounded tool for simulating quantum systems with singular interactions, pushing the limits of accuracy in spectral methods for condensed matter physics.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Developing highly accurate numerical techniques to compute the eigenpairs of the Schrödinger equation when the potential contains point singularities.

**Main result.** The proposed Asymptotic-Recovery Fourier Spectral Method (AR-FSM) significantly enhances convergence rates over standard Fourier Spectral Methods (FSM), achieving superior convergence orders for both eigenvalues and eigenfunctions.

**Method.** The core technique involves combining the Fourier Spectral Method with an Asymptotic-Recovery (AR) post-processing step that exploits the known asymptotic behavior of eigenfunctions near singular points.

**Model / system.** The study focuses on the $d$-dimensional Schrödinger operator, $H = -
abla^2 + V(x)$, where the potential $V$ belongs to a Sobolev space $H^s$ and can include singular potentials like the 3D Coulomb potential or the 1D Dirac-delta potential.

**Key observables.** Eigenvalues ($\lambda_n$) and eigenfunctions ($\psi_n$) of the Schrödinger operator; convergence error ($	ext{EigErr}, 	ext{H1Err}$).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Potential regularity index ($s$), spatial dimension ($d$), and the parameter $b$ derived from $s$ and $d$.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis assumes the potential $V \in H^s$ with $s > \max\{d/2 - 2, -1\}$ and relies on developing a rigorous framework for point singularities.

**Figures summary.** Figures compare numerical convergence rates for 1D (Dirac-delta) and 3D (Coulomb) potentials, demonstrating the superior convergence of AR-FSM over standard FSM.

**Paper structure.** The paper builds from establishing the FSM convergence rates, deriving the super-convergence phenomenon, and then introducing the AR technique. The structure involves developing asymptotic expansions, proving error bounds using spectral gap analysis, and finally validating the enhanced convergence rates for AR-FSM.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

This paper studies the Fourier spectral method (FSM) for the Schrödinger equation with singular potentials $V \in H^{s}$, where $s > \max\{d/2-2,-1\}$ and $d$ denotes the spatial dimension. This setting includes a broad class of singular potentials, such as the 3D Coulomb potential and the 1D Dirac-delta potential. First, we combine the Feshbach-Schur map with a refined perturbation argument to derive sharp convergence orders for FSM, yielding order $2s+2$ for eigenvalues and order $s+1$ for eigenfunctions in the $H^1$ norm. More importantly, the $H^1$ error with respect to the projected eigenfunction converges with a higher order $s+1+b$, where $b=\min\{s+2-d/2-\varepsilon,\; s+1,\; 2\}>0$ for arbitrarily small $\varepsilon>0$, revealing a super-convergence phenomenon. Second, in the presence of potentials with isolated point singularities, we develop an asymptotic-recovery (AR) technique to post-process the FSM solutions. The resulting method, dubbed AR-FSM, fully exploits the super-convergence property and achieves convergence orders $2s+2+2b$ for eigenvalues and $s+1+b$ for eigenfunctions in the $H^1$ norm, while the AR post-processing requires only a computational cost that is linear in the number of FSM degrees of freedom. The analysis introduces a rigorous definition of point singularities and develops a foundational framework for their study. It further establishes an asymptotic expansion of eigenfunctions consisting of a regular component in $H^{s+4}$ together with $d+1$ asymptotic functions associated with each singular point. Numerical experiments confirm the sharpness of these theoretical bounds.

</details>

### [Branch-Aware Quantum Constant Propagation for Dynamic Quantum Circuits](http://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02018v1)

**Authors:** Innocenzo Fulginiti, Yanbin Chen  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2606.02018v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper introduces Branch-Aware Quantum Constant Propagation (BQCP), a compile-time optimization technique for dynamic quantum circuits. BQCP extends standard constant propagation by explicitly tracking the classical outcomes and resulting quantum states across all possible execution branches. This allows for precise, path-sensitive simplification, leading to significantly optimized circuits compared to methods that ignore classical control flow.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work directly addresses the practical compilation challenges for near-term quantum hardware by providing a rigorous, state-of-the-art optimization pass that accounts for the complex classical control flow inherent in modern quantum algorithms.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Improving the efficiency and reliability of quantum circuits running on noisy hardware by performing compile-time optimizations.

**Main result.** The proposed Branch-Aware Quantum Constant Propagation (BQCP) method consistently achieves larger circuit reductions than existing passes, especially on dynamic circuits involving mid-circuit measurements.

**Method.** BQCP extends Quantum Constant Propagation (QCP) by tracking the classical information and corresponding post-measurement quantum states across all possible execution branches induced by conditional logic.

**Model / system.** The analysis targets dynamic quantum circuits operating on an $n$-qubit register $Q$ and a classical register $C$. The state is tracked as an abstract state $B$, which is a finite disjunction of abstract branches, combining classical and quantum information.

**Key observables.** Circuit simplification (reduction in the number of unitary gates, measurements, resets, and conditionals) and the resulting semantically equivalent, optimized circuit.

**Important parameters / regimes.** The analysis is controlled by two bounds: $n_{	ext{max}}$ (for quantum state representation size) and $b_{	ext{max}}$ (for the total number of tracked execution branches).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis relies on bounding the number of branches ($b_{	ext{max}}$) and the quantum state representation size ($n_{	ext{max}}$) to maintain polynomial time complexity, accepting potential over-approximation when these bounds are hit.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the comparison between an original dynamic circuit and the semantically equivalent, simplified circuit derived using BQCP, demonstrating the removal of redundant operations.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of optimizing dynamic circuits, details the BQCP framework by defining abstract state representations for classical and quantum components, and systematically defines transfer functions for measurements, resets, and conditionals to enable sound, path-sensitive simplification rules.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Compile-time optimization is important for improving the efficiency and reliability of quantum circuits on current noisy hardware. While many existing methods simplify circuits using structural patterns or quantum-state information, most of them target only unitary circuits and do not support dynamic circuits with mid-circuit measurements and classical feedforward. In this work, we present Branch-Aware Quantum Constant Propagation (BQCP), a compile-time analysis for dynamic circuits. BQCP extends Quantum Constant Propagation (QCP) by tracking the classical information produced by mid-circuit measurements together with the corresponding post-measurement quantum states across different execution branches. This enables path-sensitive reasoning inside conditional blocks and more precise information propagation than QCP. To keep the analysis scalable, we bound both the size of the quantum-state representation and the number of tracked branches. Using the information inferred by the analysis, we apply semantics-preserving simplifications to circuit operations. We prove the soundness of both the analysis and the simplifications. Experimental results on both application-driven and synthetic benchmarks show that, on dynamic circuits, our method consistently achieves larger reductions than other existing passes including QCP.

</details>

(g) Evolution of selected orbital projected occupations (in electrons) under electron and hole doping. Note that main changes take place in the interstitial, La-d and Ni-dx2−y2 orbitals. While the La-d and Ni-dx2−y2 occupations increase(decrease) upon electron(hole) doping, the interstitial occupation decreases in both doping regimes.</figcaption>

(c) Projected local spin- polarized DOS for the undoped C-type AFM state at U = 5, J = 0.8 eV. The DOS for a G-type state is very similar (as shown in Appendix C).</figcaption>
</figure>

</div>

**Summary.** This theoretical study investigates the electronic structure and magnetism of infinite-layer nickelates across both electron- and hole-doped regimes. Using DFT+DMFT, the authors highlight a significant asymmetry, noting that electron doping preserves antiferromagnetism while hole doping suppresses it. The work suggests that the $	ext{Ni-}d_{x^2-y^2}$ orbital governs low-energy physics in both regimes.

**Why it may be interesting.** The focus on strongly correlated transition metal oxides, particularly the comparison between electron and hole doping asymmetries, is central to understanding high-temperature superconductivity mechanisms, a key area in condensed matter theory.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The study re-examines the electronic structure, magnetism, and correlations in infinite-layer nickelates ($	ext{RNiO}_2$) by extending the investigation from the well-studied hole-doped regime to the less explored electron-doped regime.

**Main result.** The research reveals a striking asymmetry between electron and hole doping, particularly in the self-doping of the $	ext{Ni-}d_{x^2-y^2}$ band and the magnetic ground state, which remains antiferromagnetic upon electron doping.

**Method.** The authors employed a combination of Density-Functional Theory (DFT) and Dynamical Mean-Field Theory (DMFT) calculations, utilizing supercell and Variable Cell Approximation (VCA) methods.

**Model / system.** The physical system is the infinite-layer nickelate $	ext{RNiO}_2$. The calculations model the electronic structure and correlation effects arising from the $	ext{Ni-}d_{x^2-y^2}$ orbital, considering doping variations.

**Key observables.** Electronic structure evolution, magnetic response (AFM vs. FM), orbital occupations, self-doping effect, and orbital-resolved mass enhancement ($m^*/m$).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Doping level (hole vs. electron), Hubbard U parameter, and the nature of the rare earth $R(5d)$ states.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis suggests that despite the complexity, the low-energy correlation physics is dominated by the $	ext{Ni-}d_{x^2-y^2}$ orbital, potentially allowing for a single-band description.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate band structures, Fermi surface evolution at specific momenta, energy differences between magnetic states ($	ext{AFM-C}$, $	ext{AFM-G}$, $	ext{FM}$), and orbital-resolved spectral functions and self-energies across doping levels.

**Paper structure.** The paper systematically compares the electronic and magnetic properties under hole and electron doping, detailing the observed asymmetries in band filling, magnetic stability, and correlation strength using advanced theoretical techniques.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

The observation of superconductivity in undoped infinite-layer nickelates $R$NiO$_2$ ($R$ = rare earth) challenges our current understanding and calls for a re-examination of the underlying electronic structure of this family of materials. In this context, it is particularly important to extend the investigation of $R$NiO$_2$ compounds from the intensively studied hole-doped regime to the almost unexplored electron-doped one. Here, we use a combination of density-functional theory and dynamical mean-field theory to study the evolution of the electronic structure of infinite-layer nickelates in these two doping regimes. We find a striking asymmetry in the self-doping of the Ni-$d_{x^2-y^2}$ band due to the $R(5d)$ states: while this effect is strongly suppressed upon hole doping, electron doping instead leads to an increase in the size of the $R(5d)$ electron pockets, but without effectively hole-doping the Ni-$d_{x^2-y^2}$ band. This asymmetry has an important impact on the magnetic response as antiferromagnetism is rapidly suppressed upon hole doping, whereas it remains the ground state upon electron doping. Despite these differences, electronic correlations on both sides of the phase diagram are dominated by the Ni $d_{x^2-y^2}$ orbital, suggesting that a single-band description may be appropriate for infinite-layer nickelates in both the electron- and hole-doped regimes.

</details>

### [Evolutionary Discovery of Bivariate Bicycle Codes with LLM-Guided Search](http://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02418v1)

**Authors:** Juan Cruz-Benito, Andrew W. Cross, David Kremer, Ismael Faro  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2606.02418v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>
<div class="paper-figures-scroll">
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2606.02418_figures/2606.02418_fig1.jpg" alt="Figure 1">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> FIG. 1. Evolution pipeline. An LLM proposes diffs to a seed generator ansatz G(ℓ, m); each mutant is screened by a multi- stage cascade (Stage 1: k-only, Stage 2: BP-OSD distance) and inserted into a MAP-Elites archive that feeds back con- text to the LLM. Campaigns 4–5 add in-loop MILP exact dis- tance (Stage 3, added in response to BP-OSD overestimation discovered in Campaigns 1–3; see Sec. VI E); Campaigns 1–3 use post-hoc MILP (dashed bypass). Post-campaign verifica- tion (MILP distance computation →BLISS deduplication → Tanner-graph decomposability →LC equivalence for PBB) ensures all reported codes are genuine.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2606.02418_figures/2606.02418_fig2.jpg" alt="Figure 2">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> FIG. 2. Rate–distance landscape across all five campaigns. Left panels: k vs. d with Pareto frontier (dashed). Right panels: FOM = kd2/n vs. block length. Top: 97 CSS codes (weight-6 and weight-8); bottom: 368 non-CSS PBB codes. Red markers: Bravyi et al. [2] baselines; dotted line: FOM = 12 (gross code). The [[288, 24, 12]] (d = 12, k = 24) is a direct sum of two gross codes (Sec. VI A); the highest-k indecomposable code at d = 12 is [[288, 16, 12]] (k = 16). Among weight-6 codes, k &gt; 24 implies d ≤4; weight-8 codes reach k = 50 at d = 8; non-CSS codes occupy the same envelope.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2606.02418_figures/2606.02418_fig3.jpg" alt="Figure 3">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 3.</b> FIG. 4. Distance revisions across 154 Campaigns 1–3 poly- nomial representations. 147 (95.5%) had bounds tightened under the 150k-trial multi-decoder protocol, with mean reduc- tion ∼8.4 points. Even tightened bounds remain gross over- estimates: MILP reveals dtrue = 2 for codes with dBP ≤24.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2606.02418_figures/2606.02418_fig4.jpg" alt="Figure 4">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 4.</b> FIG. 3. Block logical error rate vs. physical bit-flip rate p for six CSS and four non-CSS codes (100,000 shots per rate, BP-OSD with OSD-CS order 7). Dashed line: LER = p. Weight-6 codes with d ≥12 achieve LER &lt; p through p ≈5%; the PBB [[360, 12, ≤24]] and [[72, 4, 8]] achieve LER &lt; 1% at p = 8%. The weight-8 [[288, 50, 8]] (d = 8, k = 50) crosses LER = p between p = 2% and 3%, illustrating the rate– distance tradeoff.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2606.02418_figures/2606.02418_fig5.jpg" alt="Figure 5">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 5.</b> FIG. 1. Per-batch distance distributions under the 150,000-trial multi-decoder protocol. Red dashed lines show the global minimum across all 30 batches. The gross code (k = 12) is perfectly stable; all four higher-k codes show inter-batch variance. The three evolution-highlighted codes (dMILP = 2, 4, 6) show dramatic overestimation; even the [[288, 24, 12]] (dMILP = 12; a direct sum of two gross codes) has OSD0 medians ≈2.4× the true distance, with only 2 of 30 batches finding the exact value.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper introduces an LLM-guided evolutionary search method to discover new and high-performing quantum LDPC codes, specifically Bivariate Bicycle variants. By combining generative AI with rigorous mathematical verification (like MILP), the authors efficiently explore massive code design spaces. The results confirm that this AI-assisted approach is a powerful, practical tool for advancing quantum error correction theory.

**Why it may be interesting.** The integration of large language models into the highly structured, combinatorial search space of quantum error correction codes represents a novel computational paradigm for theoretical quantum information science.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The core challenge is the systematic and reliable discovery of high-performing quantum Low-Density Parity-Check (qLDPC) codes within vast algebraic design spaces.

**Main result.** The LLM-guided evolutionary workflow successfully identified 465 distinct candidate codes, including new representatives and demonstrating that the method is a practical tool for structured quantum-code discovery.

**Method.** The authors employed an LLM-guided evolutionary workflow where language models mutate Python programs generating code ansätze, followed by a rigorous, multi-stage validation pipeline including MILP and graph theory checks.

**Model / system.** The focus is on Bivariate Bicycle (BB) and perturbed Bivariate Bicycle (PBB) quantum error-correcting codes, which are defined by polynomial structures over finite fields, suitable for near-term fault-tolerant quantum computing.

**Key observables.** Code parameters ($n, k, d$), Figure of Merit (FOM = $kd^2/n$), and error rates (LER, $p_L$) under various noise models.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Block length $n \le 360$, noise probability $p$ (e.g., up to 8%), and the computational cost ($\sim 140$ hours, $\sim$US\$400).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The reported LERs are lower bounds on the optimal depolarizing threshold, and the comparison between CSS and PBB codes is noted to be decoder-dependent.

**Figures summary.** The figures summarize code parameters, performance metrics (FOM, LER), and comparison of simulation bounds versus exact MILP distances across various noise channels.

**Paper structure.** The paper establishes the need for better code discovery methods, introduces the LLM-guided evolutionary framework, details the staged validation pipeline (from rank computation to MILP), and concludes with performance comparisons under noise models.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Quantum LDPC code discovery requires searching large algebraic design spaces while reliably certifying the parameters and equivalence classes of any candidates found. We introduce an LLM-guided evolutionary workflow in which language models mutate Python programs that generate bivariate-bicycle and perturbed bivariate-bicycle code ansätze. Across five campaigns, the system performed approximately 1{,}650 evolutionary iterations, screened about $2 \times 10^5$ candidate codes, and required ${\sim}140$ hours of computation and ${\sim}$US\$400 in LLM inference cost. Candidate codes are evaluated through a staged validation pipeline combining $\mathrm{GF}(2)$ rank computation, distance estimation and certification, mixed-integer linear programming, BLISS Tanner-graph deduplication, decomposability analysis, and local-Clifford equivalence checks. At block length $n \leq 360$, the workflow identifies 465 distinct candidate codes: 97 CSS bivariate-bicycle codes and 368 non-CSS perturbed variants. The CSS search recovers known high-performing codes and finds new finite-length representatives, including an indecomposable [[288,16,12]] code and higher-weight codes with up to $k = 50$ at distance $d = 8$. The non-CSS search produces perturbed codes matching the gross-code figure of merit at [[144,12,12]], along with additional high-distance candidates reported as certified values or upper bounds according to MILP status. Overall, these results show that LLM-guided program evolution can serve as a practical tool for structured quantum-code discovery when paired with independent evaluation.

</details>

### [Hidden $\mathfrak{u}(2,1)$ symmetry and Jordan chains in a resonant ghostly three-dimensional model](http://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02290v1)

**Authors:** Andreas Fring, Ian Marquette  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2606.02290v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

</figure>

</figure>

</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This theoretical paper studies a highly resonant, higher-derivative physical model, revealing a hidden $\mathfrak{u}(2,1)$ symmetry algebra alongside a multi-Hamiltonian structure. Despite the rich algebraic structure, the authors demonstrate that the system is genuinely singular, as the expected positive-definite conserved Hamiltonian fails to materialize.

**Why it may be interesting.** The deep connection between hidden symmetries ($\mathfrak{u}(2,1)$), non-standard integrability structures (multi-Hamiltonian systems), and the breakdown of standard quantization techniques (positive-definite Hamiltonians) is highly relevant for understanding complex, non-linear quantum dynamics.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper investigates the hidden algebraic structure ($\mathfrak{u}(2,1)$ symmetry) and the complex Jordan chain behavior in a highly degenerate, resonant, higher-derivative physical system.

**Main result.** The system exhibits a hidden $\mathfrak{u}(2,1)$ symmetry and a multi-Hamiltonian structure, but the analysis shows that the natural higher-order conserved charge is reducible, and the positive-definite Hamiltonian mechanism fails due to the resonance.

**Method.** The authors analyze the classical phase-space flow using matrix diagonalization (Jordan chains) and construct the quantum symmetry algebra using intertwining operators, followed by a multi-Hamiltonian analysis.

**Model / system.** The model is a three-dimensional ghostly Hamiltonian realization of the fully degenerate resonant sixth-order Pais-Uhlenbeck (PU) oscillator, governed by equations of motion derived from a sixth-order differential equation.

**Key observables.** Jordan chains (classical and quantum), hidden $\mathfrak{u}(2,1)$-algebra, tri-Hamiltonian family ($H_1, H_2, H_3$), and the failure of positive-definite conserved charges.

**Important parameters / regimes.** The resonance condition leading to the sixth-order PU equation; the structure constants defining the $\mathfrak{u}(2,1)$ algebra.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis assumes the existence of a hidden $\mathfrak{u}(2,1)$ symmetry and relies on the specific parameter choice leading to the fully degenerate resonant point.

**Figures summary.** Not specified.

**Paper structure.** The paper proceeds by analyzing the classical flow and its non-diagonalizable Jordan structure, then constructs the hidden $\mathfrak{u}(2,1)$ algebra and its quantum representations, followed by deriving the tri-Hamiltonian formulation and concluding with the analysis of higher-order integrals and the failure of the positive-definite mechanism.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We investigate a three-dimensional ghostly Hamiltonian realisation of the fully degenerate resonant sixth-order Pais-Uhlenbeck oscillator. On the classical level, the phase-space flow is non-diagonalisable and decomposes into two complex-conjugate Jordan chains of length three, explaining the appearance of oscillatory solutions with secular terms.   Upon quantisation, we construct intertwining operators whose quadratic combinations generate a hidden spectrum-generating $\mathfrak{u}(2,1)$-algebra. The associated descendant spaces are finite-dimensional invariant subspaces carrying non-trivial Jordan structure. Although these spaces admit a natural decomposition into irreducible modules of a distinguished $\mathfrak{sl}_2$-subalgebra, this decomposition does not in general coincide with the Jordan decomposition of the Hamiltonian.   We further derive a tri-Hamiltonian formulation from Lie point symmetries of the classical flow and show that the corresponding Hamiltonians are naturally encoded by the same hidden algebra. Nevertheless, unlike in the non-resonant case, no positive-definite linear combination of them generates the same dynamics. Finally, we analyse the common centraliser of the tri-Hamiltonian family in $U(\mathfrak u(2,1))$, showing that the natural higher-order candidate $Q$ is reducible and yields no independent classical or quantum integral.   The model thus provides a resonant higher-derivative system in which hidden $\mathfrak{u}(2,1)$ symmetry, classical and quantum Jordan structures, and multi-Hamiltonian geometry coexist.

</details>

</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2606.02531_figures/2606.02531_page6.jpg" alt="Figure 5">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 5.</b> Low-resolution page preview, page 6</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper develops a highly generalized framework for quantum error correction by defining 'Hybrid Clifford Codes' using projective representation theory and operator algebras. It extends fundamental theorems to characterize these new codes and derives necessary and sufficient conditions for their error correctability. This advances the theoretical understanding of fault-tolerant quantum computation beyond standard stabilizer codes.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work provides a deep mathematical generalization of QEC theory, connecting it to abstract algebra (representation theory) in a way that could inspire new code constructions beyond standard Pauli stabilizer models.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper aims to formulate and prove error correction theorems for a generalized class of codes called "Hybrid Clifford Codes," integrating concepts from projective representation theory and operator algebra quantum error correction.

**Main result.** The core result is the extension of the fundamental quantum error correction theorem to these new hybrid, projective representation-based codes, providing explicit formulas for code space projections and correctability conditions.

**Method.** The methodology relies heavily on advanced mathematical tools, including projective representation theory (analyzing cocycles and intertwiners), operator algebras, and group theory (induction/restriction of representations).

**Model / system.** The physical system is a quantum error correction code defined on a finite-dimensional Hilbert space $H$, utilizing a projective error model $(G, \pi)$ derived from a finite group $G$. The codes are generalizations of stabilizer and Clifford codes.

**Key observables.** Code distance $d(G, \pi, L, G, T_0)$, the orthogonal projection operators $P_C$ and $P_t$, and the group structure of stabilizers $S(G, \pi)(C)$ and logical operators $L(G, \pi)(C)$.

**Important parameters / regimes.** The finite group $G$, the projective representation $\pi$, the associated cocycle function $\sigma$, and the structure of the normal subgroup $L$.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The derivation of projections relies on $L$ being a normal subgroup of $G$, and the final correctability theorem depends on specific commutation relations involving the error operators.

**Figures summary.** Not specified in the notes, but the text describes deriving explicit formulas for projection operators ($P_C, P_t$) and establishing a necessary and sufficient condition (Theorem 2) for error correctability.

**Paper structure.** The paper systematically reviews the mathematical preliminaries (projective representations, character theory), introduces the generalized code types (Clifford subspace, subsystem, hybrid), derives the projection formulas, and culminates in proving the generalized error correction theorem.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Clifford codes are a natural generalization of quantum stabilizer codes based primarily on representation theory. This class of codes has previously been extended to the setting of quantum subsystem codes. We formulate a two-fold generalization of Clifford codes, for both the hybrid classical and quantum information and projective representation theory settings. This leads to new classes of hybrid subspace and subsystem Clifford codes. We extend the fundamental representation theoretic quantum error correction theorem to include these codes, based on the operator algebra quantum error correction framework. We also discuss several examples throughout the presentation, of both stabilizer and non-stabilizer type.

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

<a id="paper-2606.00501"></a>
### [Joint Optimization of Qubit Leasing and Quantum Circuit Distribution](http://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00501v1)

**Authors:** Anoushka Dey, Gaurav S. Kasbekar  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2606.00501v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>

The agent leases resources from multiple QCs (QC p1 to QC p|P |) connected by a quantum network. Each QC p has qubit storage capacity sp and qubit execution capacity ep, and charges costs cs p and ce p for leasing storage and execution qubits, respectively. The execution cost of gate g at QC p is cg p. The figure also shows the quantum circuit that the agent would like to run using the leased resources.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2606.00501_figures/2606.00501_fig2.jpg" alt="Figure 2">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> Fig. 2. The figure shows the total cost versus number of gates for the greedy algorithm and SA.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">

<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 3.</b> Fig. 3 compares the execution times for the same problem instances as for Fig. 2. The greedy algorithm is consistently 50 −200× faster than SA. The greedy algorithm’s execution time grows approximately as O(|G|1.2), close to the theoretical O(|G|) bound, while SA’s execution time grows super-linearly at approximately O(|G|1.8). For the largest instances (|G| = 200), the greedy algorithm completes in less than 1 second, while SA requires over 100 seconds.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2606.00501_figures/2606.00501_fig4.jpg" alt="Figure 4">

</figure>

<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 5.</b> Fig. 4. The figure shows the total cost versus number of QCs for the greedy algorithm and SA.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper tackles the complex JQLQCD problem of scheduling quantum circuits across multiple quantum computers by jointly optimizing qubit leasing, storage, gate execution, and qubit movement. After proving the problem is NP-complete, the authors introduce a fast and effective greedy heuristic that achieves near-optimal results, making large-scale quantum circuit deployment more computationally feasible.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work provides a crucial, computationally tractable framework for optimizing resource utilization in large-scale, distributed quantum computing architectures, which is a major bottleneck for near-term quantum applications.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper addresses the Joint Qubit Leasing and Quantum Circuit Distribution (JQLQCD) problem, which involves optimally scheduling and allocating qubits across multiple quantum computers connected by a network.

**Main result.** The problem is proven to be NP-complete, but the authors propose a highly efficient greedy heuristic that performs comparably to simulated annealing while being significantly faster.

**Method.** The core methodology involves formulating the problem as an Integer Linear Program (ILP) and then developing and analyzing a greedy algorithm with local search refinement to solve large instances.

**Model / system.** The model simulates a quantum circuit executed by an agent across a set of Quantum Computers (QCs) connected by a quantum network. The system must jointly manage qubit leasing, storage location, gate execution assignment, and qubit movement (migration vs. teleportation).

**Key observables.** Total system cost (minimization objective), circuit makespan ($T_{max}$), and computational execution time.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Leasing costs ($c_{s,p}, c_{e,p}$), gate execution costs ($c_{g,p}$), communication costs (migration $d$, teleportation $E$), and the weight $eta$ assigned to the makespan.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The model assumes passive QCs providing resources to a single agent, and the complexity analysis relies on bounding the circuit tree-width ($w_t$) for fixed-parameter tractability.

**Figures summary.** Figures compare the total cost and execution time of Optimal, Greedy, and Simulated Annealing algorithms across varying circuit sizes, number of QCs, and makespan weights, demonstrating the greedy algorithm's superior speed.

**Paper structure.** The paper reviews related work, presents the comprehensive ILP formulation, proves NP-completeness via reduction from $P|	ext{prec}|C_{max}$, analyzes solvable special cases, proposes the greedy heuristic, and concludes with extensive numerical performance evaluations.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We consider an agent, who would like to execute a given quantum circuit using resources leased from a set of quantum computers (QCs) connected by a quantum network. For this purpose, the agent needs to make the following four key decisions: (i) how many qubits to lease from each QC, (ii) at which QCs to store different circuit qubits in different time slots, (iii) at which QC to execute each gate in the circuit, and (iv) how to move qubits between QCs, choosing between migration and teleportation. We refer to this problem facing the agent as the joint qubit leasing and quantum circuit distribution (JQLQCD) problem, and provide a comprehensive integer linear programming (ILP) formulation for it. We show that the JQLQCD problem is NP-complete. Next, we identify several special cases in which the problem can be optimally solved in closed form or via polynomial-time algorithms. Also, we propose a greedy algorithm with local search refinement to solve large instances of the general JQLQCD problem. Finally, we evaluate the performance of the proposed greedy algorithm using extensive numerical computations.

</details>

### [Learning with Active Quantum Subspaces: Scalable Hybrid Advantage without Full Quantum Data-Encoding](http://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00932v1)

**Authors:** Jeongho Bang, Wooyeong Song, Kyoungho Cho, Taewan Kim, Yongsoo Hwang  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2606.00932v1>

Only the information- bearing subset is lifted into a coherent quantum sector, while the remaining variables stay classical. The projected readout keeps the quantum contribution low-dimensional and statistically controlled. The main analytical question of this paper is when one can simultaneously...</figcaption>

Panels (b) and (c) show two-dimensional slices of the exact projected feature q(x) for the two parities (−1)b7+b8 = ±1 after fixing ϕ3 = ϕ6 = π/2 and ϕ4 = ϕ5 = 0. Panel (a) shows clean-test accuracy versus the number of training samples N under 10% symmetric label noise. The low-order classical baselines remain near chance. An exact degree-eight interaction-only baseline on the selected 14 variables becomes nontrivial but still remains well below the hybrid projected model, which reaches essentially perfect accuracy by N = 640. Panel (b) reports the explicit feature dimensions. The exact degree-eight selected family uses 3003 features, while the cumulative interaction-only family up to degree 8 would use 12,910; Appendix E records both counts.</figcaption>

Each point is a candidate 4-subset from an eight-generator family; colors indicate its overlap with the true active support, and the exact support is marked explicitly. Panel (b) shows the distribution of the screening scores versus support overlap. Across the runs used here, the mean Pearson correlation between the screening score and the observed test-risk gain is 0.984, and the exact support is selected as the top-scoring candidate in every...</figcaption>

Panel (b) reports the sample size N95 needed to reach 95% clean accuracy versus β−2 0 ; the fitted line is approximately linear in β−2 0 , in agreement with Theorem 3.</figcaption>

**Summary.** This work proposes 'active quantum subspace data-encoding' to achieve quantum machine learning advantage by only encoding the most informative parts of a large classical input into a quantum state. The authors prove that this approach avoids the exponential resource scaling of full quantum encoding while maintaining statistically efficient learning guarantees. This offers a scalable, NISQ-compatible pathway for quantum advantage.

**Why it may be interesting.** It provides a concrete, theoretically bounded framework for achieving quantum advantage in machine learning that is directly relevant to near-term, resource-constrained quantum hardware (NISQ).

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Investigating whether quantum learning advantage can be achieved scalably without fully encoding large classical inputs into highly superposed quantum states.

**Main result.** The authors prove that a scalable hybrid advantage can be maintained by using active quantum subspace data-encoding, which keeps the quantum contribution low-dimensional and polynomially costly.

**Method.** They introduce Active Quantum Subspace Data-Encoding (AQSE) and derive structural results concerning the projected hybrid kernel, the necessary conditions for quantum improvement, and PAC sample-complexity bounds.

**Model / system.** The model involves partitioning an input $\mathbf{x}$ into a classical part $\mathbf{x}_C$ and an active quantum part $\mathbf{x}_Q$, encoding the quantum information using a polynomial-cost gate sequence on a subset of qubits.

**Key observables.** Projected hybrid kernel, projected quantum feature map $\Phi_Q(x)$, oracle reliability $eta(x)$, and sample complexity $N$.

**Important parameters / regimes.** The number of projected observables ($M$), the oracle reliability ($eta$), and the polynomial scaling of the encoding gate complexity ($G_{enc}(n) = 	ext{poly}(n)$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis relies on the projected hybrid kernel being positive semidefinite and the existence of a direction in the quantum sector orthogonal to the classical span that correlates with the residual error.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the comparison between full data quantumization and active subspace encoding, showing the projected model's superior performance and controlled effective degrees of freedom compared to global kernels.

**Paper structure.** The paper builds theoretically by defining the AQSE model, proving structural properties of the resulting kernel (Theorem 1), establishing the necessary and sufficient condition for quantum advantage (Theorem 2), and finally deriving the PAC sample-complexity bounds under noise (Theorem 3).

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We study whether quantum learning advantage can persist without fully embedding a large classical input into a highly superposed quantum state. To address this question, we introduce active quantum subspace data-encoding, in which only an information-bearing subset of the input is lifted to a quantum representation while the remaining variables stay classical. For this model, we define a projected hybrid readout and prove three structural results. First, the projected hybrid kernel is positive semidefinite and its sample regularized dimension is bounded by the number of projected observables, so the dimension blow-up of naive global kernels is avoided. Second, we give a necessary and sufficient criterion for improvement over a purely classical predictor in squared loss: the projected quantum sector must contain a direction that lies outside the classical feature span and correlates with the classical residual. Third, in a realizable noisy-oracle setting, we derive a PAC sample-complexity bound proportional to the inverse square of the oracle reliability. We then show, for a canonical Clifford active-subspace family under local dephasing noise, that this reliability can remain inverse-polynomial even when the encoding gate complexity grows polynomially with system size. Hence, the polynomial encoding cost does not by itself destroy the hybrid learning advantage. A sixty-four-qubit family and a synthetic contextual classification task illustrate how one projected quantum feature can compress a useful high-order interaction into a low-dimensional hybrid model. Our results generalize QRAM-free hybrid learning and provide a scalable route toward NISQ-compatible quantum advantage without full quantum data-encoding.

</details>

**Authors:** Mohamed El Azar, Elmustapha Feddi, Pablo Díaz, David Laroze, Ahmed Jellal  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2606.01938v1>

The spectrum immediately exhibits sharp, well-defined peaks, which are unmistakable signatures of the formation of quasi-bound states within the structure. A comparison between the massless case in Fig. 4a and the silicene configurations in Figs. 4(b,c) reveals a fundamental difference. In the graphene limit (λSO = 0), the resonances appear rela- tively broad and weak, reflecting significant electron leak-</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This work theoretically investigates how magnetic fields and intrinsic spin-orbit coupling control electron scattering within silicene quantum dots. By exploiting the SOC-induced energy gap, the authors demonstrate that confinement is significantly enhanced, leading to robust, spin-selective electron trapping. This suggests silicene is a promising platform for spintronics and quantum information applications.

**Why it may be interesting.** The mechanism of using intrinsic material properties (SOC) to engineer quantum confinement and spin filtering in a solid-state nanostructure is highly relevant for designing next-generation quantum devices.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The study analyzes the mechanism of electron diffusion and achieving robust, spin-selective confinement in silicene quantum dots (SQDs) subjected to a perpendicular magnetic field.

**Main result.** The intrinsic spin-orbit coupling (SOC) in silicene opens an energy gap, suppressing Klein tunneling and enabling superior carrier localization and spin-selective confinement when combined with an external magnetic field.

**Method.** The authors derive solutions to the Dirac equation at low energy and use continuity conditions at the dot interfaces to obtain exact expressions for diffusion coefficients and scattering efficiencies.

**Model / system.** The system is a silicene quantum dot (SQD) modeled using the low-energy Dirac equation, incorporating the effects of a perpendicular magnetic field and the intrinsic SOC.

**Key observables.** Diffusion coefficients, scattering efficiency (Q), spatial probability density (rho), and probability current density (j).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Magnetic field strength (B), SOC strength (lambda_SO), and dot radius (R).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis relies on the Dirac fermion framework and assumes the SOC gap is the dominant mechanism for overcoming Klein tunneling limitations.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the scattering efficiency (Q) vs. energy/B-field, showing sharp resonances in the SOC regime compared to the weak trapping in the massless (graphene) limit. Spatial plots show localized probability and current loops.

**Paper structure.** The paper follows a theoretical derivation (Dirac equation solutions), formal scattering analysis (matching coefficients), and numerical investigation demonstrating the synergistic effects of the B-field and SOC.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

The Klein tunnel effect phenomenon makes it impossible to permanently confine charge carriers in massless nanostructures. However, applying a constant magnetic field allows these electrons to be temporarily localized, thus forming quasi-bound states. In this study, we analyze the mechanism of electron diffusion through a silicene quantum dot (SQD) subjected to a perpendicular magnetic field. To enhance spatial localization, we exploit the spin-orbit coupling (SOC) specific to silicene, which generates a natural energy gap by acting as an effective mass. We first derive the solutions to the Dirac equation at low energy. Subsequently, by imposing the continuity conditions at the SQD interfaces, we obtain exact expressions for the diffusion coefficients. These results are then used to map the scattering efficiency together with the spatial distributions of probability and current densities. Our simulations demonstrate that the presence of this intrinsic gap significantly enhances electron trapping at the center of the SQD. Finally, we prove that the interplay between the external field and SOC breaks spin symmetry, thereby enabling robust and spin-selective confinement.

</details>

<a id="paper-2606.01121"></a>
### [On Jean-Marie Souriau's geometric quantization of the relativistic electron](http://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01121v1)

**Authors:** Géry de Saxcé  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2606.01121v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>

<img src="2606.01121_figures/2606.01121_fig1.jpg" alt="Figure 1">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> Figure 1: Coadjoint orbit method</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2606.01121_figures/2606.01121_fig2.jpg" alt="Figure 2">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> Figure 2: Coadjoint orbit of a particle with spin for Poincar´e group</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2606.01121_figures/2606.01121_fig3.jpg" alt="Figure 3">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 3.</b> Figure 3: Prequantum manifold</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2606.01121_figures/2606.01121_fig4.jpg" alt="Figure 4">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 4.</b> Figure 4: Prequantization of the particle with spin with spin 1/2</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2606.01121_figures/2606.01121_fig5.jpg" alt="Figure 5">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 5.</b> Figure 5: Prequantum manifolds of the electron and the positron</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper provides a rigorous mathematical re-examination of geometric quantization for the relativistic electron, building on Souriau's work. It successfully derives the Dirac equation and proves the conservation of both probability and spin currents. The work is significant for its systematic use of advanced mathematical tools like coadjoint orbits and Kaluza-Klein theory to establish fundamental symmetries.

**Why it may be interesting.** While highly abstract, the rigorous development of conserved currents and symmetries ($C, P, T$) using advanced geometric quantization techniques provides a deep theoretical underpinning for relativistic quantum field theory, which informs many modern quantum theories.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper revisits and rigorously justifies Jean-Marie Souriau's geometric quantization procedure applied to the relativistic electron, addressing ambiguities in the original formulation.

**Main result.** The authors successfully derive the Dirac equation and establish the conservation laws for both the probability and spin currents using a systematic geometric quantization framework.

**Method.** The core method involves applying the coadjoint orbit method, equipping the prequantum manifold with symplectic and contact structures, and utilizing Kaluza-Klein theory to systematically construct symmetries.

**Model / system.** The system is the relativistic particle with spin 1/2 (the electron), analyzed using the Poincaré group and formulated via quaternionic matrices and spinor representations.

**Key observables.** Dirac equation, conservation of probability current, conservation of spin current, and the systematic construction of $C, P, T$ symmetries.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Rest mass ($m_0$), spin ($	ext{spin}$), and the quantization constant $\hbar$.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The existence of a prequantum manifold $Y$ and the applicability of the Sommerfeld quantization condition are key assumptions.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the prequantum manifold structure for the electron and positron, and tables summarize the action of symmetry transformations ($C, P, T$) on spinors and coordinates.

**Paper structure.** The paper progresses by reviewing the coadjoint orbit method, establishing keystone theorems for symplectic/contact structures, applying these to the spin-1/2 case to derive the Dirac equation, and finally using Kaluza-Klein theory for symmetry construction.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

The aim of this paper is to revisit, in Souriau's book "Structure of Dynamical Systems", the chapter devoted to the geometric quantization where the justifications of important results and formulae are not given and are difficult to prove. After recalling the coadjoint orbit method and its application to the relativistic particle with spin, we state and prove two keystone theorems that allow to equip its prequantum manifold with the symplectic and contact structures. We apply them to the relativistic particle with spin 1/2, leading to the Dirac equation and the conservation of the probability current. We propose also an identity of conservation of the spin current and, invoking the Kaluza-Klein theory, a systematic construction of the symmetries of charge conjugation, parity transformation and time reversal which seems to us more convenient and readable than that of the classical presentations.

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

<a id="paper-2606.02235"></a>
### [Optimized Point Addition Circuits for Elliptic Curve Discrete Logarithms](http://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02235v1)

**Authors:** André Schrottenloher  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2606.02235v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>
<div class="paper-figures-scroll">
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2606.02235_figures/2606.02235_fig1.jpg" alt="Figure 1">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> Fig. 1. “Compression” circuit mapping 3 pairs (b0, b0&amp;b1) into 5 bits. On valid inputs, the last bit is always 0 and can be reused.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper presents an optimized quantum logical circuit architecture for performing point addition on elliptic curves, a critical subroutine for breaking discrete logarithm cryptography via Shor's algorithm. By redesigning the underlying modular arithmetic using a space-efficient, two-stage EEA approach, the authors achieve resource costs comparable to the best published results. This work provides a detailed blueprint for implementing complex quantum algorithms on near-term or fault-tolerant quantum hardware.

**Why it may be interesting.** While focused on cryptography, the detailed quantum circuit optimization techniques—especially the space-saving arithmetic implementations—represent advanced quantum circuit design principles applicable to any complex quantum computation.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The primary goal is to reduce the quantum resource cost (qubit and gate count) required to compute elliptic curve discrete logarithms, which is a major threat posed by Shor's algorithm to modern cryptography.

**Main result.** The authors detail a quantum logical circuit architecture for point addition that achieves results comparable to recent state-of-the-art work, offering a slight reduction in Toffoli gates compared to previous methods.

**Method.** The optimization involves implementing the Extended Euclidean Algorithm (EEA) in a two-step process: an Euclidean algorithm circuit followed by a Bézout reconstruction circuit to enable in-place modular multiplication.

**Model / system.** The model is a quantum circuit designed for point addition on elliptic curves over prime fields, specifically targeting the secp256k1 curve. The circuits operate at the logical level, abstracting away error correction details.

**Key observables.** Qubit count, Toffoli gate count (CCX, CCZ, And gates), and the total resource cost for running Shor's algorithm.

**Important parameters / regimes.** The bit-size of the curve (e.g., 256 bits), the failure probability (stated to be at most $2^{-13.3}$), and the asymptotic space complexity ($\approx 4.355n + O(\sqrt{n})$ qubits).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The circuits are not exact and include a failure probability. The analysis often relies on approximations, such as using only the most significant bits (MSBs) for modular arithmetic steps.

**Figures summary.** Table 1 compares qubit and Toffoli gate counts for point addition circuits across different methods. Table 2 compares the total resource cost for running Shor's algorithm.

**Paper structure.** The paper builds upon prior work by first detailing the structure of point multiplication via windowed addition. It then focuses on optimizing the core modular arithmetic components by adapting the EEA into a space-saving, two-step process (Euclidean Algorithm $	o$ Bézout Reconstruction) to achieve in-place modular multiplication.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Shor's algorithm represents the main threat of quantum computers to cryptography. In order to precisely understand its feasibility, many authors have worked towards reducing its costs, either at the logical level (assuming a fault-tolerant architecture), or at the physical level (taking into account the constraints of envisioned hardware). In particular, recent works by Chevignard et al. (CRYPTO 2024) and Gidney (arXiv 2025) used improved arithmetic to significantly reduce the qubit cost of factoring RSA public keys.   Even more recently, Babbush et al. (arXiv 2026) improved the cost of computing elliptic curve discrete logarithms, with a reduction of a factor 2 to 3 in gate count and qubit count compared to a previous work by Litinski (arXiv 2023). Their result relies on optimized point addition circuits on elliptic curves over prime fields. However they did not reveal their logical quantum circuits, relying instead on a zero-knowledge proof.   In this paper, we detail a quantum logical circuit architecture which gives similar results as Babbush et al., with a slightly higher number of qubits (around 1.5% increase) and a slightly smaller Toffoli gate count (between 6.5% and 10% reduction) for the curve secp256k1. We also give gate counts for a generic variant of the circuit, which is valid for any prime field.

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

**Authors:** Kun Zhang, Hai-Long Shi, Xiao-Hui Wang, Vladimir Korepin  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2606.00960v1>

</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This work analyzes unstructured quantum search by treating it as a circuit-depth optimization problem rather than just a query complexity problem. It discovers that the optimal search operators possess a palindromic structure, which allows for significant reductions in the required circuit depth. This provides a systematic method for designing depth-efficient quantum algorithms.

**Why it may be interesting.** It provides a systematic, structural understanding (palindromic construction) of how to improve quantum algorithms beyond simple query complexity bounds, which is highly relevant for designing practical, resource-constrained quantum circuits.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper addresses the discrepancy between query-optimal and circuit-depth-optimal quantum search algorithms, specifically for unstructured search.

**Main result.** The optimal search operators exhibit a palindromic structure, which systematically allows for substantial depth reductions compared to standard Grover search.

**Method.** The authors formulate the search as a circuit-depth optimization problem, defining a critical depth ratio that separates the two optimality regimes.

**Model / system.** The model is unstructured quantum search over $N=2^n$ items, utilizing the oracle operator ($O_t$) and the diffusion operator ($D_n$).

**Key observables.** Depth Ratio ($\alpha$), Critical Depth Ratio ($\alpha_G[U]$), Depth Efficiency Parameter ($\eta(P_U)$), and Minimum Expected Depth ($E_{\min}$).

**Important parameters / regimes.** The depth ratio $\alpha = \ell(O_t) / \ell(D_n)$, and the system size $N=2^n$.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis assumes that the standard Grover diffusion operator requires depth scaling with $n$ qubits, and that the palindromic structure represents the optimal pattern.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the depth-optimization formulation, comparing success probabilities for Grover search versus palindromic operators, and showing the dependence of $\alpha_G$ on mixer type.

**Paper structure.** The paper progresses by formulating the depth optimization problem, deriving the palindromic structure, establishing the depth-efficiency criterion, and applying this framework to various mixer types (local, nested local) to quantify depth reductions.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Grover's algorithm is optimal in query complexity, but not necessarily in circuit depth. We formulate unstructured quantum search as a circuit-depth optimization problem and identify a critical depth ratio separating query optimality from depth optimality. The resulting depth-efficient search operators exhibit a palindromic structure, in which shallow diffusion-like operators symmetrically replace selected Grover diffusion layers while preserving efficient amplitude amplification. This structure yields a simple depth-efficiency criterion and an analytic expression for the minimal expected depth. Applying the framework to $X$-type mixers, local diffusion operators, and nested local diffusion operators, we obtain substantial depth reductions over standard Grover search. In particular, nested local constructions reduce the total circuit depth by about $40\%$ when the oracle and Grover diffusion operators have comparable depth. These results reveal the resource-dependent nature of quantum-search optimality and establish palindromic constructions as a systematic route to depth-efficient quantum search algorithms.

</details>

### [Pauli-structured preconditioning for quantum linear system solvers](http://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01733v1)

**Authors:** Hantao Nie, Zhijian Lai, Dong An  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2606.01733v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

</figure>
