**Summary.** This paper investigates the feasibility of using preconditioning in quantum linear system solvers by analyzing the underlying algebraic structure using Pauli expansions. It demonstrates that by exploiting Pauli-structured representations, the overhead associated with composing the preconditioned operator can be reduced. This confirms that Pauli-structured preconditioning can lead to genuine improvements in the complexity of randomized QLS algorithms.

**Why it may be interesting.** It provides a rigorous theoretical framework for determining when structural algebraic properties (Pauli structure) can overcome fundamental overheads (normalization/synthesis) that limit the practical quantum advantage in solving linear systems.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper addresses whether preconditioning techniques can genuinely improve the complexity of Quantum Linear System (QLS) solvers, given that normalization overheads can negate classical condition number improvements.

**Main result.** Pauli-structured representations allow for a regrouping of Pauli products that can reduce the effective Pauli coefficient weight of the preconditioned operator, leading to genuine complexity reductions in the randomized QLS solver model.

**Method.** The authors analyze the QLS problem using Pauli-structured representations, comparing the complexity derived from separate block-encoding composition versus direct block-encoding of the preconditioned operator.

**Model / system.** The model is the Quantum Linear System (QLS) problem, $Ax=b$, where the system matrix $A$ and preconditioner $P$ are assumed to admit Pauli expansions. The analysis considers both coherent block-encoding and randomized Pauli access models.

**Key observables.** Pauli coefficient weight, effective QLS condition parameter ($\kappa_{	ext{eff}}$), and the randomized QLS per-sample depth proxy ($DRQLS$).

**Important parameters / regimes.** The Pauli coefficient weight ($w(\cdot)$), the minimum singular value ($\sigma_{\min}(\cdot)$), and the normalization factors ($\alpha_A, \alpha_P$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis assumes that the system matrix $A$ and preconditioner $P$ can be accurately represented by Pauli expansions, and it distinguishes between the limitations of separate block-encoding versus direct encoding.

**Figures summary.** Table I summarizes the performance comparison for a synthetic benchmark, showing the ratio $R_n$ (preconditioned/unpreconditioned depth proxy) and direct block-encoding diagnostics for the $A_n$ family.

**Paper structure.** The paper first establishes the limitation of separate block-encoding composition (Theorem 3), then introduces the Pauli-structured approach, deriving bounds on Pauli coefficient weights for the preconditioned operator, and finally applying this to the randomized QLS solver to show genuine complexity improvements.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Preconditioning is a fundamental technique for accelerating classical linear system solvers, and understanding when its benefits persist in quantum linear system (QLS) solvers is important for assessing the practical resource requirements of quantum linear algebra. In QLS algorithms, however, the potential advantage of preconditioning may be offset by the normalization overhead incurred by composing separate block-encodings of the system matrix and the preconditioner, as observed in recent work. This limitation leaves open whether additional algebraic structure can make preconditioning effective in quantum access models. Motivated by this question, we show that Pauli-structured representations of both the system matrix and the preconditioner allow the preconditioned operator to be accessed through regrouped Pauli expansions. In this setting, algebraic regrouping of Pauli products can reduce the Pauli coefficient weight of the preconditioned operator, thereby altering the normalization parameters relevant to quantum algorithms. We derive explicit size and coefficient-weight bounds for the regrouped Pauli representations, and we trace their consequences for both direct block-encoding constructions and randomized Pauli linear system solvers. These results identify when Pauli-structured preconditioning can reduce the effective complexity parameters of QLS algorithms, rather than merely improving the classical condition number. Numerical experiments on a finite-dimensional synthetic benchmark show reductions in norm-aware direct block-encoding diagnostics and in the randomized QLS per-sample depth proxy.

</details>

**Summary.** This work applies quantum optimization techniques, specifically a penalty-free QAOA variant (QAOAMIS), to solve the lattice protein folding problem. By restricting the search space to independent sets, the authors avoid complex penalty terms, allowing them to successfully fold proteins up to N=14 using a local search extension. This shows a promising quantum computational route for biophysics problems.

**Why it may be interesting.** It demonstrates a sophisticated application of quantum algorithms (QAOA variants) to a complex, classically hard optimization problem (protein folding), providing a pathway to tackle larger systems via local search heuristics.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper addresses the challenging discrete optimization problem of finding the minimum-energy structure of lattice proteins.

**Main result.** A novel QAOA variant (QAOAMIS) that avoids quadratic penalty terms by restricting the search space to independent sets successfully folds lattice proteins up to N=14 using a heuristic local search scheme.

**Method.** The authors adapt the Quantum Approximate Optimization Algorithm (QAOA) to solve the Maximum Independent Set (MIS) problem formulation of protein folding, supplemented by a heuristic Quantum Local Search (QLS) for larger systems.

**Model / system.** The physical system is lattice protein folding, modeled using the minimal HP (Hydrophobic-Polar) model on a 2D square lattice. The problem is mapped onto a QUBO formulation solvable via quantum optimization.

**Key observables.** Success probability (fraction of correct solutions), minimum energy ($E_{min}^{HP}$), and the number of beads ($n$) in the folded structure.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Protein length N (up to 14), circuit depth p, and the size limit of local subgraphs ($\le 26$ qubits).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The standard QAOA formulation is modified to use a mixer designed for MIS, and a heuristic QLS is necessary to overcome the exponential state space growth of full-scale QAOA circuits.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate energy landscapes $F(	heta)$ for QAOAX and QAOAXY circuits, and show the success probability vs. circuit depth $p$ for various QAOA variants on small proteins.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem, contrasts standard QAOA with the penalty-free QAOAMIS variant, validates the approach on small systems ($N=4, 6$), and extends the methodology to larger chains ($N=14$) using a heuristic local search scheme.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Identifying minimum-energy structures of lattice proteins is a challenging discrete optimization problem. Quantum approaches such as analog quantum annealing and the gate-based quantum approximate optimization algorithm (QAOA) can address this problem after mapping it to a binary representation, which typically involves introducing penalty terms to enforce valid chain configurations. However, in this and many related problems, the use of quadratic penalty terms can be avoided by restricting the search space to independent sets in a conflict graph and using a QAOA mixer designed for the maximum independent set problem. In this work, we implement and explore this QAOA variant for lattice protein folding. Here, the objective function consists solely of the protein energy together with a simple linear bias term, without quadratic penalties. We validate this approach through classical simulations of the quantum circuits for lattice proteins of lengths $N=4$ and $N=6$. To explore larger systems, we further introduce a heuristic iterative local-search scheme, with which we successfully fold lattice proteins with lengths up to $N=14$ using local subgraphs with at most 26 qubits.

</details>

**Summary.** This paper analyzes the curricula of U.S. quantum master's programs to determine how well they prepare graduates for the quantum industry. By systematically coding course content against industry skills, the study finds that while theory is well-covered, applied, computational, and professional skills show significant inconsistency. It provides a framework for improving quantum education policy and curriculum design.

**Why it may be interesting.** While not focused on physical quantum dynamics, the identification of skill gaps in applied and professional competencies is crucial for any theoretical physicist looking to transition research into industry applications.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The study aims to assess the alignment between U.S. quantum master's program curricula and the documented skill needs of the quantum technology industry workforce.

**Main result.** While programs share a strong foundation in quantum theory, there is significant variability and persistent gaps in applied skills, professional development, and cross-disciplinary integration necessary for workforce readiness.

**Method.** The researchers conducted a systematic curriculum analysis of multiple U.S. master's programs, using a structured coding framework to map course offerings across six defined quantum-relevant skill categories.

**Model / system.** The focus is on the educational system and workforce skill profile, not a physical quantum system. The 'model' is a structured taxonomy of quantum skills (QTheory, QHard, QSoft, Qcom, QSense, Non-Technical Skills).

**Key observables.** Program-level skill profiles, skill distribution across categories, and the degree of alignment/divergence between curricular emphasis and industry competencies.

**Important parameters / regimes.** The analysis uses an ordinal coding scale (0, 1, 2) and employs normalization and discretization techniques to aggregate skill emphasis across programs.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis assumes that publicly available curriculum materials accurately reflect the intended scope of the degree, and that the structured coding framework captures the necessary skill dimensions.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the mapping of coursework to skill categories, show skill distribution bar charts for individual programs, and use heatmaps/stacked bar charts to compare aggregate skill emphasis across all analyzed programs.

**Paper structure.** The paper systematically defines the problem (workforce gap), details the methodology (curriculum coding, aggregation), presents findings on common foundations vs. variability, and concludes with actionable policy recommendations for curriculum design.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Quantum technologies are increasingly recognized as a strategic priority for economic competitiveness, national security, and technological innovation in the United States. As quantum systems transition from research prototypes to deployable technologies, attention has shifted toward the preparedness of the quantum workforce, particularly the alignment between higher education and industry skill needs. While prior research has examined individual aspects of quantum education or workforce demand, few studies integrate systematic curriculum analysis with documented industry expectations. This study addresses that gap by analyzing primary U.S. masters programs in quantum science and technology, focusing on curriculum structure and skill development. Using a structured coding framework, course offerings were mapped across six quantum-relevant skill categories and aggregated to produce program-level skill profiles. These profiles were then compared with industry-identified competencies reported in recent workforce studies. The findings reveal strong emphasis on quantum theory across programs, alongside substantial variability in technical skills, applied learning opportunities, and professional development components. The results highlight areas of alignment as well as persistent gaps related to workforce readiness, cross-disciplinary integration, and emerging technological demands. This study provides a scalable framework for evaluating quantum education programs and offers evidence-based insights for curriculum design, workforce policy, and the continued development of the U.S. quantum ecosystem.

</details>

### [Quantum Dynamics of a Particle in a Linear Potential: Invariant Operator Approach and Discrete Spectrum Solutions](http://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02112v1)

**Authors:** Mustapha Maamache, Aymen Bendjoudl  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2606.02112v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>

</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2606.02112_figures/2606.02112_page3.jpg" alt="Figure 2">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> Low-resolution page preview, page 3</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2606.02112_figures/2606.02112_page4.jpg" alt="Figure 3">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 3.</b> Low-resolution page preview, page 4</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2606.02112_figures/2606.02112_page5.jpg" alt="Figure 4">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 4.</b> Low-resolution page preview, page 5</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper uses the Lewis–Riesenfeld invariant operator method to solve the quantum dynamics of a particle in a linear potential. By constructing and transforming a conserved invariant operator, the authors demonstrate that the system's dynamics are mathematically equivalent to those of a harmonic oscillator when the conserved quantity $\omega^2$ is positive. This establishes a direct and exact connection between constant-force motion and harmonic oscillator quantization.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work provides a rigorous, exact quantum mechanical description of a fundamental problem (constant force) by mapping it onto the well-understood harmonic oscillator framework using invariant theory, which is a powerful tool in quantum dynamics.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper investigates the quantum dynamics of a particle confined by a linear potential, which models motion under a constant external force.

**Main result.** The dynamics are shown to be exactly equivalent to the quantization of a harmonic oscillator when the conserved quantity $\omega^2 > 0$, yielding a discrete energy spectrum.

**Method.** The core method employed is the Lewis–Riesenfeld invariant operator technique, which constructs a time-dependent Hermitian invariant operator $I(t)$ to simplify the time-dependent Schrödinger equation.

**Model / system.** The system is a single particle governed by a Hamiltonian $H(q, p) = rac{1}{2}mp^2 - Fq$, representing motion in a linear potential. The analysis relies on finding quadratic invariants.

**Key observables.** The conserved quantity $\omega^2$ determines the spectral structure; the eigenvalues for $\omega^2 > 0$ are $\zeta_n = 2\hbar\omega(n + 1/2)$.

**Important parameters / regimes.** The sign of the conserved quantity $\omega^2$ dictates whether the spectrum is discrete ($\omega^2 > 0$) or continuous.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis focuses on the physically relevant regime where $\omega^2 > 0$ to obtain a discrete spectrum, and it assumes the applicability of the Lewis–Riesenfeld formalism.

**Paper structure.** The paper proceeds by applying the Lewis–Riesenfeld method to the time-dependent Schrödinger equation for the linear potential. It derives the general quadratic invariant, reduces it via unitary transformations to a harmonic oscillator form, and analyzes the resulting spectrum based on the sign of $\omega^2$.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We investigate the quantum dynamics of a particle subjected to a linear potential using the Lewis--Riesenfeld invariant operator method. Starting from the time-dependent Schrödinger equation associated with a constant external force, we construct the most general Hermitian quadratic invariant and derive the corresponding coupled differential equations for its time-dependent coefficients. By means of an appropriate sequence of unitary transformations, the invariant operator is reduced to the form of a harmonic oscillator Hamiltonian. This reduction enables a clear classification of the system according to the sign of the conserved quantity ω2. Particular attention is devoted to the physically relevant case ω2 >0, which yields a discrete eigenspectrum. Explicit analytical expressions for the invariant coefficients, the displacement parameters, and the transformed wave functions are obtained. The resulting formalism provides an exact quantum description of a particle under a constant force and establishes a direct connection between invariant theory and harmonic oscillator quantization.

</details>

**Authors:** David A. Meyer, Thomas G. Wong  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2606.01403v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper explores using nonlinear quantum walks to perform spatial search on incomplete graphs, moving beyond the idealized all-to-all connectivity of the complete graph. By analyzing the dynamics using nonlinear Schrödinger equations, the authors prove that specific nonlinearities can maintain the quantum speedup on graphs like Paley graphs and complete bipartite graphs. This demonstrates that nonlinear quantum search remains viable even when the underlying physical network is sparse.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work directly addresses the practical limitations of quantum search algorithms by showing that nonlinear interactions can extend the speedup capability to real-world, incomplete network structures, which is a key area of research in quantum information.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper investigates how nonlinear quantum walks can be used to achieve quantum search on graphs that are not fully connected (incomplete graphs), extending the concept beyond the standard complete graph search.

**Main result.** Nonlinear quantum walks can successfully speed up the search process on specific incomplete graphs, such as Paley graphs and complete bipartite graphs, provided the nonlinearity coefficients are appropriately chosen.

**Method.** The authors employ theoretical analysis using nonlinear Schrödinger equations, comparing the nonlinear evolution to the known linear evolution via error bounding techniques like Duhamel's formula.

**Model / system.** The model is a continuous-time quantum walk governed by a nonlinear Hamiltonian, which includes a nonlinear self-potential term dependent on the probability density. The analysis is applied to various graph structures, including complete graphs, Paley graphs, hypercubes, and cubic lattices.

**Key observables.** Success Probability (probability of finding the particle at the marked vertex), Energy Gap ($\Delta E$), and the required search time $T_N$.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Nonlinearity coefficients ($g$), graph size ($N$), and the lattice dimension ($d$). The analysis often requires conditions like $g \ll \sqrt{N}$ or $g=N-1$.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis relies on the graphs being 'sufficiently complete' to mimic the behavior of a complete graph, and the mathematical proofs depend on bounding the accumulated error term ($\eta(t)$) to be negligible.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the success probability vs. time for linear and nonlinear searches on various graphs (Complete, Paley, Hypercube, Bipartite) across different dimensions, showing the maintenance of high success probability under nonlinear control.

**Paper structure.** The paper progresses from establishing the linear search baseline on complete graphs, extending the concept to 'sufficiently complete' graphs, and then rigorously analyzing the nonlinear dynamics using error bounding techniques to prove speedups for specific nonlinearities (cubic, cubic-quintic) on these graphs.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Many-body quantum systems with effective nonlinearities have been shown to speed up quantum search on the complete graph, \textit{i.e.}, the combinatorial version of Grover's algorithm, at the expense of the number of particles needed for the effective nonlinearity to hold. Physically, however, data may not be arranged in an all-to-all network, and the task of searching incomplete graphs is the spatial search problem. We explore spatial search using a continuous-time nonlinear quantum walk on a variety of graphs. First, we consider incomplete graphs that are ``sufficiently complete'' so as to asymptotically search like the complete graph under a continuous-time (linear) quantum walk, which includes strongly regular graphs such as Paley graphs, regular graphs such as hypercubes, and irregular graphs such as complete bipartite graphs. For these sufficiently complete graphs, we analytically prove nonlinear speedups for Paley graphs and for complete bipartite graphs whose two partite sets both have size $Θ(N)$, for suitable cubic and cubic-quintic nonlinearities, and we give numerical evidence for stronger nonlinearities and for hypercubes. Second, we explore arbitrary-dimensional cubic lattices, and we numerically show that certain nonlinearities speed up search on sufficiently high dimensional lattices. Thus, nonlinear quantum search can remain viable even when the underlying graph is incomplete.

</details>

### [Spin Correlations in Two-Particle Systems: A Pedagogically Motivated Comparison of Computational Approaches](http://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02361v1)

**Authors:** S. Martins-Filho  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2606.02361v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>

The inhomo- geneous magnetic field produced by the magnet separates particles according to the measured value of the spin component along a selected direction. In this case, only two outcomes are possible, +ℏ/2 and −ℏ/2, corresponding respectively to spin “up” and spin “down” along the chosen axis.</figcaption>
</figure>

The polar angles θu and θv are measured from the positive z-axis, while the azimuthal angles φu and φv are measured in the xy-plane from the positive x-axis to the projections of ˆu and ˆv, respectively.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper compares three distinct mathematical approaches—direct algebra, matrix formalism, and symmetry analysis—for calculating spin correlations in two spin-1/2 particles. Its goal is pedagogical: to clarify the interplay between tensor structure, entanglement, and rotational symmetry. It shows that while the singlet state exhibits simple rotational symmetry, the triplet state requires a more careful treatment than naive symmetry extensions allow.

**Why it may be interesting.** The detailed comparison of calculation techniques (algebraic vs. matrix vs. symmetry) is highly valuable for students learning quantum formalism, particularly how tensor products and symmetries constrain physical observables.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper aims to provide a pedagogically motivated comparison of different computational methods for calculating spin correlation expectation values in a two-particle quantum system.

**Main result.** It demonstrates that while symmetry arguments are useful for the singlet state due to its rotational invariance, they fail for triplet states unless the state transformation is accounted for.

**Method.** The authors compare three methods: direct algebraic evaluation in the product basis, using a matrix representation based on $2	imes2$ complex matrices, and applying symmetry arguments.

**Model / system.** The system is a two-particle quantum system composed of two spin-1/2 particles, analyzed using the tensor-product structure of the total Hilbert space. Key states include the singlet and triplet spin states.

**Key observables.** The primary observable is the spin correlation function $B[\psi] = \langle\psi|S^{(1)}\hat{u} S^{(2)}\hat{v}|\psiangle$, which measures the average product of spin measurements along directions $\hat{u}$ and $\hat{v}$.

**Important parameters / regimes.** The relative angles ($	heta, \phi$) between the measurement directions $\hat{u}$ and $\hat{v}$ are crucial parameters.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The primary limitation addressed is the conceptual difficulty in interpreting the tensor-product structure, and the symmetry argument is shown to be inapplicable to the triplet state without careful state transformation.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the spin-1/2 degrees of freedom and the geometry of measurement directions on a unit sphere, while equations detail the explicit forms of basis states in the product basis and the matrix representations.

**Paper structure.** The paper progresses by introducing the problem, detailing the product-basis approach, presenting the more concise matrix representation, and finally contrasting these with the symmetry-based argument, culminating in a discussion of the limitations of each method.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

In this work we present a pedagogically motivated analysis of spin-correlation calculations in a quantum system composed of two spin-$1/2$ particles. Rather than aiming at new physical results, our purpose is to clarify and bring attention to different strategies for evaluating expectation values of the form $\langle ψ| S^{(1)}_{\hat{\boldsymbol{u}}} S^{(2)}_{\hat{\boldsymbol{v}}} | ψ\rangle$, which play an important role in discussions of entanglement and Bell-type correlations. We compare three complementary approaches. The first follows a direct algebraic evaluation in the product basis, closely related to standard textbook methods. The second uses a matrix representation of bipartite states, in which the tensor-product structure is expressed in terms of $2\times2$ complex matrices. This representation keeps the calculation close to the familiar Pauli-matrix algebra and makes the independent action of operators on each subsystem more transparent. The third explores a symmetry-based argument, highlighting both its usefulness and its limitations when applied beyond the singlet state. We show explicitly that the singlet state is rotationally invariant, which explains why the symmetry argument successfully reproduces its correlation function, while a naive extension fails for triplet states. The discussion illustrates how entanglement, tensor-product structure, and rotational symmetry interplay in spin correlations.

</details>

**Summary.** This experimental study uses advanced ARPES techniques on EuAl4 to distinguish between bulk electronic effects and surface reconstruction. By comparing spectra from surfaces cleaved at different temperatures, the authors demonstrate that complex low-temperature features are due to a thermally fragile surface reconstruction, resolving a key ambiguity regarding CDW physics in this material class.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work provides a rigorous spectroscopic methodology for separating surface physics from bulk electronic correlations in correlated materials, a crucial technique for understanding many strongly correlated systems.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The study aims to resolve the microscopic origin of complex low-temperature ARPES spectra in EuAl4, specifically distinguishing between intrinsic bulk Charge-Density-Wave (CDW) reconstruction and surface-derived spectral weight.

**Main result.** The additional, complex low-temperature spectral weight observed on low-temperature-cleaved surfaces is predominantly attributed to a thermally fragile surface reconstruction, rather than being a signature of bulk CDW-induced band folding.

**Method.** The researchers combined Angle-Resolved Photoemission Spectroscopy (ARPES) measurements—analyzing photon-energy, temperature, and cleavage-history dependence—with first-principles electronic structure calculations.

**Model / system.** The physical system is EuAl4, a correlated semimetal in the BaAl4-family, which exhibits a CDW transition. The analysis compares experimental spectra from high-temperature-cleaved versus low-temperature-cleaved surfaces against calculated 3D bulk electronic structures.

**Key observables.** Photon-energy dependence, temperature evolution of spectral weight (suppression upon warming), and the comparison of measured Fermi surface topology to bulk calculations.

**Important parameters / regimes.** The CDW transition temperature (T_CDW ~ 140 K); the temperature dependence of spectral features (e.g., suppression upon warming); and the cleavage history (HT vs. LT).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis assumes that the bulk CDW folding signal can be distinguished from surface effects by exploiting the sensitivity to cleavage history and temperature stability.

**Figures summary.** Figures compare ARPES spectra from HT-cleaved (bulk-like) vs. LT-cleaved (additional features) surfaces, showing the progressive suppression of anomalous features upon warming, and illustrating the photon-energy dependence of these features.

**Paper structure.** The paper follows a comparative analysis structure: first, establishing the bulk baseline using HT-cleaved surfaces; second, identifying anomalous features on LT-cleaved surfaces; and finally, using temperature and cleavage history dependence to argue that these anomalies are surface-derived and not intrinsic to the bulk CDW state.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Charge ordering in EuAl$_4$ has been widely discussed in connection with band reconstruction, magnetism,and topological electronic states, yet the microscopic origin of the complex low-temperature ARPES spectra remains unresolved. Here we combine photon-energy-, temperature-, and cleavage-history-dependent ARPES with firstprinciples calculations to separate intrinsic bulk bands from surface-derived spectral weight. Spectra measured on high-temperature-cleaved surfaces, both at 160 K and after cooling to 10 K, are well described by the calculated three-dimensional bulk electronic structure, whereas low-temperature-cleaved surfaces exhibit additional electron-like bands, replica-like Fermi-surface contours, and nearly kz-independent surface-derived features that are absent from the bulk calculations. These extra features are progressively suppressed upon warming, whereas the bulk-derived bands remain largely unchanged across the charge-density-wave transition within the experimental resolution. The combined analysis of photon-energy dependence, temperature evolution, and cleavage history identifies the reconstructed low-temperature spectral weight as arising predominantly from a thermally fragile surface reconstruction rather than from intrinsic bulk CDW-induced band folding. These results resolve an important ambiguity in EuAl$_4$ and provide spectroscopic criteria for distinguishing surface reconstruction from bulk charge-order effects in BaAl$_4$-family correlated semimetals.

</details>

**Summary.** This work characterizes SnSb6Te10 as a strong topological insulator by combining DFT calculations with advanced measurements like ARPES and magnetotransport. The results confirm the existence of topological surface states, evidenced by the Z2 invariant, Dirac-like behavior, and pronounced transport anisotropy. This establishes the material as a promising platform for topological transport research.

**Why it may be interesting.** The strong evidence for topological surface states and the detailed study of anisotropic transport in a layered material make it highly relevant for understanding topological phenomena in condensed matter systems.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** To investigate the electronic structure and magnetotransport properties of the layered telluride compound SnSb6Te10 to establish it as a strong topological insulator.

**Main result.** The material is confirmed as a strong topological insulator, exhibiting topological surface states, hole-type carriers, and pronounced magnetotransport anisotropy.

**Method.** A combination of theoretical calculations (DFT) and multiple experimental techniques, including ARPES, Hall measurements, and angle-dependent magnetotransport measurements.

**Model / system.** The physical system is the single crystal SnSb6Te10, a layered telluride compound. The theoretical analysis involves calculating the topological invariant (Z2) and modeling transport phenomena using models like the Hikami-Larkin-Nagaoka equation.

**Key observables.** Non-trivial Z2 topological invariant, surface-state dispersion matching ARPES, hole carrier density ($\sim 10^{21} 	ext{ cm}^{-3}$), weak antilocalization (WAL), and Berry phase close to $\pi$ from SdH oscillations.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Temperature (2 K to 5 K), magnetic field strength (up to 12 T), and angle ($	heta$) dependence for transport measurements.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis assumes the crystal structure belongs to the rhombohedral space group $Rar{3}m$, and that transport measurements involve a coexistence of bulk and surface contributions.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the crystal structure, electronic band structures (with/without SOC), surface band dispersion vs. ARPES, temperature dependence of resistance, and angular dependence of magnetotransport (WAL and AMR).

**Paper structure.** The paper follows a structure of theoretical prediction (DFT/Topological invariant) followed by experimental verification across multiple fronts: ARPES confirms surface states, Hall measurements confirm carrier type/density, and magnetotransport confirms topological signatures (WAL, Berry phase, anisotropy).

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We have investigated the electronic structure and magnetotransport properties of SnSb$_6$Te$_{10}$ single crystals using density functional theory (DFT), synchrotron-based angle-resolved photoemission spectroscopy (ARPES), and quantum transport measurements. Our DFT calculations reveal a clear spin-orbit coupling driven band inversion between the Sb-$p$ and Te-$p$ states together with a non-trivial $\mathbb{Z}_2$ topological invariant. The calculated surface-state dispersion and hexagonally warped Fermi surface contours agree well with the ARPES measurements. Temperature-dependent transport measurements indicate dominant electron-phonon scattering, while Hall measurements confirm hole-type carriers with carrier density of the order of $10^{21}$ cm$^{-3}$. Both transverse and longitudinal magnetotransport exhibit weak antilocalization behavior, while Shubnikov-de Haas oscillations observed for $H \parallel c$ yield a Berry phase close to $π$, consistent with Dirac-like surface states. Furthermore, angle-dependent magnetotransport measurements reveal pronounced anisotropy associated with an anisotropic Fermi surface topology and mixed bulk-surface transport behavior. Our combined theoretical and experimental results establish SnSb$_6$Te$_{10}$ as a strong topological insulator and a promising platform for investigating topological transport phenomena in layered telluride systems.

</details>

### [Toward Efficient End-to-End Quantum Elliptic PDE Solvers: a Multilevel Correction Algorithm for Direct Observable Estimation](http://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01270v1)

**Authors:** Xiantao Li  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** numerical methods · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2606.01270v1>

**Summary.** This paper presents a novel multilevel framework to overcome the readout bottleneck when using quantum algorithms to solve elliptic PDEs. By estimating the observable through a telescoping sum of interlevel corrections derived from the Schur complement, the method removes the polynomial dependence on the mesh size $h$. This significantly improves the scaling of quantum computation for extracting physical quantities of interest.

**Why it may be interesting.** While focused on PDEs, the rigorous analysis of error propagation and the development of multilevel correction techniques are highly relevant to quantum algorithms for simulating complex physical systems where observables must be extracted efficiently.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Developing efficient end-to-end quantum algorithms to solve elliptic Partial Differential Equations (PDEs) while accurately estimating physical observables of interest.

**Main result.** The proposed multilevel framework successfully removes the polynomial $h$-dependent readout overhead for observables with readout order $\chi \le 2$, achieving Heisenberg scaling ($	ext{eO}(\epsilon^{-1})$) with amplitude estimation.

**Method.** The method adapts Multilevel Monte Carlo (MLMC) principles by estimating a telescoping sum of interlevel corrections, utilizing the Schur-complement factorization of the corrected Green's operator.

**Model / system.** The model is the scalar elliptic PDE, $-
abla \cdot (a(x)
abla u(x)) = f(x)$, discretized using the Finite Element Method (FEM) on a multi-level mesh hierarchy.

**Key observables.** Fluxes, currents, tractions, and energy (general physical quantities of interest, QoIs), which are estimated via linear or quadratic forms.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Mesh size $h$, statistical precision $\epsilon$, and the readout order $\chi$ of the observable.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The method relies on the Ritz-complement oracle assumptions and assumes the Schur complement $\Sigma_\ell$ is uniformly conditioned.

**Figures summary.** Table 1 compares the complexity scaling of observable estimation for various methods (Direct stiffness inverse, BPX, Schur-complement) for elliptic finite element QoIs.

**Paper structure.** The paper establishes the problem of readout bottlenecks in quantum PDE solvers, introduces the multilevel framework based on MLMC, details the mathematical machinery using Schur-complement factorization of the corrected Green's operator, and analyzes the resulting complexity scaling for both amplitude estimation and direct sampling.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

A central test case for quantum linear system algorithms (QLSA) is elliptic PDEs after a finite element discretization. Most existing analyses focus on preparing a normalized solution state. But an end-to-end quantum PDE solver must also extract physical quantities of interest, such as fluxes, currents, tractions, and energy. These outputs require quantum measurement, and their observable norms may grow like $h^{-χ} $ with mesh size $h $, creating a readout bottleneck even when a quantum preconditioner reduces the condition-number dependence on $h$.   We present a multilevel framework for this readout problem, motivated by the variance-reduction mechanism of multilevel Monte Carlo (MLMC), which is naturally compatible with a multi-level finite element discretization. Instead of estimating the full fine-grid observable directly, the method estimates a telescoping sum of interlevel corrections, so that the fine-coarse cancellation is exposed before quantum measurement. Our algorithm is based on Schur-complement factorization of the corrected Green's operator through a Ritz-complement map. For quantities of interest with readout order $χ\leq 2$, the multilevel estimator removes the polynomial $h$-dependent readout overhead. With amplitude estimation, the remaining statistical dependence is $ \widetilde{O}(1/\varepsilon)$, i.e., Heisenberg scaling in the inference precision up to logarithmic factors and with direct sampling, the complexity is reduced to standard Monte Carlo scaling $\widetilde{O}(1/ε^2)$.

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

</details>

### [Non-Hermiticity-induced chirality imbalance of Weyl Landau levels](http://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00615v1)

**Authors:** Sachin Vaidya, Alaa Bayazeed, André Grossi Fonseca, Adolfo G. Grushin, Marin Soljačić, Christina Jörg  
**Type:** both · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2606.00615v1>

(c) Transfer-matrix simulation for 20 unit cells of the multilayer stack at 𝑞= 0, 𝑘𝑦= 0. Along this cut, four Weyl points with charges −1, +1, +1, −1 (from left to right) are visible.</figcaption>

(b) A schematic of the experimental measurement setup: broadband light from a halogen lamp illuminates the multilayer stack and the transmitted spectrum is recorded using a spectrometer. (c) Transfer matrix simulation of the spectrum for 𝑞= 0, 𝑘𝑦= 0 under a homogeneous B-field. The zeroth Landau levels inherit their chirality from that of the Weyl nodes shown in Fig. 2c. (d)...</figcaption>
</figure>

<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 3.</b> FIG. 3. (a) Schematic of Landau levels under an axial magnetic field (B5) and boundary loss: in the transmission spectrum, the surface states (pale blue) are highly broadened and unobservable. Upon decreasing surface loss, they become long-lived and are visible as sharp resonances that hybridize with the zeroth Landau levels (red) in both transfer-matrix simulations (d) and the experiment (e). To reduce the loss, two bilayers of cladding have been added to the top of the multilayer stack, as shown in (b) and (c).</figcaption>
</figure>

<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 4.</b> FIG. 4. Focused ion beam (FIB) cut through a typical multilayer stack revealing the different layers. The silicon layers appear dark gray while the silicon dioxide layers appear light gray.</figcaption>
</figure>

(a) For 𝐵&gt; 𝐵5 (𝛿= 0.3) the four zeroth Landau levels disperse in opposite directions, whereas for (c) 𝐵&lt; 𝐵5 (𝛿= 0.8) they disperse in the same direction. (b) At the crossover point 𝐵≈𝐵5 (𝛿= 0.6), the outer two zeroth Landau levels collapse to Weyl points.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This research demonstrates that non-Hermiticity in photonic systems can manipulate topological constraints governing Weyl semimetals. By applying an axial magnetic field, the chiral balance is spatially separated into bulk and surface states. Crucially, radiative boundary loss selectively removes the surface states, creating an observable chirality imbalance that can be recovered by modifying the boundaries.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work is highly relevant to AMO/Quantum Optics because it uses engineered photonic systems to simulate complex topological phenomena, demonstrating how dissipation (non-Hermiticity) acts as a controllable physical mechanism to modify fundamental quantum constraints.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper investigates how non-Hermiticity, inherent in open photonic systems, can induce and control an observable chirality imbalance in Weyl Landau levels, thereby bypassing fundamental topological constraints like the Nielsen-Ninomiya theorem.

**Main result.** By engineering an axial magnetic field and exploiting radiative boundary loss, the surface-localized chiral states are selectively suppressed, leading to an apparent net chirality imbalance in the long-lived bulk spectrum.

**Method.** The study combines theoretical modeling using effective Hamiltonians and transfer matrix simulations with experimental measurements of light transmission through multilayer photonic structures.

**Model / system.** The system is a synthetic photonic Weyl semimetal realized in multilayer Bragg stacks of Si/SiO2. The physics is governed by the 1D Maxwell eigenvalue equation, emulating 3D Weyl physics via synthetic momenta and engineered gauge fields (homogeneous and axial magnetic fields).

**Key observables.** Zeroth Landau level dispersion, chiral spectral flow, and the measured transmission spectrum showing the recovery of avoided crossings upon boundary modification.

**Important parameters / regimes.** The relative strengths of the homogeneous ($B$) and axial ($B_5$) magnetic fields, and the strength of the radiative boundary loss ($\eta$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The system is treated as an open, dissipative photonic structure where boundary loss dictates the observable spectral flow; the initial state assumes periodicity enforcing the theorem in the closed limit.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the unit cell structure, the transformation of Weyl nodes into Landau levels under different fields, and critically, the comparison of transmission spectra with and without a dielectric Bragg reflector, showing the re-emergence of surface states.

**Paper structure.** The paper progresses by first establishing the theoretical framework of chirality constraints in Weyl systems, then demonstrating the effect of an axial field ($B_5$) leading to spatial separation of chiral channels, and finally showing that non-Hermitian boundary loss selectively removes the compensating surface states, thus inducing the imbalance.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Weyl semimetals obey a global chirality constraint: the net chiral topological charge and any associated chiral spectral flow must vanish, as required by the Nielsen-Ninomiya theorem. Under magnetic fields, this constraint manifests through counter-propagating zeroth Landau levels associated with Weyl nodes of opposite chirality. Here, we experimentally demonstrate how non-Hermiticity can reshape this balance in a synthetic photonic Weyl semimetal. Using engineered gauge fields in one-dimensional multilayer structures, we realize both homogeneous and axial magnetic fields and directly probe the resulting Landau-level spectra. While a homogeneous field produces the expected chirality-balanced zeroth Landau levels, an axial field spatially separates the compensating chiral channels: co-propagating bulk pseudo-Landau levels carry one chirality, whereas the opposite chirality resides in boundary-localized surface states. We show that radiative boundary loss selectively suppresses these surface states, removing them from the long-lived observable spectrum and producing an experimentally accessible chirality imbalance. By reducing boundary loss, we recover the hidden chiral channel and reveal its surface-state origin. These results show that non-Hermiticity, present naturally in photonics, can control and relax fundamental chirality constraints in topological systems, enabling access to otherwise forbidden spectral responses.

</details>

### [Signatures of Rashba-Cavity-Induced Berry-curvature redistribution in the Spin-Hall Conductivity of Semiconductor Artificial Graphene](http://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01373v1)

**Authors:** Maryam Mansouri, Vram Mughnetsyan, Armen Harutyunyan, Albert Kirakosyan, Vidar Gudmundsson  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2606.01373v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** 🔥 `spintronics-quantum-optics interface` **4/5** · `analog quantum simulation` **3/5** · `correlated cavity matter` **3/5** · `exotic spin models in light-matter` **3/5** · `Tavis-Cummings & cavity-many-emitter` **2/5** · `driven-dissipative phase transition` **2/5** · `methods for driven-dissipative` **2/5** · `Keldysh / 2PI / non-Gaussian methods` **1/5** · `non-equilibrium dynamics` **1/5**

<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 5.</b> FIG. 6. Mean photon number in each miniband for the AG inside a cylindrical cavity.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This theoretical work examines how coupling an artificial graphene structure to a cavity field, while simultaneously including Rashba spin-orbit interaction, modifies its electronic band structure. The key finding is that this interplay allows for the precise engineering of topological features, such as opening gaps at specific Dirac points, which manifests as strong, tunable oscillations in the spin-Hall conductivity.

**Why it may be interesting.** The explicit coupling between spin-orbit coupling (a key ingredient in topological physics) and cavity photons (a core concept in cavity QED) to engineer transport properties like $\sigma_{SH}$ is highly relevant for designing novel quantum devices.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The study investigates the combined influence of a far-infrared (FIR) cavity field and Rashba spin-orbit interaction (SOI) on the electronic band structure and transport properties of artificial graphene.

**Main result.** The interplay between Rashba SOI and cavity coupling governs Dirac-point physics, allowing for the opening of gaps at type-II Dirac points and producing pronounced, oscillatory modifications in the spin-Hall conductivity.

**Method.** The authors employ a theoretical Hamiltonian approach, constructing a basis that is a tensor product of the electronic Hilbert space and the photon Fock space, followed by exact diagonalization and the use of the Kubo formalism.

**Model / system.** The system is artificial graphene (AG) made of InAs/GaAs quantum dots, modeled as a honeycomb lattice, coupled to a quantized cavity photon mode. The Hamiltonian includes terms for kinetic energy, periodic potential, Rashba SOI, and electron-photon interaction.

**Key observables.** Spin-Hall conductivity ($\sigma_{SH}$), band structure dispersion, mean photon number ($\langle \hat{N}_{ph} angle$), and the existence/nature of Dirac points (Type-I vs. Type-II).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Cavity geometry (cylindrical vs. linear), Rashba coupling strength, and cavity coupling strength ($g$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis uses the long-wavelength approximation for the vector potential and treats the electron-photon interaction perturbatively in some derivations.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate energy miniband dispersion for different cavity types and coupling constants, and show the evolution of $\sigma_{SH}$ as a function of chemical potential, highlighting oscillatory behavior.

**Paper structure.** The paper builds from defining the full Hamiltonian incorporating all interactions, deriving matrix elements for the coupling terms, solving the effective Hamiltonian via unitary transformations, and finally calculating the transport observable ($\sigma_{SH}$) using the Kubo formula.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We investigate the combined effect of a far-infrared cavity field and Rashba spin-orbit interac- tion on the band structure and transport properties of artificial graphene composed of quazi-2D InAs/GaAs quantum dots. The coupling to cavity photons is modeled by constructing a complete basis as the tensor product of the electronic Hilbert space and the Fock space. Our calculations for the system embedded in a linear cavity predict the existence of both type-I and type-II Dirac points which can be distinguished by their response to Rashba interaction. Namely, Rashba coupling opens a gap at type-II Dirac points, while type-I Dirac points remain gapless. For both cylindrical and linear cavities, we demonstrate the formation of electron-photon hybrid states and Rabi oscillations between energy minibands. Multiple splittings, crossings, and anticrossings between Dirac-band replicas produce pronounced modifications of the spin-Hall conductivity, including strong anisotropy and oscillatory behavior controlled by cavity geometry and polarization. Our results show that the interplay between Rashba and cavity couplings governs Dirac-point physics and provides a route toward tunable polaritonic transport and topological phases in engineered nanostructures.

</details>

### [Integer quantum Hall effect](http://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01744v1)

**Authors:** Hideo Aoki

</figure>

**Summary.** This comprehensive review details the Integer Quantum Hall Effect, establishing its foundation in topological physics. It then advances the concept by analyzing its realization in disordered systems and, most significantly, extends it to non-equilibrium Floquet topological insulators driven by light. This shows how topological quantization can emerge from time-dependent quantum dynamics.

**Why it may be interesting.** The extension to Floquet systems provides a direct link between quantum optics/AMO physics (laser driving) and fundamental condensed matter phenomena (topological quantization), making it highly relevant for open quantum systems research.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper reviews the Integer Quantum Hall Effect (IQHE), explaining its underlying physics from theoretical and experimental standpoints, and placing it within the broader context of topological systems.

**Main result.** The IQHE is fundamentally linked to topological invariants, and the concept has been extended to non-equilibrium systems like Floquet topological insulators, realizing anomalous QHE without a static magnetic field.

**Method.** The analysis employs theoretical tools ranging from effective-mass approximations and Kubo formalism to advanced concepts like Floquet theory and Berry's curvature integration.

**Model / system.** The primary systems studied are two-dimensional electron gases (2DEGs) in strong magnetic fields, realized in semiconductor heterostructures, and graphene subjected to time-periodic driving fields (Floquet systems).

**Key observables.** Quantized Hall conductivity ($\sigma_{xy}$), Landau levels, topological invariants (Chern number), and quasi-energies.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Magnetic field strength ($B$), filling factor ($
u$), magnetic length ($\ell$), and laser frequency ($\Omega$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis relies on the clean limit, the lowest subband approximation, and approximations like the self-consistent Born approximation or high-frequency expansions for Floquet systems.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the topological classification of quantum states, the energy spectra (Landau levels and Floquet subbands), and the characteristic plateaus in Hall and longitudinal conductivity ($\sigma_{xy}$ vs. gate voltage).

**Paper structure.** The paper progresses from the classical and quantum description of the IQHE in static fields, through the inclusion of disorder (localization theory), and culminates in the extension of QHE concepts to time-periodically driven (Floquet) systems.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Integer quantum Hall effect, which is the Hall effect quantized into integer times $e^2/h$ ($e$: elementary charge, $h$: Planck's constant) observed first in two-dimensional electron gases in strong magnetic fields, is reviewed from both theoretical and experimental standpoints. Basic physics underlying the phenomenon is explained. Specifically in this new edition we have a fresh look at how the quantum Hall effect is captured in a perspective of topological systems, since, while the quantum Hall effect is historically the first realization of the topological systems, the field has been delved into a much wider realm of physics of topological systems. We also mention diverse advances such as the quantum Hall effect (QHE) in various materials and contexts that include graphene, oxides and narrow-gap semiconductors, a relation with the fractional quantum Hall effect, and the quantum Hall effect as the resistance standard and further roles in the new SI system. We also expound the Floquet topological insulator (a light-matter coupled system) as a new paradigm in nonequilibrium topological systems, where an anomalous quantum Hall effect in zero magnetic field is realized as theoretically predicted to occur in graphene illuminated by a circularly-polarized laser and experimentally verified recently.

</details>

**Authors:** Sebastian Werner Schmid, Zoltán Balogh, Botond Sánta, Tímea Nóra Török, Zsombor Sinóros-Szabó, György Molnár, János Volk, László Pósa, András Halbritter

The inset shows the resistance scaling of ∆I</figcaption>

**Summary.** This paper analyzes the current noise in nanoscale VO2 devices undergoing a metal-to-insulator transition. By combining noise spectroscopy with 2D network simulations, the authors demonstrate that the large noise enhancement near switching thresholds originates from amplified phase fluctuations at the percolation threshold. This work provides fundamental insights into non-equilibrium dynamics governing phase transitions in nanoscale electronic materials.

**Why it may be interesting.** The work provides a detailed, mesoscopic model of a non-equilibrium phase transition, which shares conceptual links with critical phenomena studied in statistical mechanics, particularly concerning fluctuation-induced noise near critical points.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The study investigates the origin of enhanced current noise during the electrically driven metal-to-insulator transition (MIT) in nanoscale VO2 devices.

**Main result.** The enhanced noise is attributed to amplified phase fluctuations near the percolation threshold, which sets a lower bound on the achievable variance of the switching threshold voltage.

**Method.** The research combines noise spectroscopy measurements with detailed two-dimensional resistor network simulations.

**Model / system.** The physical system is a nanoscale VO2 Mott memristor undergoing resistive switching. The model simulates the thermal and electrical dynamics using a 2D resistor network coupled with temperature-dependent resistance and phase transition probabilities.

**Key observables.** Relative current noise (dI/I), resistive switching (RS) cycle, threshold voltages (V_Set, V_Reset), and the scaling exponent of noise with resistance.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Transition temperature (Tc = 338 K), characteristic fluctuation time constants (tau), and energy barrier variations (epsilon_IMT, epsilon_MIT).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis assumes that the noise can be decomposed into intrinsic fluctuations and phase transition noise, and that the system's behavior can be modeled by coupling thermal and electrical transport equations.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate I-V curves, noise spectra (showing 1/f behavior), and simulations comparing experimental noise measurements against models incorporating intrinsic and transition noise contributions.

**Paper structure.** The paper progresses from initial noise measurements across the switching cycle to detailed simulations that separate intrinsic noise from phase transition noise, culminating in a quantitative comparison between the predicted noise scaling and the observed threshold voltage variability.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We investigate the electrically driven metal-to-insulator transition (MIT) in nanoscale vanadium dioxide (VO$_2$) Mott memristor through noise spectroscopy and two-dimensional resistor network simulations. Our experiments focus on both the insulating phase as the applied voltage approaches the threshold voltage (set transition) and the metallic phase as the voltage is reduced toward the reset voltage (reset transition). In both regimes, we observe an order of magnitude increase in relative current noise near the transition points. To analyze the origin of this noise enhancement, we use simulations that capture the stochastic dynamics of the phase transition. The simulations indicate that the increased noise stems from amplified phase fluctuations near the percolation threshold, where competing metallic and insulating domains lead to dynamic reconfiguration of the conduction paths. In addition, we show that the precursor current fluctuations observed near the switching threshold are consistent with the threshold voltage variability measured in repeated switching cycles, indicating that the noise sets a lower bound on the achievable variance. These findings offer key insights into the non-equilibrium processes governing phase transitions in nanoscale VO$_2$ devices under electrical stimuli.

</details>

**Summary.** This work demonstrates that carrier transport in $	ext{CsPbBr}_3$ perovskites deviates significantly from equilibrium theory at low temperatures. By resolving two coupled populations—a fast hot-exciton gas and a slower localized state—the authors show that energy flow involves coupled kinetics, proving that transport and thermalization are intrinsically linked processes.

**Why it may be interesting.** The explicit breakdown of the separation between transport and thermalization provides a rich platform for studying non-equilibrium energy relaxation, which is highly relevant to open quantum systems and energy harvesting in solid-state quantum emitters.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper investigates the breakdown of the quasi-equilibrium picture for carrier transport in $	ext{CsPbBr}_3$ perovskites at low temperatures due to significant excitonic effects.

**Main result.** Transport is governed by coupled, non-equilibrium kinetics between a fast, hot-exciton gas and a slower, quasi-localized state, showing that transport and thermalization are not separable processes.

**Method.** The study combines transient photoluminescence microscopy (TPLM) measurements with detailed kinetic modeling (two-state feeding model) to track carrier populations and spatial dynamics.

**Model / system.** The system is epitaxial $	ext{CsPbBr}_3$ single crystals. The model treats carrier dynamics using a two-state kinetic framework (Hot Exciton $\leftrightarrow$ Low-Energy Exciton) coupled with diffusion equations to describe energy flow.

**Key observables.** Diffusivity ($D$), Mean-Squared Displacement ($	ext{MSD}$), effective excitonic temperature ($	ext{T}_{	ext{eff}}$), and population transfer rates ($\lambda_1, \lambda_2$).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Low temperature regime ($< 60$ K), characteristic timescales ($\sim 10-100 	ext{ ps}$), and the energy difference between exciton states ($\Delta E \approx 16 	ext{ meV}$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis assumes that the transport dynamics can be decomposed into distinct, coupled kinetic populations (hot vs. localized) rather than a single thermalized ensemble.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the convergence of transport measurements at high T, the divergence below 140 K, and the characteristic two-population signature in MSD curves and PL transients at low T.

**Paper structure.** The paper progresses by first validating the equilibrium model at high T, then demonstrating its failure at low T by resolving two coupled populations (hot/low-energy excitons), and finally quantifying the non-equilibrium nature of the energy flow.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Transport of optically excited carriers in semiconductors is typically described within a quasi-equilibrium picture, where energy is carried by a single thermalized quasiparticle population characterized by well-defined transport coefficients. Here, we demonstrate that in epitaxial CsPbBr3 perovskite single crystals, this picture holds near room temperature - but breaks down dramatically at low temperature. Using transient microscopy, we show that optically measured carrier mobilities match device-scale Hall-effect and field-effect transistor measurements across a broad temperature range, resolving reported discrepancies and validating the equilibrium framework in the free-carrier regime. Below ~60 K, however, when excitonic effects become significant, equilibrium models begin to fail. We observe two coupled populations: a transient (<100 ps) hot-exciton gas with a diffusivity ~25-30 cm<sup>2</sup>/s - greatly exceeding the diffusivity expected for thermalized excitons - and a quasi-localized state that is fed by the cooling of the hot-exciton gas. These results reveal that in CsPbBr3, transport and thermalization are not separable processes: carriers move while still redistributing among internal degrees of freedom, breaking the timescale separation that underpins equilibrium transport theory in conventional semiconductors. By resolving transport at the population level, we can directly access the competing kinetics of exciton formation, interconversion, and cooling, offering a new space for controlling energy flow in perovskite materials and their photonic applications.

</details>

### [Optically programmable and erasable cryogenic flash memory on an undoped Si/SiGe heterostructure](http://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00711v1)

**Authors:** S Rastogi, S Samanta, V Jangir, L Patra, K Modi, A Jain, G Scappucci, U Mukhopadhyay, S Mahapatra  
**Type:** experiment · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2606.00711v1>

**Summary.** This paper reports on the development of an optically programmable and erasable cryogenic flash memory using an undoped Si/SiGe HFET. By leveraging interface traps, the device achieves stable, non-volatile storage of multiple bits at 1.5 K. The results confirm its viability for integration into large-scale, solid-state quantum computing architectures.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work directly addresses hardware requirements for quantum computation by proposing a solid-state, non-volatile memory element compatible with silicon-based quantum platforms, which is highly relevant to quantum information engineering.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper addresses the critical need for scalable, non-volatile memory elements within cryogenic quantum computing architectures to overcome information shuttling bottlenecks.

**Main result.** They successfully demonstrated both binary and multi-bit memory operation in an HFET, showing stable state retention for years and endurance exceeding $10^3$ cycles at 1.5 K.

**Method.** The memory functionality is realized by exploiting the interplay between optical excitation (using an LED) and gate bias in an undoped Si/SiGe HFET to deterministically tune and 'lock' the threshold voltage.

**Model / system.** The physical platform is an undoped Si/SiGe heterojunction field-effect transistor (HFET) fabricated on a Si (100) substrate. The memory state is stored by trapping charges at the semiconductor-oxide interface, which shifts the threshold voltage ($V_{Th}$).

**Key observables.** Threshold voltage ($V_{Th}$), state retention time, cycling endurance, and the distribution of $V_{Th}$ across multiple programmed states.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Operation temperature of 1.5 K; high interface trap density ($D_{it} > 1.6 	imes 10^{12} 	ext{ eV}^{-1}	ext{cm}^{-2}$); endurance $> 10^3$ cycles; retention $> 10^4$ s.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The initial $V_{Th}$ without illumination is unstable, requiring a reset step; state stability relies on charge trapping/detrapping processes at the interface.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the schematic setup, transfer characteristics showing $V_{Th}$ tuning via LED illumination, and quantitative data on state retention over time and endurance over repeated switching cycles for both binary and multi-bit operation.

**Paper structure.** The paper details the physical mechanism, demonstrates the deterministic tuning of $V_{Th}$ using optical excitation, and then rigorously characterizes the device's reliability by measuring state retention and cycling endurance for both two-state and multi-bit operation.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Scalable cryogenic memory is a critical yet unresolved requirement for large-scale quantum computing architectures, particularly for computing-in-memory schemes. We exploit the interplay between optical excitation and gate bias in an undoped Si/SiGe heterojunction field-effect transistor (HFET) to realize non-volatile memory functionality. The device exploits a high interface trap density ($D_{it} > 1.6 \times 10^{12}$~eV$^{-1}$cm$^{-2}$), which, in conjunction with the oxide thickness and dielectric constant, enables effective "locking" of the threshold voltage to the applied gate bias over a wide voltage range. Two of these states can be selected for binary operation, while the availability of multiple stable states within the same device enables multibit data storage. Robust cycling endurance ($>~10^3$ cycles) and long-term state retention ($>~10^4$~s) of the memory states at 1.5 K confirm the suitability of this approach for integration into Si/SiGe-based quantum computing architectures.

</details>

### [The Uhlmann phase of Higher-Order Topological Insulators at Finite Temperature](http://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00479v1)

**Authors:** Shiyu Chen, Yan He  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** statistical mechanics · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2606.00479v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `driven-dissipative phase transition` **3/5** · `Keldysh / 2PI / non-Gaussian methods` **2/5** · `entanglement & information structure` **2/5** · `methods for driven-dissipative` **2/5** · `non-equilibrium dynamics` **2/5** · `non-equilibrium universality` **2/5** · `analog quantum simulation` **1/5** · `correlated / nonlocal dissipation` **1/5**

</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This work develops the Uhlmann phase as a robust topological invariant for Higher-Order Topological Insulators (HOTIs) at finite temperatures. By analyzing the BBH model, the authors show that the topological phase is characterized by the quantization of this phase, and the transition to a trivial phase is precisely located by observing where these quantized jumps vanish at a critical temperature $T_c$.

**Why it may be interesting.** The extension of topological invariants from ground-state properties (like Berry curvature) to finite-temperature mixed states using quantum information concepts (Uhlmann phase) provides a powerful, generalizable tool for studying thermal phase transitions in quantum materials.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The study focuses on characterizing the topological phase of Higher-Order Topological Insulators (HOTIs) when the system is subjected to finite temperatures.

**Main result.** The Uhlmann phase serves as a finite-temperature topological index, where the topological transition is marked by the disappearance of abrupt jumps in this phase at a critical temperature ($T_c$).

**Method.** The analysis generalizes the topological concept from zero temperature (using the Berry connection) to finite temperature by employing the Uhlmann connection and calculating the Uhlmann phase from the density matrix.

**Model / system.** The primary model is the Benalcazar-Bernevig-Hughes (BBH) model, a 2D tight-binding Hamiltonian. The analysis involves calculating the density matrix $ho$ using Boltzmann weights at finite temperature $T$.

**Key observables.** The Uhlmann phase ($\Phi_U$), which is quantized to 0 or $\pi$ in the topological phase, and the critical temperature ($T_c$).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Temperature ($T$), the parameter $m$ in the Hamiltonian, and the lattice momenta ($k_x, k_y$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis assumes that the Uhlmann phase correctly captures the topological invariant at finite temperatures, and approximations are made regarding the dominant components of the density matrix at low temperatures.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the Wannier centers of the BBH model and plot the Uhlmann phase ($\Phi_U$) or the Uhlmann overlap ($	ext{Tr}(ho_0 \mathcal{W}_U)$) as a function of temperature or momentum, demonstrating quantization or continuous variation.

**Paper structure.** The paper first establishes the BBH model and its zero-temperature topology using nested Wilson loops. It then introduces the Uhlmann connection and phase to generalize this concept to finite temperature, deriving the critical temperature $T_c$ from the behavior of the Uhlmann phase.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We have studied the finite-temperature topology of higher-order topological insulators (HOTIs) based on the Uhlmann phase, which is a phase angle of the Uhlmann overlap. As an example of HOTIs, the Hamiltonian of the Benalcazar-Bernevig-Hughes (BBH) model is constructed from Gamma matrices satisfying the Clifford algebra. This specific algebraic structure underpins the model's higher-order topological properties, including the quantization of the Uhlmann phase to $0$ or $π$. This quantization enables us to treat the abrupt jumps of the Uhlmann phase as an indication of the nontrivial topological phase of the BBH model at finite temperature. From the disappearance of these jumps, we determine the critical temperature at which the topological transition occurs. For a special choice of parameters, the Uhlmann overlap and the critical temperature can be computed analytically.

</details>

**Summary.** This work uses a graphene-FET setup to study ferroelectricity in 2D WSe2, revealing that while polarization switching is present, the measured electrical response is heavily influenced by structural disorder and charge trapping. The study distinguishes between intrinsic FE switching and disorder-dominated effects, which manifest as a temperature-dependent transition between hysteresis and anti-hysteresis.

**Why it may be interesting.** The interplay between intrinsic polarization switching (a non-equilibrium, ordered state) and disorder-induced charge trapping/relaxation dynamics provides a rich platform for studying complex, non-equilibrium many-body physics relevant to open quantum systems.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The study investigates the ferroelectric (FE) switching characteristics of CVD-grown 3R-stacked WSe2 bilayers, focusing on how intrinsic structural disorder and multi-domain kinetics affect polarization switching.

**Main result.** The research demonstrates that while FE switching is observable via hysteresis in FET measurements, the response is significantly complicated by disorder, leading to transitions between conventional hysteresis and anti-hysteresis depending on temperature and sweep rate.

**Method.** The authors used a graphene-based ferroelectric field-effect transistor (graphene-FE-FET) architecture to probe the polarization switching of WSe2 bilayers at cryogenic temperatures.

**Model / system.** The physical system is the 3R-stacked WSe2 bilayer grown by CVD, which exhibits sliding ferroelectricity. The device structure is a Graphene/hBN/3R-WSe2 heterostructure, where graphene acts as a highly sensitive charge probe.

**Key observables.** Hysteresis in the R-V_bg curve, shift in the Charge Neutrality Point (CNP) resistance peak, and the transition between hysteresis and anti-hysteresis behavior.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Low temperatures (e.g., 1.6 K), gate voltage sweep rate, and temperature dependence (showing transitions around 80 K and 200 K).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis assumes that the observed hysteresis is due to FE switching, but later notes that trap-assisted effects (Interfacial Charge Layer formation) can obscure or dominate this intrinsic switching.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the atomic stacking, device fabrication, R-V_bg hysteresis loops at low T, and temperature-dependent sweeps showing the transition from hysteresis to anti-hysteresis due to trap dynamics.

**Paper structure.** The paper progresses from establishing the basic FE switching signature (hysteresis) at low T, to analyzing the temperature dependence revealing the competition between FE switching and charge trapping, and finally detailing the role of domain kinetics in causing apparent anti-hysteresis.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Sliding ferroelectricity in van der Waals (vdW) layered systems has emerged as a promising route toward non-volatile nanoscale devices, where interlayer displacement in non-centrosymmetric bilayers generates an out-of-plane polarization. In particular, 3R-stacked bilayer transition metal dichalcogenides (TMDs) grown via chemical vapor deposition (CVD) have been shown to host such polarization due to broken inversion symmetry. However, a detailed investigation of the 2D ferroelectric (FE) properties of CVD-grown 2D films, particularly the role of intrinsic disorder, such as structural defects and domain structure, remains poorly understood. Here, we investigate the FE switching characteristics of CVD-grown 3R-stacked WSe2 using a graphene-based ferroelectric field-effect transistor (graphene-FE-FET) architecture, where graphene serves as a highly sensitive probe of induced charge modulation due to polarization switching of FEs. We show that the growth-induced structural disorder significantly impacts polarization switching, while multi-domain kinetics governs the evolution of the FE response. These findings provide important insights into the design and optimization of FE devices based on vdW materials.

</details>

<a id="paper-2606.02139"></a>
### [Phase-dependent parametric amplification of propagating spin waves in YIG nanostructures enabled by local inhomogeneities](http://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02139v1)

**Authors:** Akira Lentfert, Ephraim Spindler, Björn Heinz, Mathias Weiler, Philipp Pirro  
**Type:** both · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2606.02139v1>
