**Summary.** This work demonstrates that local inhomogeneities in YIG nanostructures can induce phase-dependent, non-adiabatic parametric amplification of spin waves. By providing extra linear momentum, these defects enable efficient amplification in larger structures, a key finding validated by both micromagnetic simulations and Brillouin light scattering experiments. This mechanism offers a promising pathway for developing large-scale, phase-sensitive magnonic circuits for future computing applications.

**Why it may be interesting.** The investigation into engineered material defects (inhomogeneities) to control wave dynamics and enhance nonlinear processes is relevant to understanding how boundary conditions or disorder can be harnessed in quantum/wave systems, analogous to engineered potentials in AMO physics.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper addresses the need for phase-conserving and phase-sensitive amplification mechanisms for spin waves in magnonics for advanced computing applications.

**Main result.** Local inhomogeneities in YIG nanostructures enable non-adiabatic parametric amplification of spin waves, allowing this effect to occur even in structures larger than the spin-wave wavelength.

**Method.** The study combines micromagnetic simulations with experimental validation using micro-focused Brillouin light scattering (BLS) spectroscopy.

**Model / system.** The physical system is Yttrium Iron Garnet (YIG) waveguides. The model investigates spin-wave dynamics under parametric pumping, contrasting ideal (adiabatic) amplification with enhanced, non-adiabatic amplification induced by engineered local inhomogeneities.

**Key observables.** Phase-dependent amplification factor, $F_{amp}(\Delta\varphi)$, and time-resolved intensity measurements of the spin-wave pulse.

**Important parameters / regimes.** The critical role of the additional linear momentum ($k_{inh}$) provided by inhomogeneities, which enables the non-adiabatic regime in larger structures.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The study assumes that the introduction of local inhomogeneities is the key mechanism to overcome size limitations inherent in achieving non-adiabatic parametric amplification.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the setup, compare simulation results for ideal vs. inhomogeneous waveguides showing phase dependence, and present time-resolved BLS measurements confirming the phase-dependent amplification factor.

**Paper structure.** The paper establishes the need for phase-sensitive amplification, presents the theoretical framework involving parametric pumping and the role of inhomogeneities, details the simulation and experimental results demonstrating the non-adiabatic regime, and concludes with implications for large-scale magnonic circuits.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

As magnonics evolves towards non-conventional computing, the development of phase-conserving and phase-sensitive amplification mechanisms becomes increasingly important. A particularly promising approach is non-adiabatic parametric amplification. In this work, the influence of local inhomogeneities on the parallel parametric amplification of spin waves in nano-scale Yttrium Iron Garnet waveguides is investigated. Micromagnetic simulations reveal that in larger pump regions, where only adiabatic amplification is expected, scattering centers provide additional linear momentum that enables non-adiabatic amplification of propagating spin waves. Importantly, the coherence of the process remains unaffected by the scattering and the generation of co-propagating spin waves enhance the effective amplification. Our simulations are confirmed by micro-focused Brillouin light scattering spectroscopy experiments, reproducing both the phase-dependent behavior and the characteristic features of the time-resolved dynamics. These findings demonstrate the flexibility of the parametric amplification process and provide a key mechanism for the development of large-scale spin-wave computing circuits.

</details>

### [Noncollinear spin textures and 90° domain walls in twisted XY magnets](http://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00604v1)

**Authors:** Guanghui Cheng, Shiva T. Konakanchi, Andres E. Llacsahuanga Allcca, Sanjeev Khare, Nithin Abraham, Yuqing Cao, Hechang Lei, Kenji Watanabe, Takashi Taniguchi, Pramey Upadhyaya, Yong P. Chen  
**Type:** both · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2606.00604v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `analog quantum simulation` **3/5** · `exotic spin models in light-matter` **3/5** · `spintronics-quantum-optics interface` **3/5** · `driven-dissipative phase transition` **2/5** · `non-equilibrium dynamics` **1/5**

</figure>

**Summary.** This work reports on the discovery and characterization of moiré magnetism in twisted $	ext{CrCl}_3$ XY magnets. By measuring tunneling magnetoconductance, the authors observe field-induced transitions indicative of coexisting antiferromagnetic and ferromagnetic domains. Crucially, they demonstrate that the noncollinear ground state, featuring $90^\circ$ domain walls, can be electrically tuned using bias voltage, advancing the field of twist-spintronics.

**Why it may be interesting.** The ability to electrically tune magnetic phases via voltage control in a moiré magnet system provides a direct platform for studying spin-charge coupling and novel spintronic functionalities, which is relevant to open quantum systems dynamics.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The study aims to explore noncollinear magnetic phases in twisted magnets, overcoming the limitations imposed by previous studies restricted to uniaxial magnetic systems.

**Main result.** The authors experimentally demonstrate moiré magnetism in twisted $	ext{CrCl}_3$, identifying a robust ground state characterized by $90^\circ$ domain walls and showing voltage control over these noncollinear spin textures.

**Method.** The research combines experimental measurements of tunneling magnetoconductance ($G(H)$) in magnetic tunnel junctions with micromagnetic simulations to model the spin configurations.

**Model / system.** The physical system is twisted $	ext{CrCl}_3$, an easy-plane XY magnet, studied in natural few-layer, twisted double bilayer (tDB), and twisted double trilayer (tDT) configurations. The magnetic behavior is governed by interlayer coupling modulated by the twist angle.

**Key observables.** Tunneling magnetoconductance $G(H)$ and its derivative $|dG/dH|$, which exhibit field-driven kinks corresponding to magnetic phase transitions.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Twist angle (e.g., $0.5^\circ$, $1^\circ$), external magnetic field ($H$), bias voltage ($V_{	ext{bias}}$), and temperature ($T$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis assumes that the tunneling probability is determined by the interlayer spin alignment, and the micromagnetic model captures the essential physics of the interlayer exchange coupling.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the XY spin model and Moiré superlattice structure; $G(H)$ vs. $H$ curves show field-induced kinks for tDB and tDT; phase diagrams map out the dependence of magnetic phases on temperature and field, and bias control is shown via $G(H)$ vs. $V_{	ext{bias}}$.

**Paper structure.** The paper progresses by first establishing the experimental platform using $	ext{CrCl}_3$ in twisted bilayers (tDB), demonstrating the two-kink feature. It then extends the analysis to twisted double trilayers (tDT), revealing the noncollinear ground state and mapping out control mechanisms via temperature and bias voltage.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Twisted moiré magnets are promising in exploring noncollinear magnetic phases, yet current experimental studies have been restricted to uniaxial magnets, limiting the accessible phase space. Here, we demonstrate noncollinear moiré magnetism based on XY magnet CrCl3. The tunneling magnetoconductance of twisted CrCl3 exhibits multiple field-driven transitions in small-twist-angle devices, attributed to the coexisting antiferromagnetic and ferromagnetic domains with distinct susceptibilities. The inferred spin configuration depends on the layer number, reflecting the interlayer coupling strength between twisted layers. This moiré magnetism is remarkably robust, persisting up to twisted double nine-layer stacks. Combined with micromagnetic simulations, we identify the ground state as the predicted "twisted-s" phase featuring 90° domain walls. Finally, we demonstrate voltage control of these noncollinear phases, highlighting the electrically tunable twist-spintronics.

</details>

### [Electronic screening of the friction acting on ions and water molecules in narrow carbon nanotubes](http://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00314v1)

**Authors:** A. W. C. Lau, J. B. Sokoloff

</figure>

**Summary.** This work theoretically models the friction acting on ions and water in carbon nanotubes, using electronic screening from conduction electrons within the Thomas-Fermi framework. The authors compare calculated electronic friction to other sources, concluding that while screening is important, phonon interactions likely dominate the observed ion transport behavior. The study is crucial for understanding nanoscale electrochemistry and ion conduction in confined geometries.

**Why it may be interesting.** The detailed analysis of charge dynamics, screening, and transport mechanisms in nanoscale conductors provides insights into open quantum systems where boundary conditions and environmental coupling (like the electron gas) dictate macroscopic transport properties.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper investigates the friction experienced by ions and water molecules moving through narrow carbon nanotubes (CNTs), focusing on how electronic screening from conduction electrons modifies this friction.

**Main result.** Electronic and image-dragging friction contributions are found to be small compared to phonon friction, suggesting that ion transport is governed by other mechanisms like the Grotthuss mechanism or osmotic pressure.

**Method.** The authors employ theoretical modeling using the Thomas-Fermi approximation to calculate the induced charge density and resulting friction forces, supplemented by analyzing charge establishment time scales.

**Model / system.** The system involves ions and water molecules confined within metallic and semiconducting CNTs. The theory models the electrostatic potential ($\phi$) using Poisson's equation, incorporating screening effects via the Thomas-Fermi length ($\lambda_{	ext{TF}}$).

**Key observables.** Flow rate/mobility of ions and water, induced charge density ($ho_{	ext{ind}}$), and various friction forces ($F_{	ext{fric}}$).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Screening length ($\lambda_{	ext{TF}}$), CNT length ($L$), and the ratio of measurement time to charge establishment time ($t$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The Thomas-Fermi approximation is used for screening, and the analysis often assumes the CNT is an equipotential region during measurement for certain regimes.

**Figures summary.** Figures reference experimental setups for measuring ion conduction and pH changes, and theoretical results illustrate potential profiles ($\phi(x)$) and force calculations.

**Paper structure.** The paper progresses from analyzing experimental observations (differences in flow rates between metallic and semiconducting CNTs) to developing a theoretical framework using the Thomas-Fermi model to calculate electronic screening effects on friction, and finally comparing these calculated forces to other known friction sources.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Li, et. al., have observed a larger flow rate, resulting from osmotic pressure, of protons and water molecules in nanometer scale diameter metallic carbon nanotubes compared to that in semiconducting carbon nanotubes. The flow rate of potassium ions, however, under an applied electric field is almost the same in metallic and semiconducting nanotubes. We propose a simple physical picture to understand these experimental results by examining the effects of screening by conduction electrons in electrically conducting carbon nanotubes on the friction experienced by protons, water molecules, and ions flowing through the nanotube.

</details>

**Summary.** This work demonstrates the ultrafast creation of large dynamic magnetic solitons in garnet films using microwave excitation. By combining advanced microscopy techniques, the authors characterize the soliton's unique formation mechanism and its subsequent collapse into propagating spin waves. This establishes a controllable, nonlinear method for manipulating magnetic states in thin films.

**Why it may be interesting.** The study of nonlinear wave phenomena, such as solitons, in magnetic systems provides a physical realization of nonlinear wave dynamics, which has parallels in nonlinear optics and other areas of AMO physics.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper reports on the ultrafast formation and dynamics of large dynamic magnetic solitons in thin ferrimagnetic garnet films.

**Main result.** They established that microwave-driven solitons are a robust nonlinear phenomenon, characterized by formation inside the linear spin-wave band and subsequent emission of short-wavelength spin waves upon collapse.

**Method.** The research combines time-resolved Brillouin light scattering microscopy ($\mu$-BLS) and scanning transmission X-ray microscopy (STXM) to track magnetization dynamics driven by a microstrip antenna.

**Model / system.** The system involves thin ferrimagnetic garnet films (Bi:YIG and Ga:YIG) exhibiting perpendicular magnetic anisotropy. The dynamics are governed by nonlinear magnetization equations, which are analyzed against linear spin-wave theory.

**Key observables.** Precession angle ($\varphi$), nonlinear frequency shift, spatial decay of magnetization, and emitted short-wavelength spin waves.

**Important parameters / regimes.** The soliton exhibits an exceptionally large spatial extent (tens of microns) and forms much faster than linear spin-wave propagation.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis assumes the soliton is a dissipative excitation requiring continuous pumping, and that the transition from nonlinear to linear dynamics is rapid.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the experimental geometry, the nonlinear shift of FMR vs. precession angle, spatial decay of the precession angle, and time-resolved measurements showing oscillations and onset delays.

**Paper structure.** The paper systematically presents experimental characterization of the system, detailing the observation of the soliton, explaining its formation via a self-limiting mechanism, and finally demonstrating its collapse and subsequent spin-wave emission.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Nonlinear magnetization dynamics offers a rich variety of phenomena ranging from bistability to chaos. Here, we report the ultrafast formation of a dynamic magnetic soliton in thin ferrimagnetic garnet films with perpendicular magnetic anisotropy, driven by the microwave magnetic field of a microstrip antenna. Using time-resolved Brillouin light scattering microscopy and scanning transmission X-ray microscopy, we directly track the build-up of the large-angle precession state. The observed soliton is distinct from other nonlinear magnetic excitations in two key aspects: (i) it forms inside the linear spin-wave frequency band, and (ii) it is exceptionally large, reaching tens of microns beyond the antenna. We explain the soliton formation by the self-limiting mechanism upon a positive nonlinear frequency shift and the spatial extent of the near-field of the antenna. At large distances from the drive, the soliton collapses and emits short-wavelength spin waves via almost instantaneous spatial wavenumber conversion. Time-resolved measurements further reveal a small finite delay during soliton formation, while coherent long-range oscillations appear essentially simultaneously over distances up to 40 micrometers. These results establish microwave-driven solitons as a robust nonlinear phenomenon in thin-film garnets and suggest opportunities for fast, nonlocal manipulation of magnetic states and for applications in novel computational schemes.

</details>

**Authors:** Joonsoo Kim, Hong-Guk Min, Sehwan Park, Jin Cheol Park, Junhyeok Bang, Youngkuk Kim, Ji-Hee Kim

**Summary.** This work demonstrates that monolayer MoSe2 can achieve ideal Carrier Multiplication efficiency, a key step toward next-generation photovoltaics. This breakthrough is attributed to the material's unique electronic structure, specifically 2Eg band nesting, combined with superior hot-carrier dynamics characterized by ballistic transport. The combination of theory and ultrafast spectroscopy validates MoSe2 as a highly promising platform for energy conversion.

**Why it may be interesting.** The study provides a clear, measurable pathway (ballistic transport and band nesting) to engineer high-efficiency energy conversion materials, which is relevant to open quantum systems where energy dissipation and transport dynamics are central.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The primary goal is to achieve ideal Carrier Multiplication (CM) efficiency in photovoltaic applications to surpass the Shockley-Queisser limit.

**Main result.** Monolayer MoSe2 demonstrates ideal CM efficiency, attributed to superior hot-carrier dynamics (ballistic transport) and favorable band nesting, significantly outperforming bulk MoSe2.

**Method.** The study combines ultrafast transient absorption spectroscopy (experiment) with first-principles DFT calculations (theory) to characterize carrier dynamics and electronic structure.

**Model / system.** The system under study is monolayer and bulk MoSe2, a transition metal dichalcogenide (TMD). The analysis focuses on hot-carrier transport, CM pathways enabled by 2Eg band nesting, and the suppression of energy loss mechanisms.

**Key observables.** Ideal CM efficiency (quantified by QY doubling at 2Eg), hot-carrier diffusion coefficient (D), ballistic transport exponent (beta), and the number of CM channels N(I1).

**Important parameters / regimes.** The critical energy threshold is twice the bandgap (2Eg); high diffusion coefficients (up to 1.0 x 10^4 cm^2 s^-1) and ballistic transport regimes are key parameters.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis assumes the ability to resolve scatter-free ballistic transport of hot carriers and that low photon density minimizes nonlinear effects.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the quantized jump in quantum yield at 2Eg, the comparison of CM efficiency across different dimensionalities, and spatiotemporal maps comparing ballistic vs. diffusive hot-carrier spreading in monolayer vs. bulk materials.

**Paper structure.** The paper progresses by first establishing the theoretical benchmark for ideal CM, then using ultrafast spectroscopy to demonstrate this ideal efficiency in monolayer MoSe2, followed by detailed analysis using DFT to explain the mechanism via band nesting and hot-carrier diffusion measurements to confirm the role of ballistic transport.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Carrier multiplication (CM), the process of generating multiple charge carriers from a single photon, offers an opportunity to exceed the Shockley-Queisser limit in photovoltaic applications. Despite extensive research, no material has yet achieved ideal CM efficiency, primarily owing to significant energy losses from carrier-lattice scattering. In this study, we demonstrate that monolayer MoSe2 can attain the theoretical maximum CM efficiency permitted by energy-momentum conservation principle, using ultrafast transient absorption spectroscopy. By resolving the scatter-free ballistic transport of hot carriers and validating our findings with first-principles calculations, we identify the cornerstone of optimal CM in monolayer MoSe2: superior hot-carrier dynamics characterized by suppressed energy dissipation via minimized carrier-lattice scattering, and the availability of abundant CM pathways facilitated by 2Eg band nesting. Comparative analysis with bulk MoSe2 further emphasizes the enhanced CM efficiency in the monolayer, attributed by superior hot-carrier diffusion and access to additional CM pathways. These results position monolayer MoSe2 as a promising candidate for high-performance optoelectronic applications, providing a robust platform for next-generation energy conversion technologies.

</details>

**Authors:** Aniruddha Chakraborty, Kanishk Modi, Dhritiman Bhattacharya, Uditendu Mukhopadhyay, Suddhasatta Mahapatra, Jayasimha Atulasimha  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2606.00824v1>

**Summary.** This paper proposes using spin-orbit-torque (SOT)-driven nanomagnets to generate localized, oscillating magnetic fields for controlling spin qubits. By simulating the system dynamics, the authors demonstrate that this method achieves fast gate operations and long coherence times, offering a highly scalable alternative to current quantum control techniques.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work directly addresses the physical implementation challenges of solid-state quantum computing by proposing a scalable, CMOS-compatible method for coherent qubit manipulation using spintronics.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The core challenge is achieving fast, high-fidelity spin rotation (SR) for quantum gates in spin qubits while minimizing decoherence caused by external magnetic fields.

**Main result.** The proposed technique using SOT-driven nanomagnets generates highly localized, oscillating magnetic fields that enable fast Rabi frequencies (e.g., 52.25 MHz) and maintain long coherence times ($T_2^* \sim 40-250 \mu s$), rivaling or exceeding existing methods.

**Method.** The authors simulate the system dynamics using micromagnetics (LLG equation) to model the SOT-driven magnetization precession, which generates the oscillating stray B-field used to drive the qubit.

**Model / system.** The system involves spin qubits in semiconductor quantum dots controlled by oscillating magnetic fields generated by SOT-driven nanomagnets (Co-Ni/Pt/W). The control mechanism relies on the Spin-Hall effect generating a torque on the nanomagnet's magnetization.

**Key observables.** Rabi frequency ($f_R$), coherence time ($T_2^*$), and the spatial profile of the oscillating magnetic field.

**Important parameters / regimes.** RF charge current ($I_c = 200 \mu	ext{A}$), nanomagnet dimensions (e.g., 100 nm $	imes$ 80 nm), and the target ESR frequency ($\approx 5.6$ GHz).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis assumes that the primary decoherence source is related to the gradient of the static stray field, and that higher harmonics of the oscillating field have negligible impact on fidelity.

**Figures summary.** Figures compare Rabi oscillations showing high fidelity (99.99%) under resonant driving, and plot $T_2^*$ and decoherence rate contributions ($\Gamma_X, \Gamma_Y$) based on the placement of multiple nanomagnets.

**Paper structure.** The paper progresses by first establishing the need for better SR control, detailing the SOT-driven mechanism via micromagnetics simulations, and finally quantifying the performance by calculating Rabi frequencies and coherence times ($T_2^*$) compared to established methods like EDSR.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Spin rotation (SR) is an essential capability for realization of single and two-qubit gates in spin quantum computing (SQC) architectures. To perform SR, resonant AC magnetic fields are either generated by microwave current pulses fed to an antenna, or voltage pulses applied to a gate, in presence of an inhomogeneous Zeeman field. While the former approach is limited by gate-speed and site-selectivity of SR, the latter adds to the decoherence of the spin qubits. Here, we propose an alternative technique for driving high-speed SR without compromising the qubit coherence, by employing spin-orbit-torque (SOT)-driven nanomagnets to produce oscillating magnetic fields, locally at the qubit site. The proposed scheme is highly energy-efficient, scalable, and compatible with the CMOS fabrication technology.

</details>

**Summary.** This work uses time-resolved X-ray Magnetic Scattering (XRMS) to investigate how chiral domain walls evolve on picosecond timescales. By analyzing the dichroic signal, the researchers successfully isolated the dynamics of the internal domain-wall mode. The key finding is that this mode's oscillation frequency softens as the pump fluence increases, providing insight into how external energy modifies the magnetic anisotropy and restoring forces within the domain walls.

**Why it may be interesting.** The use of time-resolved X-ray techniques to isolate and track internal, collective excitations (like domain-wall modes) in magnetic materials provides a powerful, non-equilibrium probe of magnetic order parameters, relevant to understanding ultrafast magnetization dynamics.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The study aims to probe the ultrafast internal dynamics of chiral domain walls within magnetic multilayers on picosecond timescales.

**Main result.** The dichroic signal exhibits a strong oscillatory response whose frequency decreases with increasing pump fluence, indicating pump-induced changes in the effective anisotropy and saturation magnetization that modify the internal domain-wall mode restoring field.

**Method.** Time-resolved x-ray resonant magnetic scattering (XRMS) is employed using femtosecond optical pumping and circularly polarized FEL probes.

**Model / system.** The physical system involves magnetic multilayers with perpendicular magnetic anisotropy hosting chiral Néel-type domain walls stabilized by the interfacial Dzyaloshinskii-Moriya interaction. The in-plane wall magnetization is modeled using a 1D Walker domain-wall profile.

**Key observables.** Helicity-summed signal ($\Delta I_+$) and the dichroic signal ($I_-$), which is sensitive to the in-plane magnetization inside the Néel walls.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Picosecond time scales, pump fluence, and the characteristic frequency of the internal domain-wall mode.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis assumes the dichroic term arises from charge-magnetic interference, and the frequency dependence on fluence is treated phenomenologically.

**Figures summary.** Figures show time-momentum maps and azimuthally integrated transient dichroic responses demonstrating the fluence dependence of the oscillation frequency for different samples.

**Paper structure.** The paper details the experimental setup and diagnostics, analyzes the time-resolved signals by separating contributions, and presents the quantitative findings regarding the frequency softening of the internal domain-wall mode.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We measure picosecond dynamics of labyrinthine stripe domains with chiral Néel-type domain walls using time-resolved x-ray resonant magnetic scattering (XRMS). At the stripe-domain wavevector, the helicity-summed signal shows ultrafast demagnetization, recovery, and a weak oscillatory contribution that we identify as a signature of laser-launched coherent surface phonons. In contrast, the dichroic signal, which is sensitive to the in-plane magnetization inside the Néel walls, shows a strong oscillatory response whose frequency decreases with increasing pump fluence. We attribute this softening to pump-induced changes in the effective anisotropy and saturation magnetization, which modify the restoring field of an internal domain-wall mode. Time-resolved XRMS thus isolates wall-specific dynamics and provides access to internal domain-wall motion in disordered stripe textures on picosecond time scales.

</details>

**Summary.** This paper investigates the optical response of $(	ext{PEA})_2	ext{PbI}_4$ perovskite slabs, demonstrating a thickness-dependent transition from simple excitonic absorption to a visible-frequency stop band. By modeling the dielectric response with a Lorentz oscillator, the authors confirm the existence of a negative real permittivity window, classifying the material as a natural excitonic Reststrahlen medium.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work directly addresses the strong light-matter coupling regime in solid-state materials, providing experimental and theoretical evidence for an excitonic stop band analogous to the infrared Reststrahlen effect, which is highly relevant to quantum optics and photonics.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The study aims to determine if the intrinsic exciton oscillator strength in layered perovskites is sufficient to drive the real permittivity ($	ext{Re}(\epsilon)$) negative, thereby creating a visible-frequency excitonic Reststrahlen-like stop band.

**Main result.** The authors show that $(	ext{PEA})_2	ext{PbI}_4$ slabs exhibit a thickness-dependent crossover from an excitonic dip to a broad near-zero-transmission interval, confirming its status as a cavity-free visible-frequency excitonic Reststrahlen material.

**Method.** The research combines low-temperature transmission spectroscopy of varying thickness slabs with transfer-matrix analysis using an effective Lorentz-oscillator dielectric response model.

**Model / system.** The physical system is the layered halide perovskite $(	ext{PEA})_2	ext{PbI}_4$. The optical response is modeled using a Lorentz-oscillator form for the dielectric function, $\epsilon(E)$, which is then propagated through the slab thickness using transfer-matrix methods.

**Key observables.** Transmittance spectra, the development of a finite negative-$	ext{Re}(\epsilon)$ window, suppressed in-plane field penetration ($|E_\parallel|^2$), and enhanced surface-normal field ($|E_\perp|^2$).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Low temperature (10 K), slab thickness ($d$), and the Rabi splitting ($\hbar\Omega_R \approx 0.5 	ext{ eV}$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis assumes the intralayer exciton manifold is the dominant oscillator driving the optical response, and the negative permittivity window is responsible for the stop-band behavior.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the thickness-dependent crossover from an excitonic dip to a stop-band, the reconstructed negative-$	ext{Re}(\epsilon)$ window, and calculated field maps showing field suppression/enhancement within this band.

**Paper structure.** The paper progresses by first observing the thickness-dependent spectral evolution (experiment), then modeling this crossover using a Lorentz-oscillator dielectric response (theory), and finally interpreting the resulting negative permittivity as evidence for a visible-frequency excitonic Reststrahlen band.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Layered halide perovskites host exceptionally strong excitons, whose optical signatures are usually interpreted as absorptive resonances on a smooth dielectric background. Strong excitons, however, can also reshape the dielectric response itself and drive the real permittivity negative, opening a reflective band: the visible, excitonic analogue of an infrared Reststrahlen band. Whether bare (PEA)$_2$PbI$_4$ slabs reach this regime has remained unclear. Here we show that low-temperature transmission of (PEA)$_2$PbI$_4$ slabs, driven by the intralayer-exciton manifold, evolves with increasing thickness from an excitonic dip into a broad near-zero-transmission interval with compressed Fabry-Pérot-like fringes. Transfer-matrix analysis with an effective Lorentz-oscillator dielectric response reproduces this crossover, reconstructs a finite negative-Re($\varepsilon$) window, and implies near-ultrastrong exciton-photon coupling. Calculated field maps show suppressed in-plane field penetration within this interval and a driven longitudinal response near the high-energy ($\varepsilon=0$) edge. These results identify (PEA)$_2$PbI$_4$ slabs as a cavity-free visible-frequency excitonic Reststrahlen material.

</details>

### [Unified Theory of Quartz Tuning Fork Resonators](http://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00681v1)

**Authors:** Hankyul Koh, Wonho Jhe  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2606.00681v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `Keldysh / 2PI / non-Gaussian methods` **2/5** · `entanglement & information structure` **2/5** · `methods for driven-dissipative` **2/5** · `analog quantum simulation` **1/5** · `correlated / nonlocal dissipation` **1/5** · `driven-dissipative phase transition` **1/5** · `measurement-induced transitions` **1/5** · `non-equilibrium dynamics` **1/5** · `non-equilibrium universality` **1/5** · `quantum measurements` **1/5**

In the simulations, hydrodynamic loading was incorporated using...</figcaption>
</figure>

**Summary.** This paper introduces a unified continuum electroelastic theory to accurately model the electrical resonance spectra of quartz tuning forks. It demonstrates that the measurable electrical signal depends critically on the overlap between the mechanical mode's excitation strength and the electrode's readout efficiency. This provides a rigorous, first-principles basis for precision electromechanical characterization of piezoelectric resonators.

**Why it may be interesting.** While focused on classical solid-state physics, the rigorous mathematical framework of projecting a complex coupled system onto observable subspaces, and quantifying observability via overlap integrals, shares conceptual parallels with analyzing coupling strengths in open quantum systems or cavity QED.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Existing theories fail to explain the robust, electrode-dependent features observed in the electrical resonance spectra of quartz tuning forks (QTFs).

**Main result.** The measured electrical response requires non-zero overlap in both the excitation ($\Theta$) and readout ($H$) coupling terms, providing a unified framework for electromechanical characterization.

**Method.** The authors developed a unified continuum electroelastic framework by combining piezoelectric electrodynamics, variational structural dynamics, and symmetry-selected observability.

**Model / system.** The system is a quartz tuning fork, modeled as an electrically driven piezoelectric resonator. The theory uses continuum mechanics and projects the full electroelastic problem onto reduced mechanical and electrode-defined subspaces.

**Key observables.** Electromechanical Visibility ($V_n$), Effective Excitation ($\Theta_{	ext{eff}n\ell}$), Effective Readout ($H_{	ext{eff}\ell n}$), and the measured current $	ilde{I}_{	ext{meas}}(\omega)$.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Resonance frequencies ($\omega_n$), damping rates ($\gamma_n$), and coupling coefficients derived from the material's piezoelectric tensor ($e_{kij}$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The electric field adjusts instantaneously to electrode boundary conditions because the mechanical resonance frequency is much lower than the electromagnetic propagation timescale.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 compares wide-band electrical spectra under mass loading, showing the evolution of mechanical poles versus observed electrical peak trajectories.

**Paper structure.** The paper develops the unified electroelastic theory, derives the effective coupling operators ($\Theta, H$), and then applies perturbation theory to analyze how imperfections (mass loading, nonuniformity) affect the measurable spectral weight and pole positions.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Quartz tuning forks, functioning as electrically driven piezoelectric resonators, have long served as exceptionally stable and widely adopted timing references in diverse domains of research and industry. Yet, electrically measured resonance spectra often exhibit robust features that remain unexplained within existing theoretical descriptions. Here we develop a unified continuum electroelastic framework that combines piezoelectric electrodynamics, variational structural dynamics, and symmetry-selected electromechanical observability. Such a first-principles theory shows quantitative agreement with experimental results, which demonstrates electrical observability emerges not just from the underlying mechanical eigenmodes alone. Our results provide a unified framework for electrically driven piezoelectric resonators as well as a rigorous basis for precision electromechanical characterization.

</details>

In the upper panel, η1 = 1, η2 = 1/ √</figcaption>

<img src="2606.02043_figures/2606.02043_fig2.jpg" alt="Figure 2">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> Figure 2. Numerical simulation showing the interaction of a tracer soliton with a bichromatic dilute SG composed of two noninteracting species. **Summary.** This paper develops a microscopic, field-based theory for interacting KdV solitons by deriving exact charge, current, and velocity fields from the Inverse Scattering Transform. It successfully tracks individual solitons through complex interactions, demonstrating that the system's collective behavior naturally recovers established macroscopic theories like Generalized Hydrodynamics. This unifies wave-based and particle-like descriptions of soliton dynamics.

**Why it may be interesting.** The connection drawn between the wave-based IST formalism and emergent particle-like descriptions, especially the recovery of kinetic theory, provides a powerful, unified framework for understanding non-equilibrium dynamics in nonlinear wave systems relevant to AMO and quantum optics.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The primary challenge is developing a microscopic framework to track individual solitons as distinct entities during strong interactions, moving beyond the limitations of asymptotic approximations.

**Main result.** The authors establish that the dynamics of interacting solitons can be described by independent continuity equations for their charges, leading to the recovery of kinetic theory and Generalized Hydrodynamics (GHD) as scaling limits.

**Method.** The work utilizes the Inverse Scattering Transform (IST) of the Korteweg-de Vries (KdV) equation to derive exact space-time fields for charge, current, and velocity associated with individual solitons.

**Model / system.** The system under study is the interaction of Korteweg-de Vries (KdV) solitons. The analysis develops a field-based description using conserved quantities derived from the IST spectral framework.

**Key observables.** Charge density ($Q_m$), current density ($J_m$), and the derived velocity field ($v_m$) of individual solitons.

**Important parameters / regimes.** The analysis explores different regimes, including the strongly interacting regime, the rarefied gas regime, and the thermodynamic limit ($N 	o \infty$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The framework assumes that the dynamics can be decomposed into independent evolutions for each soliton's charge, with interactions being encoded in initial correlations.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the computed charge, current, and velocity fields during soliton interactions, showing localization and perturbation. Other figures compare exact characteristic trajectories against approximations derived from effective velocity theories.

**Paper structure.** The paper progresses from defining the microscopic field description using IST to deriving continuity equations for individual soliton charges. It then shows how these equations yield effective velocity fields, ultimately connecting the microscopic description to macroscopic kinetic theories like GHD.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Solitons in integrable systems exhibit a dual wave-particle character, yet their identification as individual objects becomes ambiguous during interactions, where they deform and delocalize. We develop a microscopic, field-based description that resolves this issue by introducing exact space-time fields of charge, current, and velocity derived from the inverse scattering transform (IST) of the Korteweg-de Vries (KdV) equation. This framework enables individual KdV solitons to be tracked throughout interactions, providing a quantitative description of their trajectories and deformation beyond the asymptotic regime. We show that the dynamics of $N$ interacting solitons can be formulated in terms of $N$ independent continuity equations, in which interactions are encoded in initial correlations that are subsequently propagated. From this microscopic viewpoint, effective velocities and hydrodynamic behavior emerge upon coarse graining, recovering kinetic theory of soliton gases and Generalized Hydrodynamics as scaling limits. Our results establish a direct connection between the wave-based IST formalism and particle-like emergent descriptions, offering a unified framework for soliton dynamics across scales.

</details>

### [Phonon-driven nodal surface superconductivity of Fermi arcs](http://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02371v1)

**Authors:** Francesco Buccheri, Alessandro de Martino, Jeroen van den Brink  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2606.02371v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `Keldysh / 2PI / non-Gaussian methods` **2/5**

**Summary.** This work theoretically explains how phonon interactions, combined with Coulomb repulsion, can induce superconductivity exclusively on the topological Fermi arcs of Weyl semimetals. The key finding is that weak surface screening favors an inter-arc pairing channel, which results in the observationally important nodal superconducting gap structure. This suggests that surface engineering of the dielectric environment can control the superconducting properties.

**Why it may be interesting.** The detailed theoretical framework for deriving pairing mechanisms from lattice vibrations and surface topology is highly relevant for understanding emergent superconducting states in topological materials, bridging concepts from lattice dynamics and superconductivity theory.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper addresses the mechanism driving the formation of a superconducting gap specifically on the topological surface states (Fermi arcs) of Weyl semimetals, while the bulk remains non-superconducting.

**Main result.** Nodal superconductivity arises generically from the combination of phonon-mediated attraction and weakly screened long-range Coulomb repulsion acting on disconnected Fermi-arc segments.

**Method.** The authors use a continuum description, deriving effective electron-electron interactions (Coulomb and electron-phonon) and solving the resulting gap equation in the mean-field approximation.

**Model / system.** The system is time-reversal-invariant Weyl semimetals, modeled using a tight-binding approach that yields surface Fermi arcs. The theory incorporates coupling to both surface and bulk phonons and long-range Coulomb interactions.

**Key observables.** Superconducting gap structure (nodal vs. nodeless), critical temperature ($T_c$), and the relative strengths of intra-arc and inter-arc pairing channels.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Coulomb screening strength ($q_s$), phonon frequencies ($\omega$), and the geometry of the Fermi arcs.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The bulk density of states is negligible compared to the surface DOS near the chemical potential, and the analysis relies on linearizing the gap equation near $T_c$.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the Fermi arcs in the surface Brillouin zone, the competition between intra-arc and inter-arc scattering processes, and the dependence of $T_c$ on the screening momentum.

**Paper structure.** The paper progresses by first deriving the electronic structure and the electron-phonon coupling Hamiltonian, then formulating the effective electron-electron interaction, and finally solving the gap equation by distinguishing between intra-arc and inter-arc pairing channels to determine the superconducting gap symmetry and $T_c$.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

According to recent observations, the topological surface states of Weyl semimetals may develop a superconducting gap, while bulk superconductivity remains absent. What drives the formation of this novel superconducting state is an open question. Here, we show that this phenomenon can arise from the interaction of Fermi arc electrons with both surface and bulk phonons in time-reversal-invariant Weyl semimetals. We identify two competing pairing channels, intra-arc and inter-arc, whose relative strength is governed by the efficiency of Coulomb screening at the surface. The combined effect of the Fermi arcs being disconnected and the weak screening of the Coulomb repulsion at the system's surface causes nodes to appear in the superconducting gap, as observed recently by photoelectron spectroscopy experiments on PtBi2. This suggests manipulation of the Coulomb screening, e.g. by a surface layer coating, as a pathway to engineer the critical temperature, as well as size and symmetry of the surface superconducting gap.

</details>

**Summary.** This work demonstrates a novel method to enhance the detection of coherent acoustic phonons in semiconductors by generating a propagating acoustic grating using a high-repetition-rate laser pulse train. By exploiting Bragg reflection, the Brillouin light scattering signal is resonantly enhanced by factors up to 30 times compared to single-pulse excitation. This technique provides a highly sensitive tool for studying high-frequency acoustic dynamics in solid-state materials.

**Why it may be interesting.** The use of periodic excitation to engineer a macroscopic optical/acoustic resonance (Bragg enhancement) is a powerful analogy to engineered quantum systems, particularly in cavity QED or photonic structures, demonstrating controlled enhancement of a fundamental physical signal.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The study aims to enhance the detection of Brillouin light scattering (BLS) from coherent acoustic phonons in semiconductors by utilizing a propagating acoustic grating.

**Main result.** The multi-pulse approach generates a propagating acoustic grating, leading to a significant resonant enhancement of the BLS signal, observed to be 10-30-fold in GaAs and (Cd,Zn)Te.

**Method.** The technique involves using a high-repetition-rate laser pulse train to create the grating, followed by time-domain Brillouin scattering measurements in a pump-probe configuration.

**Model / system.** The experiment is performed on bulk semiconductors (GaAs and (Cd,Zn)Te) at cryogenic temperatures, utilizing a two-color differential reflectivity pump-probe setup.

**Key observables.** Resonant peaks in the spectral dependence of the TDBS amplitude, the acoustic phonon frequency (f_ac), and the enhancement factor.

**Important parameters / regimes.** The enhancement factor is limited by the number of strain pulses (N) and the finite spectral width of the probe pulse; the resonance condition is governed by the Bragg reflection relation.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis models the signal using a geometric series sum for the total scattered field, assuming normal incidence of the probe beam.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the comparison between conventional single-pulse and multi-pulse TDBS approaches, and show the spectral dependence of the enhanced signal.

**Paper structure.** The paper progresses from introducing the concept of coherent phonon generation to detailing the multi-pulse grating formation, deriving the enhanced signal mathematically via geometric series summation, and finally presenting experimental results on GaAs and (Cd,Zn)Te.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Generation and detection of coherent phonons in semiconductors by femtosecond optical pulses is a powerful tool for high-frequency acoustic control of their electronic properties. Here, we demonstrate a propagating one-dimensional acoustic grating in bulk semiconductors using above-band-gap excitation by a train of laser pulses with high repetition rate of 1 GHz. This approach enables shaping of the coherent acoustic phonon spectrum and leads to a significant enhancement of Brillouin light scattering at selected probe wavelengths in pump-probe configuration. We demonstrate this effect at a cryogenic temperature of 5 K in prototypical semiconductor systems, namely bulk crystalline GaAs and (Cd,Zn)Te, which serve as benchmark materials for the proposed method. The spectral dependence of the time-domain Brillouin light scattering amplitude exhibits resonant peaks at discrete probe wavelengths arising from Bragg reflection of the probe light by the propagating acoustic grating. A 10-30-fold resonant enhancement of the signal amplitude is observed for GaAs and (Cd,Zn)Te, determined by the finite spectral width of the probe pulse. With further spectral narrowing, enhancements of the order ~ 100-150 are expected, set by the number N of strain pulses in the acoustic grating within the sample and ultimately limited by the material parameters and sample thickness.

</details>

**Summary.** This work introduces a node-resolved framework to analyze collective dynamics in complex networks by exploiting spectral localization in the graph Laplacian. By showing that structural disorder concentrates spectral modes onto specific nodes, the authors allow stability analysis to be performed directly in physical node space rather than abstract spectral space. This restores physical interpretability and predicts the onset and nature of emergent collective behaviors.

**Why it may be interesting.** The concept of spectral localization and mapping abstract spectral modes back to physical nodes is a powerful tool for interpreting complex, high-dimensional dynamics, which has parallels in understanding collective excitations in quantum many-body systems.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper addresses how to restore physical interpretability to collective dynamics in complex, heterogeneous systems whose stability is traditionally analyzed only in abstract spectral space.

**Main result.** Structural disorder in networks induces spectral localization, allowing stability properties to be interpreted directly in node space and enabling the identification of specific nodes driving collective transitions.

**Method.** The authors develop a node-resolved framework by exploiting spectral localization of the graph Laplacian eigenvectors to decouple the full system Jacobian into localized, node-dependent components.

**Model / system.** The analysis uses general networked systems, exemplified by reaction-diffusion models (like the Brusselator) and SIS dynamics, coupled via the network Laplacian structure.

**Key observables.** Localized Master Stability Function (MSF), Mean Normalized Eigenvalue Error (MNE), and the identification of dominant nodes responsible for instability.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Network size (N), parameter heterogeneity (s), and the coupling structure (Adjacency vs. Laplacian).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The core assumption is the existence of strong spectral localization, which allows the complex dynamics to be accurately approximated by local dynamics governed by a 'host node.'

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate mode-node mapping via heatmaps of Laplacian eigenvectors, compare MNE across different network types (ER, BA, real-world), and show time series demonstrating various collective states like amplitude chimera and oscillon patterns.

**Paper structure.** The paper progresses from establishing the failure of classical spectral theory to proposing the localization-based node-resolved framework. It then applies this framework to analyze stability, predict transition onsets, and characterize various nonlinear collective states (e.g., chimera states) based on the localized spectrum.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Real complex systems often exhibit collective transitions emerging from interactions across many components. Classical stability theory describes such transitions in spectral space, where dynamics is organized by spatially extended global eigenmodes whose collective nature obscures direct association with individual physical components. Here, we show that structural disorder in empirical random networks can fundamentally alter this picture. These properties induce spectral localization, causing Laplacian modes to concentrate on small subsets of nodes and producing a mode--node correspondence in which collective dynamics becomes governed predominantly by the local behavior of a dominant node together with their effective coupling to the surrounding network. As a consequence, stability properties can be interpreted directly in node space rather than purely in spectral space. Exploiting this principle, we develop a node-resolved framework that predicts transition onsets, identifies the nodes responsible for emergent collective behavior, and restores interpretability in systems where classical modal theories fail. In heterogeneous reaction networks, the same mechanism gives rise to exotic collective states where different subsets of nodes develop distinct dynamical behaviors beyond those associated with homogeneous assumptions. Our results show that complex network structures naturally generate spectral localization, revealing the microscopic drivers of macroscopic dynamics.

</details>

**Summary.** This work reports on the use of moiré engineering in twisted WSe2 bilayers to control optoelectronic properties. By tuning the twist angle, researchers observe enhanced interlayer exciton emission and strong exciton-phonon coupling. This establishes the TBL as a highly tunable platform for studying quantum phenomena in 2D materials.

**Why it may be interesting.** The detailed study of exciton-phonon coupling in a tunable, engineered quantum material platform (TBL) provides quantitative parameters for understanding open quantum systems dynamics in 2D semiconductors.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The study investigates how engineering the twist angle in twisted WSe2 bilayers modifies the excitonic landscape and controls phonon-mediated recombination processes.

**Main result.** The moiré potential significantly enhances interlayer exciton emission and stabilizes these excitons by redistributing carriers into indirect valleys, while also suppressing defect-related emission.

**Method.** The research employs low-temperature photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy and temperature-dependent Raman spectroscopy to probe electronic and vibrational properties.

**Model / system.** The physical system is a twisted bilayer of tungsten diselenide (WSe2/WSe2) encapsulated in hBN, forming a moiré superlattice. The analysis models temperature dependence using the Varshni and O’Donnell equations.

**Key observables.** Interlayer exciton emission ($	ext{X}_{	ext{I}}$), phonon replicas ($	ext{X}_{	ext{p}1	ext{I}}, 	ext{X}_{	ext{p}2	ext{I}}$), PL intensity, and Raman mode splittings.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Twist angle ($	heta$), temperature (3 K to 300 K), and fitting parameters like Varshni ($\alpha, eta$) and O’Donnell ($S, \hbar\omega$) constants.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The electronic structure modification is assumed to be dictated by the moiré potential, and temperature effects are modeled using established empirical equations (Varshni/O’Donnell).

**Figures summary.** Figures show PL spectra comparing different stacking orders (ML, NBL, TBL) and Raman spectra demonstrating mode splitting due to moiré potential hybridization.

**Paper structure.** The paper systematically compares PL and Raman signatures across various stacking configurations (ML, NBL, ABL, TBL) to quantify how twist angle controls exciton localization, phonon coupling, and temperature-dependent bandgap renormalization.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We report light emission from the moire superlattice of a twisted bilayer of tungsten diselenide (WSe2/WSe2) encapsulated in insulating hexagonal boron nitride (hBN). The low-temperature photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy reveals signatures of moire-potential induced strong interlayer excitonic emission and phonon-assisted recombination, while the twisting significantly suppresses the emission from localized defect-bound excitons. The moire potential redistributes carriers into indirect valleys, thereby enhancing recombination efficiency and stabilizing the interlayer excitons. Our findings establish that precise control of twist angle and dielectric environment provides a new route for engineering excitonic systems for exploring exciton-phonon interactions and associated quantum phenomena in transition metal dichalcogenides.

</details>

**Authors:** Tsz Chung Cheng, Yuichiro Kurokawa, Hiromi Yuasa

<img src="2606.01114_figures/2606.01114_fig1.jpg" alt="Figure 1">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> Figure 1. Schematic diagram of the distributed 3D FFT implementation. The magnetisation is first split along the x-axis using slab decomposition. The magnetisation is padded, and the FFT is computed along the y and x axes. An all-to-all transpose is then performed to shard the vectors along the y-axis, followed by zero-padding and FFT computation along the x-axis. Then, a point-to-point multiplication is performed using the demagnetisation kernel. For the backward pass, the steps are reverted with truncation replacing padding and inverse FFT replacing the forward FFT.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2606.01114_figures/2606.01114_fig2.jpg" alt="Figure 2">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> Figure 2. Simulated magnetisation components in the muMAG standard problem 4. The results from Mumax3 and the original non-distributed version of magnum.np were compared with those from the distributed implementation using 8 ranks. The results match both references, validating the implementation of the distributed LLG-RKF45 solver, demagnetisation, and exchange effective field.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2606.01114_figures/2606.01114_fig3.jpg" alt="Figure 3">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 3.</b> Figure 3. Performance (time per iteration) of various effective fields on two single-node NVIDIA H100 Hopper GPU systems: HBM3 (solid lines, 3,352 GB/s/GPU, up to 8 GPUs via NVSwitch) and HBM2e (dashed lines, 2,396 GB/s/GPU, up to 4 GPUs via NVLink). (a) The demagnetisation field is computationally and communication-intensive; performance gains begin at around 106 cells on both systems. Despite the 40% difference in memory bandwidth, both systems exhibit near-identical relative scaling, confirming that speedup is governed by the algorithm’s communication-to-computation ratio. (b) The exchange field requires a halo exchange at each slab boundary. Significant overhead is observed below 106...</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2606.01114_figures/2606.01114_fig4.jpg" alt="Figure 4">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 4.</b> Figure 4. Performance (time per iteration) of the demagnetisation field. Non-distributed magum.np was evaluated on a single NVIDIA H100 HBM3 GPU, and a CPU compute node with 2 Intel Xeon Platinum 8490H 60-core CPUs (no NUMA-pinning). The distributed magnum.np implementation enabled NUMA-based pinning to reduce cross-NUMA communication and yielded a significant performance gain at 106 cells, bringing it to within 10 times slower than a single GPU.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2606.01114_figures/2606.01114_fig5.jpg" alt="Figure 5">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 5.</b> Figure 5. A simulated i-DMI inducing multilayer. (a) Mz at the top layer after relaxation via LLG-RKF45 solver. (b) A zoom-in view of (a) with arrows depicting in-plane components. (c) Cross-section across a domain wall where arrows depict the x and z-components. A vortex is formed in the film with weak i-DMI.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This work presents a novel, Python-native, multi-GPU framework built on PyTorch Distributed to accelerate micromagnetic simulations. By efficiently distributing the computational mesh, the authors achieve substantial speedups in calculating critical effective fields like the demagnetization field. This advancement allows researchers to model larger and more complex spintronic devices previously out of reach due to computational limitations.

**Why it may be interesting.** While focused on classical micromagnetics, the core contribution is a highly optimized, portable, and scalable numerical framework using modern deep learning infrastructure (PyTorch Distributed) for solving complex partial differential equations, which is highly relevant for simulating quantum many-body dynamics.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The primary goal is to accelerate computationally intensive finite difference micromagnetic simulations, which are essential for studying nanomagnetism and spintronics.

**Main result.** The authors achieved significant speedups, reporting up to a 7.0x acceleration for demagnetization field calculations across 8 GPUs via NVLink, and a 6.8x speedup on CPU using NUMA pinning.

**Method.** They extended the Python-native FDM solver `magnum.np` by integrating PyTorch Distributed to enable efficient, multi-GPU computation across sharded simulation meshes.

**Model / system.** The simulations model micromagnetism using the Landau-Lifshitz-Gilbert (LLG) equation, applied to systems like permalloy magnets and Pt/Gd/Co/Ni stacks.

**Key observables.** Simulation time per iteration step, calculated effective fields (demagnetization, exchange), and magnetization components ($M_z$, in-plane components).

**Important parameters / regimes.** The simulation size can reach $23.6$ million cells; performance is benchmarked across various hardware, including H100 GPUs and multi-socket CPUs.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The framework assumes the simulation mesh can be sharded along one axis, and performance scaling is limited by communication overheads like halo exchange latency.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the schematic of the distributed 3D FFT implementation, comparisons of simulated magnetization components for standard problems, and performance scaling plots comparing GPU vs. CPU execution times.

**Paper structure.** The paper details the extension of the solver using PyTorch Distributed, presents performance benchmarks across different effective field calculations (demagnetization, exchange), and validates the results against established solvers like Mumax3.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Micromagnetic simulations are essential tools in nanomagnetism and spintronics research. Although widely adopted solvers like Mumax3 and the Python-native magnum.np use GPU acceleration to improve performance, these tools are limited to single-device computation. In this work, we present the first Python-native multi-GPU micromagnetic framework by extending magnum.np with PyTorch Distributed. This leverages high-speed communication and computation across multiple GPUs while retaining the benefits of ease of installation, platform-agnostic design, and compatibility with Python. For computationally intensive demagnetisation effective-field calculations, we achieve a 7.0x speedup across 8 GPUs connected via NVLink, whereas Halo exchange required for Heisenberg exchange shows limited scaling due to kernel dispatch latency. We also demonstrated the framework's versatility by achieving a 6.8x speedup in demagnetisation field computation on CPU with NUMA pinning via the MPI backend of PyTorch Distributed. Faster turnaround times will enable researchers to explore larger, more complex systems and accelerate the design cycle for novel spintronic devices.

</details>

**Summary.** This paper numerically studies rogue wave formation in the 1D-NLSE under noise-induced modulational instability. It systematically analyzes these extreme events by comparing them against known exact solutions, finding that the dynamics are best described by the collision of a few nonlinear modes. The results suggest that RWs arise from the synchronization of these underlying nonlinear structures.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work is highly relevant to the study of open quantum systems and quantum optics because the 1D-NLSE is a canonical model for nonlinear wave propagation, and the study of extreme events like rogue waves provides insights into nonlinear energy localization and wave-wave interactions.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper investigates the formation and characteristics of rogue waves (RWs) that emerge during the nonlinear stage of noise-induced modulational instability (MI) in a plane wave.

**Main result.** The study shows that RWs are significantly more numerous than in linear systems, and their dynamics are best approximated by the collision of two nonlinear modes, such as solitons or breathers.

**Method.** The analysis combines numerical simulations of the 1D-NLSE with rigorous comparisons of the observed RWs against known exact solutions derived via the Inverse Scattering Transform (IST).

**Model / system.** The primary model is the one-dimensional nonlinear Schrödinger equation (1D-NLSE) of the focusing type. The analysis compares RWs generated from noise-induced MI against various exact solutions, including multi-soliton and multi-breather collision models.

**Key observables.** Frequency of RW occurrence (monitored via the fourth-order moment $\kappa_4$), the intensity distribution of RWs, and the deviation measure $D_\Lambda$ used to quantify the fit quality between observed RWs and theoretical models.

**Important parameters / regimes.** The analysis depends on the initial noise spectrum (Gaussian vs. MI spectrum) and the parameters defining the best-fit models, such as soliton/breather eigenvalues ($\lambda_n$) and collision parameters.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The core hypothesis tested is that RWs emerge due to the synchronization of only a few nonlinear modes (solitons or breathers) within the complex wavefield, although this is noted as potentially naive.

**Figures summary.** Figures compare the Probability Density Functions (PDFs) of intensity and phase slope across different spectral regimes (MI vs. Gaussian) and show nested rectangular regions used for calculating the deviation measure $D_\Lambda$ during model fitting.

**Paper structure.** The paper progresses from establishing the physical model (1D-NLSE) and the phenomenon (RWs from MI) to developing advanced mathematical tools (IST, dressing method) and finally comparing the numerically observed RWs against a comprehensive database of known exact solutions (soliton/breather collisions).

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

In the framework of the one-dimensional nonlinear Schrodinger equation (1D-NLSE) of the focusing type, the present paper studies numerically rogue waves (RWs) that emerge in the nonlinear stage of noise-induced modulational instability of a plane wave. For a large ensemble of simulations, all sufficiently large local maximums of the wavefield (i.e., RWs) are systematically collected and analyzed. It is shown that the frequency of occurrence of such maximums begins to increase from zero at the time, when the fourth-order moment of amplitude reaches its first (largest) local maximum, and then grows in an oscillatory manner approaching its asymptotic value at long time. Near the statistically stationary state, the 1D-NLSE generates a much larger number of RWs than a comparable linear system, but, in average, one RW affects a much smaller spatiotemporal area. The distribution of these RWs by maximum intensity represents an exponential-like function without noticeable changes in behavior, indicating a similar origin for RWs of significantly different amplitudes. The collected RWs are compared within a sufficiently large spatiotemporal window with nine exact solutions, of which two models reproduce RW dynamics much better than the others: a general collision of two solitons and a general collision of two Tajiri-Watanabe breathers; RWs are fitted with these collisions using pre-compiled databases of such solutions. The parameters of the two models turn out to be quite similar to the direct scattering transform spectrum of the whole wavefield, supporting a hypothesis that RWs may emerge due to synchronization of a few nonlinear modes (solitons or breathers) in presence of many other nonlinear modes of the wavefield.

</details>

### [Quantum Statistics and Structural Topology Govern Thermal Transport in Two-Dimensional Monolayer Amorphous Carbon](http://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01408v1)

**Authors:** Gizem Kurt, Haldun Sevincli

</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This work theoretically investigates heat conduction in 2D amorphous carbon by coupling structural disorder ($q_3$) with quantum statistics. Using the Landauer formalism, the authors show that quantum effects significantly suppress thermal conductivity compared to classical models. The findings establish that the overall degree of amorphization is the dominant factor controlling heat flow in these disordered quantum materials.

**Why it may be interesting.** The rigorous quantum mechanical treatment of heat transport in amorphous solids, particularly the comparison between quantum and classical limits, provides insights into energy dissipation mechanisms in disordered quantum materials.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The study investigates how structural topology, degree of amorphization, and quantum statistics govern the thermal transport in two-dimensional amorphous solids.

**Main result.** Quantum thermal conductivity is significantly lower than classical predictions, and the thermal transport is primarily governed by the global degree of amorphization ($q_3$) rather than specific microscopic structural details.

**Method.** The authors employ a fully quantum mechanical framework using the Landauer formalism and Green's function techniques to calculate thermal conductivity ($\kappa$).

**Model / system.** The physical system is two-dimensional monolayer amorphous carbon (MAC). Configurations are generated using various amorphization algorithms (e.g., Zachariasen-type networks) and characterized by the local bond order parameter, $q_3$.

**Key observables.** Quantum Thermal Conductivity ($	ext{TC}$), Participation Ratio ($	ext{PR}$), Local bond order parameter ($q_3$), and vibrational mode characteristics (propagons, diffusons, locons).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Temperature ($T$), degree of amorphization ($q_3$), and the ratio of classical to quantum conductivity ($\kappa_{cl}/\kappa_q$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis relies on the Landauer formalism and assumes the system can be modeled by partitioning it into a central amorphous region and semi-infinite crystalline reservoirs.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate representative MAC structures, the dependence of $\kappa$ on $T$ and $q_3$, and comparisons between quantum and classical conductivity predictions.

**Paper structure.** The paper systematically analyzes structural characterization (using $q_3$ and bond statistics), vibrational mode analysis (using PR and polarization spheres), and finally calculates $\kappa(T)$ by comparing quantum (Bose-Einstein) and classical limits.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We investigate the quantum thermal conductivity (TC) of two-dimensional monolayer amorphous carbon (MAC). We employ three distinct amorphization algorithms to generate various possible MAC configurations, ranging from Zachariasen-type continuous random networks to nanocrystallites embedded in random networks. The local bond order parameter, q3, is used to quantify the amorphousness of the structures, and TC is computed as functions of q3 and temperature. This framework enables us to assess how structural topology, degree of amorphization, and quantum statistics contribute to heat conduction in a two-dimensional amorphous solid. At room temperature, TC values are predicted to range between 3.5 to 10 W/m/K, in agreement with recent experiments. Analysis of vibrational modes reveals that, while the modes of these 2D amorphous structures fall into the usual categories, namely, propagons, diffusons, and locons, their polarization characteristics display distinct behavior. Owing to the fully quantum mechanical framework, we examine both low- and high-temperature characteristics of this 2D amorphous system. By examining the classical limit, we show that classical treatments substantially overestimate the TC of MAC; namely, the quantum TC is less than half of the classical value at room temperature and up to nearly an order of magnitude lower at low temperatures.

</details>

### [Ferroelectric hysteresis in singly aligned graphene-hBN moiré superlattices](http://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02338v1)

**Authors:** Bao Q. Tu, Tanweer Ahmed, Garen Avedissian, Suzanne Lancaster, Mayank Sharma, Kenji Watanabe, Takashi Taniguchi, Fèlix Casanova, Marco Gobbi, Luis E. Hueso

**Summary.** The researchers successfully demonstrated ferroelectric hysteresis in single-layer graphene/hBN moiré superlattices, proving that single-interface alignment is sufficient to induce this effect. By analyzing the $n_{ext} - E_{ext}$ phase space, they attributed the hysteresis to abnormal charge screening within the moiré band. This work establishes a promising, scalable platform for developing ultrathin, non-volatile electronic devices.

**Why it may be interesting.** While focused on condensed matter electronics, the study of emergent polarization and charge screening in moiré heterostructures touches upon concepts of broken symmetry and emergent order parameters relevant to quantum materials.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** To determine if ferroelectric hysteresis exists in single-layer graphene (SLG)-hBN moiré superlattices, contrasting with previous work on doubly-aligned systems.

**Main result.** A robust ferroelectric response was demonstrated in the SLG-hBN moiré superlattice, persisting from 2K to room temperature, suggesting a viable platform for non-volatile electronics.

**Method.** Electrical characterization using Hall measurements and sweeping the external electric field ($E_{ext}$) and carrier concentration ($n_{ext}$) to observe hysteretic behavior.

**Model / system.** The physical system is the SLG-hBN moiré superlattice, where the moiré potential dictates the electronic structure and charge screening mechanisms.

**Key observables.** Hysteretic behavior in sheet resistance ($R_S$) vs. $E_{ext}$, deviation of the Charge Neutrality Point (CNP) in the $n_{ext} - E_{ext}$ phase space, and the spontaneous polarization magnitude.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Temperature range (2K to 300K), external electric field ($E_{ext}$), and carrier concentration ($n_{ext}$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The hysteretic behavior is attributed to abnormal charge screening due to the moiré superlattice band structure, and that single alignment is sufficient to induce the effect.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate device schematics, $R_S$ vs. $n_{ext}$ and $n_{ext} - E_{ext}$ phase space diagrams showing hysteresis loops, and temperature dependence confirming the effect's stability.

**Paper structure.** The paper systematically investigates the ferroelectric response by comparing different alignment conditions, mapping the $n_{ext} - E_{ext}$ phase space, quantifying the hysteresis, and confirming the temperature stability of the effect.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Ferroelectric materials have the unique ability to maintain an electric polarization which can be reversed under an external applied electric field. This property makes them valuable for applications such as non-volatile random-access memories, transducers, actuators and electro optic modulators. Recently, emergent unconventional ferroelectricity has been demonstrated in moiré superlattices of bilayer graphene and hexagonal boron nitride (hBN) hosting non centrosymmetric stacking order. Whether this phenomenon is also present in noncentrosymmetric single layer graphene (SLG)-hBN moiré superlattices is still under debate. Here we demonstrate a ferroelectric response in an SLG-hBN moiré superlattice. Through Hall measurements, we pinpoint the origin of the hysteretic behavior to abnormal charge screening due to the moiré superlattice band and estimate the spontaneous polarization magnitude in the moiré superlattice structure. Temperature dependent measurements confirm that the hysteretic behavior persists from 2K up to room temperature, opening opportunities for high-mobility, ultrathin non-volatile devices

</details>

### [Universal Right-Hand Chirality of Evanescent Vector Fields](http://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01585v1)

**Authors:** Yilin Qing, Ji Zou, Yi-Pu Wang, Gerrit E. W. Bauer, Tao Yu  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2606.01585v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>
