**Summary.** This work establishes a universal physical law stating that any source-free, evanescent vector field must possess a fixed, right-handed chirality defined by the relationship between its propagation direction, spin, and surface normal. The authors derive a fundamental upper bound for this chirality, demonstrating that the absence of left-handed configurations underscores a deep physical non-equivalence between mirror-image wave states.

**Why it may be interesting.** The concept of universal chirality locking in fundamental wave phenomena (like surface waves) provides a powerful, model-independent diagnostic tool for distinguishing between source-free physical regimes and those containing internal sources or higher-order multipoles.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** To determine the general conditions under which an evanescent vector field possesses definite chirality and to prove a universal upper bound for this chirality.

**Main result.** The paper proves that any source-free, evanescent vector field must exhibit a rigid, right-handed chirality ($C_q > 0$), bounded by $C_q \le \sqrt{1 - \eta^2}$.

**Method.** The derivation uses the source-free condition ($
abla \cdot \mathbf{V} = 0$) applied to time-harmonic evanescent plane wave modes to constrain the relationship between propagation ($\hat{\mathbf{q}}$), spin ($\hat{\mathbf{S}}$), and surface normal ($\hat{\mathbf{n}}$).

**Model / system.** The analysis focuses on source-free, time-harmonic evanescent vector fields, applicable to various physical systems like surface plasmons, spin waves, and acoustic waves at interfaces.

**Key observables.** The chirality index, $C_q \equiv \hat{\mathbf{n}} \cdot (\hat{\mathbf{S}} 	imes \hat{\mathbf{q}})$, and the spin-orbit parameter, $\eta = \hat{\mathbf{q}} \cdot \hat{\mathbf{S}} / |\mathbf{q}||\mathbf{S}|$.

**Important parameters / regimes.** The chirality index $C_q$ is bounded by $\sqrt{1 - \eta^2}$; the analysis is simplified under the approximation $eta \ll |\mathbf{q}|$.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The field must be source-free ($
abla \cdot \mathbf{V} = 0$) and time-harmonic ($e^{-i\Omega t}$).

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 illustrates the universal right-handed rule and the general bound $0 \le C_q \le \sqrt{1 - \eta^2}$. Table I summarizes the chirality index for various prototypical evanescent waves, confirming the universal chirality.

**Paper structure.** The paper establishes the physical problem of chirality in evanescent waves, derives the mathematical constraints using the source-free condition, proves the universal right-handed nature and the upper bound, and discusses the physical implications of this chirality.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

A locking between propagation direction $\hat{\bf q}$, spin $\hat{\bf S}$, and surface or interface normal $\hat{\bf n}$ -- a phenomenon broadly termed \textit{chirality} -- pervades evanescent or surface waves in optics, magnetism, plasmonics, and acoustics. Yet, it is not known whether the phenomenon is universal or bound to conditions. Here, we unveil that any \textit{source-free} vector field that propagates along $\hat{\bf q}$ and is evanescent along $\hat{\bf n}$, the existence of a spin automatically enforces a rigid, right-handed locking among $\hat{\bf q}$, $\hat{\bf S}$, and $\hat{\bf n}$, that is characterized by a chirality index $C_{\bf q}\equiv \hat{\bf n}\cdot(\hat{\bf S}\times \hat{\bf q})>0$. For an arbitrary propagation direction defined by $\hat{\bf q}\cdot \hat{\bf S}\equiv η\in [-1,1]$, i.e., not necessarily transverse spin, we derive a fundamental upper bound $C_{\bf q}\le \sqrt{1-η^2}$. The universal absence of left-handed evanescent (source-less) vector fields underscores the fundamental physical non-equivalence of mirror-image configurations -- a principle echoed by parity violation, the natural excess of enantiomers in chiral molecules, and the fixed chirality of DNA.

</details>

**Summary.** This work develops a universal theoretical framework to calculate the domain-wall width in complex multi-sublattice magnets, unifying descriptions for ferro-, antiferro-, and ferrimagnetic orders. The key method links the DW width directly to the parameters of the long-wavelength spin-wave dispersion. The results provide quantitative agreement with atomistic simulations, offering a powerful tool for understanding magnetic textures across diverse spin systems.

**Why it may be interesting.** While focused on classical magnetism, the connection between macroscopic domain structure (DW width) and microscopic excitations (SW spectrum) is a fundamental problem in many-body dynamics, relevant to understanding thermal fluctuations in quantum magnetic materials.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper aims to derive a universal expression for the domain-wall (DW) width in complex multi-sublattice Heisenberg magnets, applicable across different magnetic ordering types.

**Main result.** A universal relation, $\delta_{DW} = \sqrt{A_{eta}/\epsilon_{eta}^0}$, is proposed that successfully unifies the description of DW width across ferromagnetic, antiferromagnetic, and ferrimagnetic systems.

**Method.** The derivation relies on an exact theoretical connection between the DW profile and the long-wavelength spin-wave (SW) dispersion relation.

**Model / system.** The study focuses on multi-sublattice Heisenberg magnets, modeled using Hamiltonians incorporating Heisenberg exchange and uniaxial anisotropy, tested on lattices like rock-salt, honeycomb, and kagome.

**Key observables.** Domain-wall width ($\delta_{DW}$), spin-wave spectrum parameters (gaps $\epsilon_{\pm}^0$ and stiffnesses $A_{\pm}$), and the temperature dependence of $\delta_{DW}$.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Exchange coupling constants ($J$), anisotropy constants ($D$), temperature ($T$), and the ratio $T/T_c$.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The approach assumes the validity of connecting the DW profile to the long-wavelength spin-wave theory, and it analyzes the system up to the magnetic ordering transition ($T_c$).

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the SW spectrum for various magnets, show the calculated DW width versus temperature, and display magnetization profiles confirming the theoretical predictions.

**Paper structure.** The paper establishes the problem by noting the failure of simple models, introduces the universal relation derived from SW theory, applies it to various lattice types (FM, AFM, FI), and validates the results against atomistic spin dynamics simulations, including temperature dependence.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We propose a universal expression for the domain-wall width in generic multi-sublattice Heisenberg magnets, applicable to ferro-, antiferro-, and ferrimagnetic orders. The result follows from an exact connection between the domain-wall profile and the long-wavelength spin-wave dispersion, yielding a unified framework for describing magnetic textures across distinct ordering types. The predictions show excellent quantitative agreement with large-scale atomistic spin dynamics simulations over a broad range of exchange and anisotropy values and spin multi-sublattice structures, including three-dimensional rock-salt-type magnets and two-dimensional honeycomb and kagome ferromagnets. Moreover, we establish a general microscopic foundation for the temperature dependence of the domain-wall width. Our approach offers a powerful tool for understanding domain-wall profiles in complex spin systems.

</details>

**Authors:** Kushagra Singh, Debasis Das  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2606.00744v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `spintronics-quantum-optics interface` **1/5**

Each pixel of the kernel is implemented using a SOT-MTJ bit cell along with two access transistors, shown in enlarged scale. (b) Electrical readout from the kernel, illustrating the three possible cur- rent levels—9IP for all-1 input (no edge), 9IAP for all-0 input (no edge), and intermediate summed current values for mixed inputs (edge detected). </figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper proposes an energy-efficient hardware accelerator for edge detection by leveraging the switching properties of SOT-MTJ magnetic tunnel junctions. By mapping the convolution operation onto the read/write cycles of these spintronic cells, it achieves low latency and significantly reduced power consumption compared to conventional CMOS-based algorithms. This work demonstrates a viable path for integrating advanced memory devices into real-time image processing hardware.

**Why it may be interesting.** While the core physics is spintronics/solid-state, the integration of device physics (LLGS, SOT) to solve a classical signal processing problem (edge detection) is highly relevant to emerging memory computing paradigms.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Traditional edge detection algorithms are too computationally expensive and energy-intensive for resource-constrained hardware.

**Main result.** The proposed spintronics-based approach using SOT-MTJ arrays achieves low-power, high-speed image processing, significantly outperforming standard methods like Canny detection.

**Method.** The method involves converting color images to grayscale, splitting pixel values into bit channels, and then performing convolution using the switching characteristics of SOT-MTJ cells.

**Model / system.** The system utilizes Magnetic Tunnel Junction (MTJ) devices integrated into an SOT-MTJ bit-cell array for in-memory computing. The physics is governed by the Landau-Lifshitz-Gilbert-Slonczewski (LLGS) equation, using Spin-Orbit Torque (SOT) for switching.

**Key observables.** Energy consumption (e.g., $1.5 	ext{ fJ}$ read), latency ($\sim 6 	ext{ ns}$ cycle time), and the resulting binary edge map derived from read current thresholds.

**Important parameters / regimes.** TMR (150%), $ho_{HM}$ ($100 	ext{ } \mu\Omega	ext{-cm}$), and the specific current levels corresponding to '1' (high current) and '0' (low current) inputs.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The read operation is designed to avoid read disturbance by applying a small voltage ($\sim 0.1 	ext{ V}$), and the analysis relies on specific material parameters for the MTJ structure.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 details the SOT-MTJ schematic and bit-cell operation. Figure 2 shows the image preprocessing pipeline (RGB to Grayscale to Bit Channels). Figure 3 illustrates the $3 	imes 3$ kernel convolution readout. Figure 4 compares the SOT-MTJ output against the conventional Canny output for test images.

**Paper structure.** The paper first establishes the limitations of classical edge detection. It then details the physical model of the SOT-MTJ device, including its switching physics and operational cycles. The core contribution is the implementation of the edge detection algorithm using these devices, followed by a quantitative performance comparison against established methods.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Traditional edge detection algorithms, foundational to computer vision, face significant challenges in energy efficiency and processing latency on conventional CMOS-based hardware. Existing algorithms, such as Canny, are computationally expensive, posing challenges in resource-constrained hardware where energy efficiency and low latency are critical. This study introduces a novel, hardware-efficient algorithm that leverages the intrinsic characteristics of magnetic tunnel junction (MTJ) devices. We present a detailed device-level analysis of an MTJ-based system for edge detection, outlining its operational cycles, including write, read, and reset methods. The algorithm's efficacy is evaluated against the standard Canny edge detection method. We provide a quantitative performance analysis, including metrics such as energy consumption and latency, which demonstrates that our proposed spintronics-based approach offers a promising solution for achieving low-power, high-speed image processing.

</details>

### [Enhanced Spin-to-Charge Conversion in Bi2Se3/NiFe via Interface Engineering with a Ti Spacer Layer](http://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01201v1)

**Authors:** Sourav Rajat Subhra Maitra, Poulami Manna, Ravi Prakash Singh, Chandrasekhar Murapaka, Arabinda Haldar  
**Type:** experiment · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2606.01201v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `non-equilibrium dynamics` **1/5**

</figure>

**Summary.** This work investigates how to boost spin-to-charge conversion in topological insulator spintronic devices by engineering the interface. By introducing a titanium spacer layer between $	ext{Bi}_2	ext{Se}_3$ and $	ext{NiFe}$, the researchers successfully prevented material interdiffusion, leading to a dramatic, order-of-magnitude enhancement in the spin Hall angle. This highlights the critical role of interface chemistry in realizing high-efficiency topological spintronic devices.

**Why it may be interesting.** While focused on condensed matter spintronics, the study's reliance on interface engineering, quantum confinement effects (surface states), and spin transport mechanisms (spin pumping) provides relevant experimental benchmarks for understanding open quantum systems dynamics at material interfaces.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The primary goal is to enhance the spin-to-charge conversion efficiency in spintronic devices utilizing topological insulators.

**Main result.** The insertion of a titanium spacer layer significantly enhances the spin Hall angle (SHA) by an order of magnitude compared to direct interfaces.

**Method.** The study uses spin pumping measurements via Ferromagnetic Resonance (FMR) spectroscopy and measures the resulting transverse voltage using the Inverse Spin Hall Effect (ISHE).

**Model / system.** The system is a sputter-deposited heterostructure: a topological insulator ($	ext{Bi}_2	ext{Se}_3$) coupled with a ferromagnetic film ($	ext{NiFe}$) separated by a titanium ($	ext{Ti}$) spacer layer, deposited on a silicon substrate.

**Key observables.** Spin Hall Angle (SHA), Gilbert damping parameter ($\alpha_{	ext{eff}}$), ISHE voltage ($V_{	ext{ISHE}}$), and magnetoresistance ($\Delta\sigma(H)$).

**Important parameters / regimes.** The optimal $	ext{Bi}_2	ext{Se}_3$ thickness appears to be around 4 nm, and the enhancement factor is an order of magnitude.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The enhancement is attributed to the $	ext{Ti}$ spacer layer suppressing detrimental interdiffusion between the $	ext{Bi}_2	ext{Se}_3$ and $	ext{NiFe}$ layers, thereby preserving the topological surface states.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate schematics of the spin-to-charge conversion setup, FMR spectra showing damping parameter changes with thickness, and ISHE voltage measurements comparing samples with and without the Ti spacer.

**Paper structure.** The paper systematically compares spin-to-charge conversion in $	ext{Bi}_2	ext{Se}_3/	ext{NiFe}$ versus $	ext{Bi}_2	ext{Se}_3/	ext{Ti}/	ext{NiFe}$ structures, analyzing thickness dependence, quantifying the SHA, and attributing the improvement to interface protection.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Topological insulators have attracted significant attention in spintronics due to their topological surface states and spin-momentum-locking-driven spin-to-charge conversion. Among these, Bi2Se3 has been extensively investigated because of its large bulk bandgap and single Dirac cone band structure. However, spin-to-charge conversion strongly depends on the quality of the topological insulator/ferromagnet interface. Here, we investigate spin-to-charge conversion in a sputter-deposited heterostructure comprising a topological insulator (Bi2Se3) and a ferromagnetic NiFe thin film separated by a titanium spacer layer. The Bi2Se3 layer is deposited on a silicon substrate for industrial compatibility. Pure spin current is injected into the Bi2Se3 layer through the titanium spacer via spin pumping induced by spin precession during microwave-driven ferromagnetic resonance of the ferromagnetic film. Spin pumping studies are performed by varying the Bi2Se3 thickness. The Gilbert damping parameter exhibits a significant 55% increase at a Bi2Se3 thickness of 4 nm, indicating a pure surface-state contribution. The spin Hall angle, which quantifies the spin-to-charge conversion efficiency, increases by an order of magnitude upon insertion of the titanium spacer layer. This enhancement is attributed to the suppression of interdiffusion between the Bi2Se3 and NiFe layers by titanium, thereby preserving the topological surface states. These findings highlight the important role of titanium spacer layers in spintronic devices based on topological materials.

</details>

### [High-quality Nano-patterning of Oxide Interfaces Using Transferred Gold Mask](http://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01345v1)

**Authors:** Qing Xiao, Yanling Liu, Changjian Ma, Danqing Liu, Zhiyuan Qin, Qianyi Zhao, Chengyuan Huang, Mengke Ha, Zhenhao Li, Guanglei Cheng  
**Type:** experiment · **Category:** strongly correlated electrons · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2606.01345v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

**Summary.** This work introduces a novel, solvent-free nanofabrication technique for complex oxide interfaces using transferred gold masks and oxygen-enriched ion milling. The method successfully patterns oxide heterostructures while preserving their high electronic quality, enabling the creation of high-performance nanoscale electronic devices for studying correlated phenomena.

**Why it may be interesting.** While the focus is on materials science, the ability to create highly controlled, clean nanoscale junctions in correlated oxides is crucial for realizing quantum devices, making the clean interface control relevant to quantum engineering.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The primary challenge is achieving high-quality, clean, and controlled nano-patterning of complex oxide interfaces, which are highly susceptible to contamination and defect formation during fabrication.

**Main result.** The developed solvent-free method using transferred gold masks and oxygen-enriched Ar+ milling successfully enables residue-free nanofabrication, preserving the intrinsic high carrier mobilities of the oxide interfaces.

**Method.** The technique involves depositing and patterning a transferable gold mask, transferring it onto the oxide sample, and then using oxygen-enriched Ar+ ion milling to pattern the oxide layer.

**Model / system.** The study focuses on complex oxide heterostructures, such as LAO/STO, which host rich correlated phenomena like 2DEG and metal-insulator transitions. The patterning is performed at the nanoscale to probe these intrinsic electronic properties.

**Key observables.** High carrier mobilities ($\mu$), sheet resistance ($R_s$), carrier density ($n$), and surface topography confirmed by AFM.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Feature sizes down to $\sim 100 	ext{ nm}$; $	ext{Ar}/	ext{O}_2$ gas mixture ratio (1:5); cryogenic temperatures (e.g., $2 	ext{ K}$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The method is assumed to be residue-free and that the patterned regions maintain the intrinsic electronic quality of the pristine interfaces.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the step-by-step Au mask transfer process (top-down approach) and show AFM images confirming pattern transfer and surface quality. Transport measurements compare the performance of devices patterned using the Au mask versus conventional methods.

**Paper structure.** The paper details the fabrication process using transferred Au masks combined with oxygen-enriched Ar+ milling. It presents transport measurements comparing the resulting device performance against other patterning techniques, demonstrating the method's success in preserving high-quality electronic properties.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Complex oxide interfaces, such as \mathrm{SrTiO_3} and \mathrm{KTaO_3} based heterostructures, host rich correlated phenomena with strong potential for advanced device applications. However, these interfaces are extremely susceptible to contamination and defect formation during nanofabrication, which often compromises device performance. Here, we present a solvent-free method for patterning oxide interfaces by employing high-resolution transferable thin metal masks in conjunction with oxygen-enriched Ar+ milling, which enables a clean and well-controlled nanofabrication process. Transport measurements demonstrate that the fabricated devices preserve their intrinsic properties, including high carrier mobilities, with negligible degradation compared to the pristine interfaces. This technique offers a convenient and robust route for engineering high-performance oxide electronic devices with precisely tailored transport characteristics.

</details>

**Summary.** This work uses resonant Raman spectroscopy to probe the temperature dependence of lattice dynamics in bilayer 3R-MoS2. By correlating changes in excitonic transitions with phonon scattering intensities, the authors demonstrate that the Raman response is dictated by resonant coupling rather than simple thermal equilibrium. This offers deep insight into exciton-phonon interactions in van der Waals materials.

**Why it may be interesting.** The strong coupling between electronic excitations (excitons) and lattice vibrations (phonons) under resonant conditions provides a rich platform for studying non-equilibrium dynamics and energy transfer in low-dimensional quantum materials.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The study investigates the temperature-dependent resonant Raman scattering response in bilayer 3R-MoS2, focusing on how excitonic resonances govern lattice dynamics.

**Main result.** The resonant Raman response is strongly governed by the interplay between incoming and outgoing exciton resonances, leading to deviations from simple thermal population models, particularly in the anti-Stokes/Stokes intensity ratio.

**Method.** The research combines multi-wavelength Raman spectroscopy, photoluminescence measurements, and DFT calculations to analyze temperature evolution.

**Model / system.** The physical system is bilayer 3R-MoS2, a transition metal dichalcogenide. The analysis models temperature dependence using the Varshni relation for excitonic energy shifts and the Boltzmann relation for intensity ratios.

**Key observables.** Raman intensity (Stokes/anti-Stokes), effective phonon temperature (T*), excitonic transition energies (XA and XB), and the temperature dependence of these quantities.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Temperature range (5–310 K), excitation laser energies (e.g., 1.96 eV), and the material structure (3R stacking).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis assumes that the observed deviations in the intensity ratio are due to resonant scattering mechanisms rather than solely thermal population effects.

**Figures summary.** Figures show optical microscopy, temperature evolution of PL spectra, and the energy dependence of XA/XB excitons. Other figures illustrate phonon dispersion, temperature-dependent Stokes/anti-Stokes intensities, and the effective temperature derived from the intensity ratio.

**Paper structure.** The paper systematically analyzes the temperature dependence of excitons (Varshni fitting), then examines the resulting changes in the Stokes/anti-Stokes intensity ratio, culminating in the demonstration that resonance conditions dictate the observed phonon temperature.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Raman scattering is a powerful spectroscopic technique widely employed to investigate light-matter interactions and lattice dynamics in two-dimensional materials. Here, we investigate the temperature-dependent resonant Raman response of bilayer 3R-MoS$_2$. The study combines multi-wavelength Raman spectroscopy, photoluminescence measurements, and density functional theory calculations to track the evolution of excitonic transitions and resonance conditions. We observe contributions from both zone-centre and finite-momentum phonons, a pronounced quenching of the Stokes intensity at low temperatures followed by saturation, the emergence of anti-Stokes scattering above 130~K, and a strong deviation of the effective phonon temperature from the lattice temperature induced by resonance effects. These results demonstrate that the Raman response is governed by the interplay between incoming and outgoing resonance processes, providing deeper insight into exciton-phonon coupling in van der Waals materials.

</details>

### [Electronic Hall viscosity: hidden indicator for antiferromagnets](http://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01003v1)

**Authors:** Ding Li, Tao Qin, Jianhui Zhou  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2606.01003v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>

(a) the type-III configuration of Mn3Sn and (b) selected nonzero com- ponents of the EHV. (c) the type-IV configuration of Mn3Sn and (d) selected nonzero components of the EHV.</figcaption>
</figure>

**Summary.** This work introduces the Electronic Hall Viscosity (EHV) as a novel, fundamental quantum geometric quantity to probe antiferromagnetism. It shows that EHV is related to the quadrupole Berry curvature and provides symmetry-dictated rules to detect magnetic order even when standard measurements fail. The theory is validated using DFT calculations on materials like RuO2 and Mn3Sn, offering a new design principle for spintronic devices.

**Why it may be interesting.** While focused on condensed matter magnetism, the rigorous use of Berry curvature, symmetry analysis, and the introduction of a novel transport tensor (EHV) provides a sophisticated theoretical framework for characterizing quantum geometric properties in ordered magnetic systems.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper addresses the challenge of detecting and characterizing the nontrivial Berry curvature in antiferromagnets, which is obscured by vanishing magnetization and anomalous Hall conductivity.

**Main result.** Electronic Hall Viscosity (EHV) is proposed as a new, fundamental quantum geometry quantity that can characterize antiferromagnetic ordering even when the linear anomalous Hall response is forbidden.

**Method.** The authors derive theoretical relationships between EHV and the quadrupole Berry curvature, establishing symmetry constraints using Magnetic Point Groups and calculating bounds using quantum volume moments.

**Model / system.** The study focuses on generic antiferromagnets, exemplified by the d-wave altermagnet RuO2 and the noncollinear antiferromagnet Mn3Sn, analyzed using first-principle calculations.

**Key observables.** Electronic Hall Viscosity (EHV, $\eta_{\gamma\delta\lambda}$), Berry curvature ($\Omega_{\alpha}$), and the quadrupole moment of the Berry curvature.

**Important parameters / regimes.** The analysis involves symmetry operations (e.g., $C'_{n
u}$) and is applicable in the long-wavelength limit ($\lim_{\omega	o 0}\lim_{q	o 0}$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The derivation relies on classifying constraints using Magnetic Laue Groups (MLGs) and assumes the applicability of the theory to generic antiferromagnets.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the relationship between EHV components and symmetry operations for specific materials like RuO2 and Mn3Sn, showing how different magnetic configurations yield distinct, non-zero EHV tensors.

**Paper structure.** The paper progresses from identifying the detection limitation in antiferromagnets to deriving the EHV as a diagnostic tool. It then establishes the mathematical relationship between EHV and the Berry curvature's quadrupole moment, derives symmetry constraints based on crystal/magnetic symmetries, and finally validates these findings using DFT calculations on model materials.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

The antiferromagnets with negligible stray fields and ultrafast spin dynamics play a crucial role in the fields of energy-efficient spintronics and topological electronics. However, the detection and control of the underlying nontrivial Berry curvature become extremely limited by the vanishing magnetization and anomalous Hall conductivity. Here, we show the electronic Hall viscosity is closely related to the quadruple Berry curvature of Bloch bands and is bounded by the $d$-orbit factor modulated second moment of the quantum volume. Moreover, we derive the symmetry requirement for nonzero electronic Hall viscosity that could characterize antiferromagnetic ordering even when the linear anomalous Hall response gets forbidden. We further examine our key findings in two archetypal antiferromagnets: $d$-wave altermagnet $\mathrm{RuO}_{2}$, and noncollinear $\mathrm{Mn_{3}Sn}$ through direct first-principle calculations. Thus, our work reveals a new and fundamental quantum geometry quantity of generic antiferromagnets and offers a broadly applicable way to design antiferromagnetic spintronics devices via unconventional Hall viscosity.

</details>

**Summary.** This work develops a novel, computationally efficient method to model entangled polymer networks by distilling complex Coarse-Grained Molecular Dynamics (CGMD) data into simplified Discrete Network Models (DNMs). By quantifying local entanglements using the Gaussian Linking Number, the authors create a predictive framework that accurately predicts mechanical stress while drastically reducing computational cost.

**Why it may be interesting.** While focused on polymer physics, the methodology of distilling complex, high-dimensional dynamics (CGMD) into a low-dimensional, predictive topological model (DNM) shares conceptual parallels with coarse-graining techniques used in many-body quantum systems.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The primary challenge is relating the microscopic physics of transient polymer entanglements to the macroscopic mechanical properties of elastomers and gels.

**Main result.** The proposed Discrete Network Model (DNM) successfully reproduces the small-strain virial stress predictions of the full CGMD model with a 97% reduction in computational cost.

**Method.** The authors introduce a topological distillation algorithm that converts detailed CGMD simulations into simplified DNMs by identifying load-transmitting primitive paths using the Gaussian Linking Number and a Center of Entanglement.

**Model / system.** The system is entangled polymer networks (elastomers/gels) modeled using Coarse-Grained Molecular Dynamics (CGMD) simulations based on the Kremer-Grest model. The analysis focuses on topological constraints (entanglements) and their mechanical consequences.

**Key observables.** Gaussian Linking Number ($\Theta$), Geometric Center of Entanglement (COE), virial stress ($\sigma$), and the alignment of time-averaged chain forces ($\langle\hat{f} \cdot \hat{r}_pangle$).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Packing fraction ($\phi$), Kuhn length ($b$), and the ratio of simulation time to relaxation time ($ar{	au}_R$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The COE is hypothesized to be the physical location through which entropic chain forces are transmitted, and the DNM accurately captures the essential mechanical physics despite neglecting full chain sliding.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the distillation process from CGMD to DNM, show the alignment of entropic forces along primitive paths, and compare stress-strain curves between the two models at different stretches.

**Paper structure.** The paper progresses by first defining the physical problem (entanglement ambiguity), developing the quantitative tools (GLN, COE), applying these tools to define local entanglements and primitive paths, and finally validating the resulting reduced-order DNM against full CGMD simulations for mechanical prediction.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Physical entanglements between polymer chains enhance the moduli, strength, and toughness of elastomers and gels, yet relating entanglement micromechanics to macroscopic mechanical benefits remains difficult. Experimentally investigating entanglements is challenging due to their nanoscale sizes, subsurface locations, and chemical indistinguishability from their surroundings. Computationally mapping structure-property relations is costly when using physics-based models that enable direct entanglement observation, such as coarse-grained molecular dynamics (CGMD). Entanglements are also transient, configuration-dependent features without clear quantitative definitions. To address this ambiguity, we introduce an approach that quantitatively defines local entanglements along simulated polymer backbones using the Gaussian Linking Number, and introduce a geometric center of entanglement verified to represent the position through which entropic chain forces are transmitted via Kremer-Grest CGMD simulations. Unlike existing approaches, which output a single linking number for chain pairs, our method identifies the multitude of load-transmitting inter- and intra-chain entanglements along a polymer's backbone. To bridge scales, we introduce a topological distillation algorithm that converts entangled CGMD networks into representative discrete network models (DNMs), representing entanglements as vertices and primitive paths as load-transmitting edges. Our DNMs reproduce small-strain virial stress predictions of the Kremer-Grest model with a 97% reduction in computational cost, verifying both physical accuracy and computational efficiency. This distillation procedure will facilitate physics-based, predictive modeling of entangled network mechanics, from polymers to architected metamaterials.

</details>

**Authors:** Sungjin An, Zhuo Bin Siu, Vardan Kaladzhyan, Jens H. Bardarson, Sunghun Lee, Myoung-Jae Lee, Kidong Park, Jeunghee Park, Mansoor B. A. Jalil, Jungpil Seo, Minkyung Jung

**Summary.** This paper reports on the observation of quantized conductance in Cd3As2 Dirac semimetal nanostructures, using suspended p-n junctions. By comparing conductance plateaus in different geometries, the authors distinguish between spin-resolved subband transport ($1e^2/h$) and surface-state-dominated conduction ($2e^2/h$). The findings establish the material as a promising platform for quantum electronics.

**Why it may be interesting.** The work provides experimental evidence of quantum transport phenomena (quantization, MR) in a topological material, linking macroscopic transport measurements to underlying electronic band structure physics (Dirac/Weyl fermions).

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The study aims to observe and characterize quantized conductance in high-quality three-dimensional Dirac semimetal Cd3As2 nanowire/nanoribbon p-n junctions.

**Main result.** The conductance quantization exhibits different plateaus ($1e^2/h$ vs $2e^2/h$) depending on the nanostructure width, suggesting spin-resolved subband transport in narrower devices and surface-state-dominated conduction generally.

**Method.** The research combines low-temperature, high-field transport measurements (four-terminal geometry) with detailed numerical simulations based on a low-energy effective Hamiltonian.

**Model / system.** The physical system is Cd3As2, a 3D Dirac semimetal, fabricated into suspended nanowire/nanoribbon p-n junctions. The theoretical modeling uses a low-energy effective Hamiltonian incorporating orbital and Zeeman effects.

**Key observables.** Quantized conductance plateaus at integer multiples of $e^2/h$ or $2e^2/h$, Magnetoresistance (MR) measurements (positive for $\vec{E}\perp\vec{B}$, negative for $\vec{E}\parallel\vec{B}$), and extracted effective Landé $g$-factor ($\sim 43$).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Low temperature ($T \approx 2	ext{ K}$), high magnetic fields ($	ext{B} = 9	ext{ T}$), and geometric confinement (nanowire vs. wide nanoribbon).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis assumes that the observed quantization is dominated by surface states, and the use of four-terminal measurements successfully eliminates contact resistance effects.

**Figures summary.** Figures show TEM images of the material, SEM images of the fabricated suspended devices, and differential conductance maps/plots illustrating the quantized conductance plateaus and magnetoresistance dependence on magnetic field.

**Paper structure.** The paper details the experimental setup (device fabrication, transport measurements) to observe quantized conductance and MR in Cd3As2 junctions. It then uses theoretical modeling and simulation results to interpret the observed differences in quantization plateaus ($1e^2/h$ vs $2e^2/h$) based on confinement effects and surface state physics.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We report the observation of quantized conductance in high-mobility three-dimensional Dirac semimetal Cd$_3$As$_2$ nanowire and nanoribbon p-n junctions. By employing suspended device geometries with dual local gates, we form tunable p-n junctions and realize ballistic transport across sub-micron channel lengths. In a wide nanoribbon device with a channel width of $\sim 330$ nm, conductance plateaus appear at integer multiples of $2e^2/h$ in the n-n regime under high magnetic fields. Numerical simulations suggest that these features represent unresolved spin-split subbands due to the smaller subband spacing in wider channels, and support the interpretation that the observed quantization may originate from surface-state-dominated conduction. In contrast, narrower nanoribbons and nanowires exhibit conductance steps of $1e^2/h$, demonstrating spin-resolved subbands likely due to enhanced confinement effects. From spin-resolved subband spectroscopy, we extract an effective Landé $g$-factor of $\sim 43$ for the first subband in the bulk gap, establishing these nanostructures as a prospective platform for fault-tolerant quantum electronics.

</details>

<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 4.</b> FIG. 4. Variation of OH conductivity with EF for different values of temperature is shown in (a). The Orbital Nernst (ON) conductivity for different values of JA with respect to the Fermi energy is displayed in (b).</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2606.01404_figures/2606.01404_fig5.jpg" alt="Figure 5">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 5.</b> FIG. 5. Variation of SH conductivity with EF for different values of temperature is shown in (a). The spin Nernst (SN) conductivity for different values of λR with respect to the Fermi energy is displayed in (b).</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This theoretical work explores quantized orbital and spin Hall transport in 2D materials by modeling the interplay between $sp$-hybridization, altermagnetism, and SOC. The key finding is that the Hall responses become quantized over a specific Fermi energy window when magnetic order is tuned relative to hybridization scales. This quantization is microscopically rooted in the Berry curvature of the occupied electronic states.

**Why it may be interesting.** The focus on topological transport phenomena (Hall effects) driven by orbital and spin degrees of freedom, and the explicit modeling of hybridization and magnetic order, connects to modern condensed matter physics research areas.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper investigates the emergence and quantization of orbital and spin Hall effects in systems beyond standard L-S coupling, focusing on the interplay between $sp$-hybridization, altermagnetism (AM) order, and spin-orbit coupling (SOC).

**Main result.** Quantized orbital and spin Hall responses are found when the AM order strength is tuned relative to the $sp$-hybridization scale, with the quantization being robustly linked to Fermi surface-activated Berry curvatures.

**Method.** The study employs theoretical calculations using a two-dimensional model Hamiltonian to compute orbital and spin Berry curvatures and corresponding Hall/Nernst conductivities.

**Model / system.** The model is a 2D Hamiltonian incorporating $sp$-hybridization, Rashba SOC ($\lambda_R$), and AM order ($J_A$) in a basis set spanning orbital and spin degrees of freedom.

**Key observables.** Orbital Hall (OH) and Spin Hall (SH) conductivities ($\sigma_{OH}, \sigma_{SH}$), and Orbital/Spin Nernst coefficients ($\sigma_{ON}, \sigma_{SN}$).

**Important parameters / regimes.** AM order strength ($J_A$), Rashba SOC ($\lambda_R$), $sp$-hybridization strength ($\gamma$), and Fermi energy ($E_F$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis relies on a two-dimensional model Hamiltonian and assumes the quantization mechanism is governed by Fermi surface-activated Berry curvatures.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate band structures showing angular momentum distributions, and plots show the quantized plateau structures of OH and SH conductivities as functions of Fermi energy and various coupling strengths.

**Paper structure.** The paper systematically analyzes the Hamiltonian, calculates the relevant Berry curvatures, and then computes the transport coefficients ($\sigma_{OH}, \sigma_{SH}, \sigma_{ON}, \sigma_{SN}$) across different parameter regimes to demonstrate the quantization.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We here explore the emergence of orbital and spin Hall effects, originating beyond the $L$-$S$ coupling, and investigate the interplay between inter-orbit hybridization, relativistic Rashba spin-orbit coupling (SOC), and non-relativistic SOC, namely altermagnetic (AM) order, in a two-dimensional model Hamiltonian. The orbital (spin) Hall responses are remarkably found to be quantized within a window of Fermi energy when the strength of AM order (Rashba SOC) exceeds (falls below) the scale set by $sp$-hybridization. Importantly, orbital and spin Hall quantizations are independent of Rashba SOC and AM order, respectively, while the uniform profiles of finite orbital and vanishingly small spin moments of bands around the Fermi energy. The microscopic origin of such quantization comes from the Fermi surface-activated orbital and spin Berry curvatures. The extent of the quantized regime is strongly controlled by the intra-orbital coupling strength. As the temperature increases, the quantization is significantly compromised in the spin Hall case. We extend our analysis to the orbital and spin Nernst coefficients where the pronounced dip-peak structures signal the existence of the quantization leading to experimental relevance.

</details>

<a id="paper-2606.00894"></a>
### [Structure, Composition, and High-Field Superconductivity in Metal-Rich $\mathrmη$-Carbide-Type Compounds](http://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00894v1)

**Authors:** Manuele Balestra, KeYuan Ma, Harald O. Jeschke, Fabian O. von Rohr  
**Type:** both · **Category:** strongly correlated electrons · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2606.00894v1>

**Summary.** This review synthesizes research on $\eta$-carbide superconductors, highlighting their structural tunability and unusual superconducting properties. The core focus is explaining why their upper critical fields often exceed theoretical limits, pointing towards strong electronic correlations and symmetry-protected pairing mechanisms. The combination of experimental measurements and advanced DFT calculations provides a comprehensive picture of this fascinating class of quantum materials.

**Why it may be interesting.** The investigation into Pauli-limit violation and the role of SOC in determining $H_{c2}$ is highly relevant to understanding unconventional superconductivity, a key area in condensed matter physics.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper reviews the superconducting properties of $\eta$-carbide-type compounds, focusing on understanding the mechanisms behind their high upper critical fields ($H_{c2}$) which often violate the weak-coupling Pauli limit.

**Main result.** These materials exhibit superconductivity governed by complex electronic structures, and the large $H_{c2}$ values suggest unconventional pairing mechanisms, potentially involving strong spin-orbit coupling or multiband superconductivity.

**Method.** The work combines systematic experimental characterization (transport, magnetization, specific heat) with advanced theoretical calculations, including DFT incorporating spin-orbit coupling (SOC) and $k_p$ theory.

**Model / system.** The physical system is the $\eta$-carbide-type compound ($A_4B_2X$), a class of metal-rich quantum materials with high crystallographic symmetry ($Fdar{3}m$). The electronic structure is complex, featuring multiple $d$ bands near the Fermi level.

**Key observables.** Superconducting transition temperature ($T_c$), upper critical field ($H_{c2}(0)$), specific-heat jump ($\Delta C/\gamma T_c$), and the effective electronic $g$-factor.

**Important parameters / regimes.** The Pauli paramagnetic limit ($\mu_0H_{	ext{Pauli}}$), crystal symmetry, and the degree of spin-orbit coupling (SOC).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis often assumes the applicability of BCS theory modified by strong coupling or that the observed $H_{c2}$ enhancement is due to SOC or FFLO states.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the crystal structure, map the chemical phase space of superconductors, and show $H_{c2}(T)$ curves, often comparing experimental data against the theoretical Pauli limit.

**Paper structure.** The paper follows a review structure, first introducing the material class and synthesis challenges, then detailing superconducting diagnostics ($T_c, H_{c2}$), and finally delving into theoretical explanations for the high critical fields using DFT and advanced pairing models.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

$\mathrmη$-Carbide-type compounds have recently emerged as a diverse class of materials in the study of superconductivity. These phases contribute to a growing family of metal-rich quantum materials that exhibit unusual superconducting properties emerging from complex metallic bonding. Several members of the $\mathrmη$-carbide-type phases have been found to be bulk superconductors -- such as Nb$_4$Rh$_2$C$_{1-δ}$, Ta$_4$Rh$_2$C$_{1-δ}$, Ti$_4$Ir$_2$O$_{1-δ}$, and Ti$_4$Co$_2$O$_{1-δ}$ -- with transition temperatures up to $T_{\rm c} \approx$ 10 K and upper critical fields as high as $μ_0 H_{\rm c2}(0) \approx$ 30 T. Whereas the transition temperatures may fall within the range typical for intermetallic superconductors, the pronounced violation of the weak-coupling Pauli limit in many of these crystallographically high-symmetry materials is noteworthy. Here, we review recent progress on superconducting $\mathrmη$-carbide-type phases, emphasizing how crystal symmetry, synthetic challenges, transition-metal composition, and electronic structure govern their superconducting properties. Furthermore, we outline open questions and future directions, including the possible discovery of new $\mathrmη$-carbide-type materials.

</details>
